actions.thought: UTG in a 6-handed game requires a tight opening range. 5h9h is a speculative suited hand with low cards and a 3-gap, not strong enough for an open-raise from early position. Risk of multiway pots out of position with marginal equity. Standard play is to fold and wait for better spots to build the stack over 150 hands.
Q5o is a very weak hand and does not meet the criteria for an opening range from middle position in a 6-max game. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to conserve chips.

Unopened pot in the cutoff. 9d7d is a solid suited, connected hand that plays well in position and benefits from fold equity versus the blinds and button. I’ll use a standard small open to pressure the blinds while keeping my risk controlled deep-stacked (100bb).

A2o on the button facing a 2.5x raise from cutoff. Marginal hand but I have position. Folding is too tight, 3-betting puts me in awkward spots vs 4-bets. Calling to play in position and see a flop is the best line here.

I have KK in the small blind facing a CO open to 250 with the button calling. This is a premium hand — I want to build the pot and narrow the field since I’ll be out of position postflop. A min-raise to 400 is too small and will invite multiway action; I’ll 3-bet to a size that extracts value and discourages callers. I raise to 1000 total (≈4x the open) — large enough to charge speculative hands and isolate the opener. If I face a 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies; if called I plan to continue applying pressure on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ah8c. The action has been heavy: a raise from the Cutoff to 2.5BB, a call from the Button, and then a substantial 3-bet squeeze from the Small Blind to 10BB (1000 chips). Facing a cold 4-bet situation or a call, my hand is significantly dominated by the Small Blind's 3-betting range (likely strong Aces, pairs 99+). Furthermore, the original raiser and caller are still left to act behind me, creating a high risk of a 4-bet or multi-way action where I would be out of position with a marginal off-suit hand. The reverse implied odds are high if I hit an Ace. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

We’re facing a SB squeeze to 1000 after our CO open and a BTN flat, which creates a tough sandwich dynamic: if we call, the Button still has position and can continue, leaving us in a low-SPR 3-bet pot with a speculative hand that prefers higher SPR to realize implied odds. 9d7d has decent raw equity versus a wide squeeze range, but realization is poor here and reverse-implied odds are real (dominated flushes/straights, tough postflop). A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive since we hold no A/K blockers and risk getting played back early with no history. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

Facing a 3-bet from the SB after I flatted a raise with A2o. While I have position, A2o is too weak to continue here. When I hit an ace I'm often dominated, and the hand has poor playability. Getting 2.1:1 isn't enough for this marginal hand. Disciplined fold.

I am UTG with a small pocket pair (22). In a 6-max game, this is a marginal opening hand, but with a 100BB stack, it is worth playing for its set-mining potential and to represent a strong range from early position. I will open for a standard 2.5x the big blind.

Facing an UTG open to 2.5x in a 6-max configuration, 5-3 offsuit is far too weak to continue profitably: it’s dominated when it makes a pair, has poor playability, and lacks the suited/connected qualities needed to defend. A 3-bet bluff here is unnecessary this early and from this position versus a strong UTG range. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.
T6o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG raise. UTG raises typically represent strong ranges, and T6o doesn't play well enough to call profitably. Easy fold.

A♣J♣ on the button is a strong, positionally advantaged hand vs an UTG open to 250. I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener, and get some fold equity rather than letting the blinds come along and create a multiway pot where my equity is harder to realize. A 3-bet also gets value from worse A and broadway hands and protects against small pairs and speculative hands. I’ll raise to a size that pressures the blinds and the opener but doesn’t commit my stack — 900 total (≈3.6x the open). This keeps a healthy SPR (~9–10k behind) and allows me to continue postflop in position; if I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an over-aggressive 4-bet from UTG).

I hold K7s in the Small Blind. The action involves an UTG open to 250 and a 3-bet from the Button to 900. My hand is significantly dominated by the ranges of both the UTG opener and the Button 3-bettor (which likely includes AK, KQs, TT+). Furthermore, playing out of position against two aggressors with a marginal hand is a losing strategy. The pot odds do not justify a call, and a 4-bet bluff is too risky this early in the tournament without a better blocker or dynamic. Therefore, the only correct play is to fold.

Preflop in BB with KJo offsuit facing a button 3-bet to 900 over an UTG open to 250. Pot is 1300, to call 800 for ~38% immediate pot odds, but postflop OOP against an aggressive button 3-bettor (Minni showed 3-bet willingness last hand). KJo has mediocre equity (~30-40%) vs typical polarized button 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, suited Ax, connectors), poor playability OOP, and UTG opener still to act behind (may call/4-bet). Deep stacks favor folding marginal hands OOP to avoid tough spots. Fold preserves stack for better opportunities.

I opened with a minimum pocket pair (22) from UTG and have been met with a sizable 3-bet (3.6x) from the Button. While set-mining is possible with 100BB stacks, calling 650 more out of position with the smallest pair is generally a losing play in the long run. The implied odds are barely sufficient, and I will be forced to fold on almost every flop where I do not hit a deuce. I will play it safe and fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K5 offsuit is too weak to open UTG 6-handed: dominated by better Kx, poor playability multiway, and we’re out of position versus aggressive 3-bettors (notably Minni). Limping is also bad since it invites isolation and puts us in tough spots. Preserve stack and wait for better UTG holdings.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand in middle position. Not strong enough to open or limp. Standard fold.

Cutoff with Q3s — a marginal but suited hand with some playability. With UTG and MP folded and three players to act (button + blinds), this is a reasonable steal spot. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300) to generate fold equity and avoid limp-induced multiway pots. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; if I get called I’ll c-bet heads-up and play cautiously if it goes multiway.

I am on the Button with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). The Cutoff (Minni) has opened to 300 (3bb). Minni has shown aggression in previous hands, suggesting a potentially wide range, but K2o is simply too weak to defend with. It plays poorly post-flop, is often dominated by better Kings, and has no connectivity. Calling is -EV, and 3-bet bluffing is not optimal with this specific holding compared to other bluff candidates. The standard play is to fold.

A6s is a strong hand for SB defense vs CO raise. Suited ace with good blocker to AA/AK, solid equity when called. Prefer 3-betting OOP for initiative, fold equity (esp vs aggressive Minni who has shown 3-bet tendencies but folds to resistance), and to deny equity. Minni's CO range wide, button folded weakening their position. BB light defense range. Sizing to 900 (3x open) standard pot-committing lightly deepstacked early tournament. Calling passive OOP, folding too tight.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 8h. Minni opened to 300 from the Cutoff, and Elon 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind. 98 offsuit is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, as it has poor equity against a range likely consisting of high pairs and strong Broadway cards. Calling 800 more to see a flop out of position against a strong range is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 from the cutoff with Q3s as a steal. Elon (SB) 3-bet to 900 — a standard mid-sized 3-bet, not an all-in or huge polar size. Calling the extra 600 into a 1300 pot requires ~32% equity, and Q3s has reasonable playability (backdoor spade/straight outs, can realize equity and hit a Q). 4-betting with this hand is too thin and folding surrenders position and equity. I’ll call to play the flop in position, c-bet selectively on favourable textures, continue when I hit or have strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression when clearly dominated.

Preflop 3-bet with A6o as a strong suited ace blocker play OOP for initiative and fold equity, which worked as BB folded. Heads-up vs Minni's CO calling range post-3bet (likely Ax, broadway, pairs, some suited connectors). Flop T53 rainbow is very dry, misses most of her range, favors my ace-high as likely best hand with A-blocker. No draws hit hard, low fold equity risk but high c-bet frequency standard as preflop aggressor OOP on this board texture. Bet 2/3 pot for value from worse Ax/pairs, fold equity vs floats/misses, protect equity. Deep stacks early tournament, can fold to raise. Sizing 1200 commits ~13BB into 38BB pot, standard.

I have Q♠3♠ and flopped a bottom pair (pair of 3s) with a decent queen kicker. Elon was the preflop 3-bettor so his range is stronger than an open-raise range, but his small c-bet (1,200 into a 3,100 pot) is consistent with a wide continuation strategy. Calling 1,200 gives me ~28% break-even equity (1,200 / 4,300), which my hand can reasonably achieve in position and lets me realize equity and control pot size. Raising would be a thin bluff that can be punished by better hands and by reraises; folding is too conservative given my showdown value and position. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve, and extract value when I pair up or the board presents favorable runouts.

Turn Jack of hearts is a bad card for my range: completes straight draws like J-T-9-8 or Q-9-x, adds flush draw potential with two hearts on board (5h-Jh), and pairs any Jx villain might have. My A6o remains ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw. Villain's CO calling range post-3bet/call flop includes Tx, Jx, medium pairs, Ax that called flop c-bet on dry T53 board; many improve or semi-bluff now. As preflop/flop aggressor OOP deepstacked early tournament, betting thin risks raises from better hands/draws, low fold equity on this coordinated board. Checking realizes equity safely: pot control with marginal showdown value (ace blocker beats her worse Ax/floats), induces bluffs from misses, check-behind from cautious medium strength, easy fold to bets. Maximizes EV long-term by avoiding spew.

Elon 3-bet preflop and c-bet the flop but checked the turn. I have Q♠3♠ — only bottom pair with a decent queen kicker and roughly 6 outs (~13%) to improve on the river. I’m in position and the turn check looks like either missed c-bet or pot control from a medium/strong holding. Betting or raising here is thin: I have little fold equity and risk getting it in against better pairs or a check-raise. Best line is pot control and to realize equity — check and see the river. Plan: if I pair up on the river (Q or 3) bet for value; if the river is a blank, check back or fold to large aggression and only call small probes.

River 3d pairs the 3c for a low board pair, potentially improving villain's calling range (e.g., 3x trips, Tx pairing board, Jx two-pair) while missing my air entirely (ace-high no pair/draw). Preflop 3-bet/c-bet flop/check turn line represents polarized range: strong value (sets, two-pair, overpairs, AT+/AJ) or bluffs (suited Ax, connectors). Ace blocker still valuable for thins/value calls but showdown value marginal vs Minni's CO calling range (Tx, Jx, Ax, 77-QQ, draws that checked turn). OOP first, deepstacked early (hand 3/150), pot control essential: betting risks calls from better/semi-bluffs, low fold equity on paired/coordinated board; checking induces villain value bets/thins (easy fold), bluffs (hero-call Ace blocker), or check-behind (free card avoided post-turn). Maximizes EV by realizing equity safely, avoiding spew into villain's capped range strengthened by river.

I hold Q♠3♠ — the river 3 gives me trips (3♠3♣3♦) with a decent queen kicker. Elon was the preflop 3-bettor and c-bet the flop, but checked both the turn and the river. That line is consistent with a lot of medium-strength or pot-control hands; full houses that beat me (pocket TT/JJ/55 or combos like J3/T3) are possible, but his passive turn/river makes them somewhat less likely than a thin value or missed-continue hand.

I want to extract value from the many one-pair hands and missed broadway draws that will call a reasonably sized river bet, while keeping the price manageable if I run into a full house. Betting about half the pot gives me good value against worse hands and is small enough to fold to a large check-raise (which would usually indicate a full house). Pot is 4,300 — I’ll bet roughly half (2,150) to get called by pairs/draws and protect my hand.

If Elon flat-calls I expect to be ahead of many single-pair hands; if he check-raises big I will strongly suspect a full house and will fold/re-evaluate accordingly.

Facing a river bet of 2150 into a 6450 pot (~3:1 odds) after checking through turn-river OOP with Ah6s (ace-high, heart blocker). Preflop 3-bet SB vs CO open, c-bet dry T53 flop as aggressor, check coordinated Jh turn and river to realize equity, induce bluffs, pot control. Board pairs 3s on river, helping villain's trips/sets/Tx/Jx value range, but also caps it—no obvious nuts. Minni's CO calling range (Ax, broadway, pairs, suited connectors) post-3bet/flop call includes many missed draws (flush/straight chases like 89xx, Q9xx), worse Ax (A5/A4/Ax low), floats turning into bluffs. Ah blocks her best Ax, giving decent showdown equity (~30-40% vs bluff/value mix). Early tournament (hand 3/150), deep stacks (call commits ~28% stack into growing pot but affordable post-call SPR ~1.5). Hero-call maximizes EV: fold too tight missing bluffs, raise risks vs sticky/aggressive Minni (shown raises pre), call extracts value from thins/bluffs while folding to monster rivers. Consistent with check-behind/check-call plan for marginal ace-high blocker.
96 suited in UTG is too weak to open. It's a marginal hand at best and UTG requires a tighter range. Folding is the correct play here.

4♣6♠ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability. I’m in middle position with the cutoff, button and both blinds still to act — they’ll defend or 3‑bet fairly wide, so any call or small raise will likely become a multiway pot where I’ll be out of position and dominated. Calling 100 to see a flop or raising to 200 as a bluff is unnecessary risk early in the tournament; I have a healthy stack and should wait for a better spot. Fold and conserve chips.

I am in the Cutoff with A4o. While this hand is often an open-raise from this position to steal the blinds, the table dynamics have been quite aggressive so far (heavy 3-betting in the first three hands). Elon on the Button is short-stacked (56 BB) after losing a large pot in the previous hand and might be looking for spots to re-assert or shove. Additionally, the Small Blind and Big Blind are full-stacked. A4o plays poorly post-flop and dominates very little if called, and I must fold to a 3-bet. Given the aggressive texture of the game early on, minimizing variance by folding the bottom of my opening range is a prudent adjustment.

8c3d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against any defense, and dominated by better threes or eights. Folds to the button in an unopened pot, but limping or raising this trash risks spew against a wide BB defense range from FiveTwo, who has folded to aggression before but will likely play back here. Early in tournament (hand 4/150), preserve stack for premium spots. Standard fold.

It is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold Ten-Six offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. While there is an opportunity to steal the Big Blind, the risk of being called and having to play a weak hand out of position against FiveTwo outweighs the potential reward. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG with 9♣5♥ offsuit — a very weak, disconnected hand. In 6-handed early position this is not a profitable open or limp: I'll be out of position versus five opponents and frequently dominated or crushed by typical calling/3-betting ranges. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserve chips for better spots rather than spew marginal equity. Fold.

I am holding 4s 6h in Middle Position, which is a very weak off-suit hand (6-high one-gapper). With UTG folding, I still have four players behind me, including the blinds. This hand has poor playability and very low equity. Opening here would be a losing play in the long run. Standard strategy dictates an immediate fold.

Preflop in cutoff after two folds, with J5d suited. Good position, speculative hand with suited connectors potential for flush and straight draws. Blinds are deep but likely to fold often to a raise. Standard open-raise size of 2.5x BB for fold equity and to build pot in position. Limping or folding too passive early in tournament to maximize stack growth.

I am on the button with Q2 offsuit. Elon has opened from the cutoff with a 2.5x raise. Q2o is a very weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially against a raise. Even though I have position, folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

We’re in the small blind facing a 2.5bb cutoff open with T2o. This is near-bottom of range and plays terribly out of position: dominated when we make a ten, weak kicker issues, and poor ability to realize equity from the SB. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive against a shorter (54bb) opener who can respond with more 4-bet jams, and we don’t have blockers or playability. Best EV is to let it go and avoid bleeding chips from the SB.
T8o in BB facing a min-raise from Elon in cutoff. Getting 2.7:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). T8o is marginal but playable for this price, especially closing the action. Will call to see a flop.

Flopped bottom pair (8s) on Kc As 8s. This is a dangerous board with two overcards that hit Elon's preflop raising range hard, plus a flush draw. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking is best here - betting accomplishes nothing as I only get called by better hands. Will check and reevaluate based on Elon's action.

Flop board Kc As 8s is very dry with two spades, missing my J5d suited entirely—no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (just backdoor diamond and straight possibilities). Preflop raise was standard for position and fold equity with a speculative suited connector, but we whiffed. Claude's check after calling preflop OOP suggests weakness (missed A/K or weaker pairs/draws), but betting air here risks getting called by better or check-raised. With position, deep stacks (54bb), and early in tournament (hand 5/150), checking back realizes equity, controls the pot, and keeps us in position for turn/river bluffs if needed. No need to bluff a strong board yet when free cards build multiway potential or showdown value cheaply.

I have bottom pair (8s) on Kc As 8s 5c. Elon checked the flop on this A-K high board which suggests he likely doesn't have a strong ace or king - probably missed with suited connectors or has a medium pocket pair. The 5c turn is a brick. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value - I'd only get called by better. Checking to control pot size and potentially get to showdown cheaply is the right play.

Turn pairs my 5d for bottom pair with Jack kicker. Board Kc As 8s 5c remains strong with spade flush draw and possible straight draws. Villain (Claude) check-called preflop OOP, then check-checked flop and turn, indicating likely weakness (missed high cards, weak pairs, or failed draws). Deep stacks early in tournament (hand 5/150, ~54BB). With position, betting ~1/2 pot applies pressure, protects my pair against draws/improvements, extracts value from worse hands like weaker Ax or pure air floats, and has fold equity against his capped range. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws or multiway river. Smallish bet size balances value and bluffs in our range.

I have middle pair (8s) on Kc As 8s 5c. Elon checked the flop and now makes a small 300 bet into 550 (about 55% pot). This sizing is peculiar - could be thin value with a weak ace/king, a probe with a pocket pair, or a semi-bluff with spades. Getting 3.8:1 pot odds with a pair that has some showdown value. Folding is too tight, raising accomplishes nothing as I only get called by better. Calling to see the river is the right play.

River completed with Qh on Kc As 8s 5c. I have bottom pair (8s). Elon checked flop, bet small on turn, and now it's checked to me. My pair of 8s is weak on this AKQ high board - any A, K, Q, or overpair beats me. Betting accomplishes nothing as I only get called by better. I have some showdown value so checking to showdown is optimal.

River Qh is relatively blank, pairing nothing and completing few strong draws (possible T9 straight, but villain's check after turn call suggests not a monster like sets, two-pair, or completed straights that would bet for value). Villain (Claude) check-called preflop OOP, checked flop/turn, called small turn bet (300 into ~550 pot), now checks river—range likely capped at medium strength: weak Ax (A8/A5/AQo etc.), 88/K8/K5, failed spade draws/A-high floats. My Jd5d is bottom pair good kicker, ahead of bluffs/A-high but behind most pairs/top pair. With position, deep stacks (hand 5/150, ~50BB), betting ~1/2 pot (600) extracts thin value from worse Ax/88 that showdown or call small, applies fold equity to pure air/draws, denies equity, and builds stack via aggression. Size balances as value/bluff in range (we'd bet stronger hands harder). Check-fold equity high if raised; fold to raise. Checking risks losing pot to better SDV or free showdown where behind.

I have bottom pair (8s) on AKQ85 board. Elon's line is suspicious - checked flop, small turn bet, now 600 river bet into 1150. This board hits his preflop raising range hard (any A, K, Q, JT for straight). I'm getting about 3.9:1 pot odds (600 to win 2350), needing ~25% equity. However, my hand is extremely weak on this runout - virtually any value bet beats me. His delayed aggression suggests he may have been slowplaying or is now confident his hand is good. Even with decent pot odds, I don't think I beat enough of his value range, and his bluff frequency on this scary board is likely low. Folding.

I am in UTG position with 8d 7d (suited connectors). While this is a playable hand with good post-flop potential (straights/flushes), opening from UTG is generally too loose, especially with 5 players left to act. The player in the Big Blind (Minni) has shown aggression in previous hands, increasing the risk of a 3-bet which would force me to fold. It is prudent to fold this hand from early position and wait for better spots or position.

Preflop in middle position with A5hh, a suited ace with good playability and blockers. Standard open-raise range includes A5s. Deep stacks, early in tournament. No action yet except blinds and UTG fold. Raise to 2.5BB (250) to build pot with initiative and narrow opponents' ranges. Limping or folding suboptimal.

Q3offsuit is an extremely weak hand. Elon raised from middle position, and Q3o is not a hand I want to play against a raise, especially with three players still to act behind me. It is easily dominated by Elon's opening range and has very poor post-flop playability. Folding is the only sensible move here.

Button vs a middle-position open, 9♠5♣ offsuit is too weak and dominated too often, with poor playability when called. It doesn’t make a good 3-bet bluff either (no blockers, and opener has only ~60bb so can respond aggressively). Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.
T7o is a weak hand from SB facing a 2.5x open. Poor playability and easily dominated. Not worth defending even with the pot odds. Fold.

Elon opened to 250 from middle; I’m in the big blind with Q♦6♠. To call is 150 into a 400 pot (≈27% break-even equity). Q6o is a marginal, often-dominated hand with poor playability out of position — and postflop I’ll be first to act against the opener (positional disadvantage). A light 3-bet is thin here and will frequently be met with calls or 4-bets from better ranges. With a healthy stack and many hands left in the tournament, I’ll avoid this marginal confrontation and preserve chips. Fold.

UTG in 6-handed game with K3o (3h Ks), a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated potential, and low equity. Standard tight UTG range doesn't include this. Deep stack (64BB), no need to open limp or raise with trash. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

Q8o is a marginal hand that is generally considered a fold in middle position in a 6-max game. While it has some high-card value, it lacks the connectivity and suit strength to be a profitable open. Given the early stage of the tournament and the active play from opponents like Minni, I will choose the conservative and standard route of folding to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folds to us in the cutoff with a suited connector that plays well in position and has good postflop equity realization. In a 6-max dynamic, this is a clear open to pick up the blinds and take initiative; even if called, we can leverage position and board coverage. I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing that matches the table’s prior opens and pressures the blinds without overinflating the pot versus potential 3-bets.
4-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even with position on the button, calling a raise with this hand is -EV. Easy fold.

I'll call (total 250). 9h7h is a playable suited connector with good flop equity and implied odds against a cutoff open, especially with deep stacks (effective stacks ~95 BB). Calling keeps the pot controlled and preserves flexibility; 3-betting out of position with this marginal hand is too loose. The call is cheap (200 more into a 400 pot — good pot odds), so I’ll see a flop and re-evaluate based on flop texture and any action from the big blind. If facing a large 3-bet I will fold, but I’ll continue cautiously against small squeezes or single-raiser flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s4s facing a standard 2.5x open from the Cutoff and a call from the Small Blind. The pot odds are compelling: I need to call 150 to win a pot of 600, offering me 4:1 direct odds (requiring 20% equity). Suited one-gappers play well enough in multi-way pots to realize this equity, despite being out of position relative to the raiser. I am closing the action, so there is no risk of a squeeze behind me. This is a standard defend to try and hit a flush draw, straight draw, or two pair.

The flop is 3s 3d Js and I hold 9h7h — I completely missed. This is a paired board that strongly helps typical preflop-raiser ranges (Jx, 3x, overpairs). It's a multiway pot (FiveTwo opened, Pro called) and I'm out of position, so leading bluff attempts OOP into multiple opponents are low EV. I also have almost no equity: only two outs to make a pair (9 or 7) and I cannot make a heart flush by the river (no hearts on the flop and only two cards left). With no bet to act on, the correct, low-variance line is to check and control the pot. I will fold to any meaningful bet on this flop; if it checks through I'll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue if I pick up direct equity (9 or 7) or a significantly better bluffing spot against a single opponent.

I hold 6s4s on a 3s-3d-Js board, giving me a flush draw. As the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo). Donk betting here is unnecessary and exposes me to raises while I am still drawing. I will check and evaluate the action based on whether the raiser continues or the Small Blind gets involved.

We’re the preflop raiser and both blinds have checked to us on a very static, paired board (J33) where our range contains plenty of strong hands (AJ/KJ/QJ, overpairs) and they’ll have a lot of whiffs that can’t comfortably continue multiway. With 8c7c we have little showdown value but do have some backdoor potential; a small c-bet leverages our range advantage, denies equity to random overcards/spade floats, and keeps the pot controlled if we get played back at. Multiway I want a lower frequency and smaller sizing—one stab and mostly shut down if called without improving.

Fold. This is a paired 3–3–J flop and I’m out of position in a multiway pot against the preflop raiser. 9h7h has no pair, only 6 clean outs to a single pair (~24% to hit by the river). The bet is 250 into a 1,000 pot (calling would cost 250 for ~20% immediate pot odds), which looks borderline on raw odds but my realized equity is worse vs a c-betting raiser’s range (Jx, 3x, overpairs) and with Pro still behind. If I call I’m likely to face further turn/river pressure and have poor bluffing or value options OOP. Early in the tournament with deep stacks I prefer to preserve chips and wait for a cleaner spot. This is exactly the kind of “meaningful” flop bet I planned to fold to, so I fold.

I hold 6s4s on a 3s-3d-Js board, giving me a flush draw. The Cutoff (FiveTwo) has continued with a small bet of 250 into a pot of 750 (1/3 pot). The Small Blind has folded, leaving me heads-up. I am getting 4:1 immediate pot odds (call 250 to win 1000), requiring 20% equity. My flush draw has approximately 36% equity to hit by the river and about 19% to hit on the turn. Since the required equity is well below my actual equity, calling is the clear mathematically correct play. Raising is an option to generate fold equity, but given the paired board and the small c-bet size which often induces lighter calls or induces re-bluffs, taking the guaranteed price to see the turn is the lower variance and highly profitable line. I call.

I hold a flush draw (6s4s) on a paired board (3s 3d Js Tc). I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) who c-bet the flop. The standard line is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much here as I likely only fold out hands I'm beating or chopping with, and I want to see the river as cheaply as possible if the opponent checks back. If they bet, I will evaluate based on the sizing and pot odds.

Heads-up after Minni folds, Pro’s flop call on J33 contains a lot of one-pair (Jx), medium pairs (44-99), and some A-high/spade floats. The Tc is a good card to barrel because it adds credible strong hands to my range (JJ/TT/JT/AT/KT/QT) and puts additional pressure on the medium-pair portion that called once but hates facing a second bet on a paired board. My hand has almost no showdown value, but I do have a small amount of equity via the gutshot to a 9, so a turn stab with fold equity is higher EV than checking and essentially surrendering the pot. I’ll use a medium sizing to force tough decisions without bloating the pot too much; if raised, I’m done.

I am facing a bet of 650 into a pot of 1250 on the turn with a board of 3s 3d Js Tc. I hold 6s 4s, giving me a flush draw. To make this call profitable, I need approximately 25.5% equity (650 / 2550). However, with only one card to come, my probability of hitting a flush is roughly 19.6% (9 outs). The situation is worsened by the paired board (3s 3d), which means I could be drawing dead if the opponent has a full house, or I could lose on the river if the board pairs again. The Ts is a particularly dangerous out as it puts a second pair on board, giving any Ten a full house. Given the negative expected value (EV) based on direct pot odds and the risk of reverse implied odds, folding is the correct disciplined decision.

I am in the UTG position with 9h 8s. In a 6-max game, this hand is too weak to open from the earliest position. Opening 98 offsuit here would put me at a significant disadvantage post-flop against players who will have position on me and likely stronger ranges. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re first in from middle position with 8c7h (offsuit). In 6-max, this hand is too weak to open profitably from MP: it makes dominated one-pair hands and lacks suited equity, and if we limp we invite squeezes/iso-raises from the button and blinds, putting us in bad positions. With no dead money beyond blinds and plenty of better opens available, folding preserves stack and avoids marginal, low-EV spots.

8d6h is a weak offsuit hand from the cutoff. Even with two folds before me, this hand is below my opening range. Minni on the button has been active and has a big stack. Not worth trying to steal with such a weak hand that plays poorly postflop.

Button with KcTc — a strong late-position opener: good playability (top-pair potential, straight/flush outs) and high fold equity against the blinds. With 150bb effective and two players to act (SB and BB) I want to take the initiative and isolate in position rather than limp. Table norms show 2.5x-ish opens (and the interface’s min is 200), so I’ll raise to 250 to apply pressure while keeping the sizing standard. If I get called I’ll play postflop in position; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold KTo vs a polarized shove/3-bet range, or re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet.

I hold AJo in the Small Blind facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button (Minni). Minni has been active and aggressive, suggesting a wide opening range. AJo is strong enough to 3-bet for value, but doing so out of position creates a large pot where I might be dominated if called or 4-bet. Flat calling is a prudent, lower-variance play that keeps the opponent's range wide (including hands I dominate like A-low, K-high) and avoids isolation against a strong range. It also invites the Big Blind to call, giving good pot odds, though playing multi-way is acceptable with top-pair potential. I choose to call to see a flop.

Preflop in the big blind with QTo facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB) after folds and a small blind flat call. The pot is 600 and it costs 150 more to call (total 250 from me), offering excellent pot odds of about 4:1. QTo is a playable defending hand against a wide button iso-raise range, with queen and ten blockers, decent high card strength, and straight potential. The SB call is likely weak or speculative, and with deep stacks (63BB effective), playing postflop multiway is fine for value realization. Folding would be too tight this early in the tournament (hand 8/150). Calling to control pot size and see flop.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with AJ on a J-4-3 board with a flush draw. This is a very strong hand, but I am out of position against two opponents, including the pre-flop raiser (Minni) on the Button who is aggressive. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser. This protects my checking range and gives Minni the opportunity to make a continuation bet (c-bet) with a wide range, including bluffs or semi-bluffs. Donk betting (leading out) often folds out weaker hands that might bluff and isolates me against strong hands. I will check and evaluate the action, planning to call or potentially raise if the bet sizing indicates weakness or a draw.

Flop board Js4d3s is drawy with two spades and low cards connecting possible wheel straights or sets/pairs for callers. Facing a button iso-raise (Minni wide range: broadway, suited connectors, Ax) and SB flat (Pro weak speculative: small pairs, suited gappers). My QhTs is queen-high with backdoor spade and straight draws (needs runner-runner), but currently weak with low showdown value multiway. Preflop call was correct for odds and position, but postflop checked to me in BB. Donk betting risks folding out better/equity-denying worse hands; thin value unlikely against villainous button. Best is check to induce bluffs from Minni, pot control, and realize equity passively. Will check-call small bets or check-fold larger aggression given ~62BB stacks early (hand 8/150).

I was the preflop aggressor and both blinds checked/called, so I should try to take the initiative on this fairly dry J♠-4♦-3♠ board. K♣T♣ has two overcards and a backdoor straight but no pair; against two defenders I don’t want to overcommit, so a small continuation bet is appropriate. The pot is 750, so a ~1/3-pot c-bet of 250 should fold many weak hands and bluff-catchers while keeping future decisions cheap. If called I’ll reassess on the turn and only continue with improved equity or a favorable card, folding to strong aggression.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AJ) on a J-4-3 two-tone board. The Button (Minni), who has been aggressive, c-bets a standard small size (1/3 pot, 250 chips). Check-raising here would charge flush draws but would also fold out all of Minni's bluffs (air) and isolate me against sets or strong draws. Since I am out of position, I prefer to keep the opponent's range wide and allow them to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands on later streets. The bet size is small enough that giving a free card to a draw isn't catastrophic compared to the value of keeping the pot manageable and the opponent's range wide. I will check-call.

Multiway pot on a draw-heavy flop Js4d3s after checking through to Minni's small c-bet of 250 into what was ~775, now 1250 after Pro's call. My QhTs is queen-high with a weak backdoor spade flush draw (Ts) and minimal straight potential (backdoor broadway). Equity is low (~25-30% vs wide button range, less multiway), but pot odds are excellent at 250 to win 1500 (~6:1), and effective stacks are deep (~60BB). Early in tournament (hand 8/150), defending wide OOP vs small bets is standard to realize thin equity and induce bluffs on turns/bricks. Pro's check-call suggests pair/draw but caps high end; Minni's small sizing indicates polarization (value or air). Folding is too tight; raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Call to pot control, see turn cheaply, and re-evaluate (fold to aggression likely unless improves).

The turn card is the 4c, pairing the board. This is a relatively safe card for my Top Pair Top Kicker, though it does introduce the possibility of trips for the Big Blind (who has 4s in their defending range). My hand remains strong, but being out of position against two opponents, one of whom is the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), favors a check. Leading out (donking) would likely fold out bluffs and only get action from better hands or strong draws. Checking allows the aggressive Button to fire a second barrel with bluffs or value hands I beat (like KJ, QJ), and it lets me evaluate the Big Blind's action. My plan is to check-call a reasonable bet.

Turn board pairs the 4c on a draw-heavy Js4d3s flop, now Js4d3s4c, which bricks most straight/spade draws but improves 4x hands to two pair (stronger value), trips possible with 44/J4 etc. My QhTs remains queen-high showdown, no pair/draw, low equity multiway (~20% vs combined ranges: Minni polarized value/bluff, Pro capped pair/small draw). Pro's check OOP after calling flop suggests weakness (marginal pair, missed draw), unlikely monster. Pot 1500, checked to me in BB OOP multiway, deep stacks (~59BB). Betting risks folding out worse (induce folds from bluffs/draws) while getting raised by better; thin value bet poor sizing. Best: check to pot control, induce Minni continuation (likely vs checked board), realize equity passively, re-eval river. Folding impossible (to call 0). Early tournament, defend wide in position spots but control OOP. Check standard.

I opened preflop and c-bet the flop; both blinds called. The turn paired the board (4♦ → 4♣) and both opponents checked through. I have not improved — K♣T♣ is just K‑high with no realistic flush/straight possibility on the river — and this is a multiway pot where my bluff equity is low. Betting here would be a pure bluff against two callers who have shown willingness to float small C‑bets; better to control the pot and use position to see the river for free. I’ll check, keep the pot manageable (pot = 1,500), and re-evaluate on the river — look to bluff only on very favorable cards or call small river bets with showdown potential, but fold to sizable aggression.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AJ) on a board of Js-4d-3s-4c-9c. The pot is 1500. The pre-flop aggressor (Minni) checked back the turn, which strongly suggests she has a capped range—likely weak showdown value (like a mid-pair or weak Jack) or a failed draw, rather than a strong value hand. The Big Blind (Elon) also checked the turn, indicating weakness. 

On the river, leading out (donk betting) into two opponents would likely fold out the hands I beat (missed draws, small pairs) and isolate me against the few hands that beat me (trips) or chop with me. By checking, I give opponents the opportunity to bluff their missed spade draws. If it checks through, I almost certainly have the best hand and win the pot. If someone bets, I can comfortably call as a bluff-catcher given the turn action. Checking maximizes my expectation by inducing bluffs and avoiding value-owning myself.

River 9c is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete obvious draws (spades missed, club flush unlikely with low cards, no new straights). Paired board with 44 favors hands like Jx, 4x (now trips or two pair), 9x new pair over my queen-high TsQh (spade backdoor missed). Multiway OOP pot checked to me after villain checks: Pro (SB caller) capped at weak pairs/draws all streets; Minni (button raiser/c-bettor) polarized but turn/river check suggests medium strength checking back (Jx, Ax high) rather than monsters. Queen-high has thin showdown value vs air/missed draws but loses to any pair (~60-70% equity loss multiway). Deep stacks (~59BB) early tournament (hand 8/150): betting risks calls from better/raises from value, poor bluff FE OOP vs two opponents with position. Thin value impossible. Check to realize equity passively, pot control, showdown if Minni checks behind or fold to bet (pot odds-dependent). Consistent with prior passive BB defense strategy.

I never improved — KcTc is just K‑high on J♠‑4♦‑3♠‑4♣‑9♣. This is a multiway spot (two callers to my flop c‑bet) so my bluff equity is low and any bet will often be called by any pair or a weak J/9. Both opponents checked the turn and river, showing weakness, but that also makes it a poor spot to build a pot with no pair. I’ll check and take a free showdown; if either opponent leads a small bet I can consider a fold or a very narrow call depending on sizing, but I won’t bluff into two players for a large amount.

6-handed UTG ranges can be fairly wide, and J9s is a playable suited connector-type hand with good postflop playability and board coverage. Limping UTG invites multiway pots and makes it harder to realize equity; opening applies pressure and can win the blinds immediately. Given opponents have shown willingness to fold pre and standard opens have been ~2.5x, I’ll use a consistent sizing to 250 to build the pot when called while keeping risk controlled.
K4o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG raise. With 4 players behind me who could 3-bet, this is an easy fold. Not worth defending with such poor equity and bad position.

I’m in the cutoff with 9♠7♣ facing an UTG open to 250 (2.5x). UTG open from early position represents a relatively strong, top-heavy range, and 9‑7 offsuit will be dominated a lot and plays poorly multiway. There are still players to act (button, SB, BB) who can isolate or 3‑bet, and I don’t want to play a marginal, low-equity hand out of linepostflop. With a deep stack I can wait for a better spot or a suited connector/stronger hand to contest. I’ll conserve chips and fold here.

I hold Pocket Kings (Ks Kd) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened to 250. Given their early position open, they likely have a reasonable hand, but I am well ahead of their range. I want to build the pot immediately for value and isolate the opener, rather than allowing the blinds to enter cheaply. A standard 3-bet in position is roughly 3x to 3.5x the open. I will raise to 850 to extract value from lower pairs (QQ-99) and Ax hands, while discouraging a multi-way pot.

Preflop in SB with Ad7s facing an UTG open from FiveTwo followed by a button 3-bet to 850 from Pro. Pot is 1250, to call 800 OOP getting ~1.56:1 direct odds but poor implied odds with A7o, which is offsuit ace with weak kicker likely dominated by better aces or crushed by pairs/sets from strong ranges. FiveTwo UTG open suggests strength, Pro button 3-bet wide but still strong. Early in tournament (hand 9/150), stack 5850 deep effective, no need to speculate OOP with marginal ace. Folding preserves stack for better spots; squeeze raise too thin with this hand.

I am holding 2c 3d in the Big Blind. An UTG player has opened to 2.5x and the Button has 3-bet to 8.5x. 23o is arguably the weakest starting hand in poker and has almost zero equity against the ranges of an UTG opener and a Button 3-better. Folding is the only correct move to preserve my stack.

We opened a relatively marginal UTG hand and now face a fairly large BTN 3-bet (to 850). Against a typical UTG-vs-BTN 3-bet range (generally value-heavy), J9s performs poorly out of position: it’s often dominated by better broadways and doesn’t realize equity well in a bloated pot. Since J9s also has poor blocker value for a 4-bet bluff, the best stack-preserving and EV-consistent play is to release it rather than defend too wide from UTG.

A6o from UTG is a marginal hand. In early position with 5 players to act behind me, this hand is easily dominated by better aces and doesn't play well multiway. Standard play is to fold A6o from UTG in a 6-handed game.

I will open-raise to the minimum (200 total). Ah-2c is marginal from middle, but in a 6-max game an open here is standard: it gives initiative, fold equity, and uses my Ace as a blocker to stronger Ace combos behind. Limping lets later players act with position and often creates multiway pots where A2o’s equity collapses — I prefer to either take the pot now or play heads-up with initiative. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet I will fold (A2o is a weak hand to continue vs a strong reraise); vs a small 3-bet from the blinds I may occasionally defend depending on opponent and sizing, but generally I want to keep the pot heads-up. If called, continuation-bet favorable flops; if it becomes multiway I’ll proceed cautiously and often check back missed flops. Committing 200 keeps the line simple and preserves stack depth for postflop play.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kh Kd), the second-best starting hand, in the Cutoff. Minni has opened with a min-raise to 200 from Middle position. Minni has been active and aggressive in previous hands. My goal is to build a large pot for value and isolate the opener to play heads-up in position, rather than inviting a multi-way pot by just calling. A standard 3-bet size in position is roughly 3x to 3.5x the open. In the previous hand (Hand #9), I 3-bet a 2.5bb open to 8.5bb with the same hand. Here, facing a 2bb open, I will raise to 7bb (700 chips) to extract value and deny equity to the Button and Blinds.

Preflop on the button facing an MP open-raise to 200 (2BB) and a CO 3-bet to 700 (7BB). K7o is offsuit king-high with a weak kicker and poor postflop playability. Pro's 3-bet range from cutoff over an open is likely strong (e.g., JJ+, AKo, AQs+), giving K7o ~25-35% equity heads-up at best, worse multiway if Minni continues (she flats/raises wide based on history). Calling 700 into 1050 pot offers ~1.5:1 but requires ~40% equity and commits 12% of my 5850 stack to a marginal spot. 3-betting as a bluff has fold equity but K7o blocks few strong hands and vulnerable to 4-bets/shoves from Minni/Pro. Blinds to act post-me but deep stacks (hand 10/150), preserve for premium spots in position. Standard disciplined fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 5s. There has been an open from Minni in Middle position to 200 and a 3-bet from Pro in the Cutoff to 700. My hand is extremely weak and playing out of position against two active opponents, one of whom has shown significant strength with a 3-bet, is not profitable. Folding is the clear decision.

Facing an open to 200 and a 3-bet to 700, J5 offsuit is far too weak to defend out of position. Even with decent pot odds, this hand performs poorly versus both Pro’s 3-bet range and Minni’s continuing range, and it suffers heavy reverse implied odds when we make dominated top pairs. No good 4-bet bluff candidate either given blockers and opponent strength. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack.

I opened to 200 with Ah2c and Pro in the cutoff 3-bet to 700. My preflop plan was to fold A2o to a large reraise — A2o is frequently dominated and has poor postflop playability out of position. Calling 500 requires ~32% equity (500 to win ~1550), which A2o rarely has versus a typical cutoff 3-bet range heavy in Ax and strong broadway combos. A 4-bet bluff would be risky without a clear read and the sizing here looks like a real reraise. I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

UTG with Q♣4♦ — a weak, offsuit queen with poor playability and lots of domination risk. The pot is just the blinds (150) and calling would be a limp into a 6-handed pot out of position; raising to the minimum (200) is thin and will get me playing marginal postflop spots or face 3-bets from players who often raise/3-bet. With deep stacks and plenty of hands to wait for, the correct, risk-averse play is to fold and preserve my stack for better position/stronger holdings.

I am in Middle Position with JTo. In a 6-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open from this position as it is easily dominated by hands like AJ, AT, KJ, KQ, and plays poorly post-flop out of position or against 3-bets. With four players left to act behind me, including a short stack in the cutoff who might shove, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot or position.

Pocket fives in cutoff after two folds is a strong hand for an open-raise. Standard sizing from late position is around 2.5-3x the big blind (250-300). Raising builds the pot for value when ahead, denies equity to worse hands, and sets up post-flop play. Limp-fold or limp-call is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative. Folding is too tight pre-flop. Raising to 300 (3x) is balanced and fits the observed opening sizes from prior hands.

I am on the button with 9h 8h, a very strong speculative hand that plays excellently in position. Elon, in the cutoff, has raised to 300 (3x BB). This hand is too strong to fold and perfectly suited for a call to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to play a pot in position with a hand that realizes its equity well post-flop, especially against Elon's likely wide opening range from the cutoff.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb open from a shorter stack (Elon) plus a button flat from Flash. 7♣4♣ has poor high-card value and will realize equity badly out of position in a likely multiway pot, with significant reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs. A squeeze would need good fold equity and better blockers/hand quality than this. Even with decent pot odds, this is a clear -EV defend spot from the SB.

I have K5o in the BB facing a min-raise from Elon and a call from Flash. I need 200 to win 750, getting nearly 4:1 odds. K5o is weak but closing the action with good pot odds makes this a standard defend. I'll call and see a flop, looking to hit top pair or fold on a miss.

I completely missed this flop with K5o. No pair, no real draw (just backdoor straight at best). On an A-J-4 board with two spades, I have zero equity worth betting. Checking is the clear play - no reason to bluff into two players on an Ace-high board.

Pocket fives on an Ace-high flop with a spade flush draw and Jack is a marginal holding multiway. We have a pair but are likely behind Ax, Jx, and any sets or two-pair combos. The board is coordinated, favoring calls from better hands and draws. BB checked, giving us a chance to check behind for pot control and realize equity safely on later streets if we improve or if opponents miss. Betting risks getting raised by better or called by worse that can improve, and folding equity is low multiway. Checking maintains balance, disguises our hand, and avoids bloating the pot out of position effectively.

The flop is As Jh 4s. I have 9h 8h, which has no direct connection to this board. Both the Big Blind (Claude) and the pre-flop raiser (Elon) have checked. While Elon's check might suggest he missed the board, betting into two players on an Ace-high board with zero equity is risky. By checking behind, I get to see a free turn card and avoid bloating the pot with a hand that currently has no value.

I picked up bottom pair (5s) on the turn. Both opponents checked the flop on an Ace-high board, suggesting they likely don't have strong Aces. My K5 with bottom pair has some showdown value now, but betting into two opponents with such a weak holding is risky. I also have backdoor spade draw. Best play is to check and see a free river - if I bet and get raised, I'm in a terrible spot. Check for pot control.

Turn 5c gives me trips (5s-5h-5c) on a board of As Jh 4s 5c, an extremely strong hand multiway. Previously checked flop with pocket 5s for pot control on an ace-high, draw-heavy board. The 5c bricks most flush draws and doesn't complete obvious straights, while improving me massively ahead of Ax, Jx, sets, or draws. Claude checks first on turn, indicating possible weakness. Betting extracts value from Ax (top pair/top kicker likely), Jx, weaker pairs, and any stubborn draws. Checking risks giving free cards or missing value if they check back. Pot is 950; a 2/3-pot bet of 600 builds the pot while leaving room for calls/raises we can handle with 5550 stack. Balanced sizing consistent with prior aggression. Avoids overbetting OOP multiway.

I have 9-high with no draws on an Ace-high board. Elon has bet 600 into a 950 pot after the flop was checked around. Given I have zero equity and no showdown value, there is no reason to continue in this hand.

I have bottom pair (5s) with K kicker on As-Jh-4s-5c. Elon checked flop then bet 600 on the turn - this delayed c-bet line often indicates either a real hand (Ax, Jx) or a probe bluff. I'm getting ~2.6:1 odds needing ~27% equity. However, bottom pair with K kicker is extremely marginal here. Elon's preflop raise suggests broadway cards, and his turn bet after checking the A-J-4 flop indicates strength. My hand loses to any Ace, Jack, or pocket pair. While I might occasionally be ahead of a pure bluff, the risk/reward isn't there. Fold and preserve chips.

I am in UTG position with AJo. While this is often an opening hand in 6-max cash games, it sits at the bottom of the UTG range. I have been very active in the last few hands (playing Hand #8, and 3-betting with KK in Hands #9 and #10). Opening from UTG immediately after this sequence might invite light 3-bets from aggressive opponents like Minni (BB) or Elon (MP), against whom AJo plays poorly out of position. Folding here protects my stack from high-variance spots and helps re-establish a tighter image after a burst of activity.

Preflop, unraised pot after UTG fold. Holding 9d8d, suited connectors with good playability, straight and flush potential. In middle position 6-handed, deep stacks (~65BB), this is a standard opening range hand. Open raising isolates and builds the pot while denying equity to worse hands. Raising to 3x BB (300) is a good size for position and stack depth. Limping risks multiway without initiative; folding too tight.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Five offsuit. Elon, who has been active in previous hands, has opened for 300. Q5o is a very weak hand that does not play well against a standard opening range, and with three players still to act behind me, including the button, there is a high risk of being squeezed or dominated. Folding is the correct mathematical and strategic play here to preserve my stack.

On the button versus an MP 3bb open, JTo is a marginal-but-profitable defend due to position and decent connectivity. I don’t want to 3-bet: offsuit JTo plays poorly in bloated pots, gets dominated when called (AJ/KJ/QJ, AT/KT/QT), and Elon has shown willingness to continue rather than fold to pressure. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes equity in position, and lets me outplay postflop (continue on pairs/strong draws, otherwise peel selectively and avoid big reverse-implied spots).

K3o is a weak hand, especially out of position against two players who have already shown interest. I'm getting roughly 3:1 odds but will be first to act on all streets. This hand plays poorly multiway and OOP - it's dominated by better kings and has no real drawing potential. Disciplined fold.

3♦8♠ is a very weak, offsuit, low-card hand with poor playability and high chance of being dominated. The pot is 750 and I must call 200, so I need about 200/(750+200) ≈ 21% equity to break even. Facing an MP open and a button caller turns this into a multiway spot where my realistic equity is well below that threshold and I will be out of position postflop. A small 3-bet to steal is possible but unlikely to fold out both opponents and would put me in a marginal spot with a hand that rarely realizes enough equity to justify further investment. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Flop board 5s 7d 4d is draw-heavy with two diamonds and connected cards, hitting button calling ranges (flush/straight draws, pairs). Holding 9d8d gives me a strong combo draw: nut flush draw (9d8d with 7d4d for 13 flush outs minus backdoors) plus gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs), total ~18-20% equity heads-up, more vs bluffs. OOP as preflop raiser, pot control is a concern, but checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and induces bluffs only selectively. FiveTwo (BTN caller) has wide range including Ax suited, small pairs, suited connectors; they've shown aggression (e.g., Hand 7 trips value bet). C-bet for value against weaker draws/pairs, protection, and to build pot with my equity. Size 2/3 pot (500) balances value/protection without overbetting into raises. Deep stacks (~60BB) allow fold to check-raise if needed. Folding equity high vs floaters.

Elon’s 40% pot c-bet on a low, connected board is likely range-heavy (lots of broadways/overcards and some medium pairs) and will often slow down on many turns. With position and two overcards, I’m getting a reasonable price to continue and potentially win later when he checks (or improve with a J/T). Raising here would be a mostly equityless bluff with poor backup and risks getting played back at with an SPR that can get awkward. Folding feels too tight versus this sizing in position.

On the turn, the board is 5s 7d 4d Ts. My 9d8d still holds a strong nut flush draw (9 outs) and a gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs), giving me around 25% equity against most calling ranges. The Ts brings a spade flush draw into play and possible straight draws (e.g., 6-8, J-Q), but doesn't complete many strong hands from FiveTwo's BTN calling range, which includes pairs, weaker draws, and floats. As OOP after a flop c-bet that was called, continuing aggression risks getting raised off my equity or bloating the pot with a marginal draw. Checking realizes equity, induces bluffs from missed draws/floats (FiveTwo has shown aggression but also check-calls), allows pot control, and sets up cheap rivers. Deep stacks (57BB effective) support check-calling a reasonable bet. Betting again would be a bluff that overplays position disadvantage and faces fold equity reduction after flop call. Check is balanced and +EV long-term.

Elon’s turn check after a small flop c-bet is a clear range-cap/slowdown spot: a lot of his air (AQ/KQ), overcards, and medium pairs will give up here. We picked up top pair (T) with a decent kicker, so we should bet for value from worse pairs (7x/5x), pocket pairs (66–99), and some worse Tx, while also denying equity to diamond draws and random overcards. I’ll use a medium sizing to keep worse hands in and avoid overcommitting against a turn check-raise range that’s often sets/two-pair/strong draws.

Facing a 900 bet into 2650 pot (~3.95:1 odds, needing ~20% equity) on a turn that adds Ts, bringing spade flush draws and straight possibilities but missing many of villain's flop calling range. My 9d8d holds the nut diamond flush draw (9 outs) plus gutshot to the 6 (4 outs), ~28-32% equity vs typical BTN calling range (pairs like 66-TT, weaker two-pair/draws like 6x, diamond draws, Ax suited). FiveTwo's BTN call preflop then flop call vs my c-bet suggests medium strength or draws, and their aggression (e.g., Hand 7 value betting) but also folds (Hand 11). Raising semi-bluff has merit for fold equity (~25-30% vs floats/pairs) and builds pot, but OOP risks getting shoved or called by made hands, bloating pot (~5700 stack). Calling realizes equity optimally, controls pot size (effective SPR ~1.2 post-call), induces river bluffs from missed draws, and leverages deep stacks for river payoff if flush/straight hits (high implied odds). Folding discards too much equity. Consistent with pre-turn plan to check and check-call reasonable bets. Call.

River Kc is a blank for my range and completes few new draws from villain's BTN calling range. My 9d8d missed its combo draw entirely (nut diamond flush draw and gutshot to 6), leaving pure air with no pair, straight, or showdown value. Board: 5s7d4d Ts Kc favors villain's likely holdings: medium pairs (66-TT, 88-JJ), two-pair combos (54s,74s, etc.), weaker flush/straight draws that missed, or AK/QK pairing the river. Preflop call + flop call vs c-bet + turn bet shows strength or strong draw; checking turn and calling bet doesn't represent monster but could be draw/value. Acting first OOP with nothing: betting/raising is a pure bluff with low fold equity (villain can call/raise profitably with top of range or improved hands) and commits stacks poorly (~48BB effective, SPR low). Checking realizes no equity, induces bluffs/check-behinds from medium strength, and allows controlled fold to bet. Consistent with pot control from turn call; no value or semi-bluff justification. Deep tournament, preserve stack for better spots over 150 hands.

River K is a bad card to thin-value bet: it improves a meaningful portion of Elon’s turn check-call range (especially K♦X♦ diamond-draw combos that now make top pair), while the hands we target (7x/5x/underpairs) are more likely to bluff-catch turn and then fold river facing another bet on an overcard. Since we still beat all missed diamonds/straight draws and many underpairs at showdown, and a bet risks value-owning ourselves vs Kx/stronger pairs or facing a check-jam, the highest-EV/lowest-variance line is to take the showdown.

Preflop UTG in a 6-handed game with 48BB stack. Holding T2o, one of the weakest hands in poker: offsuit, gapped, low cards, no high card strength or playability. Standard play is an automatic fold from early position to avoid tough spots OOP postflop. Opponents have deep stacks, but no reason to enter the pot with trash. Prioritizing position and hand strength to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I am in middle position with Queen-Eight offsuit (Qc 8h). This is a weak starting hand and does not meet the standards for an open raise in a 6-max tournament setting, especially with aggressive players like FiveTwo, Minni, and Pro yet to act behind me. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

We’re in the CO with Td4d and no raise yet. While we could attempt a steal, this hand is very low equity and suffers heavy reverse implied odds when it makes a dominated top pair. The players left to act (Claude BTN, Minni SB, Pro BB) have shown willingness to 3-bet/defend, especially Minni and Pro, making a light open or limp likely to get punished or played in poor position postflop. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better steal candidates.

On the button with 92o - this is a very weak hand. Even though I have position and only the blinds remain, 92 offsuit is too weak to profitably open raise. It's near the bottom of possible hands with minimal playability post-flop. Folding is the correct play here.

I'm in the small blind with 5s7c offsuit — a weak, out-of-position hand. The cost to see a flop is only 50 chips (bringing my total contribution to 100), so it's cheap to realize equity against the big blind's wide defending range. Raising OOP with this hand is unnecessary and risks a 3-bet; folding would concede too much given the odds. I will call to see the flop and proceed cautiously (fold to a substantial preflop 3-bet, otherwise evaluate the flop before committing more).

I am in the Big Blind with 2h3h. The action folded to the Small Blind who limped. I have the option to check for free or raise. Given my hand is 3-high (the lowest possible suited connector), raising to bloat the pot is unnecessary and risky despite having position. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize whatever equity this speculative hand has. The pot is small, and I have position post-flop, which is advantageous. I will check.

I have 5♠7♣ on 4♠ Q♥ 6♦ — no pair but an open‑ended straight draw (need a 3 or an 8; ~8 outs, ~31% to hit by river). I’m out of position and the pot is small (200) relative to our deep stacks, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with just a draw. Checking preserves fold equity vs a wide checking range from the BB and lets me realize equity cheaply. Plan: check—call a small-to-medium bet (reasonable pot odds to chase the draw), re-evaluate on the turn if I pair or complete the straight, and fold to heavy pressure if the price is poor or if the action indicates I’m behind.

I hold 2h3h on a 4sQh6d flop. The pot is limped (200 chips) and my opponent (SB) checked. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) and a backdoor flush draw. Betting has little merit as I have almost no showdown value and my fold equity in a limped pot against a generic range on a Q-high board is uncertain. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting my straight or picking up a flush draw on the turn. There is no reason to bloat the pot with 3-high.

I’m OOP with 5♠7♣ on 4♠ Q♥ 6♦ K♥. My only equity is the open‑ended straight draw to a 3 or 8 (8 outs, ~17.4% to hit the river). Pot is small (200) and there’s no bet to call on the turn. With so little immediate equity and being out of position, don’t bloat the pot with a bluff — many hands will call a small bet and I’m not folding out enough better hands. I’ll check to realize my equity for a free river, plan to call small-to-medium bets that give correct pot odds, and fold to heavy turn aggression. If checked to the river and I hit the straight I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll reevaluate a targeted bluff only if the river and opponents’ actions justify it.

I hold 2h3h on a 4sQh6dKh board. I have turned a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 5), giving me roughly 12 outs. The opponent (Small Blind) has shown weakness by checking the flop and turn in a limped pot. While a semi-bluff bet could take down the pot or build it for when I hit, the pot is very small (200), and I have high equity that I want to realize for free. Betting risks a check-raise which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to see the river guaranteed, effectively giving me a free card to try and hit my straight or flush. Given the low stakes of the current pot, realizing equity is the prudent play.

I missed my straight with 5♠7♣ and have essentially no showdown value. The pot is 200 and the opponent (Pro) checked both flop and turn, which suggests weakness or pot‑control. The A♣ on the river is a credible scare card that lets me credibly represent a made hand. A small half‑pot bluff (100 into 200) is low risk and can fold out medium pairs and unpaired high cards while preserving most of my stack if it fails. I will make a targeted bluff with a 100 chip bet; if I face a raise I’ll fold and conserve chips.

I hold 2h3h on a board of 4sQh6dKhAc. I missed my straight and flush draws. My hand value is simply playing the board (Ace-King-Queen-Six-Four high). My opponent, the Small Blind, limped pre-flop and checked flop and turn, then led out for 100 on the river. Since I am playing the board, I lose to any hand that has a pair (even pocket deuces) and any hand with a kicker higher than a 4 (e.g., 7-2 plays A-K-Q-7-6). I only split the pot against exactly 3-2. Given I am beating absolutely nothing in her range and receiving a bet, I have 0% chance of winning and a negligible chance of chopping. Folding is the only correct play.

I am currently Under the Gun (UTG) with Ten-Nine offsuit (Th 9s). In a 6-max game, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for this position. Playing such a weak hand out of position against five opponents would likely result in a negative expected value over time. I have a healthy stack of over 90 big blinds, so there is no pressure to force a play with marginal hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re first in from middle position after UTG folds. Pocket 7s is well within an MP opening range in 6-max: it plays profitably by taking down blinds, can flop strong (sets) and has decent equity when called. With mostly deep stacks behind (and a shorter BB), I prefer raising rather than limping to deny equity and define ranges. Use a standard, non-committing open size that still applies pressure and keeps our range consistent.

4-5 offsuit is a weak hand. Facing a raise from middle position with players still to act behind me, this is a clear fold. The hand lacks sufficient playability and is easily dominated even when hitting the board.

I’m on the button with Q♦6♦ — a suited queen with decent post‑flop playability. FiveTwo opened to 250 from middle; the pot is 400 and a call costs 250 (~38.5% break‑even equity). Versus a likely fairly wide opening range, calling in position is the +EV line: I retain positional advantage, keep the pot controllable, and avoid turning a marginal holding into a bloated confrontation with the SB/BB still to act. I’ll call to see a flop and continue only with top pair, strong draws, or favorable board dynamics; fold or proceed cautiously if I face significant aggression.

I am in the Small Blind holding A8o facing a raise from Middle Position (FiveTwo) and a call from the Button (Minni). While the pot odds are tempting (calling 200 to win a pot of 650), playing A8o from the worst position against two opponents when 100bb deep is generally a losing strategy. My hand is easily dominated by the opener's range (which likely includes better Aces), and I suffer from significant reverse implied odds if an Ace flops. Given the stack depth and positional disadvantage, the disciplined play is to fold.

Preflop in the big blind with 2h4h, a very weak suited hand. FiveTwo raised to 250 from middle position, showing aggression in past hands, and Minni flat-called from the button, suggesting a wide calling range but strength. Pot odds are decent at 150 to call into 650 (about 4.3:1), but 24s has poor playability, low equity against raiser's range (high cards, pairs, suited connectors), and limited implied odds due to low cards that rarely make the nuts. Multiway pot postflop would be tough with reverse implied odds if I hit. Stacks are deep, but folding trash here preserves stack for better spots in early tournament phase. Standard fold.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on a very dry A-J-6 rainbow board, I have a meaningful range advantage (I can credibly have strong Ax and AJ), while Button’s flatting range contains a lot of medium hands and overcards that will often fold to one small bet. With 77 specifically, I’m not trying to build a pot for value; I’m betting small to deny equity to hands like KQ/QT/T9 and to pick up the pot immediately at a good frequency. If I get called, I can often shut down and play turns cautiously; if raised, I can comfortably fold.

I’m on the button with Q♦6♦ — bottom pair on A♣ J♥ 6♠. FiveTwo (MP opener) makes a small C‑bet of 225 into 875 (~25% pot). That sizing and position suggest a wide c‑bet range (Ax, Jx, broadways, medium pairs and floats), and I have positional control. Calling 225 (need ~20% equity) is the prudent +EV line: it keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and extract value if I improve to two‑pair/trips, and preserves bluffs in my range. Raising with a lone 6 would be thin — it will get called or 3‑bet by better hands (A/J) and is unnecessary given the cheap price. Plan: call and reassess on the turn — continue with improvement or proceed cautiously vs aggression, and fold to large turn raise if I don’t improve.

Turn 9h is a bad barrel card for 77: it improves a lot of Button’s flop-calling range (A9, J9, T9, 98, 9x floats) and adds more semi-bluffing equity, so a second small bet won’t generate enough folds and just builds a pot when we’re usually behind Ax/Jx/9x. We still have some showdown value versus missed broadways/underpairs, so the best line is to pot-control and let Minni bluff or take a cheap river when they check back.

I have bottom pair (6s) with Q♦6♦ on A♣ J♥ 6♠ 9♥. FiveTwo opened from MP, c‑bet small on the flop and now checked the turn — that looks like pot control, missed draws or medium/weak hands rather than strong value. With only second pair and no meaningful draw, I want to keep the pot small and use my positional advantage to see a river card for free. Betting here risks getting raised off my hand by Ax/Jx/strong pairs and gains little from worse hands, so I’ll check and reassess on the river (call small bets if I don't improve, fold to large aggression, and only inflate the pot if I make two‑pair or better).

River is a total brick and our 77 has some showdown value against Minni’s flop-calling floats (KQ/QT/KT, some backdoor stuff) that checked back turn. If we bet, we mostly get called by Ax/Jx/9x and fold out the hands we already beat, turning our hand into a low-quality bluff with poor blockers. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets us bluff-catch selectively versus a small stab.

Check. I have Q♦6♦ — just second pair (6s) with a weak kicker on A♣ J♥ 6♠ 9♥ 2♣. FiveTwo opened from MP, made a small flop c‑bet (225) and then checked turn and river, which reads like pot‑control with Ax/Jx, a medium pair, or a missed c‑bet/float — not a range I can extract reliable value from with only second pair. A river bet will get called or raised by stronger hands and will fold out very few worse hands, so betting is thin/low EV and creates unnecessary risk. Pot (1,100) is small relative to my stack; I’ll check to see showdown and avoid bloating the pot or folding to a river raise.

We’re 6-handed but still first to act, and 8♠5♥ offsuit is too weak to open from UTG: it’s dominated when we make top pair, lacks strong playability, and will struggle versus the relatively aggressive 3-betting we’ve seen (Minni/Pro/Elon). With deep stacks there’s no urgency to force marginal opens—best to preserve EV and wait for better spots.

2h3h is a weak suited connector in middle position. While suited, these are the lowest possible connected cards. With 4 players still to act behind me and poor implied odds, this is a clear fold from middle position.

4♣9♥ is a weak, unsuited, low-connectivity hand with poor playability from the cutoff. The pot is unopened (just blinds), but standard cutoff open-raise range is much stronger — this hand performs poorly OOP versus a defending button or the blinds and has bad reverse implied odds. Limping (calling 100) is also suboptimal: it invites pressure and leaves us in tough postflop spots. With deep stacks and competent opponents (button and blinds likely to defend or 3‑bet), the +EV play is to fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure or play a stronger holding.

Action is on me on the Button with T9o after three folds. This is a standard spot to attempt to steal the blinds. T9o is within a standard Button opening range (roughly top 40-50% of hands) due to its connectivity and high card value relative to random blind hands. I have position on both remaining opponents (Elon in SB, Flash in BB). A standard raise size of 2.5bb (250 chips) puts pressure on the blinds while minimizing loss if I face a 3-bet, to which I would fold.

Preflop in SB with As4s facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB) from Pro. A4s is a strong suited ace with nut flush draw potential, good blockers to AA/AKs, and solid playability. Button open range is wide (~25-40% of hands), giving my hand around 45%+ equity OOP. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more (total 250) into 400 pot (~3:1). Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP with a marginal hand against deeper stacks. Calling realizes equity well, keeps pot small, and allows BB to act. Postflop, suited ace plays well multiway or heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9c 7s. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Elon) called. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 750. This gives me pot odds of 4:1 (20% equity needed). While 97o is a marginal hand, the excellent price and the fact that I am closing the pre-flop action make this a standard defend to see a flop.

First to act OOP on Qs5d2c flop with As4s (nut spade flush draw, 9 outs). Board is dry rainbow, multiway pot (3 players). Preflop call was correct with suited ace vs wide button range. Betting out risks bloating pot OOP with a draw while opponents can have Qx, sets, or floats. Checking realizes equity, controls pot size, potentially induces bluffs/c-bets from Pro's wide button range that I can raise, or allows free card if checked around. Pot odds excellent if bet into later. Folding draw too weak; no bet sizing justifies commitment now. Standard check OOP with strong draw multiway.

I have completely missed the flop with 9-high and no direct draws. The board is dry (Qs 5d 2c), which favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Since Elon checked from the Small Blind, and the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is still to act behind me, I will check and hope to see a free turn card or fold if facing significant action.

The board is Qs-5d-2c, which is very dry. While this texture favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, playing a multi-way pot (3 players) significantly reduces the frequency with which I should c-bet, especially with pure air. I hold T9o, which has no showdown value and very little potential to improve (no immediate draws). Betting into two opponents requires getting two folds, which is harder than in a heads-up scenario, as one of them likely connected with a piece of the board (like a pair of 5s, 2s, or a sticky pocket pair/Queen). Checking back allows me to realize what little equity I have for free, avoid being check-raised, and potentially hit a Ten or Nine on the turn. It is the low-variance, disciplined play in a multi-way spot with a low-equity hand.
