Turn board Qs5d2cKd in a multiway pot (Pro button raiser, me SB, Flash BB). Holding As4s for nut spade flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity to river), no pair or straight draw. Flop checked through, consistent with wide ranges missing or trapping. OOP first to act multiway on turn: betting risks isolating with a draw against better hands (Pro's Qx/Kx/set range) or folding out worse, while bloating pot OOP. Checking realizes equity passively, controls pot size (~7.5BB pot), induces c-bets/bluffs from Pro's wide button range (which I can raise as semi-bluff with fold equity + draw) or cheap calls/free cards if checked around. Deep stacks (44BB); no need to commit now without made hand. Pot odds favorable if bet into (~2:1 or better). Folding absurd with strong draw.

I have 9-high with no draws on a board of Qs-5d-2c-Kd. Everyone checked the flop, and Elon has checked the turn from the Small Blind. Given the board contains two overcards (King and Queen) that likely hit my opponents' ranges better than mine, and the fact that I have zero showdown value and no equity to continue, I will check. My goal is to see a free river or fold if there is any betting action, as bluffing into two players here is unlikely to be profitable.

On the Turn, the board is Qs 5d 2c Kd. I have Tc 9d, giving me a gutshot straight draw (a Jack makes a straight). The action checked to me again. While the King is a scare card that hits my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, betting into two opponents who have already called pre-flop is risky. They could easily have a piece of the board (a King, Queen, or lower pair) and decide to call one street. By checking, I realize my equity (hitting the Jack) for free. If I bet and get raised, I would have to fold and lose my draw. The pot is small (750), and checking back manages pot size and variance while keeping my range somewhat ambiguous.

River Kc pairs the kings, bricking my nut spade flush draw (As4s for ace-high only). Multiway pot (Pro button raiser, Flash BB), checked through flop/turn consistent with missed draws/medium strength. First to act OOP on scary paired board: no made hand, just ace-high with no strong blockers to value ranges (KQ/KQs hit top pair/top two, K5s/K2s unlikely but possible; sets overpairs rare). Betting risks calling stations with king or better folding out bluffs while bloating pot OOP. Checking controls pot (~7.5BB), realizes equity passively (pot odds ~3:1+ to call any bet), induces bluffs/value from Pro's wide button range/Flash's calling range that I can hero-call selectively with ace-blocker or fold. Deep stacks (44BB); preserve chips for better spots. Bluffing multiway river first-in too thin, low fold equity.

The board has been checked through the flop and turn, and Elon (SB) has checked again on the river. I have 9-high with zero showdown value. While a bluff might represent a King or a Queen given the passive action, bluffing into two opponents with total air is risky in a small pot. Most likely, someone has a small pair or Ace-high that they will use to call a small 'stab' at the pot. Since I am in the middle and Pro is still to act behind me, the most prudent move is to check and fold if there is any betting action, or simply see if 9-high somehow wins in a checked-down pot (unlikely as it is).

I hold Ten-high (Tc 9d) on a board of Qs 5d 2c Kd Kc. The action has checked through on the flop and turn, and checked to me on the river. While the passive action suggests my opponents are weak, attempting to bluff two opponents on the river after checking back twice is risky. My check on the Turn (a King) caps my range, making it difficult to credibly represent a King or better now. Opponents may look me up with weak pairs (like 2x, 5x, or pocket pairs) or even Ace-high in a small pot. Without credible representation of a strong hand and zero showdown value, the variance of a bluff here is not justified by the small pot size (750). I will check back and preserve my stack.
5-2 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. From UTG position, I need a strong range since I'll be out of position against most opponents. This is a clear fold.

6-max game, deep stacks (~138bb) — K♦T♣ is a standard open from middle. Limping is passive and invites multiway action/3-bets from aggressive players (Elon, Pro, FiveTwo). I want fold equity and to keep the pot controllable, so open-raise to a normal 3bb size to isolate the blinds and define ranges. Plan: if I get a single call I'll continue with C-bets on dry/advantageous flops; if I face a sizable 3-bet (especially from button/cutoff/BB) I’ll usually fold KTo (may call very small 3-bets from blinds depending on size and stack dynamics).

I hold Ac 3s in the Cutoff. Minni, the chip leader, has opened to 300 (3bb) from Middle Position. While having an Ace blocker is nice, A3o is a weak hand that is often dominated by the opening range of a solid opponent in MP. It plays poorly post-flop, suffering from reverse implied odds (winning small pots, losing big ones). I have 3-bet Minni successfully before (Hand #10), but that was with Kings; doing so here with A3o is risky against a deep stack. Flatting invites squeezes and leads to a difficult post-flop spot. The most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket kings (KK) is one of the strongest preflop hands, second only to AA. I'm on the button with excellent position on Minni's MP raise and the blinds behind. The pot is 450, to call 300, but calling is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots and denies equity. 3-betting isolates the raiser, builds the pot with a premium hand, and applies pressure. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard, balanced, and commits about 17% of my 5200 stack while leaving fold equity vs. Minni's range. Blinds are deep but unlikely to defend wide vs. 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h4c, which is a very weak starting hand. There has been an open raise to 300 from Minni and a 3-bet to 900 from Elon. Given the strength shown by both players and my poor hand and position, this is a clear fold to preserve my stack.

QThs is a strong, connected suited broadway that plays well in position postflop relative to a short 3-bettor, and we’re getting a reasonable price (800 to win 1350) with good implied odds if Minni continues. A 4-bet here risks running into Minni’s stronger continuing range behind and creates awkward shove/call situations versus Elon’s short stack. Calling keeps weaker hands in, realizes equity well, and preserves flexibility if Minni 4-bets.

I opened to 300 with K♦T♣ from middle. Elon (button) 3-bet to 900 and the BB (FiveTwo) called, creating a multiway pot. KTo is marginal and frequently dominated by the button/BB calling ranges (AK, AQ, Kx, medium pairs, suited Ax), and its postflop equity falls off sharply multiway. Calling 600 into the ~2150 pot (so ~2750 after my call) requires roughly 22% equity to break even — KTo is unlikely to have that against two calling ranges. 4-betting to the minimum (1500) or shoving to isolate is risky here: Elon can shove/call (he’s fairly short at ~4300) and FiveTwo may still come along, putting me in a bloated pot with a marginal hand. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot, consistent with my preflop plan to fold KTo to a sizable 3-bet from a late position.

Heads-up vs Elon (the 3-bettor) on a paired, low flop (8-8-7) where his range can c-bet very frequently and our QTh has no pair and only backdoor potential (hearts/straight via 9J). Leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting raised off equity; checking keeps his bluffs in, lets us respond appropriately to sizing, and preserves our stronger check-raise range on this texture.

Preflop, KK is a premium hand, and the 3-bet to 900 was standard to isolate and build the pot from the button. FiveTwo's cold call OOP from the BB after squeezing out Minni suggests a strong-ish range: pocket pairs (including 88?), suited broadways, suited connectors like 98s/76s, AQs+, etc. The flop 8c8d7s is extremely dry and favorable: paired board, straight draw possible with 65/96/54 but narrow; no flush draws active. My KK is an overpair, way ahead of villain's top pair, draws, or smaller pairs. Villain checks, giving me a free opportunity to bet for value against his 6-max calling range and to charge draws/protect. Checking risks giving free cards to 65s, 96s, or Ax hoping to improve, and multiway check potential if he had trips (unlikely). Pot is 2150, SPR ~2:1 with my 4300 stack. Betting 2/3 pot (1400) extracts value from worse (TT-JJ, 87, 98, A8, etc.), denies equity to draws, and sets up stack-off by turn if needed. Smaller bet risks too many calls from air; larger overbets the board. FiveTwo's history shows aggression but also calling stations postflop (e.g., Hand 7), so value bet sizing balances thin value and protection while maintaining fold equity vs. pure air.

Elon’s 3-bet sizing with a 29bb stack is likely fairly linear/strong (big pairs, AQ+/AK, some strong suited broadways). On 8-8-7 we have no pair and only thin backdoors; versus that range our equity is often below the ~28% needed to call 1400 into 3550, and the bigger issue is his remaining ~1500 behind: if we call, we’ll frequently face a turn jam and be pressured to continue in a spot where we’re not realizing enough equity OOP. With essentially no profitable check-raise bluff (he’s too pot-committed to fold), the best EV is to cut losses and preserve our stack.

T♥2♥ is a very weak holding to open from UTG in a 6-handed game. I’ll be out of position against five players for the rest of the hand, my hand is often dominated by better tens and is hard to play postflop, and limping/calling invites multiway action with poor implied odds. Raising to 200 to try to steal is thin — the table shows capable callers and 3-bettors, so that would be unnecessarily risky. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left to play, folding and waiting for a better spot is the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play here.

I am holding 8c 6s in Middle Position, which is a statistically weak hand (unsuited one-gapper). The player UTG has folded. With four players left to act behind me, including the Button and Blinds, opening with this hand carries significant risk and very little reward. The hand has poor playability and equity. At this early stage of the tournament (Hand 17/150) with a healthy stack of roughly 107 big blinds, there is no strategic incentive to play this marginal holding. The correct and standard play is to fold.

Preflop in cutoff with Kd3s offsuit. This is a weak hand with poor playability, dominated by better kings, and low equity against calling ranges. No prior action, standard blinds posted. Folding is optimal to preserve stack and wait for better spots in position. Cutoff range doesn't include K3o.

I am on the button with 5c 2c. While suited, this is an extremely weak hand. Although it is tempting to raise to steal the blinds when action folds to the button, 5-high suited is generally too weak to open profitably unless the blinds are extremely tight. Given the early stage of the tournament and the lack of value in this hand, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

Folds to me in the small blind, so this is a heads-up blind battle where we should open very wide and leverage fold equity/initiative. Q7s has decent high-card value, plays well postflop with top-pair potential and some straight/flush prospects, and we cover the BB so applying pressure is profitable. I’ll raise to a standard small-blind open size to try to win the pot now and build a pot with position disadvantage but range advantage from opening wide.
K9o in the BB facing a min-raise from SB. Getting good pot odds (150 to win 350, about 2.3:1). K9o plays decently heads up and I have position postflop against the SB. This is a standard defend.

Heads-up blind battle, I have the preflop initiative and this paired-A flop heavily favors the raiser’s range (I have far more Ax). BB has lots of hands that completely miss and will fold to a small c-bet. With Q-high and no meaningful draws, checking invites a stab that I can’t profitably continue against, so I’ll take the low-risk fold-equity line with a small continuation bet and plan to give up on most turns if called.

Board is Ah-Ad-4h - paired aces. FiveTwo bets small (150 into 500). My K9o has almost zero equity here. Even if FiveTwo is probing without an ace, I have no real showdown value or draws. Small bets on paired ace boards often indicate strength wanting calls. Folding K-high is standard - continuing just leads to losing more chips on later streets.

I am in UTG position with A5o (Ace of Spades, Five of Clubs). With 107 BBs, this hand is generally too weak to open from early position in a 6-handed game. It is easily dominated by calling ranges (AT+, AJs+) and plays poorly out of position post-flop. Standard charts suggest folding A5o from UTG. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J3 offsuit is a trash hand with no straight potential, low high card, and offsuit. In middle position pre-flop with 5 players to act behind (including button and blinds who may defend wide), this hand has negative EV to play. Pot is 150, to call 100 into multiway pot is -EV. Deep stacks, better to wait for playable hands. Standard fold to preserve stack.

King-Queen suited is a premium starting hand, especially in late position like the Cutoff. Since it has been folded to me, I will make a standard 3x open-raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or play a strong range post-flop.

Button vs Cutoff 3bb open, KJo is a strong enough candidate to 3-bet in position: we deny equity, reduce the chance the blinds squeeze/come along, and we have a blocker to strong Kx. Flash has shown willingness to fold to 3-bets earlier, so picking up the dead money is a good outcome. If Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold this hand at these stacks.

7s3s is a weak suited hand. Facing a raise and 3-bet, I'm being asked to cold-call 900 from the small blind out of position. This hand doesn't have the playability or equity to justify calling a 3-bet multiway. Easy fold.

Fold. Cutoff raised to 300 and the Button 3-bet to 950 — I’m in the BB with J♥8♣ and would need to call 850 into a 1,400 pot. If I call the pot becomes 2,250, so I need roughly 850/2,250 ≈ 38% equity to break even. J8o has poor equity and terrible playability out of position against a 3-bet range that is likely value-heavy here. A light 4-bet/shove with deep stacks is too high-variance and likely to be called or re-shoved by better hands. Preserve my healthy mid-stack (13,425) for a better spot rather than making a marginal call OOP against two players — fold.

King-Queen suited is a premium broadway hand that is too strong to fold to a single 3-bet from the Button. While FiveTwo is showing aggression, their range on the Button is likely wide enough that KQs has significant equity. Calling allows me to realize the hand's potential on a variety of flops without bloating the pot further out of position. I have the stack depth to play this hand post-flop and the pot odds are roughly 3:1, making a call the most standard and effective play.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot against FiveTwo, who has shown significant aggression. The flop is 6h-Th-Ts, which is a paired board. While I have King-high with two overcards, I do not have a piece of this board yet. Standard play in a 3-bet pot as the caller is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. Leading into them here doesn't make much sense given my range, and checking allows me to evaluate their bet size and potentially realize my equity or fold if they show extreme strength.

As the preflop 3-bettor in position, I retain a meaningful range advantage on this paired T-high board (overpairs and strong broadways). Flash’s check contains a lot of unpaired overcards and middling pairs that will fold or at least be put under pressure by a small sizing. With KJ (plus the Kh giving some backdoor heart equity), I don’t want to bloat the pot, but I do want to deny equity and keep initiative. I’ll c-bet small and comfortably fold if check-raised.

The board is 6h-Th-Ts and I have Kc-Qc. The pot is 2050 and the bet from FiveTwo is only 500, which gives me nearly 5:1 pot odds. I need approximately 20% equity to justify a call. While I am behind any pair or a Ten, my two overcards provide potential outs if a King or Queen hits. The small bet size from FiveTwo could be a standard continuation bet with air or lower cards like Ace-High. Given the price, folding King-Queen here would be too exploitable. I will call to see a turn card.

I have improved to top pair (Queens) with a King kicker on the turn. While this is a strong hand, the board is paired with Tens, and FiveTwo was the pre-flop aggressor. I will continue to check to the aggressor to control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff from hands like AK or smaller pocket pairs. If FiveTwo bets, I have a clear call given the strength of my hand, but leading out myself risks being raised by a Ten or an overpair (AA, KK).

Turn brings the Q♠, which is a good barrel card for my 3-bet range (AQ/KQ/QQ and overpairs) and it also gives me strong equity with KJ via an 8-out straight draw (A or 9). Flash’s check after calling flop often contains a lot of underpairs/A-high that hate this card and will fold to pressure. I want to keep leveraging range advantage and fold equity while setting up a workable river SPR if called. If Flash check-raises/jams, I can comfortably fold given my draw equity versus a strong/value-heavy raising range.

I have turned top pair with a King kicker (Kc-Qc) on a board of 6h-Th-Ts-Qs. While the board is paired with Tens, I cannot fold top pair in a 3-bet pot to a single turn bet, especially since I'm getting decent pot odds (1700 to win a total pot of 6450, approximately 26% equity needed). FiveTwo's range includes overpairs like AA or KK and potential trips (ATs, KTs), but it also includes bluffs like AK, AJ, or semi-bluffs with heart draws. Raising here would likely only be called by hands that beat me, so I will call to see the river and potentially catch a bluff or win against a worse Queen.

On this board of 6h-Th-Ts-Qs-5d, I have Top Pair (Queens) with a King kicker. However, the board is paired with Tens, and FiveTwo has shown significant aggression as the pre-flop 3-better and by barreling both the flop and turn. While my hand improved on the turn, it is still vulnerable to any Ten in FiveTwo's range (like ATs or KTs) and overpairs like AA or KK. Betting into FiveTwo here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, as most hands I beat (like JJ or AK) would likely fold to a river lead, whereas hands that beat me will certainly call or raise. By checking, I control the pot size and can potentially induce a bluff from missed draws or a check-back from marginal hands, allowing me to reach showdown and realize my equity safely.

River is a brick (5d) and I’m sitting on K-high after double-barreling. After Flash calls both the flop c-bet and the large turn barrel in a 3-bet pot, his range is heavily weighted toward Tx (never folding) and Qx (often calling, especially AQ/KQ/QT/QJ), with far fewer underpairs or pure floats remaining. To make a river bluff profitable—especially if I jam—I’d need a very high fold frequency that I’m unlikely to get versus this condensed, showdown-heavy range.

Checking back also avoids the problem of bluffing out the few hands I actually beat (missed straight draws / weaker K-high holdings) while getting called by better. So I take the free showdown and live with it.

UTG in a 6-handed game with T8o, an offsuit speculative hand with poor playability and low strength. Folding is standard to avoid playing out of position with a weak hand. No history suggests limping or raising is profitable here. Pot odds don't justify a call pre-flop.

I am holding King of Diamonds and Four of Spades in middle position. K4o is a weak hand to open with in this position, especially with several aggressive players behind me. Standard opening ranges for 6-max middle position do not include King-low offsuit hands. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

We’re in the cutoff with Tc2s, which is too weak to profitably enter the pot even in position. It has poor playability, weak top-pair potential, and no meaningful blockers (2 is irrelevant; a single T isn’t enough) to justify an open/iso bluff, especially with competent blinds who can 3-bet or defend and realize equity. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better stealing candidates.

I have 5d3s on the button - one of the weakest hands in poker. While I have position and could attempt a steal, 53o has terrible playability postflop. Looking at Minni and Pro's history, they both defend and 3-bet reasonably well. Not worth risking chips with such a trash hand even from the button.

9♣2♥ is one of the weakest hands. Facing only the big blind but being out of position postflop, calling 50 to see a flop is a marginal, likely negative-EV play and a min-raise would be a very thin bluff that can be easily punished. I preserve chips for better spots and fold, conceding the small blind.

I am UTG (Under The Gun) in a 6-max game with King-Ten offsuit. While KTo is a decent hand, it is generally considered too weak to open from the earliest position because it is easily dominated by the ranges of players behind me (like KJ, KQ, or AJ). In a high-stakes tournament setting, maintaining a disciplined opening range is crucial. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity or a better position.

7♠4♠ is too weak and dominated to open from middle position with three players left to act plus the blinds, and limping is a low-EV line that invites isolation and puts us in tough spots postflop. With no dead money beyond blinds and plenty of better steal candidates, we preserve stack and wait for stronger/openable hands or better positions.
AKo in cutoff is a premium hand. With two folds in front, I should open raise to 250-300 (2.5-3x BB). This is a standard play to build the pot and thin the field.

QcTc on the button vs a CO open to 3bb is a very playable, positionally advantaged hand. With 133 BB effective stacks I can 3-bet as a polarized play, but the min-raise to 500 is tiny and a small 3-bet will just invite calls from the blinds and bloat the pot out of position. I prefer to realise equity and use position — calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop with initiative on later streets, and preserves stack flexibility against the SB/BB ranges. Call 300 to play postflop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 6c, a weak unsuited connector. Facing a raise from the Cutoff (Claude) to 300 and a call from the Button (Minni), the pot odds are roughly 3.6 to 1, requiring about 27% equity to call. However, playing 56o out of position against two opponents (and potentially the Big Blind) is a losing proposition due to difficult playability and reverse implied odds. The hand is not strong enough to set-mine or speculate with cold-calling a 3bb raise from the worst position. Folding is the clear optimal choice.

T2o is a speculative trash hand with very low equity multiway against a cutoff raiser (likely strong range) and a button caller (decent range). Out of position in BB, postflop playability is poor. Pot odds are ~3.75:1 (200 into 750 becoming 950), but implied odds are insufficient given stacks and domination risks. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities early in the tournament.

I raised preflop with AKo and got called by Minni. Flop is 7c-4h-Jc - I completely missed but have two overcards and backdoor nut flush draw with Ac. As preflop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here. The board is relatively dry and I can represent Jx or an overpair. A small c-bet of ~1/3 pot (250) should take this down often enough to be profitable.

Board Jc-7c-4h, I hold QcTc on the button. I have a very strong draw: four clubs (9 club outs) plus three other Qs and three other Ts as additional pair outs — roughly 15 raw outs (~54% to improve by river) and about a 35% chance to make the flush by the river. The pot is 1000 and it costs 250 to call, so I only need ~20% equity to justify a call (250 / (1000+250) ≈ 20%). Claude’s small flop bet (250) looks like a wide c-bet from the CO, so calling to realize equity in position is the best line. A tiny raise to 500 is unlikely to fold many hands and would bloat the pot out of position if called. I’ll call 250, keep the pot manageable, use position on the turn to extract value if I improve (or bluff/shove selectively), and fold or proceed based on the turn card and his action.

I have AKo with just overcards on a 7c-4h-Jc-6d board. Minni called my flop c-bet, indicating they have something - likely a J, middle pair, or a draw. The 6d turn is a brick that doesn't help me. I don't have a flush draw (only one club in hand with two on board). With just two overcards and no real draw, continuing to barrel is risky. Pot control by checking makes sense here - I can try to hit an A or K on the river for free, or potentially bluff if checked to again.

I’m on the button with QcTc on Jc-7c-4h-6d. I have a very strong draw: 9 remaining clubs + 3 remaining queens = 12 outs from the turn (12/46 ≈ 26.1% to improve on the river). Claude opened CO and made a small flop c-bet (wide range) then checked the turn — that line looks weak/capped and is exactly the spot to apply pressure. The pot is 1,250; betting ~half-pot (625) is a semi-bluff that gets a lot of his missed hands to fold while preserving ~26% equity when called. As a sanity check, a half-pot bet only needs ~33% fold frequency to break even in the absolute worst case (if I had no equity); because I do have ~26% to hit, the required fold frequency is lower, so this is +EV. Plan: bet 625 to take the pot now or realize equity cheaply; if Claude check-raises large I will usually fold and re-evaluate only to moderate sizing.

I have AKo with just overcards on 7c-4h-Jc-6d. Minni called my preflop raise and flop c-bet, then bet 625 when I checked the turn. This betting pattern suggests they have a made hand - likely a Jack, middle pair, or possibly a straight with 85/53. I have only 6 outs (3 Aces, 3 Kings) for about 12-13% equity. Getting 3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity), calling isn't mathematically justified with just overcards. Even if I spike an A or K, I'm not guaranteed to be good. Folding is the disciplined play here.

We’re UTG 6-handed with 8♣5♥, an offsuit, disconnected hand that performs poorly versus typical calling/3-betting ranges. Opening it from the worst position invites tough play from multiple players behind and creates negative EV spots with little playability. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.
T7o is a weak offsuit hand in middle position with 4 players behind me. Not strong enough to open - easy fold.

Cutoff with J♦4♠ facing a bet that costs 100 to call (min-raise to 200). J4 offsuit is a weak, often-dominated hand with very poor postflop playability. Calling here would frequently put me out of position against the raiser or face further aggression from the button/SB/BB; 100 is small relative to my ~140bb stack but this is not a spot to invest in marginal equity. With many better hands to open/3-bet in position later, I preserve my stack and wait for a clearer opportunity. Fold.

Action has folded to me on the Button. I hold King-Ten offsuit, which is a strong enough hand to open-raise from this position to steal the blinds. My stack is deep (over 100 BB), and playing in position against the blinds is profitable. A standard raise size of 2.5 BB (250 chips) is consistent with the table flow and puts pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind to defend with better hands. If called, I will play the post-flop phases with the advantage of position.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 250 (200 to call). My hand is J7o, offsuit, with mediocre high card value, straight potential but poor playability OOP. Button ranges are wide for steals, but J7o is easily dominated by better Js or higher cards in villain's calling range. Postflop position is terrible in SB. Stack is 63BB, no need to defend marginal hands. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities. History shows Pro opens decently but button is stealing spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qh 7h. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button. The pot is currently 400, and it costs me 150 more to call, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.7 to 1 (requiring about 27% equity). While Q7 offsuit is a marginal hand, defending the Big Blind against a Button open of 2.5x with a Queen-high hand is generally standard in a 6-max format to avoid being over-exploited. I have a healthy stack of 12,050 and can afford to see a flop and play out of position against a potentially wide button opening range.

I am out of position as the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the Button (Pro). The flop is 4d-Th-4h. My hand is Queen-high with a backdoor heart flush draw (7h). This board is relatively dry, although it does favor a Button opening range which often includes Tens and pocket pairs. Standard play in this situation is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will check and see how Pro responds to the board.

I have flopped Top Pair (Tens) with a strong King kicker on a paired board (4-T-4). This is a very strong hand in a Button vs Big Blind scenario. The board texture includes a flush draw (two hearts), so betting serves to extract value from worse Tens, pocket pairs (55-99), and flush draws, while also denying free equity to random overcards. Checking back would allow the opponent to realize equity for free. A small continuation bet of roughly 35-40% of the pot is standard on paired boards to keep the opponent's calling range wide while still charging draws.

I am holding Queen-high (Qc 7h) on a paired board of 4d-Th-4h. Pro has made a standard continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550. While the price to call is relatively cheap (200 to win 750), I have no made hand, no direct draws, and am out of position. Floating with pure air here against a button open is generally a losing play over the long term, as I would be forced to fold to most turn bets or hope to hit a marginal pair. I'll fold and look for a better spot.

Pocket 4s in UTG. Small pair that plays well for set-mining. With 80BB effective stack, I have good implied odds if I hit a set. Standard open raise to 2.5x is appropriate - I'll fold to a 3-bet but look to see a cheap flop and hopefully spike a set.

Facing an UTG open to 250, I have Qh3d — a weak, off-suit queen that is heavily dominated by an UTG opening range. Calling 250 into a 400 pot gives poor pot odds (~38% equity needed) and I’ll be out of position with poor playability and reverse implied odds if I hit a single Queen. A 3-bet light isn’t attractive here against an UTG opener and there are several players left to act who could squeeze. With a healthy stack I should preserve chips for better spots rather than spew with Q3o. Fold.

I am in the Cutoff with [5s 6h], which is an unsuited connector. The player in UTG (Claude) has raised to 250 (2.5bb). 65o is a very weak hand to play against an Under-the-Gun opening range, as it lacks flush potential and is often dominated or difficult to realize equity with post-flop. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me who could squeeze or call. Folding is the standard and disciplined decision here.

Preflop on the button with 2h3s, an extremely weak offsuit hand with no connectivity, low cards, and no suited potential. Facing an UTG raise from Claude to 250 into a 400 pot, which represents a strong range. My stack is 6300 (63BB deep), but this hand has zero playability postflop, negligible fold equity, and is a standard fold even from the button against an early position raise. Preserving stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

Claude raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong opening range. My hand, Ace-Seven offsuit, is dominated by many of the hands Claude would be raising with (like AJ+, 77+, or better Aces). Additionally, I am in the Small Blind, meaning I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling is not ideal because of the position disadvantage and the likelihood of being behind, and 3-betting as a bluff against an UTG range is risky. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

Claude is opening UTG 6-handed to 2.5bb, which is usually a fairly strong range. ATo is comfortably strong enough to defend the big blind getting a good price, but it’s not a great pure 3-bet for value here because when UTG continues it’s often with hands that dominate us (AJ+/AQ/AK) and we’d be building a bigger OOP pot with reverse-implied-odds issues. Flatting keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity with a hand that can make top pair/strong draws, and preserves stronger 3-bets for hands like AJs+/AQo+/99+ while still allowing us to play postflop based on texture (more aggressive on favorable boards; more cautious on A-high, dry boards where domination is likely).

This flop (9-6-2 rainbow) is fairly neutral-to-slightly better for the BB defender in terms of having more 9x/6x and some low pairs, so I don’t need to lead to “protect” my range. With ATo specifically I have two overcards and some backdoor potential, but no immediate pair/draw, and building a pot OOP vs an UTG range that contains lots of overpairs/strong 9x is unnecessary. Checking keeps my range protected, lets Claude c-bet his range (so I can realize equity by calling small bets or take it away on good turns), and avoids spewing into a strong opening range.

I have pocket 4s on a 962 rainbow board - my pair is below all board cards. FiveTwo checked from BB after calling preflop. While I was the preflop aggressor and could c-bet this dry board, my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher now. Checking for pot control makes sense - I have some showdown value against unpaired hands but betting and facing a raise would put me in a tough spot.

Turn brings the Ace, giving us top pair (A♦T♠) in a pot that stayed small after a flop check-through. Because Claude checked back flop, his range is often weighted toward underpairs (77–KK), some 9x that didn’t want to bloat the pot, and unpaired broadways that will fold to pressure; he can still have some Ax, but our A♦ blocks a chunk of those combos. Leading small here accomplishes three things: (1) gets thin value from 9x/underpairs that will frequently call one bet, (2) denies equity to hands like KQ/KJ/QJ that might take a free card, and (3) sets our own price OOP while keeping the pot controlled versus his stronger Ax. If raised large, we can comfortably let it go given position and UTG strength.

Pocket 4s on 6-2-9-A board. My pair is below all board cards. The Ace on the turn is a bad card for me - it's very likely in FiveTwo's BB calling range. Their small 200 bet (1/3 pot) could be a blocker or thin value. I'm only drawing to 2 outs for a set. Given my underpair has minimal showdown value against any ace or pair, folding is correct here.

8♥7♥ in a 6-max game with a 140 BB stack is a playable suited connector — good implied odds and postflop playability. From UTG I prefer to open-raise rather than limp: it seizes initiative, isolates the field, and preserves fold equity. I’ll make a standard 2x open (to 200) — small enough to keep pots manageable with speculative holdings but large enough to discourage pure limpers. If I face a large, polarized 3-bet I’ll fold; versus a small/standard 3-bet from looser opponents I’ll consider calling and playing the flop because of deep stacks and implied odds. Postflop plan is to pursue straights/flushes, c-bet selectively on favorable textures, and avoid bloating the pot OOP without clear equity.

I am holding 8s 2s in middle position facing a min-raise (200) from the UTG player (Minni). This hand is extremely weak (disconnected low suited cards) and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an UTG range. There is no strategic merit in calling or raising. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

Preflop in cutoff facing a UTG raise to 200 from Minni, who has been aggressive in prior hands but UTG opens indicate a strong range. My holding 2h5s is offsuit, unconnected, and low-ranked trash with no playability or speculative value worth calling 200 into a 350 pot. Position helps but equity against raiser's range is abysmal (~20-30% at best), and implied odds are poor with this hand. Deep stacks don't justify a loose call. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten suited, which is a very strong speculative hand that plays exceptionally well in position. The UTG player, Minni, has opened for a small 2x raise. While a 3-bet is a viable option to take control of the hand, calling is often more effective with this type of hand to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage post-flop. JsTs has great playability and can flop strong draws or top pairs that I can value bet or use to bluff later streets. I will call the 200 to see a flop.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a button flat, with the big blind still behind. J5s plays poorly multiway and out of position: it’s often dominated when we make a jack, and our flush/straight potential isn’t strong enough to justify defending versus an early-position range plus a caller. Even with a decent immediate price, the reverse implied odds and likelihood of going 3+ ways make this a low-EV defend. Best is to cut losses and wait for a better spot.

J6o is a weak hand, but I'm getting 5.5:1 pot odds (100 to win 550) to close the action from the BB. With these odds, I can profitably see a flop and potentially hit a strong hand. Folding would be too tight given the price.

I flopped top pair with J6o on a 4s-Jc-7s board. There's a spade flush draw out there. I'm first to act with two opponents (Minni and Flash). I should bet for value and protection - my top pair is vulnerable to the flush draw completing. Betting around 1/2 pot (300-350) makes sense to charge draws while getting value from worse hands.

I have 8♥7♥ on 4♠ J♣ 7♠ — a vulnerable middle pair with modest showdown value. Claude's 325 into the ~650 pot is a standard half‑pot probe from the BB after I checked; his range includes lots of one‑pair/overcard c‑bets and some floats. I prefer to call 325 rather than fold (too weak) or raise (too thin and bloats a multiway, out‑of‑position pot). Calling keeps the pot under control, realizes equity, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn — if I improve (two pair/trips) or get a favorable runout I'll extract value; if I miss and face heavy pressure I'll fold.

I have flopped top pair (Jacks) with a ten kicker and a flush draw. This is an incredibly strong hand that has both immediate value and massive drawing potential. Claude, the big blind, has led into the pre-flop raiser and the button, which often indicates a strong made hand or a significant draw. Minni's call suggests she likely has a piece of the board or a high pocket pair. By raising now, I take control of the hand, build a pot for when my flush hits, and charge any other draws. Given the pot size and the bets before me, a raise to 1050 is a strong play that puts pressure on both opponents while utilizing my positional advantage.

I have top pair weak kicker (J6) on a 4s-Jc-7s board. Flash raised to 1050 after I bet 325 and Minni called. Flash's raise represents significant strength - likely a better jack (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT), a set, two pair, or a strong semi-bluff with a flush draw. My J6 is near the bottom of top pair hands. With Minni still to act behind me and my weak kicker, I'm often dominated or drawing thin. The 725 call into 2350 gives me decent odds, but facing aggression from the button with a marginal holding and another player behind, folding is prudent.

I have 8♥7♥ on 4♠ J♣ 7♠ — middle pair (7s) with an 8 kicker. Pot is 2350 and Flash check‑raised to 1050; I need to put in 725 more. That call costs 725 into a 2350 pot, so I’m getting ~23.6% pot odds to continue. Flash’s check‑raise range will include heavy value (sets, Jx, some overpairs) but also some semi‑bluffs/floats from the button, so my hand has enough equity to justify a relatively cheap call. Raising would be too thin out of position and folding is too tight given the pot odds and my showdown value. I’ll call to see the turn and re‑evaluate — fold to clear turn/river aggression if I don’t improve, extract value if I hit two‑pair/trips or villain shows weakness.

I check. On the turn (4♠ J♣ 7♠ 3♣) my 8♥7♥ is still only a single pair with a weak kicker — a hand with some showdown value but vulnerable to Jx, sets and overpairs. Flash’s large check‑raise on the flop heavily weights his range toward value, so betting or raising first into him out of position would be thin and likely to get me raised off my hand. Checking controls the pot (current pot 3075), keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me fold to significant aggression without risking a big chunk of my deep stack. Plan: if Button bets a small amount (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 1,000) I will call to realize equity; if he presses with a large bet (>~1/2 pot ≈ 1,500) I will fold. If he checks back I’ll evaluate river for value or a possible bluff-catch.

I currently hold top pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker and the nut flush draw (spades). This is an exceptionally strong hand. On the flop, I raised to 1050 to take control of the hand and build equity. Minni's call suggests she likely has a Jack or a flush draw of her own. The turn 3c is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since Minni checked to me, I will continue to bet for value and protection. A bet of roughly half the pot (1550) allows me to extract value from weaker Jacks (like J9 or J8) and draws while maintaining a healthy stack if I am check-raised or if the river is unfavorable. I am also happy to see a fold and take down the pot now.

I fold. Flash’s line (check-raise big on the flop to 1050, then a strong turn continuation of 1550) is heavily weighted to value (Jx, sets, overpairs) on this board. I only have a vulnerable middle pair (7 with an 8 kicker) out of position and raising would be too thin. The turn bet of 1550 into the ~3075 pre-bet pot is large — calling would require roughly 25% equity (1550 to win a ~6175 pot) which my hand is unlikely to have vs his polarized range. Preserving stack and waiting for a better spot is the correct play here.

I am holding 4d 5h in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand (low unsuited connectors) with very poor playability, especially from early position where I have to act first and have 5 opponents behind me. With a deep stack of 110 BB, there is absolutely no reason to enter the pot with this holding. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

T7o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated by better tens or higher cards. In middle position after UTG fold, standard play is to fold preflop to preserve stack and wait for better spots. No reads from history suggest loosening up here, and with deep stacks, no need to speculate.

Holding 5-4 offsuit in the cutoff is a standard fold. This hand has very little equity and does not perform well enough to justify an opening raise, even with several players already folded. The risk of being re-raised or called by players in the button or blinds with superior hands is too high.

Folded to us on the Button with only the blinds left. K3o is not a great hand, but in position it’s strong enough to open very wide and profit from immediate fold equity versus the blinds, while also playing well enough postflop when we make top pair. I’ll use a standard 2.5x sizing to pressure both blinds and avoid giving them too good a price to defend.

Q9o in the small blind facing a button min-raise. This is a marginal hand but getting decent odds (200 to win 400). FiveTwo has been active from late positions. I'll call and see a flop, though I'm out of position. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but worth defending at this price.

Fold. I have J♣2♦ in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 with the small blind calling — a 3-way pot. Calling 150 into a 600 pot requires about 20% equity to be profitable; J2o in a multiway pot is heavily dominated and has much less equity (~10–12%). I would also be out of position to both players for the rest of the hand, with very poor implied odds and kicker problems. A 3-bet bluff here is not credible enough versus these ranges and stacks. Better to conserve chips and wait for a stronger hand or a better spot.

Flopped top pair (9s) with queen kicker on 6-7-9 with two hearts. This is a decent but vulnerable hand - there are flush draws and straight draws out there (8 makes a straight). I should lead out for value and to charge draws. Betting around half pot to 2/3 pot is appropriate.

We opened very wide on the button, but K3o on 6s7h9h has essentially no equity: no pair, no real straight draws, and no backdoor flush. Claude’s small-blind donk/lead for ~60% pot is often a made hand or strong draw on this wet texture, and a bluff-raise would get continued against too frequently, putting us in tough spots on many turns. This is the bottom of our range and a clear spot to cut losses and preserve stack.

UTG in 6-handed play with T5s, a marginal suited hand at best. Standard tournament strategy calls for a tight opening range from early position to avoid difficult post-flop spots with a weak hand like this. No specific reads on opponents justify opening. Deep stack (63BB) allows waiting for better spots. Folding preserves stack.

I am in the Hijack position with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 6-max game. After the UTG player folded, I want to enter the pot with a standard raise to take control of the hand and build the pot. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is consistent with the table's betting patterns and allows me to define my range while putting pressure on the players yet to act.

Flash has opened to 3bb from middle position and we’re in the cutoff with KJo and position. Flatting is viable, but it invites the Button/SB/BB to squeeze and puts us in more multiway, dominated-equity spots. I prefer a position 3-bet here to (1) leverage fold equity preflop, (2) deny the blinds an easy squeeze, and (3) take initiative while still having a clear plan versus a 4-bet (fold). Standard in-position sizing vs 3bb is ~3x.

Q3 suited on the button facing a 3-bet to 900. This is a clear fold - Q3s is way too weak to cold-call a 3-bet, even with position. The hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify putting in 900 chips here.

I have Th3s in the small blind facing an open to 300 and a 3-bet to 900. This is a very weak, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability and I’ll be out of position to the raiser(s). The pot is 1,350 and calling 850 would commit ~6.7% of my 12,675 stack to see a flop (pot would be ~2,200) with little implied odds. 4-betting as a bluff to 1,500 is thin and likely to be called or shove-adjacent; I don’t want to play this marginal spot multiway. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 4d. There has been an open to 300 by Flash and a 3-bet to 900 by FiveTwo. Cold-calling a 3-bet from the Big Blind with low suited connectors is generally a losing play, especially with the original raiser (Flash) still left to act behind me who could 4-bet. Playing out of position against a strong range with a weak speculative hand and ~80BB effective stacks does not offer sufficient implied odds to justify the call. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Hijack position with Ace-Jack offsuit. I opened for 300, and FiveTwo in the Cutoff has 3-bet me to 900. FiveTwo has shown a high level of aggression and has 3-bet my opens previously (Hand 18). While AJ is a strong hand, it performs poorly out of position in a 3-bet pot, especially against a player who is frequently applying pressure. Calling leaves me playing a bloated pot without initiative or position, and 4-betting would likely only be called by hands that dominate me (AK, AQ, JJ+). Given my stack size and the early stage of the tournament, I will choose to fold and look for a more advantageous situation.

King-Deuce offsuit (K2o) is a very weak hand to open from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 6-max tournament. It is easily dominated by any other King that might call or raise, and it lacks the connectivity or suit to play well post-flop. Standard strategy dictates folding such a weak hand from early position.

6-handed and first in with UTG folded, K6 suited is within a reasonable MP opening range. Limping invites the Button/SB to isolate and puts us in tougher spots; raising leverages fold equity versus the blinds and keeps our range uncapped. Hand has decent playability (suited, some top-pair potential) and we’re deep enough to realize equity postflop when called. If we face a sizable 3-bet from the stronger/aggressive stacks behind, we can comfortably fold.
AQs is a premium hand in the cutoff. FiveTwo raised to 250 from middle position. Standard play is to 3-bet here for value and to isolate. I'll raise to around 3x their bet size.

3♣4♠ is a very weak, unsuited, low connector and is poorly positioned to handle a 3‑bet. FiveTwo opened to 250 and Claude re‑raised to 750 — a CO 3‑bet that represents a range with lots of pairs and broadways that dominate my hand. Calling 750 into a 1,150 pot gives ~2.53:1 pot odds (~28% equity needed), but 3‑4 offsuit has far less equity vs a 3‑bet range and has poor postflop playability. I’ll conserve my stack on the button and fold to this 3‑bet rather than investing chips in a marginal spot.

I am in the Small Blind holding KJo. Facing an open raise from Middle Position to 250 and a 3-bet from the Cutoff to 750, my hand is significantly dominated. Cold-calling a 3-bet out of position with KJo is a losing long-term strategy as I am likely behind the 3-bettor's range (typically 99+, AJ+, KQ) and risk a 4-bet from the original raiser. The pot odds do not justify the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage.

Preflop in the big blind facing a raise from FiveTwo (250) and a 3-bet from Claude (to 750). My hand is Qh2s, offsuit, disconnected, and queen-high with a terrible kicker. This hand has very low equity against the likely strong ranges of both raisers, especially Claude's 3-bet from cutoff. Pot is 1150, to call 650 into roughly 1800 effective pot is poor odds for a speculative hand with no suitedness or connectivity. No reason to defend this trash hand; folding preserves stack for better spots. Early in tournament (hand 26/150), no need to gamble.

We opened K6 suited as a playable MP hand, but Claude’s 3-bet to 750 is a meaningful size and we’ll be out of position if we continue. While the immediate price (500 to win 1150) looks tempting, K6s has poor equity and, more importantly, poor realization versus a CO 3-bet range (dominated Kx, tough top-pair spots). A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive with weak blockers and limited fold equity at these stacks. Best decision is to cut losses and preserve stack for better spots.

UTG in a 6-handed game, 23o is at the very bottom of the range with poor playability and no meaningful blocker value. Opening it burns EV and invites 3-bets/isolations from position. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Q8 suited in middle position is a playable hand. With good stack depth (79 BB) and only one fold before me, I'll open with a standard raise. Suited queens have decent playability postflop with flush draw potential and top pair possibilities.

J♠6♠ is a marginal but playable hand from the cutoff facing a standard MP open to 250. Claude has opened to a relatively small sizing and has shown a tendency to raise often, so his range is likely fairly wide. I don't want to 3‑bet bluff here with such a weak kicker and three players (button, SB, BB) yet to act — that invites a 3‑bet or complicated postflop spots. Calling preserves stack, keeps the pot manageable, and gives me position on Claude on later streets so I can realize equity (flush/straight/paired J) or fold cheaply if I miss. Plan: call 250, evaluate flop — continue aggressively only with strong equity or good reads, otherwise control pot and fold to heavy pressure.

I am on the Button with Ad-2c. Facing a raise from Claude (Middle) and a call from Minni (Cutoff), the pot is multi-way. A2o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by stronger Aces and plays poorly post-flop, especially in a multi-way pot where reverse implied odds are high. Despite having position, the risk of domination and the difficulty of navigating post-flop with this hand make folding the most prudent choice to preserve my stack.

68s is a weak suited gapped connector from the small blind. Facing a middle position raise to 250 by Claude and a call from Minni in cutoff, their ranges are strong (Claude's opening range likely includes broadways, pairs, suited aces; Minni's calling range similar). Pot is 650, calling 200 OOP multiway (BB still to act and could squeeze or continue) offers poor implied odds for this hand which rarely makes strong hands. Postflop play would be difficult out of position with reverse position disadvantage. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the big blind holding 7h 3c. Claude raised to 250 from middle position and Minni called from the cutoff. Although I only need to put in 150 more to see a flop and the pot odds are roughly 5.3 to 1, 7-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. It has very poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. Defending such a weak hand here would likely lead to losing more chips in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I missed this flop with Q8h - no pair, just backdoor flush draw and one overcard (Queen). Board is 4c 7s Jh - fairly dry. I have position on Minni who flatted my preflop raise. A standard c-bet here makes sense to apply pressure and potentially take it down. Around 1/3 pot sizing is appropriate on this dry texture.

I have top pair (J♠6♠) on J♣-4♣-7♠ with a very weak kicker. Claude was the preflop opener and now c‑bet a small 225 into this pot — a sizing consistent with a wide continuation range rather than a strong value-only line. I'm in position and the call is cheap relative to my stack, so I want to control the pot and keep worse Jx and many bluffs/overcards in. Raising risks getting committed or turned over by better Jx/strong hands and bloats the pot with a vulnerable kicker. Plan: call 225 to see the turn, then re-evaluate — fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve or get a favorable card that lets me extract value.

Turn brought 7d, pairing the board. I have Q-high with no pair and no meaningful draws - my backdoor flush draw is dead. Minni called my flop c-bet showing some interest. Firing a second barrel here with complete air against a player who showed strength would be burning chips. I should check and potentially give up or catch a Queen on the river. If Minni bets, I'll fold.

I check. I have top pair with a very weak kicker (J♠6♠) on a paired board (4♣–7♠–J♥–7♦). Claude’s tiny flop c‑bet and subsequent turn check point to a wide/medium range or a trap; betting here mainly bloats the pot and gets me check‑raised or committed by hands that already beat me (7x trips or Jx with a better kicker). In position I prefer pot control — see the river cheaply, induce bluffs, and avoid turning a marginal hand into a bloated pot. Plan: check to the river, call reasonable sizing for value from worse Jx or bluff catchers, and fold to large aggression unless I improve or get a clear read.

River brought 8d giving me a pair of 8s with Queen kicker. I improved from complete air to a made hand. Minni checked back the turn which suggests weakness - likely a marginal jack, small pair, or missed draw. My 8s beat missed draws and small pairs but lose to any Jack, any 7, or overpairs. Given the ambiguous situation, checking for showdown value is best. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a tough spot. Checking allows me to see a cheap showdown or potentially call a small bet.

I check. I have top pair (J♠6♠) but with a very weak kicker on a board of 4♣-7♠-J♥-7♦-8♦. Claude’s line (open pre, tiny 225 flop c‑bet, then checking turn and river) looks wide/capped rather than a strong value-only line. Betting here is thin value — few worse Jx will call and I risk a check-raise or turning the hand into a big pot when I’m often behind to KJ/AJ/QJ or 7x/trips. With the pot only 1,100 and a healthy stack (~12.2k) pot‑control is correct: take it to showdown and call small river bets as a bluff-catcher, fold to large aggression.
4c5d offsuit is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position where I need to play tight. Folding is the clear play here.

A♦8♦ in middle position is a raise-first-inable hand: an Ace with a suited connector gives a lot of postflop playability and an Ace-blocker reduces opponents' strong Ace combinations. The table has frequent open-raises around 250–300, and I have a deep ~133BB stack so I can apply pressure without overcommitting. I will raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to take the initiative, narrow the field and gain fold equity against the blinds and cutoff/button. Plan: if I get a single call I'll use position to c-bet and probe; if I face a large 3-bet I'll fold to avoid playing dominated with marginal kicker; consider calling a small 3-bet depending on who and sizing.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kh Kd) in the Cutoff, the second-best starting hand in the game. Minni has opened from Middle Position to 300 (3BB). With a premium hand, my goal is to build the pot immediately and isolate the raiser. Flat calling would be a mistake as it invites multi-way action which devalues high pairs. A standard in-position 3-bet size is typically 3x the opening raise. In previous hands (#9 and #10), I 3-bet to roughly 3.4-3.5x and received folds. To keep Minni's range (which likely includes weaker pairs, AQ, AK) in the hand while still building value, I will raise to 900 (3x). This size is substantial enough to charge draws but not so large that it forces out all hands I beat.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand facing a raise from MP (Minni) and a 3-bet from CO (Pro). Both have strong ranges. Pot odds to call 900 into 1350 (effective ~2:1 after call) require too much equity multiway, especially with blinds to act. No strong blockers for a 4-bet bluff. Position on button is nice but not enough value or playability. Standard fold to preserve stack.

I am holding Q2 offsuit in the Small Blind. Minni opened with a raise and Pro responded with a 3-bet to 900. My hand is extremely weak and significantly behind the likely ranges of both active players. Calling would put me out of position against strong opponents with a dominated hand. Folding is the only sensible play.

We’re facing an open to 300 and a 3-bet to 900, and we’d have to continue out of position against at least the 3-bettor (and potentially the original raiser) with 7♠5♣ offsuit. Even with decent pot odds, this hand realizes equity very poorly multiway and OOP, and it’s too weak to justify a squeeze/4-bet bluff given stack depths and the strength signaled by Pro’s 3-bet. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

Call to 900 total. A♦8♦ is a playable, suited Ace with an Ace-blocker and good postflop playability; Pro’s 3-bet to 900 is a standard size and not large enough to fold this hand outright. With deep stacks and reasonable pot odds, I prefer to see a flop and leverage fold equity and my drawing potential rather than 4‑betting with a marginal hand (which often gets dominated). Plan: play cautiously out of position — continue aggressively if I hit an Ace or diamond draw, otherwise fold to heavy postflop aggression.

I opened preflop with A♦8♦ and called Pro’s 3‑bet to see a flop. The board 2♣3♠7♦ is very dry and misses most of a standard 3‑bettor’s value range (QQ+, AK, big pairs). I hold an Ace‑blocker (reduces opponent Ace combos), two overcards and a backdoor diamond (runner‑runner flush ~4% to complete), so this is a very good spot to apply pressure and try to take the pot down. Pot is 1,950 — a bet of 1,000 (~50% pot) gives meaningful fold equity while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. Plan: if Villain gives a small raise I can re‑evaluate (call with plans to fold to large further aggression); if I face a big check‑raise I’ll fold (range advantage to them). If called I proceed cautiously and only invest more if I pair up or hit the diamond on the turn.

I hold Pocket Kings on a dry, low flop (2c 3s 7d). The opponent, who called my pre-flop 3-bet, has led out (donk bet) for 1000 into a pot of 1950. This line is polarizing; it often represents a set (22, 33, 77) looking to build the pot or a medium pocket pair (88-QQ) betting for protection/information. Raising here would likely fold out the hands I beat (like 88-QQ or random bluffs) and isolate me against sets that crush me. Calling is the superior option as it keeps her range wide, allows her to continue bluffing or overvaluing worse pairs on the turn, and controls the size of the pot in case I am behind to a set.

Check. The turn (2♥) does not improve A♦8♦ — my backdoor diamond is dead and I’m left with only Ace‑high. Pro’s line (3‑bet preflop, check‑call on a very dry 2♣‑3♠‑7♦ flop) looks like a fairly strong, sticky range (overpairs, some broadway A‑cards, medium pairs or disguised sets) and the board pairing to deuces increases the chance that a calling hand now beats me. I don’t want to inflate a 3,950 pot with air and risk a check‑raise or getting called by better hands. Pot control and seeing a cheap river is the right line — if Pro fires I can fold most rivers; if he checks behind I can reassess on the river. Action: check (0).

I hold Pocket Kings on a board of 2c 3s 7d 2h. The turn pairs the board with the 2h. Minni, who called my pre-flop 3-bet and led out (donk-bet) on the flop, has now checked to me. Her line of betting the flop and checking the turn often indicates a medium-strength hand (like 88-QQ) exercising pot control or realizing that her flop aggression didn't end the hand. It could also be a trap with a full house (77, 33), though less likely given the flop lead. The board is very dry with no draws. If I bet, I risk isolating myself against her nut range (sets/boats) or folding out the hands I beat (88-QQ) that might not pay off two more streets. Checking back controls the size of the pot, avoids getting check-raised, and induces her to potentially bluff the river or call a value bet on the river with a wider range. Given the SPR and the board texture, pot control is the prudent play.

I hold A♦8♦ and the river (6♦) does not give me a made hand — I’m still only Ace‑high. Pro’s line (3‑bet preflop, check‑call my dry c‑bet on the flop, passive check on the paired turn) is consistent with a sticky one‑pair/overpair or a slowplayed made hand; many of those hands will call a river bluff. The board pairing to deuces and the passive action on turn reduce my fold equity substantially, so betting here would be a low‑EV bluff and needlessly risks chips. I checked the turn to control the pot and should do the same on the river — take showdown for free rather than bluff into a range that calls. If Pro fires into me after my check I will fold since I have no pair and poor showdown equity.

I hold Pocket Kings on a final board of 2c 3s 7d 2h 6d. The opponent (Minni) called my pre-flop 3-bet, led out (donk-bet) on the flop, and then checked the turn and river. This line—aggressive on the flop then passive—strongly suggests a capped range, likely a medium pocket pair (88-QQ) or top pair that is now pot-controlling. It is highly unlikely she holds a monster (Full House or Quads) given she missed value bets on both the turn and river. With the board paired and relatively dry, my overpair is almost certainly the best hand. I should bet for value to target her medium pairs which might call a reasonable size. A bet of 1500 (approx. 40% of the pot) is small enough to induce a call from worse hands like JJ-88 or Tx, while maximizing value compared to checking back.

Line analysis: I opened, got 3‑bet pre and Pro then check‑called my sizable c‑bet on a very dry 2c‑3s‑7d flop — a line consistent with sticky, fairly strong hands (overpairs, medium pairs, some Ax). I checked the paired turn to control the pot; the river (6♦) gives me no pair or flush — I have only Ace‑high and can only win if Pro is pure bluffing. Pot is 5,450 after his 1,500 river bet; calling 1,500 requires ~21.6% equity (1,500 / (5,450+1,500)). Versus a 3‑bettor’s check‑call range on this board my showdown equity with bare A8s is well under that threshold, and I block only a small portion of his Ax bluffs. A raise as a bluff is too risky (he can call or shove with many better hands). Per my plan to fold to a river fire, I fold and preserve chips for better spots.

5♠6♠ is a small suited connector and my position is UTG with five players yet to act. From early position this hand is too marginal — it plays poorly OOP in multiway pots and is vulnerable to the frequent raises shown at this table. Calling 100 is cheap but will often commit me to a difficult postflop decision out of position or induce a raise behind; raising myself with this hand is overly ambitious and unnecessarily bloats the pot. With a healthy 114 BB stack I should preserve chips for better spots (late position, suited connectors with fewer opponents, or strong pairs). Fold and wait for a higher EV opportunity.

I hold 7h 9s in middle position with a 129BB stack. This is a weak off-suit one-gapper that performs poorly multi-way and is easily dominated. With active players behind me and no strategic reason to loosen my opening range this early in the tournament (Hand 29/150), the standard and correct play is to fold.

Pre-flop in cutoff position after UTG (Minni) and MP (Pro) both fold, facing unopened pot with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100 to limp). JTo is a strong opening hand from late position due to its playability, straight potential, and blockers to strong hands. Standard aggressive open-raise to build pot and take initiative with position advantage over button (Flash), SB (FiveTwo), and BB (Claude). Historical opens are around 250, which is ~2.5x BB. Raising to 250 applies pressure and fits tournament stack dynamics (61BB effective). Folding or limping is too passive for this spot.

I am on the button with a strong pocket pair (9c 9h). Elon, who has been active and aggressive from late positions in previous hands, opened with a raise to 250 from the cutoff. Pocket nines are well ahead of a typical cutoff opening range. While calling is an option to set-mine, 3-betting is the superior play here to seize the initiative, isolate the raiser, and potentially end the hand pre-flop. A raise to 750 (3x the open) is a standard sizing that puts pressure on the blinds and forces Elon to define his hand strength while I maintain positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

QJh is a strong suited broadway and plays well versus a likely wide button 3-bet range (especially given the CO open). Folding is too tight getting a reasonable price, but cold 4-betting from the SB creates a big, high-variance pot OOP where we’ll often get called by better (AQ/KQ/AJ/TT+) and don’t have great blocker coverage. Calling keeps in bluffs, realizes our suited equity, and keeps the pot manageable while we evaluate postflop textures and pressure.

4c5h is a weak offsuit hand facing a 3-bet and a cold call. Even with pot odds of ~2.8:1, this hand doesn't play well multiway against strong ranges. No suited value, low cards that will often be dominated. Easy fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash on the button after my standard CO open with JTo, followed by a flat call from FiveTwo in the SB. This creates a multi-way pot scenario where JTo offsuit has marginal playability: it's often dominated by BTN's stronger Ax, KQ, etc., or SB's suited connectors/speculative hands. Postflop, I act before the BTN but after SB, limiting my position advantage. Pot odds to call 500 into 1850 are decent (~4:1), but effective SPR remains high (~20+ BB), and JTo struggles multi-way without strong initiative. 4-betting is off-table with this hand against likely calling ranges. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament stage with deep stacks (60+ BB), avoiding variance in tough postflop dynamics. Historical aggression from Flash (e.g., Hand 25) supports folding to avoid spew.

This A-high, two-diamond flop strongly favors the preflop 3-bettor’s range (lots of Ax, big broadways, overpairs), so leading is unnecessary and often just isolates us versus stronger hands. With QhJh we have a valuable open-ended straight draw (8 or K) plus some backdoor heart potential, so our plan is to check and respond to Flash’s c-bet with an appropriate continue (often call vs larger sizing, sometimes check-raise vs small sizing to apply pressure and leverage fold equity).

I have flopped a set of nines, which is an extremely strong hand. The board (Td 9d Ah) is quite coordinated, featuring both a diamond flush draw and several straight draws (KQ, QJ, KJ). FiveTwo called a 3-bet out of the small blind, which often indicates a range of pocket pairs, suited Broadways, or strong suited connectors. Since FiveTwo checked, I must bet for value and protection. I want to get paid by Ax hands, Tens, and draws, while also charging those draws to see the turn. Checking back is too risky given the number of turn cards that could complete a straight or flush. A bet of 1000 into a pot of 1850 (roughly 54% pot) is a standard sizing to build the pot and price out the draws.

We’re facing a smallish c-bet (1000 into 2850), giving us strong immediate pot odds. With QhJh on AhTd9d we have an open-ended straight draw (8/K) plus some backdoor heart equity, which is comfortably enough to continue. Check-raising would bloat the pot OOP on a range-favorable A-high board and will often get continued by Ax/strong made hands/diamond draws, while we don’t hold a diamond to block continues. Calling keeps his bluffs and weaker broadways in, realizes our equity, and lets us apply pressure on favorable turns (K/8) or peel and reassess on bricks/diamond turns given the low SPR.

The 5c is essentially a brick: it doesn’t improve our QhJh (still just an open-ender to K/8) and it doesn’t meaningfully shift the range advantage away from the preflop 3-bettor on an A-high board. Leading here would often just fold out his bluffs while getting called by Ax/strong made hands and some diamond continues, bloating the pot OOP without having fold equity clarity. Checking keeps his range wide, lets him continue barreling with worse, and if he checks back we realize our equity with a free river.

I have a set of nines on a board where my hand is extremely strong, likely the best hand currently. The 5c on the turn is a complete brick that doesn't complete any of the obvious draws (diamonds or straights like KQ, QJ). FiveTwo's check-call on the flop suggests a range containing top pair (Ax), draws, or middle pairs. Since he checked again, I must continue betting for value and to charge any remaining draws. A bet of 2000 (roughly 52% of the pot) is large enough to extract value from an Ace and build the pot for a possible river shove, while still being a price that draws might feel compelled to call.

Turn pot odds: calling 2000 to win 7850 (pot after call) requires ~25.5% equity. With QhJh on AhTd9d5c we effectively have an open-ended straight draw (K or 8) = 8 outs ≈ 17–18% to hit on the river, so it’s a bit short on pure immediate odds. However, stacks are now shallow relative to the pot (low river SPR), and when we hit an 8/K we can often win additional chips from Flash’s double-barrel range (many Ax hands, some two-pair/overpairs), making up the gap via implied odds. Raising/jamming as a semi-bluff is unattractive here because this A-high texture heavily favors the 3-bettor and we don’t block his strong continues (no diamond), so we’d mostly fold out bluffs and get called by better. Plan: call; if we hit 8/K, value bet aggressively (often shove or choose a size that gets crying calls from Ax); if we miss, generally check-fold versus a bet, and consider bluffing only if checked to on select rivers.

River is a total brick for our hand: QhJh missed the open-ender (no K/8) and we have essentially zero showdown value on AhTd9d5c3c. In this 3-bet pot after we’ve called two barrels, Flash’s range is heavily weighted to Ax and strong made hands that won’t fold at this low SPR; we also don’t hold a diamond to block his value/draw continues, and we actually block some of his natural bluff combos (QJ/Jx broadways). A bluff lead/jam would need ~40% folds (5125 into 7850) which is unrealistic versus his value-heavy range, and smaller “blocker” bluffs just burn chips and can get jammed on. Best line is to check and give him the chance to check back with his misses; versus a bet we can comfortably check-fold.

I have a set of nines on a board that has mostly bricked on the river (3c). The only hands that beat me are AA and TT, which are highly unlikely given FiveTwo's passive line of check-calling two streets. He likely has a strong Ace (AQ, AJ) or perhaps AT, and has committed a significant portion of his stack already. With the pot at 7850 and FiveTwo having 5125 remaining, the optimal move is to put him all-in to maximize value. He is heavily pot-committed and will likely find a call with any top pair.

River we have QhJh on AhTd9d5c3c: we missed all draws and have essentially zero showdown value. Flash’s line (3-bet pre, c-bet flop, barrel turn, large river bet) is heavily weighted toward Ax and strong value that won’t fold, and we don’t hold a diamond to block his value range or unblock his missed draws in a favorable way. Calling 5125 to win 180? Actually to win 12975+? We’d need Flash to be bluffing around ~39% of the time versus this sizing, which is unrealistic given the A-high range advantage and his triple-barrel line. Preserving our remaining stack is higher EV than hero-calling with Q-high here.

I am in UTG position with Ace-Five offsuit (5c As). This is a weak hand to open from early position at a 6-handed table, especially with 129 BBs deep. It is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly out of position post-flop. The standard and most profitable play is to fold.

Preflop in middle position, unopened pot after UTG fold. Td7d is a suited connector but gappy and marginal for a 6-handed tournament range from MP. Standard open-raise range here includes stronger suited connectors like 98s+, pairs 66+, broadway hands. Raising risks playing out of position postflop with a speculative hand against aggressive opponents like Flash and FiveTwo who often 3-bet or call wide. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative. Folding preserves my 59BB stack (one of the shorter ones) for premium spots to maximize EV over 150 hands.

I am currently the chip leader with 17,325 chips. Holding Ten of Spades and Six of Spades in the Cutoff is a marginal situation. While it is a suited hand, the gap is large, and it does not perform well against most defending ranges from the Button or the blinds. Given that I have been playing aggressively in recent hands (notably Hand #29), my table image is likely perceived as wide, which increases the probability of opponents 3-betting or calling my open. I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage rather than opening a weak speculative hand from this position.

Folded to me on the Button with J6 suited. This is comfortably within a standard BTN opening range: we have decent playability, flush potential, and strong positional advantage. Taking the initiative also puts immediate pressure on the blinds and lets us realize equity well when called. I’ll use a straightforward steal sizing (2.5bb) to deny some equity and keep ranges clearer postflop.

4h2d is a very weak hand - one of the worst starting hands in poker. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds, this hand plays terribly postflop, is easily dominated, and has very little equity against a button raise range. Easy fold from the small blind.

I’m in the big blind with 7♣4♦ facing a button open to 250 (I’ve already posted 100; call = 150, pot would be 550). 7-4 offsuit is a very weak, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. Calling requires ~27.3% equity to break even (150 into a 550 pot) and I don’t have the hand strength or blockers to justify a profitable call or a light 3-bet here. The small blind has folded and the raiser is in position with a moderate stack — better to preserve my 11,300 stack and wait for a cleaner spot. Fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 6h9s offsuit. This is a trash hand: low cards, disconnected, offsuit, poor postflop playability especially multiway. Standard tight UTG range folds this easily. No position advantage, multiple players behind who have shown aggression. Preserve stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV.

I am in Middle Position with Ten of Hearts and Six of Diamonds (T6o). This is a weak, off-suit hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening raise from this position. Given my current chip lead and the tendency of players like Pro and FiveTwo to 3-bet or play aggressively, there is no reason to enter the pot with a sub-par hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

AcQc is a strong cutoff open in 6-max: it dominates many calling ranges, blocks premium continues (Ax/Qx), and plays well in position with initiative. With blinds likely the only money in (pot 150), raising is higher EV than limping because it pressures button/blinds to fold and builds a pot when we’re ahead. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to keep ranges wide while still generating folds.

I have 32s on the button facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo. While I have position, 32s is at the very bottom of playable hands. The suited aspect gives some flush potential, but there are still two players behind me who could squeeze. With such a weak holding, folding is the disciplined play here.

Cutoff opened to 250 (2.5x). I’m in the small blind with Qh Js — a playable hand that wants to see a flop but is vulnerable out of position and can be dominated by stronger Queens (AQ/AQx/AJ) or face 3/4-bets from the big blind. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal 3-bet, so I’ll defend by calling the extra 200 for a total committed amount of 250. Calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes equity, and lets me evaluate the flop against the CO and BB ranges.

I am in the Big Blind holding 2d3d facing a raise from the Cutoff to 250 and a call from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 600 chips (250+250+100), and I need to call 150 more. This gives me direct pot odds of 4:1 (20% equity required). Although 23s is a weak hand, it plays well multi-way due to its potential to make straights and flushes, and I am closing the action which guarantees I see the flop. My stack depth (128 BBs) allows for speculative calls. Folding is too tight given the price.
