Flop KH 5s Jc — I hold Js Qh: second pair (pair of Jacks) with a decent kicker. This is a marginal value hand in a multiway pot and I’m out of position versus the CO raiser and the BB. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and commit me to difficult decisions against likely Kx, Ax or stronger broadway hands in their ranges. I’ll check for pot control and to let the preflop aggressor either c‑bet or bluff — I’m happy to call a small-to-medium c‑bet to realize equity, but will fold or strongly re‑evaluate to a large flop check‑raise or heavy turn aggression that represents a King/two‑pair. If checked to, I’ll check back and take showdown; if I improve to two pair/sets or pick up strong redraws, I’ll start building the pot.

I hold 2d3d on a Kh-5s-Jc board, giving me 3-high with virtually no draws (no flush draw, very remote backdoor straight possibilities). This board texture interacts well with the pre-flop raiser's range (FiveTwo) and the Small Blind's calling range. Leading out (donk betting) into two opponents with zero equity is highly -EV. The standard and correct line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If faced with a bet, I will fold immediately.

Multiway flop as the preflop raiser on K-J-5 gives us a solid range advantage (we have strong Kx/overpairs/sets). With AcQc we also have a gutshot to the T plus two overcards to the 5 and a backdoor nut-flush draw, so a small c-bet both applies pressure to their wide calling ranges (small pairs, suited connectors, weak Jx) and sets up profitable barrels on A/Q/T or club turns. In a 3-way pot I’ll keep sizing small to risk less when they continue strong.

Call 250. I have second pair (Js) with a decent Q kicker on Kh-5s-Jc. FiveTwo’s bet is very small (~25% pot), so calling gets excellent pot odds and keeps a lot of worse Jx and bluffs in the pot. I’m out of position and multiway, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise — calling controls the size, realizes equity, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now and fold to clear, strong turn/river aggression that heavily represents a King or two‑pair/set; if I improve to two pair/sets or face only small continued pressure, then start building the pot.

I hold 2d3d on a board of Kh-5s-Jc. I have missed the flop completely, holding only 3-high with no flush draw and virtually no straight equity. Facing a continuation bet of 250 from the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and a call from the Small Blind (Minni), the pot is 1250 and I need to call 250. Despite the attractive pot odds (16.6%), my equity is near zero against two ranges that connect well with this broadway-heavy board. Continuing is strictly -EV.

I have Js Qh on K♥ 5♠ J♣ J♦ — that gives me trips (J♠ plus the two Jacks on board). The pot is heads-up against the cutoff who opened and c‑bet a small flop sizing; his range is wide (Kx, Qx, broadways, some pairs and bluffs). This is a clear value situation: most of his continuing range (Kx, Qx, Tx, weaker Jx, and bluffs) will pay a reasonable bet. I don’t want to check and let him realize equity for free or miss value; I also don’t want to overbet and fold out thin calls or create a bloated pot where a rare KK slowplay crushes me. I’ll bet about half‑pot (~625 into 1250) to extract value from one‑pair hands and worse broadways while keeping worse hands and some bluffs in. Plan if raised: call reasonable single raises and re‑evaluate (he may have KK or a monster if he check‑raises big); fold only to an over‑the‑top shove that commits too much against likely KK combos. Betting now maximizes value while controlling downside against the small number of hands that currently beat me.

Turn pairing the J is generally better for the preflop raiser, and Minni’s small 1/3-pot lead looks like a probe/blocker with a lot of medium-strength hands (Kx, 5x, TT-99) and some bluffs, in addition to some Jx. With AcQc I have a clean gutshot to the T for Broadway plus some additional (but not always clean) equity from hitting an A. Raising here risks isolating myself versus her strongest continues (Jx/boats) and getting jammed off my hand, while a call keeps her bluffs in, realizes my equity in position, and preserves the option to take it away on many rivers if she checks. If she barrels large on most bricks, I can comfortably fold; if she checks, I can evaluate a value-check (when improved) or a bluff (on favorable runouts).

I have Js Qh on K♥ 5♠ J♣ J♦ 7♦ — trips (three Jacks). Pot is 2,500 and the only remaining opponent (FiveTwo) opened from the cutoff, c-bet a small flop and called my half‑pot turn raise. That line suggests a wide continuing range (Kx, Qx, weaker broadways, some pairs) — many of which are behind my trips. The 7♦ on the river only gives a few specific hands a full house (pocket 77, 55, KK or combos containing the lone remaining Jack paired with K/5/7), but those combos are limited. I want to extract value from Kx/Qx and other worse hands without bloating the pot versus the small subset of full houses. A medium value bet (~half‑pot = 1,250) accomplishes that: it induces calls from thinner value hands and worse pairs while keeping my commitment reasonable if Villain check‑raises or jams. Plan: bet 1,250 for value; if he jams over this I will treat it as a very strong/full‑house line and fold; I’ll call small single raises but avoid getting stacked by an over‑the‑top shove.

We get to the river with AcQc as pure ace-high after missing the Broadway gutshot. Minni’s line (call flop, lead turn on the paired J, then half-pot lead again on the river) is heavily weighted toward made hands that want value/protection: lots of Jx (trips/full houses), Kx, and some 5x/medium pairs that turned into thin value/blockers. The key issue is that there aren’t many natural missed draws here, and our blockers (Ac/Qc) actually reduce some of the more plausible bluff candidates (AQ/QT/Qx floats), making this a poor bluff-catcher.

We’re getting 1250 to win 5000 (~25% required), but against a range that is mostly value and very few bluffs, calling is -EV. Raising as a bluff is also unattractive given stack-to-pot and the likelihood she continues with most value (especially any Jx). Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the UTG position with J2o, which is one of the weakest hands in the deck. In a 6-max tournament, opening from early position requires a much stronger range. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand, especially with several aggressive players behind me.

We’re first-in after UTG folds, but 7♦4♦ is too weak to open from middle position at ~41.5bb. It plays poorly versus 3-bets and realizes equity badly multiway; limping for 100 is also unattractive because we invite squeezes and end up in dominated, low-connectivity spots. Preserve stack and wait for a better open/defend candidate.
3d 6c is a very weak hand - offsuit, not connected, low cards. From the cutoff position, this is a clear fold. No reason to put chips in with this garbage hand even though I only need to call 100.

I’m on the button with K♥2♦ and it’s folded to me with only the blinds to act. K2o is marginal but, in position, it’s a reasonable steal candidate — calling 100 keeps a lot of dead money but invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB): large enough to generate fold equity and often isolate the big blind, but small relative to my stack. If I face a sizeable 3-bet I’ll fold K2o; if I get called I’ll continuation-bet selectively on king/ace or very favorable textures and play cautiously on dry or coordinated boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 5h, a very weak unsuited hand. Minni raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. Although Button raises are often wide, 95o is not strong enough to defend, especially being out of position and with the Big Blind still to act. The pot odds do not justify calling with a hand that has such poor playability. Folding is the only standard option.

Preflop in BB with TcJc suited connectors. Excellent speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Minni's button raise to 300 after three folds is likely wide, as button ranges are broad in multi-table tournaments. Pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into 450 gives ~31% implied odds needed, but hand plays well multiway or heads-up OOP due to playability. SB folded, so heads-up vs aggressive Minni. Folding too tight in BB defense; 3-bet possible but prefer calling to realize equity postflop with position disadvantage mitigated by hand strength. Standard BB defense vs button.

Flop completely misses our TcJc: no pair, no flush draw (clubs), no straight draw on Qs5d2s board. Open-ended would require connectors fitting gaps, but J-T gaps too much from Q/5/2. Dry board with two spades favors button c-bet range wide from button raise. OOP as BB, standard check-fold to aggression; no strong bluff incentive with weak blockers and deep stacks. Preserve chips for better spots in tournament.

I raised preflop and isolated the big blind; heads-up on a fairly dry flop 5♦ Q♠ 2♠. I hold K♥2♦ — bottom pair with a decent kicker. A large portion of the BB’s defending range (Kx, Ax, missed broadways, small pairs) missed this board, so I have both value and fold equity. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a small-to-medium continuation bet to either take it down now or get called by worse one-pair hands/draws that I can play from position. Bet size: 250 (~38% of the 650 pot). If he folds I win; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn — check/control unless I improve, and fold to a strong raise since his raising range will be weighted to Qx/sets/strong draws.

Facing a button c-bet of 250 into 900 on a dry Qs5d2s flop with TcJc, which completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (T-J doesn't connect for gutshot or OESD here), no flush draw. Board is low, uncoordinated with two spades, favoring Minni's wide button raising range to c-bet frequently for value/thin value/protection. OOP as BB, pot odds require ~18% equity (250/(900+250+250)), but we have ~0% showdown equity and poor playability. Raising as a bluff is suboptimal OOP with no blockers to Q/sets and deep stacks allowing villain to continue wide; better to fold and wait for better spots in tournament where we've defended wide preflop but realize no equity postflop. Preserve 5600 stack for premium hands.

6-handed UTG with KQo is strong enough to open for value and initiative. Limping invites multiway pots where offsuit broadways realize equity poorly, and folding is too tight at this depth. I’ll use a standard ~2.5bb open size that’s been common in this game to apply pressure, narrow ranges behind, and still allow a disciplined fold if faced with a large 3-bet from tight positions.

2d8d is a weak suited hand. Against an UTG raise from FiveTwo, this hand doesn't have enough value to call profitably. There are still players behind who could squeeze, and even if I hit my flush draw, it would be low. Easy fold.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 — an early-position open in 6-max represents a fairly strong range, so K♠2♠ is often dominated by his Kx/AK combos. That said it's suited (nut-flush potential) and very cheap to see a flop relative to my 13,025 stack (250 ≈ 2%). Calling keeps the pot controllable, preserves stack, and gives me position vs the opener on later streets. I won't 3-bet a hand this weak vs an early opener; I'll fold to a large 3-bet but otherwise see the flop and play based on texture (aggressive continuation only on favourable boards, otherwise check/fold vs heavy action). Flat-call for equity and positional advantage.

I hold 2h 3s on the Button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, unconnected low cards). Facing a raise from an Under-the-Gun player (FiveTwo) and a call from the Cutoff (Minni), my hand has very poor equity against their likely ranges. Despite having position, the cost to call (250) is not justified by the low potential of the hand. A squeeze play is also ill-advised given the strength of an UTG opening range. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Td9c is a marginal offsuit speculative hand. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in past hands, and a call from Minni in the cutoff. This suggests a strong range from FiveTwo and Minni likely calling wide. In the small blind, I'm out of position postflop, and multiway pots OOP with this hand are -EV due to poor playability. Pot odds (200 to call into 650) are about 3.25:1, requiring ~23% equity, but vs. UTG range, equity is lower, especially multiway if BB calls. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with dominated hand or facing 4bet. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. FiveTwo raised to 2.5bb from UTG, and Minni called from the Cutoff. The pot is currently 650, and it costs me 150 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 4.33 to 1, meaning I only need about 19% equity to make a profitable call. Although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents, the price is too good to fold a hand with a King and decent kicker, especially given FiveTwo's relatively active opening range and the short stack they are playing.

On this Ace-high flop, the board favors the pre-flop aggressor's (FiveTwo) range. I have King-high with no draw and limited equity. Checking is the standard play here to see how the other players respond. If there is any significant betting action, I will likely fold.

Multiway, I need to be more selective with c-bets, but this A-high, dry A-4-3 rainbow board still favors the preflop raiser’s range (strong Ax, overpairs that may still bet small, and sets). My actual hand (KQo) has very little equity and no real draw, so checking would often just concede initiative and let them stab freely. A small c-bet targets their whiffs (broadways, mid pairs that hate an Ace, random suited hands) at a good price. If I get called, I’ll mostly shut down unless I improve (K/Q) or get a very favorable turn; if I face a raise, I can comfortably fold.

Fold. I flat-called preflop to keep the pot small and use position, but this flop bet from an early-position opener is exactly the spot I expected to be dominated. Pot is 1050 and Villain bets 250 — I need ~19.2% equity to justify a call (250 / (1050+250)). With K♠2♠ on A♠-4♦-3♥ I have no pair, the Ace on board smashes my showdown value, and my only realistic outs are runner‑runner (two spades) or pairing the K (which is often still behind). Runner‑runner flush odds are ~4.2%, pairing the K by river is small and usually not good enough versus an UTG c‑bet range heavy in Axs/Ax. Against an UTG open/c‑bet I’m beating very little and calling here is -EV. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

On an Ace-high flop like this, the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) is very likely to have an Ace or a strong hand that outdraws my King-high. I have no draws and very little equity against their continuation bet. Since Minni has already folded and I am out of position, there is no reason to continue with this hand.

J9o from UTG is a weak hand to open. In early position, I need a tighter range. This hand is easily dominated by better Jx and isn't suited for added equity. With aggressive players behind me, folding is the disciplined play.

UTG (Claude) folded and I’m in middle with Ks9d — a playable, marginally strong hand (king with a decent kicker and a spade blocker). With five players behind (cutoff, button, SB, BB) I want to take the initiative and get fold equity while isolating the blinds. Table tendencies show frequent 2.5x–3x opens and loose blind defense, so a standard 3x-sized open is appropriate. I’ll raise to 300 to price out the weakest callers, build the pot when I’m likely ahead, and retain room to fold to a large 3-bet. Plan postflop: c-bet on dry, uncoordinated flops to leverage position and initiative; slow down or check when multiway or on highly connected/flushy boards; fold to heavy action without improvement. If faced with a big 3-bet from Pro/Flash, I’ll respect it and let go of K9o.

I hold [8d 4h], which is an extremely weak hand (unconnected, offsuit, low ranks). Minni raised to 300 (3BB) from Middle Position. I am in the Cutoff. With such poor holdings, I cannot profitably call or raise against an early/middle position open. The standard play is to fold immediately.

Preflop on the button with 6s9c offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor postflop playability. Facing a raise to 300 from Minni in middle position, who has shown aggression in prior hands (frequent raises and calls). Pot is 450, calling 300 gives insufficient odds for this hand's equity against her range, which likely includes strong holdings. Folding preserves stack for better spots. No value in 3-betting as a bluff given her stack (12475) and tendencies. Standard fold from button out of position effectively.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7d 2d, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from middle position. Even though it is suited, 7-2 is the worst starting hand in poker, and playing it out of position against a raise is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Minni’s 3x open from middle in 6-max is likely fairly wide, and we’re getting a good price in the BB (200 to win 450, ~31% needed). A8o has decent raw equity and an Ace blocker; folding is too tight. I don’t love 3-betting here with our stack and being out of position because we’ll often get called by better Ax and broadways and end up in tougher postflop spots with a dominated kicker. Best is to defend by calling and play cautiously postflop (continue on good Ace-high/8-high textures, proceed carefully on top-pair-A boards versus pressure).

We flop top pair with a weak kicker on an A-K-T texture that heavily favors the preflop raiser (lots of better Ax, AK/AT two-pair, and QJ that already has a straight). Out of position with a marginal made hand, the highest-EV line is to keep our range protected and control pot size by checking. We can comfortably continue versus a reasonable c-bet size, but we don’t want to start building a big pot by leading into a range that has us in tough shape when raised.

I have Ks9d on Kc Th Ad — top pair but a weak 9-kicker. Heads-up vs the BB who defended wide preflop, so his range includes lots of A, T, K and medium pairs. The flop is rainbow and relatively dry (few draws) but the Ace is a scary card for my kicker. I still have initiative as the preflop aggressor and should charge worse Kings and medium pairs while denying free cards. I’ll make a standard c-bet of ~1/2 pot (325) to get value and fold equity. If he raises very large or shoves, I’ll respect that strength and fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate if the board pairs or he shows further aggression.

We have top pair (A) with a weak kicker on an A-K-T board that strongly connects with Minni’s preflop raising range (better Ax, AK/AT, and QJ already made). Her flop bet is small (325 into 650), so we’re getting a very good price and our hand is well ahead of her c-bet bluffs and some thinner value/protection bets. Raising would mainly bloat the pot out of position and isolate ourselves versus a range that dominates A8 a lot, while also risking getting 3-bet/stacked by two pair+ and QJ. Best is to call, keep her bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the turn—continuing on many blanks versus reasonable sizing, but tightening up versus big barrels on cards that increase her nutted advantage.

Turn pairs the T, which meaningfully strengthens Minni’s continuing range (Kx becomes two pair, Tx becomes trips, AT becomes a boat) while our hand stays as one pair with a weak kicker. With SPR ~2, leading mostly bloats the pot into a range that can apply a lot of pressure and rarely folds better. Best is to check and play bluff-catch versus reasonable sizing, keeping her bluffs/probes in and controlling pot size out of position.

I have K♠9♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker on K♣ T♥ A♦ T♦. The turn pairing the Ten makes trips a real possibility for any caller holding a Ten and also leaves many callers with A× ahead of me. FiveTwo called my flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, which looks like either pot‑control with a better hand or a potential trap. With a vulnerable top pair and a paired board, I prefer pot control rather than building the pot further. I’ll check and see a river for cheap; if he fires a large bet I’ll concede and fold, while I’ll call smaller/medium river bets for showdown when appropriate.

River after Minni c-bets small then checks turn: her range is a mix of give-ups and a lot of hands that already beat A8 here (any Kx is now two pair with the paired T, QJ is still a straight, Tx/AT/AK are very strong). Our A8 has decent showdown value versus missed broadways/small pairs/air, but it’s hard to get called by many worse hands while we’re very likely to get called (or raised) by better. Turning it into a bluff is unnecessary and low-EV given her strong continuing range. Best is to check and realize our equity.

Board: K♣ T♥ A♦ T♦ 7♠. I hold K♠9♦ — top pair with a very weak kicker. Villain (FiveTwo) defended wide preflop, called my 325 flop c‑bet and then checked the turn and checked the river. That line is consistent with pot‑control with A‑x or K‑x, or a weak/marginal hand that doesn’t want to build a pot; trips or big two‑pair hands would more often have taken a more aggressive line. Betting the river has very little fold equity against Ax and will often get called by better hands while folding out only worse Kx occasionally — so it’s thin value and unnecessary risk with a vulnerable kicker. With the pot small relative to stacks and no reliable way to fold out better hands, the correct play is to pot‑control and go to showdown for value. I will check and call a small river bet, but concede to large raises.

UTG with T5s — a weak, easily dominated hand in early position. In 6-handed play I should be opening a much tighter range from UTG; limping is passive and will invite raises and multiway pots out of position, and raising with this hand is reckless because it’s often dominated and will put me in tough spots vs 3-bettors (Flash, Pro) or callers who have better broadway/connected cards. Stacks are deep (~121 BB), so preserving chips for better spots is the correct long-term play. Fold.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ac As), the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in middle position and the action has folded to me. My goal is to build the pot immediately and extract value. Limping is too passive and invites multi-way action which decreases my equity. A standard open raise in this tournament has been around 2.5x to 3x the big blind (250-300). I have previously opened to 250 with weaker holdings (e.g., Hand #21 with KTs), so using the same sizing balances my range and disguises the strength of my hand. Therefore, I will raise to 250.

Preflop in cutoff facing a MP raise to 250 into a 400 pot. Holding 9d6s, which is offsuit, low, and poorly connected with minimal playability or equity against a raising range. Pro's range from MP is strong (pairs, broadways, suited connectors), and 96o has poor equity against it (~30-35%). Calling or 3-betting would bloat the pot OOP with a weak hand that plays poorly postflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots; position doesn't justify speculative play here with 55BB effective stack. Long-term EV positive to fold trash.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o). Pro raised to 250 from middle position. While T9o has some playability in position, it is generally too weak to call a raise from an active opponent when there are still two players left to act in the blinds who could potentially squeeze. To preserve my chip lead and avoid being dominated in a bloated pot with a marginal hand, I will fold.

Pro opens from middle position to 2.5bb, and we’re in the small blind with J6o. Calling is unattractive: we’ll be out of position against the opener, often dominated when we make a pair (Jx/6x), and the big blind can come along giving us multiway problems. Although the price is decent, our realized equity is poor OOP versus a reasonably strong MP range. A 3-bet bluff isn’t appealing either since J6o has weak blocker value and plays badly if called. Best decision is to conserve chips and fold.
73o is a trash hand. Even with 2.7:1 pot odds facing a min-raise, this hand plays too poorly post-flop to defend. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with AJo (Ace of Clubs, Jack of Hearts). In a 6-handed game, AJo is typically strong enough to open-raise, although it is near the bottom of a tight UTG opening range. My stack is deep (127 BB). I noticed in Hand #35 I raised to 250 with AA, so raising to 250 here maintains a balanced range and disguises my hand strength. The player to my left (Elon) has been active, so I will open to 250 to take the initiative but be cautious if faced with a 3-bet.

K7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from Pro, who tends to raise with decent strength from early position based on history (e.g., Hand 15, 21). Middle position with multiple players behind, including deep stacks like Flash. Standard fold to preserve stack in a tournament aiming to maximize over 150 hands. No strong reasons to speculate with 55BB effective stack.

I am holding Ten-Three offsuit in the Cutoff position. The player in UTG (Pro) has opened with a raise to 250. Ten-Three is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a raise from early position. Even with my chip lead, there is no reason to waste chips calling or attempting a low-probability bluff here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 2.5x UTG open 6-handed, 6♦4♦ is at the very bottom of playable suited connectors. While we have position, our hand realizes equity poorly versus a tighter UTG range, and we also risk getting squeezed by the blinds (especially the deeper BB). With no strong blockers for a light 3-bet and no need to defend this marginally, the highest-EV line is to fold and preserve our 53bb stack for better spots.

Pocket 3s in SB facing UTG raise to 250. Small pairs are good for set mining. With my 7150 stack, I have ~28:1 implied odds if I hit a set. Pro's UTG raise suggests strength, but calling 200 to potentially stack him is worthwhile. Minni behind could squeeze, but that's a risk I'll accept for set mining equity.

UTG (Pro) raised to 250 and the SB called — I’m in the big blind with J♦7♥. Pot is 600 and I must call 150, which gives me ~20% break-even equity (150/(600+150)=20%). J7o is marginal and often dominated by UTG’s opening range, but with the SB in the pot it’s a multiway spot where c-bets will be less effective and I’m getting a cheap price to see a flop. A tiny reraise to 400 is just a limp-raise that won’t achieve much and 3-betting as a bluff OOP is unnecessary with this hand. I’ll call to see the flop, plan to play cautiously postflop (look to fold to heavy aggression unless I pair the J, pick up a strong draw, or can realize equity cheaply).

Missed my set with pocket 3s on a T-4-2 board with two hearts. I have an underpair - any overcard likely beats me given Pro's UTG raising range. Board is relatively dry but has a flush draw. With first to act and two players behind including the preflop raiser, checking is the right play. No reason to build the pot with the weakest pair when I could face aggression.

I have J♦7♥ on a T♦-4♥-2♥ flop in a 3-way pot (UTG open + SB caller). I’m unpaired with only a backdoor heart draw (needs both turn+river — very unlikely) and a few pair-outs that are frequently dominated. Facing an UTG opener and the SB, my equity is poor and I’m out of position, so bluffing OOP here is unnecessary and high-risk. Checking controls the pot, lets me fold to meaningful aggression, and preserves chips to exploit better spots. Plan: check and fold to a substantial c-bet; only continue if I pair or see a very small probe bet I can call to realize equity cheaply.

I opened UTG with AJo and was called by the SB and BB, creating a 3-way pot of 750 chips. The flop is Td 4h 2h. I have two overcards and the Jack of Hearts, giving me a backdoor flush draw. In a multi-way scenario, c-betting frequency should decrease compared to heads-up, especially with a hand that has showdown value but is vulnerable. The board texture connects reasonably well with the calling ranges of the blinds (sets, pairs of tens, flush draws). Betting folds out worse hands (King-high) but isolates me against better hands (pairs) or strong draws that will not fold. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, see a free turn card, and avoid getting check-raised. It also protects my checking range. Therefore, I will check behind to control the pot size.

Turn 7c doesn't change much. Everyone checked the flop which is interesting - Pro declined to c-bet. I still have an underpair with pocket 3s on T-4-2-7 board. No reason to bet - I have showdown value against air but fold to any aggression. Checking maintains pot control with a marginal hand.

I have J♦7♥ — bottom pair (7s) with a Jack kicker in a 3‑way pot against the UTG opener and the SB. Both opponents checked flop and turn, so ranges look weak or controlling; betting OOP for thin value is unlikely to be profitable and could get me raised. I’ll check to control the pot and see the river for free, planning to call small bets with showdown value or fold to large aggression; if checked to on the river and I improve or have clear value, I’ll consider a small value bet.

On the turn, the board is Td 4h 2h 7c. The action checked to me again after a checked-around flop. I hold AJo, which is currently Ace-high. The 7c doesn't significantly change the board texture for my hand, though it adds some straight possibilities for opponents. Both opponents have shown weakness by checking twice, but betting here yields little value: worse hands (like KQ, QJ) will fold, and better hands (any pair) will likely call. By checking, I realize my equity for free, seeing a river card that could improve me to top pair, and I keep the pot small with a marginal showdown hand. I have position, so taking the free card is the low-variance, +EV play.

River 2d pairs the board. I have pocket 3s giving me two pair (3s and 2s), but this is very weak. Any Tx, 7x, 4x beats me with better two pair, and any 2x has trips. Everyone checked flop and turn showing weakness, but betting here only gets called by better. I have pure showdown value against missed flush draws and complete air. Checking to showdown is optimal.

River: I improved — I hold J♦7♥ and the board is T♦-4♥-2♥-7♣-2♦, so I have two pair (7s and 2s). This was a fully checked, multiway pot (UTG open + SB caller + me) and both opponents have shown passive lines; that makes strong value-heavy holdings (trips/full house) possible but not likely to have been bet. A river bet here would often fold out worse hands (thin value) and only get called by hands that either beat me (2x trips/full houses) or by sticky one-pair hands — while risking a check‑raise from trips. With a healthy stack and a paired board that makes my hand vulnerable to a deuce or a 7, the prudent line is to check and take the showdown. I will check to see what my opponents do and call a small river bet but avoid bloating the pot or committing to raises without stronger reads.

The river is the 2d, making the final board Td 4h 2h 7c 2d. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn, and now checked to me on the river by both the Small Blind and Big Blind. My hand is Ace-Jack high (playing the board's pair of Deuces with Ace-Jack-Ten kickers). 

Given the passive action from both opponents, they likely hold missed draws (hearts), weak pairs, or weaker high cards. Betting here has little utility: worse hands like King-high or missed draws will fold, so I extract no value. Better hands like a pair of 4s, 7s, or a weak Ten might call out of curiosity or suspicion, meaning I would only lose more chips. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value, which is sufficient to win against busted draws and lower high cards. It is the lowest variance play in a small pot.

UTG with 6 players, holding 4c7d offsuit, a very weak hand with poor connectivity, low cards, and no suited potential. Standard poker strategy dictates folding such trash hands from early position to avoid playing out of position post-flop with a dominated range. Pot is small (150), but committing chips here risks my stack unnecessarily when better spots await. Tight play preserves stack for premium hands.

I am holding a premium pocket pair (Tens) in middle position. Since the action has folded to me, I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind to build a pot and narrow the field. Pocket Tens is a strong hand that performs well against most ranges at this stage of the tournament.

We’re in the cutoff facing a 3bb open from Flash with 6♠2♣, one of the weakest holdings. Calling creates reverse implied odds (we make dominated pairs and weak draws), and 3-betting is a pure punt this deep with players left to act (button and blinds). Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.
J4o is a weak hand facing a raise from middle position. Even on the button, this hand doesn't have enough playability to call or 3-bet profitably. Easy fold.

I’m in the small blind with Q♥9♠ facing a 3bb open to 300 from Flash. Q9o is marginal but has reasonable playability (can make top pair, backdoor straights/flushes) and with stacks deep the implied odds justify seeing a flop for a small additional investment. I’ve already posted 50, so calling 250 more (300 total committed) is a small price to realize equity. I don’t want to 3-bet with this hand — it’s often dominated and I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously postflop (fold to large 3-bets/overbets, continue with top pair or good draws, float selectively). If the big blind 3-bets significantly I will fold unless the sizing gives great odds.

I am in the Big Blind with A2o facing a raise from Flash in Middle Position and a call from Minni in the Small Blind. The raise size is 3bb. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 900, requiring roughly 22% equity), but my hand plays very poorly in a multi-way pot, especially from a sandwiched position (acting between the Small Blind and the original raiser post-flop). A2o suffers significantly from reverse implied odds; if I hit an Ace, I am often dominated by Flash's opening range. My realized equity will be lower than my raw equity due to the positional disadvantage. Against an MP open and an SB flat, A2o is typically outside the profitable defense range. A fold is the disciplined, +EV decision here.

I completely missed this flop with Qh9s — no pair and no diamond draw on 3d‑5c‑2d. I’m out of position to the preflop raiser (Flash), and this board heavily favors a C‑bet from his range. Pot is small (~700) and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to inflate a pot OOP with nothing. Plan: check and give up to any reasonable continuation bet. I will only continue if Flash checks (free turn) or makes a very tiny C‑bet I can plausibly float (e.g. ~¼ pot) based on observed tendencies; otherwise I fold and preserve stack. If I pair on a later street I’ll reassess. Action now: check.

I have an overpair with pocket Tens on a low, connected board (3-5-2). This is a strong position, but there are potential straight draws (A4, 46) and a diamond flush draw available. Minni checked to me, so I will place a continuation bet to extract value from smaller pairs, draws, and Ace-high hands while also protecting my equity. A bet of around 65% of the pot is appropriate here.

I checked the flop as planned and Flash C‑bet 450 into a ~1,150 pot. Calling 450 to win ~1,600 (after my call) requires roughly 28% equity. I have Q♠9♠ (no pair, no diamond draw) and I'm out of position, so my real equity vs a reasonable MP c‑bet range (Ax, broadways, pocket pairs, plus diamond draws) is well below that. Raising as a bluff OOP with Q9o is too risky and unlikely to get it through given his sizing and stack depths. I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I am in UTG (Under the Gun) position with Ten of Spades and Nine of Hearts (T9o). In a 6-handed game, T9o is a weak hand to open from early position as it doesn't play well against the likely calling ranges or 3-bets from the players behind me. Given my chip lead and the need to maintain a solid image, I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

Qs3h is far too weak to enter from middle position in a 6-max game. It’s dominated by the queens and broadways that continue versus opens, has poor playability postflop, and we don’t need to risk chips trying to steal with a hand that can’t realize equity well. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.
A7o in the cutoff with two folds in front of me. This is a standard open-raise hand from this position. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to steal the blinds or play in position against callers.

I’m on the button with 3♣3♦ facing a standard cutoff open to 250. With ~123 BB effective this is a classic set‑mining spot — calling preserves position and gives me the implied odds to hit a set (≈11.8% on the flop, ≈19% by the river). A 3‑bet with a small pocket pair is unnecessarily risky (I’d often bloat a pot OOP or face a 4‑bet), and folding is too tight given my position and stack depth. Plan: call, see the flop; if I hit a set I’ll build the pot, if not I’ll use position to probe or fold to heavy action depending on texture and opponents.

I hold J2o in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button, I am out of position against two players. Even with pot odds of roughly 3.25:1, my hand has poor equity and very poor playability. It is not profitable to call, and definitely not suitable for a bluff raise in this spot. The correct and standard play is to fold.

Facing a cutoff raise to 250 from Claude and a button call from Minni, both of whom have shown aggressive tendencies in prior hands (Claude raises frequently, Minni calls wide). In the big blind with T8h suited connectors, I get excellent pot odds to call 150 more into a 650 pot (pot will be 800, requiring ~19% equity, which T8s exceeds with playability). Suited connectors thrive in multiway pots with implied odds from deep stacks (mine 5450, Claude 6700, Minni 12100). Raising OOP risks isolation with a speculative hand and poor post-flop position. Folding is too tight given the price. Calling keeps the pot multiway, allows set-mining/flush/straight potential on favorable flops, and exploits opponents' wide ranges.

On this flop (4d 2d 9c), I flop an open-ended straight draw with T8hh (8-9-T, 8 outs to the nuts straight: 4x7 + 4xJ, ~32% equity heads-up, even higher multiway). Backdoor heart flush draw adds value. Board is draw-heavy with diamond flush draw possible, low coordinated cards favor speculative hands like mine. OOP first to act in a bloated 800-chip pot multiway vs. aggressive opponents (Claude's raise, Minni's wide call). Betting risks isolation/raises from better draws/sets/pairs and poor post-flop position; checking realizes equity for free if checked to, controls pot size, disguises strength, and invites bluffs/small bets I can call profitably with deep stacks (mine 5300, Claude 6700, Minni 12100). History shows frequent check-calls post-flop (e.g., Hands 8,14,27,36), so pot likely stays small. Folding impossible with this equity; raising too aggressive OOP.

I have A7o on a 4d-2d-9c flop. I raised preflop and have position on both callers. This is a dry board where I should c-bet frequently. I have ace high with a backdoor nut flush draw in diamonds. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot should take this down often against overcards that missed, while building the pot when I have equity.

I called preflop with 3♣3♦ to set‑mine. Flop is 4♦‑2♦‑9♣ and Claude (CO) — the preflop raiser — made a small c‑bet of 250 into a ~1050 pot (~24% pot). I have an underpair (no set). Calling 250 would make the pot ~1300 so I’m getting ~19% pot odds. My chance to hit a set by the river is only ≈8.4%, so calling purely to hit a set is +EV negative. That said the bet is small, I have position, and Claude’s cutoff c‑bet range will contain many overcards/missed hands that can fold to turn aggression. With deep stacks and a cheap turn price I’ll call to use position to probe the turn: if I hit a set I’ll build the pot; if I miss I’ll check and evaluate based on his turn action (call small bets, fold to large pressure, or stab if he shows weakness). A raise here as a bluff is too risky (could face a re‑raise or caller behind).

Facing a small bet of 250 into a 1300 pot after my check, with Minni's call behind, I have outstanding pot odds: calling 250 to win 1550 (pot becomes 1800 post-call, ~14% equity needed). My Th8h open-ended straight draw (8 outs to nuts straight: 4x7 + 4xJ, ~32% equity by river heads-up, ~25% multiway) plus backdoor flush draw and overcards crushes this requirement, especially with deep effective stacks (min 5300-11850). Board is dry-low with diamond flush draw, favoring my speculative hand over typical CO raise ranges (overpairs like JJ+, AQ suited, broadway) and BTN calling ranges (pairs, suited connectors, Ax). Raising OOP risks bloating pot/facing 3-bet shoves from better draws/sets/pairs while isolating from Minni's equity-sharing call; folding discards massive equity/implied odds in multiway pot. Calling realizes equity cheaply, disguises hand strength, induces further small bets/bluffs (opponents' history: frequent check-calls, small bets like Hand 37 Flash 450), controls pot size OOP, and sets up turns where I hit ~17% of time or float profitably. Tournament stage favors patient play with positionally disadvantaged but equity-rich spots to build stack gradually over 150 hands.

On the turn Kd, the board is 4d 2d 9c Kd, completing the diamond flush draw while my Th8h retains an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to the nuts: 4x7s for 7-8-9-T, 4xJs for 8-9-T-J; ~32% equity heads-up, solid multiway). Kd is a flush-completing card but also a high overcard that may have hit Claude's CO raising range (KQ+, AK) or Minni's calling range, though it bricks my hand directly. Acting first OOP in a 1550 pot multiway (me 5050, Claude 6450, Minni 11850 effective), betting risks a check-raise from completed flushes/sets/two-pairs (board coordinated low + flush), isolates from Minni's equity-sharing range, and commits me forward awkwardly if called/raised given position. Checking realizes equity for free, controls pot size, disguises my draw as weakness (consistent with BB defense history), induces bluffs/value bets from better hands I can call profitably with excellent implied odds/deep stacks, and invites small bets like flop (history: frequent small post-flop aggression e.g., Hand 37 Flash 450, Hand 31 Minni 625). Tournament phase (hand 38/150, ~50BB stacks) favors patient, equity-maximizing play over thin value/semi-bluffs OOP multiway; checking sets up cheap calls on river if I miss (~17% hit rate) or extraction if I hit. Raising/folding absurd with this equity.

The turn brought the Kd, giving me the nut flush draw with my Ad. I have excellent equity here - any diamond gives me the nuts. Both opponents checked, showing weakness. I should bet to charge any weaker draws, protect against random pairs, and build the pot for when I hit. A bet around 60% pot (~950) applies good pressure.

I’m on the button with 3♣3♦. After calling preflop and the small flop c-bet I called, the turn K♦ changes texture — Claude now bets/raises 950 into a 2500 pot. I have 11 clean outs on the river (two remaining threes and nine diamonds) ≈ 24% equity to improve. Calling 950 would get me pot odds of about 950/(2500+950) ≈ 27.5%, so a pure pot-odds call is slightly negative vs a single opponent. However I have position, can realize my equity, and can extract extra value when I hit; raising here is unattractive (no real value, limited fold equity, multiway pot with the BB likely to continue, and I’d face a dangerous shove from Claude). Folding is too tight given the inexpensive price to see the river and the implied fold/river value. Plan: call the 950, preserve positional advantage; if the river is a 3 or diamond I will bet for value/possibly shove depending on sizing and opponents; if I miss I will check and fold to large bets or look for a cheap showdown/bluff if Claude shows weakness.

Facing Claude's turn bet to 950 into ~1550 after my check, followed by Minni's flat call, creating a 3450 pot, offering excellent 3.6:1 odds (need ~22% equity to call 950). My Th8h holds a clean open-ended straight draw (8 outs to the nuts straight: 4x7s/4xJs; ~17% river hit rate), plus Th overcard, backdoor hearts, totaling ~20%+ equity multiway conservatively, enhanced by implied odds from deep stacks (my 5050 →4100 post-call, Claude 5500, Minni 10900). Kd completes diamonds, polarizes Claude's CO raise/flop bet/turn aggrobet range to flushes (AdXd/KdXd), K-hits (AK/KQ), value monsters (99/22/44/two-pair), or bluffs (missed draws/overpairs), while Minni's calls suggest medium strength/draws/pairs. Folding discards massive equity/implied in tournament mid-phase (hand 38/150, ~50BB); raising OOP multiway commits stacks awkwardly (~half stack), risks cooler CR from flushes/sets, isolates equity-sharing Minni. Calling realizes equity cheaply, controls pot OOP, disguises strength (consistent BB check-call history: Hands 3,5,8,etc.), induces river small value/bluffs (opponents' patterns: small bets/calls e.g., Hand 37), sets up paid straights (~17% hit, stacks them) or cheap folds if bricked vs nuts. Patient play maximizes EV over 112 hands remaining.

Missed the open-ended straight draw entirely on the 6♣ river brick. Hold ten-high (Th8h) on a 2♣ 4♣ 6♢ 9♢ K♣ board with no pair, no flush, no remaining draws. Pot 4400 with 4100 behind OOP first to act vs. Claude (CO aggressor: preflop raise, flop bet 250, turn bet 950) and Minni (BTN persistent caller pre/flop/turn). Their ranges polarized to diamond flushes (AdXd, KdXd), Kx top pair (AK/KQ/KT), sets (KK/99/44/22), two-pair combos, or thin value/overpairs after flush-completing Kd turn. Minimal bluff equity given action history and deep stacks. Bluff-betting pot (~4400 but capped at 4100) lacks fold equity multiway OOP vs. capped calling stations/value heavies; risks isolation raise/call-down OOP. Checking controls pot OOP, disguises nothing-hand range (consistent BB spec play), induces value bets/checks-behind from medium strength I beat rarely (worse than any pair), preserves 4100 stack (~41BB, healthy) for superior equity spots in tournament phase (38/150 hands, 6 players). No showdown value; folding to post-check bet optimal. Patient preservation over spew maximizes long-term stack growth.

I missed my nut flush draw - the river 6c is a brick. I have ace-high only. Both Minni and Elon called my flop and turn bets, showing they have something on this board. Bluffing into two opponents who've called two streets is -EV. Best to check and potentially showdown ace-high, though I likely lose.

Check. I have 3♣3♦ and never improved — I missed my set. Claude’s line (CO open, small flop c‑bet, then a sizable 950 turn bet) represents real strength (Kx/9x or two diamonds that become a made hand on the turn), and Elon has been passive but stuck around. With three diamonds on the turn the board allows strong showdowns (or two‑diamond combos) that beat my small pair. Betting as a bluff into two callers who have shown strength has very poor fold equity; there’s almost no value to extract. The lowest‑variance play is to check to showdown and fold to a large river raise (only consider calling a very small, clear blocking/river‑bluff sizing).

We’re 53bb UTG in a 6-max game with QTd (a suited broadway that flops strong draws and reasonable top-pair value). This is comfortably within an UTG opening range. Raising is preferred over limping to leverage fold equity, define ranges, and avoid going multiway out of position. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x open to keep the pot manageable versus aggressive 3-bettors while still applying pressure to the blinds.
4c2d is one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a UTG raise with this trash hand is an easy fold. No reason to waste chips here.

Fold. 4♦7♣ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability and little chance to realize equity against an UTG open. There are still the button, SB and BB to act — this spot is likely to become multiway or to face a 3‑bet, both of which make the hand worse. Calling 250 chips to play a dominated, low-equity hand out of position is +EV negative; likewise, a 3‑bet bluff is inappropriate here. Preserve my 15,300 stack and wait for a better spot in position with a hand that can compete.

Opponent FiveTwo is opening from UTG very frequently (observed roughly 50% frequency in recent orbits), which suggests a wide range. My hand, Ad6d, has solid equity against a wide UTG opening range (approx. 56% against top 50%). Being on the Button gives me a significant positional advantage. While FiveTwo has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets, calling allows me to keep their range wide and play a pot in position with a hand that plays well post-flop (nut flush potential) without bloating the pot or isolating myself against a stronger range. I choose to flat call to utilize position and equity.

Preflop in SB with 6c5s facing a UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo (50BB stack, aggressive history) and a button call from Pro (deep). Pot 650, calling 200 into it offers ~3.25:1 odds, but 65s is a speculative suited connector with low equity OOP against an UTG raising range (likely strong pairs, broadways). Multiway pot likely with BB behind, and I'll be out of position postflop. No strong implied odds given stack depths (my 40BB). Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 150 hands total.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ac6h. UTG raised to 250 and the Button called. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 650, giving me excellent pot odds of roughly 4.3 to 1. While A6o is a weak hand to play out of position, the price is too good to fold, especially with the potential to hit an Ace or a semi-coordinated board. 3-betting against an UTG opener and a caller with this hand is unnecessarily risky given my chip lead, so I will take the price and see a flop.

I have checked the flop with Ace-high on a board of Qh-5h-Ks. This texture significantly favors the ranges of the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and the button caller (Pro), both of whom are likely to have combinations of Kings, Queens, or heart draws. With no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart draw, I have little reason to bet. My plan is to check and fold to most significant bets, as my hand lacks the equity to continue against aggression on this coordinated board.

Multiway (BTN + BB) on a KQx two-tone flop, our QTd is a medium-strength made hand (second pair) that’s often dominated when action continues (Kx, AQ/KQ) and doesn’t have a strong draw to fall back on (no hearts; only backdoor diamonds/straight possibilities). A flop c-bet will get called frequently by better queens, kings, and heart draws, bloating the pot out of position and putting us in tough turn spots. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets us realize our equity, and we can still continue versus reasonable bets depending on sizing and bettor.

On a board of Qh 5h Ks, I have Ace-high with Ad 6d. The pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and the Big Blind (Flash) have both checked to me. While FiveTwo's check on this board texture (which favors their range) indicates weakness or a trap, the board hits general ranges quite hard (Kings, Queens, flush draws). Betting here with only Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw is risky as I likely won't fold out a King or Queen, and semi-bluffs (hearts) will call. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (catching an Ace) for free and keeps the pot small with a marginal hand. I will take the free card.

On the turn, the board has paired with a second Queen (Qh 5h Ks Qs). The flop checked through among all three players, which suggests that neither FiveTwo (the pre-flop aggressor) nor Pro (the button caller) likely holds a King or a strong Queen. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs. By checking, I can potentially see a free river or evaluate the strength of my opponents if they decide to bet. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like a King or a slow-played Queen), and since I am out of position against two players, checking is the most prudent play.

Turn pairs the queen giving us trips (QQQKT) with QdTd. After a flop check-through multiway, both opponents’ ranges are capped and contain a lot of Kx, 5x, heart draws, and underpairs that will now continue versus a reasonable value bet. We should start building a pot and deny equity to hearts/overcards, while keeping sizing controlled in case we run into the small portion of hands that beat us (AQ, KQ, 55, KK). A ~60% pot bet accomplishes this and sets up a river value decision.

On the Turn, the board pairs the Queen (Qh 5h Ks Qs), making trips possible and keeping the King in play. FiveTwo, the original raiser, bets 500 into an 800 pot after checking the flop. This line (Check-Call or Check-Back Flop, Bet Turn on pair) often signifies a King going for value, a Queen that was trapping or slow-playing, or a semi-bluff with a draw like J-10 or hearts. My hand, Ace-high with no draw (Ad 6d), has very poor equity against a King or Queen (drawing to 3 outs or dead). Even against a pure bluff, I am only marginally ahead and would face a difficult decision on the river. Furthermore, the Big Blind is still left to act behind me. Calling here is not profitable given the board texture and the aggressor's renewed interest. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am holding Ace-high (Ac6h) on a board of Qh-5h-Ks-Qs. In the pre-flop action, FiveTwo raised from UTG and I called from the Big Blind. The flop checked through, which initially suggested weakness from the aggressor. However, on the turn, the board paired with a second Queen and FiveTwo has now bet 500 into a 800 pot. While the flop check might indicate a missed hand, FiveTwo's UTG opening range is generally strong. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing with missed cards like AJ, AT, or suited heart draws, most of those hands (Ace-high with a better kicker) currently beat my A6. Calling here with no pair and no draw out of position is a losing play in the long run, as I would be forced to guess again on the river. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.
9d6s is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is an easy fold - the hand has no real value and I'm first to act with 5 players behind me. No reason to put chips in with this hand.

I have J♣8♥ in middle position at a 6-handed table with deep stacks (I have 153 BB). This is a marginal but playable hand in 6-max — I want initiative and to avoid multiway pots. The blinds are 50/100 (pot 150, call 100, min-raise 200), so I open-raise to 3x the big blind (300) to get fold equity, narrow the field, and build a pot when I hit the flop. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll reassess (likely fold to a strong isolation 3-bet, consider calling a small squeeze depending on sizing and players).

I hold A5o in the Cutoff facing a 3bb raise (300 chips) from Minni in Middle Position. Minni has been active but demonstrated willingness to call 3-bets in Hand #28. A5o is easily dominated by an MP opening range (AQ, AJ, AT, better pairs) and plays poorly post-flop, often suffering from reverse implied odds. With a stack of 121BB, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot. 3-betting is risky given Minni's tendency to defend, and calling is loose. Folding is the most prudent long-term play.

Preflop on the button with 9s2s, a weak suited gapped hand with low playability and poor postflop potential. Minni raised to 300 (3x BB) from middle position, pot is 450, calling 300 for ~1.5:1 implied odds not justified for this trash hand. Multiway pot likely with blinds behind. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities; standard fold vs UTG/mid raise.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o, which is a weak starting hand. Minni opened for 3x from a middle position. Q7o is easily dominated by Minni's opening range, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand given the current table dynamics and the weakness of my cards.

Minni opens to 3bb from middle position and we’re out of position in the BB with 83o. Even with decent pot odds, this hand is highly dominated, realizes equity poorly OOP, and doesn’t have strong playability (no suitedness, weak straight potential). With ~58bb effective for us, a low-quality defend or speculative 3-bet bluff isn’t worth the variance here; best is to preserve stack for better spots.

UTG with 5♥7♠ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor post‑flop playability and dominated by most opener/defender ranges. This is early position in a 6‑hand game: open‑raising range should be tight, and limping/calling 100 into a 150 pot (you’d need ~40% equity to break even) is not justified here, especially since this hand performs worse multiway and out of position. A raise is also unjustified — the SB and BB have medium stacks and the table has aggressive players who can 3‑bet or shove and put me in a very difficult spot. Preserve my deep stack and fold to wait for a better spot.

I am in Middle Position with Queen-Seven offsuit (Q7o). This is a statistically weak hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, especially from this position with four players left to act, including the button and the blinds. Opening this hand would be loose and negative EV. The standard play is to fold.

Preflop in cutoff with two folds before me (UTG and MP), unopened pot. A4o is a playable opening hand from this late position due to ace blocker, decent high card strength, and strong postflop playability with position against blinds. Standard open-raise size of 2.5x BB to build pot and take initiative. FiveTwo and Claude may defend wide but my position allows control postflop. Stack depth supports this without overcommitting.

I am on the button with 9c 5d, which is a very weak hand. Elon, who has a relatively short stack (38 BB), raised from the cutoff. There is no reason to play this hand profitably here, as I'd be calling or raising with trash against a range that likely dominates me. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

CO open to 2.5bb is likely fairly wide 6-handed, and we’re getting a good price (200 to win 600 total after our call, ~2:1 immediate) with a suited hand that can make playable flush/straight/paired boards. A 3-bet bluff is unattractive versus Elon’s shorter ~38bb stack (higher jam frequency, poor hand to continue), and folding is a bit too tight given the odds. We’ll defend by calling and play a controlled, mostly check-to-raiser postflop plan OOP.
J2o is a weak hand. Getting 4:1 pot odds but will be out of position against 2 players postflop. J2o plays poorly multiway and is easily dominated. Folding is the disciplined play.

We flop top two (T6) on a board where the preflop raiser will c-bet frequently with a wide range (overcards, Tx, heart draws, overpairs). Checking keeps his range wide and lets him put money in with worse hands, and it also sets up a comfortable value check-raise to start building a pot toward stacks given the still-high SPR. If he checks back, we can lead turn for value/protection versus hearts/straight turns.

Preflop raised A4o from CO, called by SB FiveTwo, who checks flop. Flop 6h3cTh is dry and disconnected, missing most of villain's calling range while hitting some weak pairs or draws. My hand has Ace-high showdown value, a gutshot straight draw to the 5 (3-4-5-6), backdoor club flush draw, and position for postflop control. This is a standard spot for a continuation bet to apply fold equity against a weak SB defending range, deny equity to his floats/draws, and build pot when ahead. Half-pot size balances value/protection with fold equity without overcommitting stacks (my 3800, pot 600). FiveTwo's history shows check-calls but also folds to aggression postflop.

We flopped top two (T6) on a fairly dynamic board with two hearts. Elon’s small 1/3-pot c-bet comes from a very wide range (overcards, Tx, overpairs, heart draws), and with his ~30bb effective stack we want to start building a pot immediately while denying cheap equity to hearts/overcards. A check-raise also sets up an easy stack-off line versus his likely continue/jam range (Tx+, overpairs, strong draws). If he jams over this, we snap-call.

Facing a check-raise from the SB on a relatively dry flop (6h3cTh rainbow with two hearts). My As4c blocks some Ax, provides ace-high showdown value, and a gutshot straight draw to the 5 (for 3-4-5-6 straight), plus backdoor club draw. Preflop, SB called my CO open with a wide range. However, check-raising after checking flops often indicates polarization: strong hands like sets (TT, 66, 33), two-pair combos (T6s, rare), top pair good kicker (AT, KT suited), or semi-bluffs (hearts flush draws like KhQh+, straight draws like 78, 45, 57). Pot odds require ~25% equity (900 into 3000), and my hand has decent equity vs draws (~50%) but poor vs made hands (~15%). FiveTwo's history shows postflop aggression and occasional folds (e.g., Hand 16 to cbet after calling 3bet, Hand 24 to check-raise), but check-raises polarize, and this board favors his defending range. With 3500 stack (35BB), folding marginal ace-high preserves stack for better spots in 150-hand tournament, avoids reverse implied odds on turn/river where villain can have monsters or improved draws (hearts, straights). Calling commits ~25% stack in position but risks difficult decisions ahead; raising/shoving too thin without domination. Discipline: fold to aggression here and re-steal later.

I am in UTG position with 9s 7d (offsuit one-gapper). This is a very weak starting hand, especially from early position with 5 players left to act behind me. The hand has poor playability and is easily dominated. With a stack of 121 BB, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with trash hands. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Preflop, first in after UTG fold in middle position with T8d suited connectors. Playable hand with good postflop potential due to suited, connected nature. Effective stack 35BB, so raising for value, fold equity, and to build pot or steal blinds. Standard sizing around 3x BB. Minni in BB is deep but aggressive; button FiveTwo active. Raising commits reasonably without overplaying.

Elon has been raising frequently from early and middle positions throughout the tournament. While Ace-Seven offsuit is a playable hand in some late-position scenarios, it is significantly behind a standard middle-position opening range and plays poorly post-flop after calling. Given Elon's relatively short stack (32BB) and the presence of aggressive players like Minni still to act behind me, 3-betting as a bluff is risky. Folding is the most disciplined approach to maintain my current stack advantage.

Elon is opening from middle position for 3bb with ~32bb behind, which makes 3-bet bluffing unattractive because he can comfortably 4-bet jam and put us in a miserable spot. Flatting on the button is normally fine with hands that realize equity well (suited connectors, suited gappers, broadways), but T5 offsuit is too dominated and plays poorly postflop, plus the blinds (especially Minni with a big stack) can squeeze. Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I have QQ, a premium hand, in the small blind facing a middle position raise to 300. This is a clear 3-bet for value. I'll size to around 900-1000 to build the pot and potentially isolate. A 3x sizing of ~900 is standard here.

Fold. 3♠5♠ is a very weak, low-suited connector and this spot is not profitable to defend. Elon opened to 300 and Claude (SB) 3-bet to 900 — a polarized/strong range. Calling 800 into a 1,300 pot would make the pot 2,100 and requires ~38% equity to break even; 3♠5♠ does not have that vs a 3‑bet range and its equity only worsens in a possible multiway pot if Elon peels. We’d also be out of position postflop and have no fold equity or good showdown value. A small 4‑bet or cold‑4 is nonsensical with this hand. Preserve the stack and fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 from SB Claude after my MP open-raise with T8d suited connectors. Pot would be 1900 after calling 600, with ~2600 behind (SPR ~1.4), committing the stack OOP heads-up where speculative suited connectors perform poorly without multiway action or position. Claude's history shows some aggression but not frequent light 3-bets from SB; likely strong range. 4-betting T8d lacks blockers or premium value, and min-raise to 1500 offers marginal fold equity not worth the risk with 32BB stack. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in tournament.

UTG position in a 6-handed game with Ts4h, an offsuit, unconnected trash hand with poor playability and low equity. Standard fold to avoid spew from early position. Pot is small (150), but committing chips preflop with this hand risks my 32BB stack unnecessarily. Prioritize tight play to maximize long-term stack growth over 150 hands.

I am in Middle Position (MP) with Ace-Eight of hearts. Given that I am the chip leader with over 17,000 chips and the table is 6-handed, A8h is a standard opening hand. I want to apply pressure to the shorter stacks behind me (FiveTwo and Claude) and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300.

We’re in the cutoff facing a 3bb open from Flash with 9♠2♦. This hand has very poor equity, terrible playability, and no meaningful blocker value for a 3-bet bluff. Calling invites squeezes from the button/small blind/big blind and puts us in tough postflop spots even in position. Preserving stack and waiting for better steal/defend candidates is higher EV.
J4o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from Flash in middle position, this hand doesn't play well enough to call or 3-bet, even with button position. Easy fold.

I’m in the small blind with T♥2♥ facing a 3x open to 300 from middle (Flash). This is a very weak, low suited hand with poor playability out of position against a raiser’s likely MP opening range. To continue I must call 250 into a 450 pot (pot would be 700 after my call) — I’d need roughly 250/700 ≈ 36% equity to break even — T2s’s equity versus a standard MP open is well below that. A min-raise to 500 has virtually no fold equity and commits me to a bloated pot OOP versus two competent stacks. With a healthy 15,300 stack and many better spots to apply pressure, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold here.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from Flash in Middle Position. My hand is Q6s. Pot odds are roughly 31% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). While Q6s has approximately that much raw equity against a standard opening range, realized equity out of position is significantly lower. The 3x sizing makes a call less attractive compared to a min-raise. Q6s suffers from reverse implied odds (kicker problems if I hit a Queen). Therefore, folding is the disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Queen-Five of hearts. While suited, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for a 6-handed game from this position. With a healthy stack of over 170 big blinds, there is no need to play marginal or weak hands out of position, especially with active players like Claude and FiveTwo behind me. Elon in the Big Blind is also short-stacked (31 BBs) and might look to shove over an open. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

J8o is a marginal open from middle position in 6-max and plays poorly when called/3-bet (dominated by common continues like AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, and weak postflop equity). With multiple active, capable players left to act (CO/BTN/SB) and a short BB who can jam over opens, this is a low-EV spot to force. I’ll pass and wait for a better stealing hand/spot.
92 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even in late position with two folds ahead, this hand has poor equity and playability. No reason to get involved with such a marginal holding.

Folded to me on the button with 6♠4♣ — a weak, disconnected hand but in the best seat at the table. With both blinds to act and healthy stacks around the table, this is a spot to attempt a steal. I’ll raise to 250 (≈2.5x BB) — a bit above the minimum to generate fold equity and discourage loose defends while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet or shove (I don’t want to call big off with 6-4o); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously to any strong resistance.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7d 7h) in the Small Blind. The action has folded to Minni on the Button who raises to 250 (2.5bb). Minni is an active player with a wide opening range from the Button. 77 is a strong hand that is ahead of Minni's range, but playing out of position in a 3-bet pot can be difficult if overcards flop. Flat calling allows me to 'set mine' with good implied odds given the deep stacks (I have ~120bb, Minni ~150bb) and keeps Minni's range wide. While calling invites the Big Blind to enter, it is a lower variance play than 3-betting a mid-pair OOP. I am getting good pot odds to see a flop.

Preflop in the big blind with 5c3h, a completely unplayable offsuit hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and no suited potential. Facing a button raise from Minni (who is aggressive and deep-stacked at 15050) and a small blind call from Pro (11750 stack), the pot odds of calling 150 into 750 (post-call pot would be) are not sufficient given the terrible equity, reverse implied odds out of position, and high likelihood of being dominated or outdrawn postflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament with 150 hands total and my modest 31BB stack.

I hold Pocket Sevens on a board of Qs 9d Ah. This flop connects very well with the Button's opening range, which includes many Ax, Qx, and broadway hands. My hand is currently an underpair to the board and has very low equity against any value hand. Being out of position with a weak hand on a board that favors the aggressor, my best course of action is to check. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given the strength of their range on this texture. Leading out (donk betting) serves no purpose here as I unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call.

I opened preflop with 6♠4♣ as a button steal, but this flop (Q♠ 9♦ A♥) is very bad for my hand. I have no pair and essentially no realistic draws (only one spade on board + one in my hand — not enough for a flush; no meaningful straight outs), while the caller from the small blind defends a wide range that includes many A/Q/9 combinations and pocket pairs that connect well here. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep, so a c-bet is unlikely to fold out better hands and would commit chips with almost no equity. I’ll check to control the pot, give up to significant aggression, and only continue to small probes or when the turn gives a clear bluffing opportunity or free cards to improve.

The turn card is the King of Clubs, making the board Qs 9d Ah Kc. This is an incredibly coordinated board heavily favoring the pre-flop raiser's range (Button), which contains many broadway cards (Ax, Kx, Qx, Jx, Tx). Any Jack now makes a straight, and many two-pair combinations are possible. My pair of Sevens is now significantly counterfeited and has negligible showdown value. Minni checked back the flop, which could imply a weak Ace, a mid-pair, or a draw, all of which likely improved or remained ahead on this turn. Leading out would be throwing chips away as I cannot credibly represent the nuts (JT) easily, and better hands will call. The best play is to check and fold if faced with any aggression.

Check. With 6♠4♣ on Q♠ 9♦ A♥ K♣ I have no pair and essentially zero showdown equity or meaningful draws. SB defended a wide range and has shown passive lines (call pre, check flop, check turn) — that range contains many A/K/Q pairs and pocket pairs that all beat me. A turn bet here would be a pure bluff with poor fold equity and would unnecessarily bloat the pot with deep stacks. I’ll control the pot and check back to the river, folding to a bet this street. If checked to on the river and it’s a clean blank (doesn’t pair the board or complete obvious draws), I’ll consider a targeted small bluff; otherwise I’ll give up.

I hold Pocket Sevens on a board of Qs 9d Ah Kc Qd. My final hand is Two Pair, Queens and Sevens, with an Ace kicker. Minni (Button) raised pre-flop but has checked back both the flop and the turn. This passive line strongly suggests Minni does not have a strong Queen, Ace, King, or the straight (JT), as those hands would likely bet for value or protection on such a connected board. Minni's range likely consists of weak pairs (like a 9, or pocket pairs 22-66, 88) or complete air/missed broadway draws. My hand beats all unpaired hands and lower pocket pairs. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (which will fold) and will only be called by better hands (like a 9 or a trapped top pair). Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply and potentially win against Minni's weak check-down range or induce a bluff from missed draws.

I raised preflop as a button steal and was called by the SB. After passive check-check on the flop and turn, the river pairs the queen (Qs 9d Ah Kc Qd). With 6♠4♣ I have no pair, no real showdown equity, and no credible draws. The opponent has shown a very passive line (call preflop, check flop, check turn) — a range that contains many A‑x, K‑x, Q‑x and pocket pairs that all beat me or at least chop/win at showdown. Pot is only 600 while stacks are deep, so a bluff here would be thin: the paired river actually makes a bluff less believable and reduces fold equity. Given the line and board texture, the least costly, highest EV line is to check and give up to a bet (I will fold if they bet).

AQs is a clear UTG open in 6-max: strong top-pair value, good blockers to AA/QQ/AQ, and it plays best with initiative rather than limping into a multiway pot. With ~64 BB effective vs the deeper stacks, I want to build the pot for value while still keeping the open size standard and not inflating it unnecessarily. A 2.5x open also pressures the shorter SB stack and discourages the blinds from realizing equity too cheaply.
J6o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Out of position with poor card strength - this is a clear fold. No reason to call 250 with this marginal holding.

Call 250. 8♣7♦ is a marginal but playable, connected hand and I'm deep (≈150BB). FiveTwo's UTG open to 2.5x represents a stronger range, so a 3-bet bluff with 87o is thin (poor blockers, easy to be dominated). Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position/late-seat leverage, and buys me the implied odds to hit straights/two‑pair against deep stacks. Pot odds to call are borderline but acceptable given implied odds; I'll fold to a large 3‑bet/shove, but otherwise see the flop and play aggressively with strong draws or made hands and proceed cautiously on dry boards.

I am on the Button with As3c (A3o). The UTG player (FiveTwo) opened to 2.5bb, and the Cutoff (Minni) called. A3o is a weak hand multi-way, especially against an UTG opening range which dominates me (AQ, AJ, AT). I have very poor playability post-flop and high reverse implied odds if an Ace hits. Calling is losing money, and squeezing is too risky against an UTG opener and a caller who covers me. The disciplined and correct play is to fold.

Pocket sevens is a strong premium hand, roughly top 5% of hands. Facing an UTG raise from aggressive FiveTwo (6k stack) and a flat call from loose Minni (14k stack), 3-betting is optimal to deny equity to worse hands, build the pot with value, and take initiative. Calling risks multiway pot where 77 has reverse implied odds against overcards. 900 is a standard sizing (~3.6x the raise, accounting for dead money and caller), committing 900 from my 3050 stack (30bb effective). BB Flash (17k) may fold or call light, but FiveTwo likely folds weaker hands often (history shows folds to 3-bets). If shoved on, 77 is ahead of UTG calling range. Folding is too tight preflop.

I am holding pocket Tens (TT), which is a premium pair in a 6-handed game. The Small Blind (Elon) has made a 3-bet and is very short-stacked with only 2,200 total. By 4-betting to 2,200, I am effectively putting Elon all-in and putting maximum pressure on the original raiser (FiveTwo) and the caller (Minni). This strategy aims to isolate the short-stack and prevent a multi-way pot, where the value of a pocket pair decreases. Given my large stack and the dead money in the pot, this is an ideal spot to be aggressive.

Facing a cold 4-bet after our UTG open (and with a caller + a short SB 3-bettor creating lots of dead money), flat-calling is awkward: we’ll be out of position, SPR will be low, and we’ll frequently miss and be forced to fold while still having committed a big chunk of our stack. AQs has strong blocker value (A/ Q reduce AA/QQ/AQ) and plays best by realizing equity immediately. With Elon effectively committed and likely to put the last 1300 in very wide, the dead money improves our shove’s EV and reduces the equity we need when Flash calls. Best line is to 5-bet jam to maximize fold equity vs any light 4-bets and avoid poor realization postflop.

Action so far: UTG (FiveTwo) opened to 250 and I called with 8♣7♦. Elon 3‑bet to 900, Flash 4‑bet to 2200 and FiveTwo shoved/5‑bet to 6175. It’s 5925 to me into a pot of 9525 — pot odds ≈ 5925/(9525+5925) ≈ 38.4%, so I need ~38% equity to break even. Facing a multiway, polarized line (FiveTwo’s huge 5‑bet/shove and Flash’s 4‑bet) I’m almost certainly up against very strong ranges (JJ+/AK from the shover, QQ+/AK from the 4‑bettor, and a strong 3‑bet from Elon). 8♣7♦ offsuit has very poor realized equity multiway (well under the required ~38%) and calling would commit ~40% of my stack with little chance to win at showdown. Raising as a bluff is unrealistic — the shove already polarizes and I don’t have meaningful blockers. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Facing a 4-bet from BB Flash (deep stack, aggressive history with large bets like in H18, H25, H29) after my SB 3-bet, followed by UTG FiveTwo's 5-bet shove to 6175 (his ~6k stack, now 250 left; FiveTwo is aggressive UTG raiser but shows strength here after action escalates, history of folding weaker to 3-bets but jamming premiums/overpairs/AK). Pocket 77 (~top 5%) is strong but crushed by 4/5-bet ranges (AA/KK/AK primarily; equity ~20-35% vs AA/KK/AK combo). Pot odds ~4.3:1 (need ~19% equity), but Flash acts behind and likely jams/call with nuts (worsening equity/multiway). No fold equity (all-in call). Postflop unplayable. Tournament stage (H45/150, 22bb effective) favors folding to preserve stack vs likely -EV spot; better reload spots available. FiveTwo's UTG+multi-action strength too polarized.
