I am holding pocket Tens (TT) and have already committed 2,200 chips to the pot. FiveTwo has 5-bet to 6,175, leaving himself with only 250 chips behind. This effectively makes the hand a race or a situation where I am slightly behind his range (typically JJ+, AK). To call, I need to commit 3,975 more chips into a total pot that will be approximately 13,500 (including my call). This gives me pot odds of roughly 2.4:1, requiring about 29% equity to be profitable. Against a standard 5-bet range of [JJ+, AK], TT has approximately 36% equity. Against an even tighter range like [QQ+, AK], it still maintains about 31% equity. Given the pot odds, my significant stack depth, and the 'dead money' from Elon and Minni's previous folds, calling is mathematically the correct decision to realize my equity.

On this flop of Js-5c-4s, I am holding pocket Tens. While the Jack is an overcard, the pot is already 13,500 chips and my opponent, FiveTwo, has only 250 chips remaining in his stack. Given the massive pot odds (over 50:1), I am mathematically obligated to put him all-in. Even if his pre-flop 5-bet range includes overpairs like QQ, KK, or AA, or a set of Jacks, the cost to see the rest of the hand is negligible compared to the potential reward. I will bet 250 to put him all-in and proceed to showdown.

We’re getting absurd pot odds: calling 250 to win 13,750 means we need ~1.8% equity. Even though AQs has whiffed this J-5-4 flop (no spade, no straight draw), two overcards still give us meaningful equity versus almost any range, and with only 250 left we’re effectively committed. Folding would be a huge EV mistake given the price and our remaining stack.

UTG with T6o is a weak, easily dominated hand. Facing the full table out of position, limp/call would put me OOP against multiple players and into many multi-way pots where T6o has poor equity. A raise as an isolation or steal is too speculative from UTG with many callers and deeper stacks — I can wait for a better spot. Folding preserves my 148bb stack for stronger opportunities and avoids marginal, low-expected-value decisions. Therefore I fold.

I am in UTG+1 with Queen-Three offsuit (Qd 3c). This is a very weak hand with poor playability, ranked near the bottom of starting hands. With three players yet to act behind me, including a short stack on the button who might shove and a massive stack in the small blind, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

On the button with 2200 stack (22BB), two folds ahead (UTG and UTG+1), 7c6h is a speculative offsuit connector with good playability postflop in position. Excellent spot to raise for value, steal blinds (pot 150, dead money), or build pot OOP advantage against blinds. Standard button open sizing around 3x BB (300 total). Flash (SB, deep) and Claude (BB, 57BB) can defend wide, but position allows profitable play. Raising wider from button maximizes edge over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five of diamonds. Elon, who is the button and currently short-stacked with only 1,900 chips (19 BB), has raised to 300. While my hand is suited, it is very weak (gapped and low ranking). Calling out of position against a short-stack who is likely looking to commit the rest of his chips on the flop or turn is not ideal. Furthermore, calling allows Claude in the Big Blind a cheap opportunity to enter the pot or squeeze. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 9d3h in the BB facing a 300 raise from Elon on the button. This is a very weak hand - 93 offsuit. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (need ~31% equity), 93o is at the very bottom of defendable hands, and I'll be out of position postflop against a player with a short stack who could easily shove on any flop. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o). This is a statistically weak hand that plays poorly out of position, even at a 5-handed table. With 4 players left to act behind me, including the chip leader on the button and a short stack in UTG+1 who might shove, the most profitable long-term play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

54o is a speculative hand with poor playability and low equity out of position from UTG+1. Facing a standard limp opportunity (pot 150, to call 100 for blinds), folding trash early preserves stack for better spots. Multiway pots would be unfavorable with this hand. No reads from this hand justify entering.

On the button in a 5-handed game, J8o is a marginal hand. While it's often within an opening range to steal the blinds, the table has shown significant aggression with frequent 3-bets (as seen in hands 1, 2, 10, 25, and 45). Claude in the small blind has a medium stack and may look to defend or squeeze, and Minni in the big blind is also active. Given my significant chip lead, I'll choose the low-variance path and fold this weak hand rather than opening and potentially being forced to fold to a 3-bet or play a bloated pot with a mediocre holding.

2-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Even though everyone folded to me in the SB, completing or raising with this hand against the BB (Minni with 14700 chips) is -EV. Better to fold and save the 50 chips for better spots.

KTs is a strong suited broadway hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. My stack is 23.5BB, so I want to play it proactively by raising for value, protection, and fold equity against blinds. Standard open-raise size is around 3x the BB given the pot of 150. Opponents have deep stacks except relative to mine, but KTs plays well postflop. Raising to 300 builds the pot appropriately without overcommitting.

Elon (UTG) raised to 300, and he is extremely short-stacked with only 2050 chips total. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, is too weak to call a raise from UTG, especially with three active players behind me who could easily 3-bet. Given Elon's stack size, he is likely looking to commit his chips soon with a wide range of hands that still often dominate Q9o, or he has a premium. There is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand in this position.

T4 suited is a weak hand. Elon raised from UTG (typically a strong range), and even though he's short-stacked with only ~1750 behind, calling here with such a marginal holding isn't profitable. The blinds could still squeeze, and T4s doesn't play well enough postflop to justify the call. Easy fold.

Elon opened UTG to 300 but he’s short (~20 BB). I’ve got 5♥6♥ in the small blind. Calling the extra 250 (300 total) is cheap relative to my 148 BB stack and keeps my range flexible — suited connectors have good playability and implied odds against a likely wider short‑stack open. A min‑raise to 500 is unattractive here: it risks isolating Elon and invites a shove or a 3‑bet from the deep big blind, and I don’t have the fold equity or hand strength to justify that OOP. Plan: call to see a cheap flop, continue only with clear equity (pair, two hearts, strong draw) and fold to substantial 3‑bets or jams. So I’ll call 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind with K6s. Facing a 3x raise from UTG (Elon, 20BB stack) and a call from the Small Blind. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 700, offering 3.5:1 or requiring ~22% equity). Although K6s is easily dominated and I will be sandwiched post-flop, the suited nature of the hand and the low price relative to my deep stack (122BB) make this a standard defend. I will call and evaluate the flop, wary of Elon's short stack shoving range.

I flopped a made straight: 3-4-5-6-7 with 5h6h on 4d-7s-3c. This is almost certainly the best hand — the only way to beat/tie me is someone holding both a 5 and a 6 (extremely unlikely). There are two opponents (short‑stack opener Elon and the deep BB Pro) so I want to both extract value from single pairs/top pairs/sets and charge any speculative hands. I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot to build value and discourage free cards while still allowing the short stack to shove (which I’ll call). Bet 600 into the 900 pot; if I get called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and commit appropriately.

On a board of [4d 7s 3c], I hold [Kc 6c] (King-high with backdoor flush and straight draws). Minni (SB) has led out for 600 into a pot of 900, which is a significant donk bet (2/3 pot). This action typically indicates strength (sets, two pair, or strong top pair) or a strong draw like 56. I have very little immediate equity. Furthermore, Elon (UTG) is behind me with a short stack (~20BB) and a range that likely includes overpairs that crush my hand. Floating here with just backdoors and one overcard in a multi-way pot is negative EV. The prudent play is to preserve my stack.

Preflop, KTs suited is a standard UTG open from 20.5BB effective stack. Flop 4d7s3c completely misses my range: no pair, no strong draw, just King-high overcards and backdoor heart flush draw. Minni's 600 into ~900 (pot-sized donk bet from SB) after calling my raise is polarized toward value (sets, two-pair like 47/34/75, strong pairs) or semi-bluffs (56, 25, A5, etc. for gutshots/OESD). My equity is low (~20-30% at best vs her range), and pot odds are 600 to win 2100 (~3:1), not justifying a call with air. Raising as a bluff has merit due to my UTG image and her SB calling range, but Minni's deep stack (139xBB) and history of aggression (frequent raises/calls, wins pots like Hand 31) suggest she'll continue wide, and with only 20BB left post-call, it's high variance to bluff here. Better to fold and preserve stack for better spots; 102 hands left, ICM pressure not acute yet with 5 players.

In a 5-handed game, Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from under the gun. I have the largest stack at the table, allowing me to play aggressively. Elon is very short with less than 20 big blinds, so I should be prepared for a potential shove from him. Opening to 3x (300) is a standard size that allows me to take control of the pot early.

I have JJ, a premium pocket pair. Flash opened to 300 from UTG. With pocket jacks, I should 3-bet to build the pot and isolate. A 3x raise to 900 is standard here. My stack is 5650, so this is a comfortable sizing that allows me to continue profitably post-flop.

Facing an UTG open to 300 and an immediate 3-bet to 900 from UTG+1, Q♠7♣ is a marginal, often-dominated holding. Claude’s 3-bet range from early position in a 5-handed game will be value-heavy (pockets and broadways) and my hand has poor equity vs that range; calling 900 into a 1350 pot requires ~28% equity which Q7o won’t provide reliably. A light 4-bet is the wrong shape here (no blockers, weak kicker) and risks getting isolated by better hands or a short-stack shove from the BB. With a healthy stack and many hands left, preserving chips and folding this marginal spot is the correct long-term play.

I hold 9s8s in the Small Blind. The action began with an UTG open from Flash to 300, followed by a 3-bet from Claude in UTG+1 to 900. This action (UTG+1 3-betting UTG) typically indicates a very strong range (QQ+, AK). Although 98s is a playable suited connector, calling a 3-bet cold from the Small Blind puts me out of position against strong ranges. Additionally, the Big Blind is short-stacked and could shove, or the original raiser could 4-bet, forcing me out after investing chips. The implied odds are also reduced given Claude's stack size of roughly 47 big blinds. The prudent play is to fold.

Facing a UTG raise from Flash (tight range likely) and a 3-bet from Claude to 900. My K9o is offsuit, mediocre hand with poor equity against premium 3-bet ranges (JJ+, AK, AQs+). Pot odds are about 1.7:1 (1350 pot to 800 call), requiring ~37% equity, but I estimate <30% vs Claude's range. With 19.5BB stack, shoving has low fold equity as Claude (47.5BB) and Flash (245BB) can call comfortably. Position BB but no initiative. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

I opened UTG with ATo, which is a standard open in a 5-handed game. Claude, the player to my direct left, 3-bet to 900. While the price to call is relatively attractive (600 to win a pot of 1350), ATo is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by Claude's 3-betting range (which usually consists of AQ+, 99+). Playing ATo out of position in a 3-bet pot is difficult and often leads to losing larger amounts when hitting top pair with a weak kicker. Given my chip lead, I prefer to fold and avoid high-variance situations with mediocre hands.

Q5s from UTG is too weak to open. In early position, I need stronger hands - this is a speculative suited hand with poor playability post-flop when called. With 61 BBs, I should be selective with my UTG opens and wait for better spots.

82 offsuit is a clear fold. I’m UTG+1 (early) and this hand has almost no showdown or post‑flop play value out of position — not connected, not suited, no pair. Limping or opening here invites pressure from a big, aggressive BB (Flash) and the button (Pro); with 15450 chips I should conserve equity for better spots and premium hands. No reason to invest 100 to play a marginal hand preflop, so fold.

I am on the Button with Ten-Three suited (Tc 3c). While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, especially 5-handed, this hand is at the very bottom of the suited disconnect range and typically falls outside a standard 40-50% opening range. The Small Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (19 BB) and likely in a push-or-fold mode, making him dangerous to steal against with a hand that cannot call a shove. The Big Blind (Flash) is the chip leader with over 240 BB and can defend widely or apply pressure. Given the weak hand strength and the stack dynamics of the blinds, folding is the most disciplined and positive EV decision.

Hand #50, preflop in SB with J9s suited connectors, a strong speculative hand with good playability. Folds from UTG, UTG+1, and Button, so facing only BB (Flash with deep stack 24400). My effective stack ~19BB (1900), short enough to need to accumulate chips without waiting. SB vs BB dynamic favors wide raising range for dead money (pot 150, posted 50 already, call 50 or raise). Folding too tight; limping OOP risky. Standard play: raise to ~3x BB (300 total) to steal blinds or build pot with position advantage postflop if called. J9s has straight/flush potential, dominates many BB calling hands. Balances my range as aggressor from blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Elon, the Small Blind, has raised to 300. Elon is currently the shortest stack at the table with only about 16 big blinds remaining. While I have position on him post-flop and the price to call is 2:1 (200 more to win a total pot of 400), Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak hand that does not flop well. Given Elon's short stack, he is likely to commit the rest of his chips on many flops, and I don't want to bleed chips against a desperate player with a sub-par hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my chip lead.

5-handed and deep-stacked (≈154bb), Q♦T♠ is a reasonable open from UTG — it has postflop playability and fold equity. Limping/calling 100 would invite multiway pots where QTo is often dominated; folding is too nitpicky. I want to seize initiative and avoid cheap flops for the blinds (and deter callers), so I’ll make a standard 3x-ish open to 300. This sizing is in line with table norms, gives good fold equity, and keeps options for postflop play. If the short-stack on the button jams ~20bb, I can call given his wide shove range; against a large 3-bet from a deep stack I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent.

I hold Ac5c in UTG+1 (effectively the Cutoff in this 5-handed configuration). Minni has opened to 300 from UTG. While A5s is a playable hand with good implied odds (flush and straight potential), facing an UTG open puts me at a range disadvantage. Calling is risky because it invites squeezes from the players behind, particularly the short-stacked Elon on the button who might shove his 20bb stack, or the deep-stacked Flash in the SB. 3-betting is a possibility as a bluff, but Minni's UTG range should be reasonably strong, and I am fairly deep (120bb) but covered by Minni. The most disciplined and lower-variance play, avoiding domination issues and squeeze spots, is to fold.

Pocket threes is a decent speculative hand on the button facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has shown aggression in the past. Pot odds are favorable: calling 300 into a pot that will be 750 gives about 2.5:1 (40%). Set-mining has positive implied odds against Minni's deep 15150 stack if we hit (12% chance). Position allows controlling the pot postflop and realizing equity multiway if blinds call. Folding wastes position; shoving risks dominated calls from Minni's strong range with only ~30-35% equity; 3-betting smaller commits too much of short 20BB stack. Call to see flop cheap.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. Minni opened from under the gun and Elon called on the button. J4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an UTG open. Even with the pot odds offered, I would be playing a marginal hand against two opponents with a high likelihood of being dominated. Given my chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips in this spot.

K4 suited in BB facing a raise to 300 with one caller. I'm getting 3.75:1 pot odds (200 to win 750). K4s has playability with flush potential and a decent high card. Closing the action with good pot odds makes this a reasonable defend.

I have K4 clubs on 8s 7d Qd - completely missed this flop. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. With just K-high and no equity, I should check and see a free card. No reason to bluff into two opponents with air.

Board Q♦‑8♠‑7♦; I hold Q♥T♠ — top pair with a decent kicker. I opened UTG and both the button and BB defended, so their ranges are wide and contain many missed hands plus straight/backdoor diamond possibilities (e.g. 9‑10, double‑diamond holdings). Checking invites free cards and gives draws equity; I want to extract value from worse Qx and thin pairs while charging draws. I’ll make a standard value/protection c‑bet of about half the pot to keep weaker hands and draws calling but avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily. If I face a large raise I’ll re‑evaluate (short‑stack shove from the button I’m willing to call more often than a big deep‑stack reraise).

Preflop set-mining with 33 on button vs UTG open was reasonable given position, odds, and implied odds, but flop missed completely: 8s7dQd offers no pair above 3s, no flush draw (3s suited but board not), no straight draw. Underpair to lowest card on board. Minni's UTG range strong: QQ+, AKo/s, AQo+, KQs, suited connectors like JTs-98s. Board hits this range hard (top pair, sets, two-pair, combo draws). Her flop bet 500 (~half pot) is standard C-bet value/ protection. My equity <20% vs that range (set outs 2x3 ~8%, runner-runner str8 unlikely). Pot odds 500-to-1450 (~26% needed) not met. Shoving 1450 more (~3x pot raise) gets called wide by her deep stack (TPTK+, draws), equity too low (~15%). Calling commits ~30% remaining stack, poor SPR oop multiway (Claude behind). Preserve 1450 chips (~14.5BB) for premium spots or steals as shortstack in tournament. Fold.

I have K4 clubs on 8s 7d Qd - complete air. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. Minni bet 500 into 950, I need 500 to win 1450 (~3:1). With just K-high and zero equity, this is an easy fold. Can't profitably continue with nothing.

I am in UTG position holding 2d 5h, which is widely considered one of the worst starting hands in poker due to its low rank and lack of connectivity or suitedness. With 5 active players and plenty of hands remaining (hand 52/150), there is absolutely no strategic merit in entering the pot from early position with this holding. My stack is healthy at 120 BBs, so I can comfortably fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG+1 position with 9h4d offsuit, an extremely weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. My stack is 1750 (17.5BB), and entering the pot preflop with this would commit a significant portion of my stack to a hand with poor equity and post-flop playability against 4 active opponents, many with deep stacks. Standard strategy dictates folding trash hands from early position to preserve stack for better spots and avoid difficult decisions out of position.

I am on the button with Q7o in a 5-handed game. While the button is a prime position to steal the blinds, Q7o is at the very bottom of a 5-handed opening range. Both Claude (SB) and Minni (BB) have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. Given my significant chip lead, I see no need to risk chips on a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop if called. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening hand.
Folded to me in the SB with KTs - a solid hand heads-up against the BB. I should raise for value rather than limp. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x) to put pressure on Minni.

A♠K♥ is a top-tier opening hand and should not be just called out of the big blind facing a small-blind open to 250. Claude (5,550) has been opening to ~250 frequently in the history, which indicates a relatively wide/opening/steal range. I want to both isolate him and extract value / fold equity rather than play a multiway or a passive call. A 3-bet to ~900 (roughly 3.5x his open) is a standard size here: big enough to deny cheap flops and get folds from weaker holdings, but small enough to leave plenty of postflop playability if he calls. If he calls I’ll proceed postflop as the aggressor with c-bets on favorable boards; if he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (fold to extremely large 4-bets that commit most of his stack, call or shove against smaller 4-bets depending on sizing). Primary goal: isolate the SB and build a pot with a dominating hand.

KTs is a decent hand but facing a 3-bet from Minni while out of position makes this a tough spot. I need to call 650 into 1150 (~1.77:1 odds). KTs doesn't play well OOP against a 3-bet - I'll often be dominated by AK, and pairs/suited connectors play better in position. Minni has shown aggression throughout the tournament. With my stack at 5550 (~55BB), I should preserve chips rather than play a bloated pot OOP with a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined play.

UTG position with 3d8c, a complete trash hand - offsuit, disconnected low cards with no high card strength or suited potential. Early position requires tight range. My stack is only 17.5BB, so preserve chips for better spots rather than spewing with marginal holdings. No reads from history suggest loosening up here. Standard fold.

In a 5-handed game, my position (UTG+1) is essentially the Cutoff. King-three suited is a reasonable opening hand in this spot to apply pressure on the remaining three players, especially given my significant chip lead. Elon has already folded, so I only have the Button and the blinds to worry about. I will raise to 2.5 big blinds to take control of the pot. If I face a 3-bet from the short stack (Claude), I can easily fold without much loss.
5s8d is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, this is not strong enough to call a raise. I should fold and wait for better spots.

I have pocket 4s in the small blind with deep stacks (≈163bb). Flash opened UTG+1 to 250; the pot is 400 and I need to commit 200 more (250 total including my 50 SB). With a small pocket pair the correct line here is set-mining: calling is cheap relative to the stacks and gives strong implied odds if I hit a set. A 3-bet to isolate is suboptimal — I’m out of position vs the original raiser and would be committing many more chips and reducing my implied odds. I’ll call to see the flop; if I hit a set I’ll look to extract, otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy action.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h6c facing a raise from the Cutoff (Flash) and a call from the Small Blind (Minni). The pot offers 4:1 odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 750), which requires roughly 20% equity. While 76o has reasonable raw equity against wide ranges, it suffers significantly from poor playability and low equity realization (EQR) in a multi-way pot, especially when sandwiched between opponents. Offsuit connectors are prone to domination and reverse implied odds. Despite the attractive price, the expected value of calling is likely negative due to the difficulty of navigating post-flop out of position. Therefore, folding is the correct disciplined decision.

I called preflop as a set-miner with 4♣4♠, but the flop A♠ 9♣ A♦ completely misses me — I have an underpair to a paired-ace board and only two outs to make a set. Flash was the preflop raiser so his continuation range is heavy in A’s and other strong hands; I’m behind most hands that will value-bet here. With deep stacks I shouldn’t bloat the pot with a weak holding or bluff into a range that will call or raise me off. Pot control is the correct line: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re-evaluate only if he checks back or I pick up a clear improvement on the turn.

On a paired board like AA9, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage. I opened from the Cutoff, and Minni called from the Small Blind. My range contains many more strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) than hers, as she would likely 3-bet those for value or protection from the blinds. Since she checked to me, a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200 chips) is highly effective. It puts pressure on her non-pair hands and small pocket pairs while allowing me to take down the pot immediately. If she calls, I can check back most turns to realize my King-high showdown value or fold if she shows aggression.

I called preflop as a set-miner, but the flop A♠ 9♣ A♦ completely misses my 4♣4♠. I only have 2 outs to make a set (≈8.4% to hit by river). The pot is 800 and Flash's bet is 200, so calling costs 200 for ~20% pot equity — my actual equity (~8%) is far below the required break‑even. Flash opened UTG+1, so his continuation range is heavy in A‑cards and other hands that beat me; his small c‑bet is a standard continuation and not a reliable fold. Out of position with such poor equity, the correct, lowest-variance line is to fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I am holding King-Five offsuit in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position in a 5-handed game. While short-handed play allows for a wider opening range, K5o is generally too weak to open from first position, especially with a very short stack (Elon) in the Big Blind who is likely looking for any opportunity to shove. Maintaining my chip lead is priority, and there is no need to enter a pot with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop and doesn't dominate many calling ranges. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Qs9s is a playable suited hand in UTG+1 after UTG folds. Not premium but has decent playability with flush and straight potential. With 55BB stack, I'll open with a standard 2.5x raise to 250. This gives me initiative and can take down blinds or build a pot with position on the blinds.

I’m on the button with A♣2♥ facing an UTG+1 open to 250. Claude’s early-position raise represents a fairly strong range, and A2o is easily dominated by his value hands. In position I prefer to call — a tiny 3‑bet to 400 won’t generate meaningful fold equity and a larger 3‑bet with this weak kicker is high variance and difficult postflop. Calling 250 keeps the pot manageable, preserves stack depth (I still have 16100), and lets me use position to fold to heavy action on bad flops or extract value on good ones. I’ll re-evaluate if the blinds commit or if Claude shows more aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 2c facing a raise from Claude (UTG+1) and a call from Minni (Button). The pot is 650 and it costs me 200 to call. While the immediate pot odds are decent (approx 23.5%), playing 5-2 suited out of position against two opponents is a losing play long-term. Additionally, the Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (16.5 BBs) and presents a squeeze risk; if he shoves, my equity realization drops to zero as I would have to fold. The hand has poor playability and high reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and prudent decision here.

A7o is a marginal ace offsuit in the BB facing an UTG+1 raise from Claude (53BB stack) and a button call from deep-stacked Minni. Pot odds are favorable (150 to call into 650, ~19% equity needed), but postflop playability is poor multiway with a weak kicker, no suited potential, and SPR ~1.75 after calling (my remaining ~1400 into ~800 pot). With only 16.5BB effective stack, committing without a premium hand risks busting against better aces/pairs. Opponents show calling tendencies in history, reducing fold equity if shoving. Better to fold and preserve stack for a stronger spot in this tournament phase.

Flop is 5h5d Jc - paired board. I have Qs9s which is complete air here - no pair, no draw (only backdoor spades). Against a button caller who has a wide range that connects with Jx hands and pocket pairs, c-betting with zero equity is risky. Better to check and potentially give up, or take a free card.

Claude (UTG+1) opened preflop so his range is relatively strong. Flop J♣ 5♥ 5♦ is a paired, low-draw board and my A♣2♥ has very little showdown value — I’m often behind any Jx or Ax and only have an overcard plus an Ace blocker. Claude checked the flop, which looks like weakness or pot-control rather than an invitation to bluff all streets. I want to keep the pot small in position and preserve stack depth rather than bluff into a likely calling/raising range. Plan: check to control the pot, fold to sizeable turn aggression, call very small probes selectively, and re-evaluate if an Ace or a clear bluffing line appears on later streets.

I have Qs9s on 5h5d Jc 3c - complete air with no pair, no draw. Minni has checked twice showing weakness, but I have zero equity if called. The 3c doesn't help me. A delayed c-bet could work as a bluff, but with no outs and Minni potentially trapping with a jack or pocket pair, I'd rather take a free card. Checking keeps the pot small and gives me a chance to hit Q or 9 on the river.

Claude opened from UTG+1 and has shown pot‑control by checking both the flop and the turn. On J♣ 5♥ 5♦ 3♣ my A♣2♥ has almost no showdown value vs an early‑position raiser and I have no real draws. Betting here would be a thin bluff that won’t fold enough of Claude’s continuing range and could get me into a bloated pot or a check‑raise. I’m in position so I’ll keep the pot small, check to the river, and re‑evaluate — calling small river bets with the Ace if it looks reasonable, folding to large river aggression, or betting/capturing value only if an Ace or a clear bluffing line appears.

River 6s completes nothing. I have Qs9s - complete air with queen high. Minni has checked all three streets showing weakness, but a river bluff after checking twice looks suspicious. My Q-high might even beat ace-high that gave up or similar garbage. With a small pot of 650 and no credible story for a bluff, checking is the best option - I might occasionally win at showdown or minimize losses.

Claude opened UTG+1 and then checked both the flop (5♥5♦J♣) and the turn (3♣) — a pot‑control line consistent with Jx or a medium pair. My A♣2♥ has almost no showdown value on this paired, essentially dry board; I block some A5 combos but not enough to make a river bluff profitable. Betting here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity (Jx, 55, and most pocket pairs will call), and there’s no value to extract. Pot is only 650 and Claude still has 5.3k behind; I’m not willing to bloat the pot with Ace‑high. Action: check to get to showdown and avoid unnecessary risk. If Claude now bets a very small probe (≈1/3 pot) I may call, but I’ll fold to larger river aggression.

J7 suited from UTG is too weak to open. UTG requires a tight range and J7s doesn't make the cut - it's a marginal suited hand that plays poorly out of position in multiway pots. With 53 BBs, I should preserve my stack for better spots.

I’m UTG+1 with J♣9♦ — an offsuit, marginal holding in an early position with four players left to act (button + SB + BB). My stack is deep (165bb) but the table has a very deep, capable defender (Flash, 246bb) and a decent-sized button (Pro, ~118bb); Elon (SB) is short (~16bb) and could shove. Raising here would be a loose open from early position and exposes me to 3-bets or a shove out of position where J9o performs poorly. Limping/passive-calling also invites multiway action and squeezes my postflop equity. Standard strategy: tighten UTG+1 — J9o is outside my opening range. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position or a stronger hand.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand, 3c 4h (3-high offsuit connectors). While the position is advantageous for stealing blinds, the Small Blind is short-stacked (16BB) and prone to shoving over a raise, and the Big Blind is the chip leader (246BB) capable of defending widely. The hand has too little equity to justify a steal attempt, especially if called or raised. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

84o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and terrible postflop playability. In SB facing BB in an unopened pot (early/mid/late folds), completing for 50 into 150 pot offers insufficient equity even with dead money. BB (Flash with deep stack) will defend wide, and my 1600 stack plays poorly postflop with this hand. Raising to steal risks getting called or 3-bet by a wide defending range. Optimal play is to fold and preserve stack for premium spots in this multi-table tournament dynamic.

UTG with J♦9♠ (offsuit) in a 5‑handed game is a marginal hand and is easily dominated by the ranges behind me. Pot is 150 and calling 100 is cheap, but playing out of position multi‑way with J9o is a losing line long term — I’ll face aggression from players with deeper and wider ranges (Pro, Flash, Claude, Elon). Opening here would be spewy from UTG and invite multi‑way action; better to preserve a healthy 165bb stack and wait for a cleaner spot. Fold.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively the cutoff in a 5-handed game) with Q6o. This is a very weak unconnected hand that performs poorly against any calling range. With a short stack on the button who might shove and a deep stack in the small blind, there is no incentive to open this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with A5 offsuit after UTG and UTG+1 folds, this is a prime stealing spot against the blinds. A5o plays well in position: ace blocker reduces premium hands, decent high card value, and straight potential. My stack is 16BB, so applying pressure preflop is key to chip accumulation. Raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) for initiative; blinds will fold often, especially with my position advantage. Flash is deep-stacked but SB may fold wide, Claude 52BB in BB defends but not always light. Standard play maximizes EV.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 8s. Elon, the Button, has raised to 300. Elon is a very short stack with only 1300 total chips (1000 remaining after the raise). J8o is a weak hand to defend from the Small Blind out of position, especially against a player who is effectively committed to the pot. I will fold and wait for better opportunities to leverage my chip-leading stack.
72 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. Even with decent pot odds (200 to win 450), this hand has no playability and I'll be out of position against Elon's button raise. Easy fold.

In a 5-handed game, UTG position is effectively the Hijack. King-Jack suited is a strong broadway hand that falls comfortably within a standard opening range (top 15-20%) for this position. With a stack of over 100BB, I want to build the pot and take the initiative. A standard open raise of 2.5BB (250 chips) is appropriate to balance value and fold equity, while leaving room to maneuver post-flop or fold if the short-stacked player behind me (Elon) jams.

Ts5d is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or playability from UTG+1 position. Facing an UTG open-raise to 250 (2.5x from a strong range), calling commits ~14% of my 1750 stack into a pot where I'll be out of position postflop against Pro's strong range. No fold equity for a 3-bet given my short stack and hand strength. Previous hands show Pro can be aggressive but UTG opens warrant folding marginal hands to preserve stack for better opportunities in a tournament with 93 hands left. Clear fold to avoid spewing chips.

I am holding a pocket pair of fives on the button. Pro opened from UTG with a standard raise to 250. Given my position and the depth of our stacks, calling is a solid play here. I want to see a flop cheaply to either hit a set or use my positional advantage to take the pot post-flop. Raising (3-betting) is an option, but with 55, I prefer keeping the pot controlled and realizing my equity in position.

K3o is a weak hand, especially out of position in a multiway pot. While pot odds are ~3.25:1 (200 to win 650), I'll be playing out of position against at least Flash and possibly Minni. K3o doesn't play well multiway - my king is dominated by better kings and the 3 provides little backup. Better to save chips and wait for a stronger spot.

I'm in the big blind with 9♠9♣ facing an UTG open to 250 and a button call — pot is 650 and I need to put in 150 more (total 250). Pocket nines are a strong medium pair but lose significant equity multiway against overcards; I prefer not to bloat the pot out of position with a weak small 3-bet (raising to 400 is too small to get folds and too little value). With deep stacks (164bb) I can play postflop for value and implied odds (set/mining upside) or fold if the flop is hostile. Calling keeps the pot manageable, retains flexibility, and lets me see a flop with a hand that plays well heads-up or against one opponent.

I flopped a set of 9s on 3c-9h-7c — an extremely strong made hand but the board has two clubs, so there are some flush-draw combos out there. With the UTG raiser and the button caller still in, I want both value and protection (charge club combos and weaker pairs) rather than giving free cards. I’ll lead out for a sizable bet (500 into an 800 pot, roughly 2/3 pot) to extract value from pocket pairs, pair+club hands and strong Ax/connector clubs, and to make drawing expensive. Plan: if one or both call I’ll continue extracting on safe turn cards; if I face a large raise I’ll strongly consider committing with trips (call/re-raise/shove depending on size), and if both check back I’ll control pot size on later streets.

The flop comes [3c 9h 7c], which connects better with the Big Blind's defending range than my UTG opening range. Minni donk leads for 500 into a pot of 800 (62.5%), which represents strength or a strong draw (like a flush draw or top pair) that wants protection. I hold KhJh, giving me only two overcards and backdoor equity (hearts/straight). With no made hand, no immediate draw, and a player still to act behind me on the Button, floating this bet is effectively losing chips. The prudent play is to fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

On a flop of 3c-9h-7c, Minni has led out (donk bet) for 500 into a pot of approximately 800 after calling from the Big Blind pre-flop. My pocket fives are currently an overpair to the 3 but are beaten by any 7 or 9, both of which are heavily in Minni's defending range. The original pre-flop raiser (Pro) has already folded, suggesting Minni's lead is strong. Given the coordinated nature of the board (straight and flush draws) and the fact that almost any overcard on the turn makes my hand even more difficult to play, I will fold and preserve my chips. There is no need to make a high-variance stand with a marginal pair in this spot.

UTG with 17.5BB stack, T8h is a speculative suited connector but marginal in early position out of position against aggressive opponents with deeper stacks. Shoving risks stack against multiple callers; limping OOP is poor; standard raise commits too much of short stack. Folding preserves stack for better spot like premiums or late position pushes. Opponents show aggression in history, so wait for equity edge.

K3s is a marginal hand in early position (UTG+1) even in a 5-handed game. As the chip leader with a significant lead, I prefer to avoid entering pots with weak holdings against the other medium-to-large stacks (Minni and Pro) who occupy the blinds. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids potentially difficult post-flop situations with a weak kicker.
Button with 79o - marginal hand but great position. Two folds already, so I should raise to steal blinds or play in position if called. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Facing a standard button open to 250 while I'm in the small blind with Kh2d. Calling 200 to see a flop leaves me out of position against Claude with the big blind still to act; K2o is a very weak, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability. It's a poor 3-bet candidate (would either get called and play OOP or face a 4-bet), so folding and preserving my deep stack for better spots is the correct play here.

I hold AdKd, a premium starting hand, in the Big Blind. The Button (Claude) has opened to 250. Given the positional disadvantage, 3-betting is the standard and superior play to build the pot for value, deny equity, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Claude's opening range on the Button is likely wide, and AKs dominates much of it (Ax, Kx, suited connectors). A standard 3-bet size from out of position is roughly 3.5x to 4x the open raise. With the effective stack being 49 BBs, a raise to 900 applies appropriate pressure.

Pro 3-bet me to 900 after I opened with 79o. This is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet - I'm dominated by most of Pro's 3-betting range (pocket pairs, broadways, Ax). I'd need to call 650 into a 1200 pot, which is about 35% pot odds, but 79o doesn't play well enough postflop to justify this. Easy fold.

I am the chip leader with 24,450 chips. In a 5-handed game, K8s is a borderline hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG). While it has some value, opening here puts me out of position against three other players, and the Big Blind (Elon) is a very short stack (16.5 BB) who is likely looking for any excuse to shove. If I raise and Elon shoves, I would be forced to decide whether to call 1,650 total with a hand that is often dominated or flipping. To maintain my stack and wait for higher-equity situations where I have a positional advantage, I will fold this hand.
Q7 suited in UTG+1 is too weak to open from early position. With 49BB stack, I should be playing tight from this position. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the Button with J♣5♥ and it’s folded to me — pot is 150, to call 100, min-raise to 200. J5o is a marginal hand to play heads-up, but on the Button I want to be aggressive and try to steal the blinds. The big blind (Elon) is short (~1,650 ≈ 16.5 BB) and is likely to fold or shove wide; the small blind (Pro) is deep (~11,850) and will defend more selectively. A minimum raise to 200 is cheap, applies pressure, preserves position post-flop if called, and gives me fold equity against both blinds. If Elon shoves (~1,650) I can comfortably call — it’s a small portion of my 17,000 stack and many of his shoves are wide, so my fold equity + postflop playability makes the raise + call-if-shoved line profitable. If Pro 3-bets large I can release and protect the stack. Action: raise to 200 to attempt a steal while keeping options flexible.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ac Ah) in the Small Blind, the best starting hand. The Button (Minni) has min-raised to 200. To maximize value and protect against multi-way pots while out of position, I should 3-bet. Looking at my history (e.g., Hand #10 with KK), a 3-bet to roughly 3.5x-4x the open is effective. The Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (16.5 BB) and my raise might induce a shove from him or isolate the Button. A raise to 750 is a strong, standard size that charges draws and inferior pairs appropriately.

Facing a 3-bet from Pro (SB) to 750 after Minni's button open to 200. My 97o is a trash hand with poor playability, no blockers, and low equity against strong 3-bet ranges. Stack is 1650 (16.5BB), pot 1050, calling 650 commits ~40% of stack OOP with a weak hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I opened to 200 with J♣5♥ to steal the blinds, but Pro (SB) has 3-bet to 750. Calling would cost me +550 into a pot of 1,050 (pot would be 1,600 after my call), so I need ~34.4% equity to break even. Versus a typical SB 3‑bet range (value-heavy: TT+, AK/AQ and some suited broadways, with occasional bluffs) J5o is a significant underdog — equity likely well below what I need. A 4‑bet bluff to 1,300 with J5o is not credible and unnecessarily risky. I planned to fold to a substantial 3‑bet when I raised as a steal; preserving a deep stack for better spots is the higher EV line here. Fold.
T2o is a trash hand, especially from UTG with 4 players behind me. Easy fold.

Raise to 300 (3x). In a 5-handed, deep-stacked game 7♥6♥ is a playable, position-sensitive hand — it has good postflop playability and implied odds. Limping (calling 100) from early position surrenders initiative and invites squeezes from the big stacks (Flash, Pro); a tiny min-raise to 200 gives correct odds to callers and encourages multiway pots where my equity realization is worse. A 3x open takes the initiative, gains fold equity, and keeps the field narrower so I can play postflop with position against the button or defend against the blinds. If I run into heavy 3-bets or an all-in from the short SB, I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large shove/3-bet depending on sizing and opponent tendencies), but I want to open the action here and play the hand aggressively when I hit a draw or a decent piece of the board.

I hold Jd 8c on the Button. Minni, playing from UTG+1 with a large stack, has raised to 300. J8 offsuit is a weak hand that performs poorly against an early position opening range, which is typically stronger. Despite having position, the hand has significant reverse implied odds and lacks the playability to justify a call or a bluff 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Short stack at 16BB in SB facing an early position raise to 3BB from Minni, who has a strong range. J4 suited has speculative value with suited connectors but is too weak to defend OOP against EP strength, especially with limited stack depth for post-flop play. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call into 700), insufficient for the hand's equity realization out of position. Better to fold and wait for stronger hands or better dynamics to shove or defend profitably, preserving stack for tournament survival.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six suited, which is a solid hand to defend against a standard open. Minni raised to 3x the big blind from middle position. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a 450 pot) and the playability of suited Aces, a call is standard. While Minni has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets in previous hands (Hands 10, 16, 59), I have a massive stack lead and can afford to see a flop out of position without bloating the pot unnecessarily. Calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially trap or outplay her on favorable boards while keeping the variance low.

I am first to act on a very wet, monotone flop (Qh 4h 5h). My hand, Ac 6c, has no hearts and very little equity on this board. In this situation, leading out is ineffective because it is highly likely the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) has at least one heart or a piece of the board that won't fold. Checking is the most prudent play to see how she reacts and to avoid bloat-potting with a hand that currently has almost no showdown value.

Flop Qh-4h-5h with 7h6h — I have a made flush (queen-high), which is very strong but not the absolute nuts (only A♥ or K♥ beat me). Flash is the lone caller from the big blind with a wide preflop calling range that includes many hearts, Qx hands and medium pocket pairs, so I expect many worse hands that will call a reasonable bet. I want value and to deny free cards, so I size for ~60% pot to extract from worse hearts, pairs and Qx while still getting called. Bet 400 into the 650 pot; if I face a large check-raise/shove (polarized to A♥/K♥ or a set), I’ll re-evaluate and fold to extreme overbets but call smaller/standard raises and proceed cautiously on the turn.

The flop is Qh 4h 5h, a monotone board that I have completely missed. I am holding Ac 6c with no hearts, meaning I have zero flush equity and very little showdown value against Minni's pre-flop raising range and subsequent continuation bet. Calling 400 into a 1050 pot requires about 27% equity, which I do not have here. Attempting to bluff or represent a flush without even a heart blocker is too risky against an opponent who has shown they can play aggressively. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

5-handed table so ranges should be wide — J‑T offsuit is borderline but playable UTG in a short‑handed game. I want to seize initiative, avoid multiway limps, and apply pressure to the SB/BB and the short button (Elon). Standard 3x open is appropriate here: raise to 3×BB (300) to get fold equity and play heads‑up or IP postflop. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops, fold to a large 3‑bet (JTo struggles OOP vs polarized 3‑bet ranges), and re-evaluate if isolated against the short stack or called by one player.

I hold 7c 2c in UTG+1 facing a raise from Minni in UTG. This is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite being suited, it has very poor playability and equity against a tight UTG opening range. Calling would be a losing play in the long run due to reverse implied odds and the high likelihood of being behind. Folding is the only correct decision.

Qd2d is a weak suited hand with poor playability and large gaps. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has a strong range from early position. On the button with 16BB effective stack (1600), calling 300 commits 19% of stack into a pot that would be 750, but the hand is easily dominated and lacks strong postflop potential. Raising as a bluff is not ideal against UTG strength and my short stack prefers better spots to shove or call. Folding preserves stack for premium hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 4s. Minni has opened from UTG with a 3x raise. King-four offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially against an early position open. I would be out of position for the rest of the hand with a weak kicker that is likely dominated. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, folding is the most prudent action.

9-6 offsuit is a weak hand. Facing a raise from Minni to 300, I need to call 200 more. Out of position with a marginal hand that plays poorly postflop. Even with reasonable pot odds (~2.25:1), 96o is below my defending threshold against an active raiser. Fold.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Three offsuit (Qd 3h). This is a very weak hand with no connectivity or suited potential. Playing this hand from early position is a losing strategy in the long run. I have a healthy stack of 122 BBs and there is no need to get involved with marginal holdings. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Preflop, UTG+1 with 16BB effective stack in a short-stacked spot. KTo offsuit is a weak hand from early position, lacking suitedness, connectivity for flush/straight draws, or domination potential against likely calling ranges. Facing deep-stacked opponents who have shown aggression in history, folding preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity worth risking with a push given position and hand strength. Standard fold to avoid tough postflop decisions with marginal equity.

I am on the button with Q6o. While this is a potential steal position, Q6o is generally too weak to open, especially with active opponents like Claude and Minni in the blinds. Claude's stack size makes him prone to 3-bet shoving, and I'd rather wait for a hand with better playability or blockers. I will fold and maintain my chip lead.

Heads up against the BB with 94o - a weak hand. Everyone folded to me. I can complete for 50 to see a flop cheaply. Folding is an option but I'm getting 3:1 odds. Completing is standard here with a weak hand rather than raising with such poor equity.

Qc2d is a weak offsuit hand — not worth building a pot with here. There was no open raise, only Claude (SB, short stack ~4.7k) limped; Flash (BTN) folded. I prefer to keep the pot small and see a flop in position rather than iso-raise and risk getting jammed or bloating the pot with a dominated hand. Checking preserves stack, lets me use postflop position versus the SB, and fold to aggression if I miss. Check and evaluate the flop.

Flopped middle pair with 9c on a very dangerous 9d Kd Td board - all diamonds. I have no diamond, making my hand extremely vulnerable. Any diamond on turn/river completes opponent's flush draws. Board also has straight potential with QJ. Heads up in a limped pot, checking for pot control is best. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a terrible spot with no backdoor outs.

Check. Qc2d on Kd–Td–9d is a huge draw but not a made hand: I have one diamond (4 to a flush) and a gutshot to a 9–10–J–Q–K straight. Roughly 9 diamond outs + 3 non-diamond Js ≈ 12 outs (~25.5% to hit on the turn, ~45% by river). Claude limped from the SB and checked the flop, so his range is likely wide or cautious. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our deep stack, so I prefer pot control and to realize my equity in position rather than bloating the pot with a semi-bluff that can be raised or jammed by a stronger K/T combo. I’ll check to see how he behaves on the turn — call a reasonable bet to chase my draw, and if a diamond or a J arrives I can take the initiative. If he shows heavy strength (big bet/shove) I’ll re-evaluate and fold unless pot odds justify a call.

Four diamonds on board now (9d Kd Td 8d). I have no diamond with 9c 4h. My middle pair is worthless - any single diamond in Minni's hand makes a flush that beats me. I should check and fold to any bet. Betting here would be a pure bluff with no equity when called.

I have Qc2d on a 9d–Kd–Td–8d board: I’ve completed a diamond flush, but the board now shows four diamonds. Any opponent holding a diamond already has a flush and will almost certainly beat my 2d kicker. With 8 diamonds left in the unseen cards, Claude has roughly a 32% chance to hold at least one diamond, and his limp/passive checks don’t rule that out. The pot is tiny (200) versus our deep stack, so I want pot control with this vulnerable, likely second-best flush — check and let him show aggression. I’ll call a small river bet to realize equity against non-diamond hands but will fold to a large shove since I’m probably dominated by any diamond in his range.

Four diamonds on board plus a straight possible with any Jack. My 9c 4h has no diamond and just a weak pair of 9s. Any diamond or Jack in Minni's hand beats me. Minni checked through flop and turn - could be weak or trapping. No reason to bet here - I have marginal showdown value and betting would fold out worse hands while getting called/raised by better. Check and hope to win at showdown.

Heads-up vs Claude (SB) on the river. Pot is tiny (200) and my hand is Qc2d on 9d–Kd–Td–8d–Qh. I do have a diamond flush (2d + four diamonds on board) but it’s a very weak flush — any diamond in his hand beats me. There are 8 diamonds left among 45 unknown cards, so the chance he holds at least one diamond is ≈ 32.7% (1 - C(37,2)/C(45,2)). Claude limp‑checked the hand and showed passive lines on flop/turn, so his range is wide and likely contains a nontrivial diamond frequency. Because the pot is tiny relative to stacks and my flush is easily dominated, I don’t want to bloat the pot or bluff; checking controls the pot and lets me realize equity. Plan: check to showdown; call a small river bet to extract vs Kx/Tx/9x but fold to a large shove since a big bet is likely a dominating diamond.

UTG position with 2d7d, one of the worst starting hands in poker, even suited. Short stack at 16BB, but raising or calling preflop from early position with this trash hand risks the stack unnecessarily. No steals viable UTG with 5 players. Preserve stack for premium hands.

I am dealt 7c 4c in UTG+1. While suited, this hand is extremely weak and does not warrant a raise from this position, especially with several aggressive stacks remaining to act behind me. I have a significant chip lead and do not need to play marginal hands from early/middle positions. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I have 3s4s on the button - a suited connector with some playability. With two folds in front and only the blinds behind, this is a good spot to attempt a steal. Raising from the button with position is standard here rather than limping.

I’m in the small blind (OOP) facing a standard button open to 250. My hand 9♦7♥ is weak — offsuit, with poor playability and often dominated by the button’s opening range. Calling 200 to play out of position against the raiser (and possibly the big blind) is a negative EV spot; a light 3-bet to 400 would be an overly thin bluff and could get punished by a stronger 4-bet or call. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better position/hand, so I fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em due to low rank and lack of connectivity or suit coordination. Facing a standard raise from the Button (Claude) to 250, I am getting pot odds of roughly 27% (150 to call into a total pot of 550). Although my raw equity against a wide button range might hover near 27-29%, this hand has extremely poor playability and equity realization out of position. It will rarely flop well, and when it does, it is vulnerable to higher pairs or kickers. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I am in the UTG position with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that I am first to act in a five-handed game, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to protect my stack.

56o from UTG+1 is a clear fold. This offsuit connector is too weak to open from early position, and I'd be playing out of position against multiple opponents. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Button with J♦2♦ — a weak but suited hand that plays well in position. With only the blinds left, I want to apply pressure and leverage position/fold equity rather than limp and allow the small blind to raise uncontested. Opponents: Pro (SB) is deep and capable of 3-bets, Elon (BB) is short (~15BB) and likely to shove or defend wide. I’ll open a standard 3x sizing to 300 to balance fold equity and avoid an undersized raise that invites too much action; if Pro 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand, but if Elon shoves I’m willing to call given his wide shove range and the pot odds.

I am in the Small Blind with [Ad 8d], facing a Button raise to 300 from Minni. A8s is a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop, with about 57% equity against a typical wide button opening range. The pot odds are favorable (calling 250 to win a pot that will be 750, requiring 33% equity). Although I will be out of position, 3-betting might bloat the pot unnecessarily or force a fold from worse hands that I dominate. The Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (15bb), so a call might induce a squeeze shove, but my hand is strong enough to evaluate that if it happens. Flat calling is the standard, lower-variance play here to realize my equity.

Jh7s is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential against a button raise and SB call. Pot odds are 200 into 700 (22%), but multiway with aggressive opponents like Minni, equity is insufficient and postflop position is bad in BB. Effective stack 1500, better to preserve chips for better spots. Standard fold.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Eights) on a board of [8s 2c Ac]. This is a very strong hand, currently well ahead of the Button's opening range, which likely consists of many single-pair Ax hands, flush draws, or air. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to make a continuation bet with her strong hands (AK, AQ) or bluffs/draws. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out her air and isolate me against a stronger range. I intend to check-raise or check-call depending on the size of the bet to extract maximum value.

Check. On A♣‑8♠‑2♣ I have middle/second pair (2♦) but the flop heavily favors caller’s defending range — lots of A‑x, club draws and bigger pairs. As preflop aggressor a c‑bet here will be called by most hands that beat me (A♣/A‑x, pocket pairs) and by club draws, so I won’t get folds often and I risk bloating the pot out of position. I have position and a real but vulnerable made hand, so I prefer pot control: see a free turn, keep the pot manageable, and use position to extract value or fold to clear strength. Plan: if SB checks back we can reassess and potentially bet small on safe turn cards; if SB fires a small bet I’ll call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to a large polarizing bet (representing an Ace or a set).

I hold top two pair (Aces and Eights) on a board of [8s 2c Ac Ks]. The flop was checked through, which suggests the opponent (Minni) likely does not have a monster (sets, strong Ax) and has a capped range consisting of weak Ax, underpairs, or air. The King on the turn is a good card for my range but also connects with hers (Kx, broadway draws). Since the board is somewhat wet with two flush draws (clubs and spades) and straight possibilities (QJ), I cannot afford to give free cards. Betting now extracts value from weak Aces and Kings that might check back, and charges any draws. I will bet slightly more than half pot to build the pot while keeping her calling range wide.

I have J♦2♦ on the button with a pair of deuces (board 8♠-2♣-A♣-K♠). Pro (SB) called preflop and checked the flop, now leads 400 into a 700 pre‑turn pot (pot shown 1100, to‑call 400). This is a medium turn probe that can include Kx, Ax, pocket pairs, and some bluffs. Folding relinquishes cheap equity; raising is unattractive — my kicker is weak, I don’t block many strong Ax/Kx combos, and a raise will get me into a bloated pot dominated by better hands. Calling is the best play: the price is small relative to the pot (calling to win ~1500 gives ~3.75:1 pot odds, needing ≈21% equity), I keep position, realize equity, and can re‑evaluate on the river. Plan: call now to see the river; if I improve (2 or J) extract value; if the river is a blank I may check‑back or make a small thin value bet; fold to big, polarizing river aggression representing Ace/King/set.

On the river [8s 2c Ac Ks 6s], the backdoor spade flush has arrived. While I hold top two pair (Ad8d), the runout is dangerous. My opponent checked back the flop and called the turn, which is consistent with marginal made hands (Ax, Kx) or draws (clubs, spades, straight). Club draws missed, but spade draws hit. Betting here is thin value; worse hands (weak Ax, Kx) may fold fearing the flush, and better hands (flushes, sets) will call or raise. Being out of position, checking is the superior line to control the pot size, induce bluffs from missed club draws, and avoid getting raised off my hand. I plan to evaluate her action; I can likely call a reasonable bet given my hand strength, but I avoid bloating the pot against a potential flush.

River decision: I hold J♦2♦ on 8♠‑2♣‑A♣‑K♠‑6♠. I have second pair (deuces) with a weak kicker. Villain (Pro) cold‑called preflop, checked the flop and then bet a modest 400 on the turn — that line looks like Kx, mid pocket pairs, some Ax, or a few bluffs. Betting the river is thin: hands that beat me (A/K/sets/two‑spade combos) will happily call, while the worse hands I can get value from will often fold. Raising is dominated and only bloats a pot I’m likely behind in. With pot control and showdown value the highest EV play is to check and take it to showdown rather than commit more chips with a marginal hand.
4c 8s is a weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. From UTG position, I need to play a tight range. This hand is a clear fold.

5-handed and deep-stacked, A♣7♦ (Ah7d) is worth opening. UTG folded so I have initiative from UTG+1 and want to apply pressure to the small blind (short, 14.5bb) and the big blind who defends a lot. Table open sizing has been around 250–300; I’ll make a standard 3xBB open to 300 to get fold equity and isolate or take the lead postflop. Plan: if called, play straightforward — c-bet favorable Ace-high or dry boards, exercise pot control on connected or multiway flops. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll lean to folding (discard marginal A-x out of position); if the SB shoves his ~1,450 short stack I will call given his wide shove range and the stack dynamics.

I am holding King-Six offsuit (K6o) on the Button facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni in UTG+1. K6o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by an early position opening range (often composed of stronger Kings, Aces, and pocket pairs). Although I have position, calling is unprofitable due to reverse implied odds (if I hit a King, I am likely outkicked). 3-betting as a bluff is risky against a large stack opener who has shown strength. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Preflop in the small blind with K9o facing a raise to 300 from Minni in UTG+1 (early position), which indicates a strong range. My hand is offsuit, marginal at best, and playing out of position postflop would be difficult. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (calling 250 into 450+250=700), but equity against an early position opening range is insufficient (~30-40% at best, often less). With ~14.5BB effective stack, no need to gamble here; folding preserves chips for better spots in a long tournament (65/150 hands). Minni has been aggressive historically but early raises warrant respect. No fold equity for a 3bet, and calling OOP is -EV long-term.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 5d, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG+1. Even though I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, my hand has very poor playability out of position against a strong early-position range. I have a healthy stack and do not need to defend with marginal hands. I will fold.

UTG with 3♦4♥ — a very weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with poor playability and little fold equity. There are four players to act behind (including a short button who may shove and aggressive deep stacks who will 3-bet/isolate), so calling 100 to see a flop would put me out of position and likely dominated or crushed by ranges I’ll face. Raising is even worse — I don’t want to commit chips with such a rag. Preserving my 16.8k stack and waiting for a much better spot in position is the correct long-term decision. Fold.

I hold AKo (King of Spades, Ace of Diamonds) in UTG+1 (effectively UTG after Minni folded) with a healthy stack of 129 BB. This is a premium hand that should be opened for a raise to build the pot and define ranges. With 5 players left, AKo is extremely strong. A standard opening size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here to balance my range and offer a reasonable price while charging the blinds. If the short-stacked Elon (14.5 BB) shoves, I am comfortable calling. If the deep-stacked Flash plays back, I have a hand strong enough to continue.

Preflop on the button with 6c4s offsuit, a speculative trash hand. Pro raised from UTG+1 to 250 (2.5BB), indicating a strong range. My stack is 14.5BB, short-stacked, but this hand has poor playability, no suitedness or connectivity for flush/straight draws, and low equity against raiser's range. Pot odds are about 2:1 (250 to call into 650), but implied odds are minimal with this hand. Shoving for fold equity is poor due to blockers and Pro's deep stack (126BB) likely calling wide vs short stack shove. Folding preserves stack for better spots like premium hands or suited connectors in position.

I am holding 3s 2c in the small blind, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Pro has raised from UTG+1. There is no strategic value in defending such a weak hand out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I have AcTs in the BB facing a UTG+1 open to 250. This is a solid holding - suited ace with a broadway card. Against an early position raise, ATs is a clear defend. I could 3-bet but calling to see a flop and playing in position-relative postflop seems fine. Calling 150 more into a 400 pot is getting good odds.

I have AcTs on K52 with two diamonds. Completely missed - no pair, no flush draw (wrong suits). Pro opened UTG+1 and this King-high board hits their range well. Standard check here to see what they do. If they c-bet I'll likely fold as I have minimal equity.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) on a K-5-2 board with two diamonds. This is a very strong hand given the board texture and action. My opponent, Claude, defended his Big Blind and checked to me. His range likely consists of weaker Kings (KQ, KJ, KT), pocket pairs, flush draws, and some complete misses. I want to extract value from his weaker Kings and charge any flush draws. Checking back would allow him to realize equity for free and miss a value street. A continuation bet of roughly 45% of the pot (250 chips) is appropriate to get value while keeping his range somewhat wide enough to call with worse hands.

I have AcTs on K52 with two diamonds. Completely whiffed - no pair, no diamond draw. Pro c-bet 250 into 500, which is standard. Their UTG+1 range connects well with this K-high board. I have ace-high with no equity improvement potential. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game with a healthy stack of 132 BB. My hand is ATo (Ace-Ten offsuit). In a 5-handed configuration, UTG is effectively the 'Hijack' or 'MP' in a full ring, or just one spot off the Cutoff. ATo is a standard opening hand from this position due to its high card value, although it can be vulnerable to domination by AQ/AK. The player to my left (Elon) is short-stacked (14.5 BB) and may shove, but I can re-evaluate if that happens. The other players are deep enough that I can comfortably fold to a 3-bet. I will open for a standard raise of 2.5x BB to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

J9o is a marginal offsuit hand from UTG+1 facing an UTG raise to 2.5BB. Pro's range is strong (TT+, AQs+, AKo, etc.), and calling 250 into 400 pot (pot odds ~3:1) commits 17% of my 14.5BB stack with poor postflop playability, position, and equity (~30-35% vs. strong range). Folding preserves stack for better spots like premiums or steals. No strong history read suggests Pro is overly loose preflop from UTG.

Q7s is a marginal hand in this situation. Against an UTG raise (which in a 5-handed game is the equivalent of the Hijack position), it's generally slightly below the threshold for a profitable call on the button. Pro's raise to 250 represents a standard open, and while I have the advantage of position and a large chip lead, Q7s faces significant reverse implied odds against a typical UTG opening range (which often includes stronger Queens and better suited hands). Folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack advantage.

Q3o is a weak hand to defend against an UTG open. Pro's UTG raising range is typically tight, and Q3 offsuit plays poorly postflop even when we hit. With Minni still to act behind us, we risk being squeezed. Easy fold here.

Pro opened from UTG to 250 — a fairly tight, strong range in this 5-handed game. I hold 8♥3♠: a poor, unconnected offsuit hand with very low equity and terrible post‑flop playability out of position. Pot is 400 and I must call 150 (~27% break‑even). Versus an UTG opening range my equity is well below that threshold, so calling or raising would be throwing money away. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot with position or a stronger hand.

Pocket 77 is a premium short-stack hand UTG in 5-handed play. With only 14.5BB, folding is too tight, limping (call 100) is weak and plays poorly postflop. Standard tournament strategy calls for an open shove here for maximum fold equity against deep-stacked opponents while denying them equity. 77 has excellent equity when called (over 40% vs typical calling ranges). Opponent tendencies from history show loose calling (esp. Minni/Pro), but fold equity compensates. Shoving builds stack aggressively in tournament with 82 hands left.

Elon has moved all-in for 1,450 from UTG. I am sitting in UTG+1 with pocket deuces. While I have a significant chip lead, calling here is problematic because there are still three players left to act behind me (Claude, Minni, and Pro). Pocket deuces are the weakest possible pocket pair and are frequently dominated by any higher pair or are a coin flip against two overcards. I do not want to commit chips to a pot where I am likely behind or facing a squeeze from the remaining players. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

32o is one of the worst hands in poker. Elon shoved all-in for 1450, and I'd need to commit 32% of my stack with terrible cards. Even against a wide shoving range, 32o has very poor equity. Clear fold.

Elon shoved to 1450 (now all‑in). I hold 8♦3♠ — one of the weakest hands preflop and heavily dominated by any UTG shoving range. The pot is 1600 and it costs me 1400 to call, so I need roughly 46.7% equity to break even. 8‑3o has far less equity versus a reasonable UTG all‑in range (likely well under that), and the big blind (Pro) is still to act — calling risks a multiway pot that would further destroy my equity. My stack (16,650) is healthy; folding preserves chips for much better spots and is the +EV play here. I fold.

I am in the Big Blind with QsTs. Elon shoves all-in from UTG for 1450 (14.5 BBs). Everyone else folds. The pot is 1600, and I need to call 1350, offering pot odds of roughly 46%. QsTs is a decent speculative hand but performs poorly against a UTG shoving range, which likely consists of pairs (77+), strong Aces (AT+), and strong broadways (KQ, KJ). Against a realistic range like top 10-15% of hands, my equity is approximately 36-40%, which is below the required 46% to break even. Given the stack depth and the risk of being dominated, folding is the mathematically correct decision.

I am dealt J5o in the UTG position. This is a very weak hand to play from early position, even in a 5-handed game. My goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands and protect my chip lead. With a short stack (Elon) in the Big Blind, I should be looking for better spots to apply pressure rather than entering the pot with marginal holdings. I will fold this hand.
2c 3s is one of the worst starting hands in poker. UTG+1 is an early position where I need strong hands. Easy fold.

Button — with 9♣7♦ I’ll open here to take the initiative and steal the blinds. This hand is marginal but perfectly fine to raise from the button: I want to avoid multiway limp pots where 97o performs poorly and use position post‑flop. I’ll size to 300 (≈3x BB) — bigger than the min to increase fold equity and discourage light squeezes from the SB, but still a standard, controllable size. I’m aware Elon (BB) is short (1,500) and may shove; if he jams I’ll evaluate the shove but am comfortable calling a reasonable short‑stack all‑in given pot odds and his wide shoving range; versus a large 3‑bet from the deep SB I’ll likely fold and preserve stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K8s facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni). While K8s is a suited King, it is a marginal hand to play out of position against an aggressive button opener. Additionally, the Big Blind (Elon) is sitting on a short stack of 15bb (1,500 chips), which increases the likelihood of a squeeze shove behind me. Calling would put me in a difficult spot if the BB shoves, and playing post-flop OOP with a dominated King is generally unprofitable. The 3x sizing makes the pot odds slightly less attractive than a min-raise. Folding is the disciplined decision here to avoid high-variance spots with marginal holdings.

Short stack of 15BB in BB facing a button raise to 3BB after two early folds and SB fold. 7c6s is offsuit with straight potential but poor high cards and plays poorly OOP postflop. Pot odds are decent (200 to call into 450), but villain's button range is wide yet strong enough that my equity is marginal (~35-40% vs typical button open), and with low SPR post-call (~2:1), I'll be committed often in bad spots. No position advantage, better to preserve stack for premium hands or better steal opportunities.
9-2 offsuit is a trash hand, especially from UTG position. Easy fold.

UTG+1 with K♦4♠. I'm out of position to the Button and the big blind, facing only the blinds (to call 100 or min-raise to 200). K4o is a weak, easily dominated hand with a poor kicker. Limping surrenders initiative and likely puts me into multiway pots where this hand performs poorly; raising thinly from early position with such a hand is invite for 3‑bets from deep stacks (Flash) or shove pressure from the short SB (Elon). With a healthy 168bb I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot or a stronger holding. Fold and preserve stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with 8c 7h (87o). The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, 87o is generally below the standard opening threshold, even for a wide Button range (which usually includes suited connectors like 87s but folds off-suit ones). Additionally, the Small Blind (Elon) is short-stacked with ~14.5 BBs and is in a push-or-fold mode; opening here risks facing a reshove that I cannot profitably call. The Big Blind is also deep-stacked and can defend widely. Therefore, the most EV+ decision is to fold.

Short stack of ~14.5BB in SB, heads-up vs BB after all others folded. A4o is a marginal hand with an ace blocker and decent high-card value, providing reasonable equity against a typical BB defending range (~40-50% equity). Calling the additional 50 into a 150 pot offers excellent implied odds and pot odds (3:1), allowing cheap access to flops where ace-high can win unimproved or improve to top pair/top kicker. Raising risks overcommitting stacks with poor postflop playability due to offsuit nature and weak kicker. Folding is too tight in position with dead money and blinds posted. Limping balances range and exploits BB's wide defending tendencies seen in prior hands.

I am in the Big Blind with T2o. The Small Blind (Elon) has completed the bet. Since the pot is limped and I have a weak hand, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play, especially since Elon is short-stacked and might be looking to gamble.

On this dry, paired Q-Q-3 rainbow flop, ace-high has decent showdown value and blocks strong hands like AQ/AK/AA. As the pre-flop limper OOP with a short stack (~14BB), betting thin value or as a bluff risks getting raised by sets, two-pair, or better queens, committing too much of my stack. Flash's BB check pre-flop suggests a wide range, many of which miss this board. Checking allows pot control, induces bluffs from worse hands, and realizes equity cheaply if checked back. If bet into, I can fold to large sizes or call small bets with backdoor potential and live ace. Prioritizing stack preservation while exploiting BB's wide range post-flop.
