In this limped pot, the board has paired Queens. I have Ten-high with no draw. Elon has checked to me. Since the pot is small and Elon is playing a very short stack, there is little to be gained by bluffing here as any piece of the board or even an Ace-high might call or shove. I will check behind to see a free turn and potentially improve or win at showdown if the board runs out favorably.

Short stack ~14BB OOP heads-up vs BB on turn with ace-high no pair no draw on Q-Q-3-9 rainbow board. No improvement from flop; still just showdown value blocking top pair aces. Flash's double check (pre-flop and flop) indicates wide weak range including high cards, gutshots, or air that missed. Betting risks overcommitting stack to better queens/sets/two-pair while thin value vs worse is minimal (few hands call profitably lighter than ace-high). Raising commitment too high. Checking maintains pot control, realizes equity cheaply to river where ace-high wins vs many bluffs/misses, induces Flash bluffs on river, and preserves stack for better spots. Consistent with prior flop check strategy exploiting BB wide range while minimizing variance as short stack.

In this limped pot, the board has paired Queens and now added a Nine. I have Ten-high with no draw. Elon, who is very short-stacked, has checked to me for the second time. Given the small pot size and the opponent's stack depth, there is no value in betting a hand with no equity. I will check behind to take a free look at the river and potentially win if the hand goes to showdown with Elon holding a weaker air hand.

River OOP as short stack (~14BB) with ace-high on Q-Q-3-9-K rainbow board vs BB who checked preflop, flop, and turn, indicating a weak wide range capped without strong queens, two-pair, or sets (which would typically bet for value/protection). Ace-high has excellent showdown value, blocking AA/AQ/AK, beating all king-high, queen-high, and bluffs/misses common in BB range after multi-street checks. Betting thin value risks folding out worse hands (e.g., K-high, J-high) while facing raises from any strength we lose to, and overcommitting short stack with poor fold equity. Checking realizes equity at showdown cheaply, wins frequently uncontested vs worse, preserves stack for premium spots, and exploits opponent's passive play seen in prior hands (e.g., frequent check-backs). Consistent with preflop/flop/turn strategy of pot control and stack preservation to maximize tournament survival.

In this limped pot, the board has run out Qd-3c-Qs-9h-Ks. I hold Ten-high. Elon has checked on the flop, turn, and river. While I have very little showdown value, betting into a short stack who has checked every street in a 200-chip pot is rarely profitable, as they may call with any pair or Ace-high. I will check behind to see the showdown for free.

Pocket sevens in a 5‑handed game are a clear open from UTG — they have good showdown value and play well post‑flop. I want to take the initiative, narrow the field and isolate the short stack on the button, so I’ll open‑raise to a standard 3x (300). This size gives fold equity against the blinds and keeps pot control with deep stacks. If Elon (1600) shoves I can call; versus a large 3‑bet from Flash/Pro I’ll re‑evaluate (often folding to very big 3‑bets, calling smaller ones to set‑mine or proceed post‑flop).

I hold 3c 4h in UTG+1, facing a raise to 300 from Minni in UTG. 3-4 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker with very poor playability and equity, especially against a UTG opening range which is typically strong. There is no strategic merit in calling or bluffing here given the early position and stack depths. The correct play is to fold immediately.

43o is a complete trash hand from the button facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has been active in previous hands. No suitedness, poor connectivity, dominated by any calling range. Calling 300 (19% of my 1600 stack) into a 450 pot offers insufficient equity even in position. Folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize stack growth over remaining hands.

I have Ad Qd in the Small Blind, which is a premium starting hand in a 5-handed game. Minni opened from UTG for 3x the big blind. While UTG opens are generally tighter, AQ suited is well ahead of an average opening range and too strong to simply call, especially out of position. By 3-betting to 1,000, I take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or define Minni's range while building a pot with a very strong hand. This also puts pressure on Claude in the Big Blind, who has a relatively short stack compared to Minni and me.
T3o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. I'd need to call 900 into a 1400 pot with poor equity against Flash's 3-bet range. No playability, bad position. Easy fold.

Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1000 after my 300 UTG open; this is now heads‑up. I have 7♠7♥ — too strong to fold to a single 3‑bet, but not a hand I want to 4‑bet with out of position for thin value. The pot is ~1400 and I must call 700 (need ~33% equity to break even); with deep stacks I have very good implied odds to set‑mine and realize equity against a reasonably wide SB 3‑bet range. Calling keeps the pot controlled OOP, preserves fold equity on some flops, and allows me to play aggressively if I flop a set or big two‑pair. I’ll call to see a flop and re‑evaluate based on texture and opponent aggression.

I have the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and Queen) on a 9-7-3 board with two diamonds. I was the pre-flop aggressor, having 3-bet Minni. This flop is relatively disconnected, though it connects with some of Minni's calling range (like 88-JJ or suited connectors). By betting 1,000 into a 2,100 pot, I am making a standard continuation bet to maintain initiative. With my significant equity (the nut flush draw plus overcards), I am comfortable building the pot and would likely call or jam if raised, but a lead here often wins the pot immediately against hands that missed or puts pressure on medium pairs.

I flopped a set (7♠7♥) on 9♦7♦3♠ — a very strong hand heads‑up. Flash opened pre and 3‑betted, then makes a small continuation bet of 1,000 into this pot; that line looks like a wide/voluntary c‑bet or a draw attempt. I want to both build the pot for value and charge the many diamond draws/overcards that can still improve. Raising also isolates and gets value from top pairs, overpairs and hands like 99/TT that will often continue. With deep stacks I should be aggressive here but not commit the whole stack yet. I’ll raise to 3,000 total on the flop: big enough to charge draws and extract value while keeping post‑flop maneuvering possible. If he calls, I’ll continue to extract on turn barring a scary diamond; if he jams or raises very large I’ll seriously consider calling (only 99 realistically dominates my hand).

I have the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and Queen) on a board of 9-7-3. This gives me massive equity against almost any hand Minni can hold here. Minni's raise to 3000 represents a strong range, likely a set or a strong one-pair hand. By calling, I am getting immediate pot odds of roughly 3-to-1 (2000 to call into a pot that will be 8100), which is more than enough to justify continuing with a 9-out flush draw plus potential overcard outs. Shoving is an option to maximize fold equity, but calling allows me to see a turn card and maintain control over my stack size in this high-roller format, especially since I still have the nut draw if Minni is indeed very strong.

On this turn (Kh), I have the nut flush draw (Ad Qd) on a board of 9d 7d 3s Kh. I was the pre-flop 3-better and led the flop, but was raised by Minni. Minni's flop raise indicates significant strength, likely a set (99, 77, 33) or two-pair (97s), or potentially a strong draw. The King of Hearts is a card that technically favors my 3-betting range more than hers, but given she raised the flop, she is unlikely to be intimidated. By checking, I allow myself the opportunity to see the river for free if she checks behind with a marginal hand or a draw. If she bets, I have the necessary equity and pot odds (9 flush outs, potentially Ace/Queen outs) to justify a call, while also controlling the pot size against a range that currently beats me.

I flopped a set (7s7h) and raised the flop to build the pot and charge diamond draws/overcards; Flash called. The turn K♥ is not a diamond and he checks, so I should continue for value and protection. I’m ahead of almost his entire calling range (the main hand that beats me is 99), and I want to charge Kx, 9x, pocket pairs and any remaining diamond draws while denying a free river. A sizable, but not committing, bet around 2/3 pot achieves that — it extracts value from worse hands that will call and prices many draws. If he jams I’ll call given the strength of a set; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river. Bet 5,400 into 8,100.

On the turn, the board is 9d-7d-3s-Kh. I have the nut flush draw with Ad Qd. Minni, who raised the flop, has bet 5,400 into a pot of 8,100, making the total pot 13,500. To call, I need to commit 5,400, which gives me pot odds of approximately 2.5:1, or 28.5%. My direct equity with 9 diamond outs is approximately 19.6%. While the direct pot odds are slightly unfavorable, the implied odds are significant; Minni has 7,400 remaining in her stack, which I can likely win if I hit my flush on the river. Furthermore, as the pre-flop 3-better, Ad Qd is at the top of my range here, and folding the nut flush draw in such a large pot would be overly tight and exploitable. Calling is the standard professional play in this situation to realize my equity and potentially stack the opponent.

I missed my nut flush draw on a board that paired the nine on the river. Given Minni's aggression on the flop and turn—raising my continuation bet and then firing a large second barrel—she likely has a very strong hand, such as a set, two-pair, or at least a strong King. With only Ace-high and the pot being very large relative to her remaining stack, she is almost certainly pot-committed. A bluff is unlikely to succeed here as she only needs to call her remaining 7,400 to win a pot that would be over 26,000. I will check and likely fold to a final bet, as my Ace-high has virtually no showdown value against the range she has represented throughout the hand.

I flopped a set and built the pot (raised flop, bet turn) — I’m still very strong on the river (7♠7♥ on 9♦7♦3♠ K♥ 9♥). The only hands that now beat me are very specific: pocket 99 (quads) or 9x combinations that make a full house (9+3, 9+7). Those combos are rare. Flash’s line (SB 3‑bet pre, call my flop raise, check‑call my turn bet, then check the river) looks like a medium‑strength/call‑down range (Kx, some overpairs, missed diamond draws) rather than a clear value monster — his river check signals either weakness or a trap, but given the rarity of full houses/quads I shouldn’t barrel off my short stack here. Pot is 18,900 and my effective stack is 7,400 (SPR ≈ 0.4) so any sizeable river bet would effectively commit me and expose me to the small number of catastrophic combos. Checking back is the lower‑variance +EV line: I get to see showdown with a very strong hand, avoid folding out hands that will call a value bet, and preserve my stack if Flash does have the nuts and decides to check‑raise. If Flash instead bets, I’ll call reasonable wagers for value (and call shoves given how rarely he beats me). Therefore I check to take the showdown.

I am in UTG position with J9o (Jack of Diamonds, Nine of Hearts). In a 5-handed game, this hand is below the standard opening range for the first position. Additionally, the player to my immediate left (Elon) has a short stack of 16 BBs and could easily shove over a raise, forcing me to fold equity. With 132 BBs, there is no need to loosen up my opening range with marginal off-suit hands from early position. The correct play is to fold.

UTG+1 with 2d4s offsuit, one of the weakest possible starting hands. Poor playability, low equity against ranges, especially multi-way. Early position requires tight play. Stack is 16BB, so preserve for premium hands. Folding is standard to avoid difficult post-flop situations.

I am on the button in a 5-handed game. With the action folded to me, K6o is a standard opening hand to attempt to steal the blinds. Claude (SB) has a relatively short stack compared to the rest of the table, and Minni (BB) is the chip leader and very active. A 2.5x raise is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot with positional advantage.

I have 76 suited in the SB facing a button open to 250. This is a decent suited connector with straight and flush potential. Good playability against a likely wide button range. Calling 200 more with position on BB but OOP to button. Worth seeing a flop with these implied odds at 44bb effective.

I defend the big blind with A♠4♦ by calling the 150 more chips (total 250 committed). The button’s open to 250 is a standard, fairly wide raising range; Claude in the small blind has already called, so this is likely to be a multi-way pot. Pot is ~600 and calling 150 buys me a spot with a break-even equity of ~150/(600+150)=20% — A4o has reasonable showdown and postflop playability against wide ranges, and the call is cheap relative to the pot. A small 3-bet here (min to 400) would be a marginal bluff that creates a multiway pot and puts me out of position; folding would be too tight given pot odds and stack depth. Plan postflop: see a flop, proceed aggressively if I hit an Ace (but respect kicker issues vs heavy action), and otherwise check/float or fold to large continuation bets; overall calling preserves stack and maximizes realizable equity.

Flopped bottom pair (6s) on A-5-6 board. No real straight draw - just have 5-6-7 which needs two more cards. Backdoor heart flush draw. Ace-high board is dangerous against button opener and BB caller. With such a marginal hand OOP, checking to see what develops is best. Don't want to build a pot with bottom pair when an ace is out there.

Flop: A♦ 5♠ 6♦; I hold A♠4♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker in a three-way pot (BTN raised pre, SB called). With two opponents and diamonds/straight possibilities, checking hands the initiative to the button and gives free cards to many draws. Betting protects my hand and extracts value from worse A-x, small pairs and draws. I’ll make a measured half‑pot c-bet to charge draws and keep worse hands in; size is large enough to deny cheap equity to many draws but small enough to get called by worse Aces and pairs. If I face a big raise I’ll re-evaluate (I won’t be committed with this weak kicker against heavy aggression and will likely fold to clear strength); if I get called I’ll base turn play on the card (continue for value/protection on safe cards, slow down or fold to big action if the board bricks or pairs/diamonds complete).

I raised pre-flop with K6o as a standard button steal attempt and was called by both blinds. The flop comes Ad-5s-6d, giving me second pair (sixes). Claude (SB) checks, and Minni (BB) leads out for 300 into a 750 pot. Although I have second pair, an Ace-high board is generally favorable for the blinds' defending ranges. Minni is the chip leader and has been very active; her lead into two players often signifies either an Ace, a strong draw (like diamonds or a straight draw), or occasionally a semi-bluff. With a weak kicker and another player (Claude) still to act behind me on the flop, calling here is marginal and could lead to difficult decisions on later streets if the board texture changes. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for more advantageous spots.

I have bottom pair (6s) with a gutshot straight draw (need a 4) and backdoor hearts. Minni's 300 bet into 750 is small (40% pot). Getting 3.5:1 odds, I need ~22% equity. Bottom pair + gutshot gives me enough equity to continue. The small sizing often indicates a probe bet or weak made hand. Raising accomplishes nothing - I'd only get called by better. Calling to see the turn.

It's checked to me on the turn. I have bottom pair (6s) with 7 kicker and a gutshot straight draw (need an 8). Minni's flop bet and turn check suggests she may have given up or is trapping with a strong hand. Betting accomplishes little - better hands call, worse hands fold. My hand has showdown value but is marginal. Checking back to control pot size and see a free river is optimal.

I have top pair A♠4♦ but a very weak kicker. I c‑bet the flop (300) to charge draws and got one caller (Claude). The turn 9♠ is a scare card — it improves lots of hands in his calling range (A9, 9x, and it completes 7‑8 straights) and it also kills the backdoor diamond possibility I had. Claude checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control or a marginal holding. With a single opponent and a vulnerable kicker, I prefer pot control rather than bloating the pot and risking a raise or river shove that I’d be forced to fold to. Checking keeps worse Aces and pairs in a position to bluff/overbet on the river (where I can make a disciplined decision) and avoids paying to see a river when I’m likely behind to some of his turned combos. Plan: check turn to control the pot; on the river I’ll value‑bet small if he checks a safe card, call reasonable bets from thin value/bluffs, and fold to large overbets that represent clear domination.

River brought Qd completing diamond flush. I have bottom pair (6s) with 7 kicker - very marginal showdown value. Minni bet flop then checked turn, suggesting weakness or a draw. Betting serves no purpose - I only get called by better hands (any A, Q, 9, or flush). Checking to showdown is optimal to realize my equity against missed draws or weaker holdings.

I have top pair (A♠4♦) but a very weak kicker. The river Q♦ is a fairly dangerous card — it gives Qx two-pair/top-pair value and completes diamond-based draws that could have been floating/calling on the flop. Claude called my flop bet and then checked the turn and river, which looks like pot‑control with a marginal holding or a missed draw rather than strength. Betting here risks a raise/shove that I don’t want to face with a vulnerable kicker; a small bet might extract from worse A‑x or small pairs, but the upside is limited compared to the downside of folding to big action. Pot control and taking this to showdown is the prudent +EV line. I check to avoid bloating the pot and to lose the minimum when behind; I’ll fold to a large river raise (if it occurs) and collect at showdown when Claude shows weakness.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with QTo offsuit and a short stack of 16BB. QTo is a marginal hand with poor post-flop playability, dominated by better Qx and Tx hands. Folding preserves stack for better spots like premium hands or suited aces/broadways from later position. Limping is poor with short stack as it invites multiway pots and difficult SPR decisions. Raising or shoving UTG is -EV given the hand's equity against calling ranges. Opponents have deep stacks except Claude, but no action yet favors waiting.

I am holding King-Five offsuit in UTG+1. While a King has some high-card value, K5o is a weak hand to open with in early/middle position. The table still has some aggressive players, specifically Minni who is the chip leader and has been active. Opening here would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop or facing a 3-bet. Given the stack sizes and the hand's strength, folding is the standard and most disciplined play.
ATo on the button is a strong hand. With two folds in front, I have a great opportunity to steal the blinds or build a pot in position. Standard button open to 2.5x BB.

I'm in the small blind with A♦ J♥ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, to call 200). AJo is a strong, playable hand but I don't want to play multiway out of position. I 3-bet to 1000 (~4x the open) to gain fold equity and try to isolate Claude on the button. This sizing is large enough to discourage callers but not so huge that it unnecessarily commits my very deep stack. Plan: if called I'll play postflop for position control — c-bet favorable A/heart/low-card flops, pot-control on dangerous boards. If Claude or Pro 4-bets to an all-in I will call a shove from Claude (he can shove a wide range from 36bb) but be prepared to fold to a large, non-shove 4-bet from Pro that commits too many chips without clear equity. Overall, 3-betting maximizes EV by avoiding marginal multiway OOP spots and extracting value versus Claude's opening range.

I hold 9c 2h in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The action ahead of me is heavy: the Button opened to 250 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. Facing a 3-bet with trash cards makes this an effortless fold. I need to preserve my stack of 13,100 rather than bleeding chips in a spot where I am crushed by both ranges.

Minni 3-bet me to 1000 after my button open. With ATo, I'm in a tough spot. Against a 3-betting range, ATo is often dominated by AK/AQ/AJ or flipping against pairs. My stack is 36.5 BB - not desperate but not deep either. Options: 1) Fold - lose 250, 2) Call - creates awkward SPR ~1.4 postflop, 3) Shove 3650 - could get folds but if called I'm often behind. Minni has 26k stack and has been aggressive throughout. Given tournament considerations and ATo being marginal vs a 3-bet range, folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with AhTh, a strong suited broadway hand that is well within my opening range. My stack is healthy at 14,200. I want to open the pot to take control and build value. Given Elon's very short stack of 1,500 in the big blind, I expect he might defend aggressively or shove, which I am comfortable calling given my hand strength. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total).
82s is a very weak hand, even suited. I'm OOP against an UTG raiser with a short stack (36.5 BB). This is not a profitable spot to defend - easy fold.

On the button with pocket 4s (4d4h) facing an UTG open to 300. With deep stacks (I have 27450) and position, set-mining is the highest EV line — pocket fours hit a set ~11.8% on the flop and I have huge implied odds if I connect. 3-betting risks getting a short-stack shove from Elon (BB 1500) or bloating a pot out of position against the opener. I’ll keep the pot manageable and retain positional advantage — call the 300 to see the flop.

I hold 5h 6h, a suited connector, in the Small Blind. The UTG player (Flash) opened to 300 (3BB) and the Button (Minni) called. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 250 to win 750) to see a flop. With effective stacks over 100BB against the opener and caller, my hand has high implied odds if I hit a strong draw or made hand, which justifies playing out of position. The Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (15BB) and presents a squeeze risk, but the cost to call is less than 2% of my stack, making this a profitable speculative call.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash, called by Minni on the button and Pro in SB. Pot is 1000, calling 200 more (total 300 from BB) gives excellent pot odds of about 5:1. Kd6d is a suited king, speculative hand with straight/flush potential, playable multiway from BB getting these odds. My stack is 1500, committing only 13% here. Folding too tight preflop. Raising or shoving too strong for this hand against UTG range; better to see flop cheaply and play postflop dynamics. Opponents: Flash aggressive UTG, Minni loose caller with deep stack, Pro calling station-ish. Plan to play flops aggressively if hits.

I have flopped 5-high on a very coordinated board of 8s-Td-9h. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and a backdoor flush draw, my equity is extremely low. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser and callers who likely have broadway cards or pairs. Being out of position in a 4-way pot with essentially air, my only viable option is to check and fold to any aggression.

Flop of 8s Td 9h is highly coordinated, hitting Flash's UTG raising range (possible 7x, Jx, Tx, overpairs) and Minni/Pro's calling ranges with straight draws, pairs, etc. My Kd6d has king-high with a weak gutshot straight draw (4 outs to 7x) and backdoor diamond flush draw, but no pair or strong draw multiway. Pot 1200, stack 1300 (shallow), checked to me (Pro checked). Betting risks calling stations (Minni/Pro) or strong hands from Flash, low fold equity multiway, and commits ~46% stack without much equity. Checking keeps pot small, realizes equity cheaply if missed by others, allows turn control or free river card. Plan to fold to heavy action but check/call small if floats or weak bets, given pot odds.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (AhTh) on a board of 8s-Td-9h. While this is a strong hand, the board is extremely coordinated, offering many straight and two-pair possibilities for my three opponents in this multi-way pot. Betting here risks a raise from a hand that already has me beat, such as QJ, 76, or various two-pair combos like T9 or 98. By checking, I can see how Minni (the button) acts and keep the pot manageable. Given the high probability of a scare card on the turn, pot control is the priority.

Check. I came into this hand set‑mining preflop with 4♦4♥ and I missed the flop (8♠–10♦–9♥). I only have a small pair with very low showdown value vs the UTG open/caller ranges and only ~8.4% to hit a set by the river, so the +EV line is pot control and seeing a cheap turn rather than bloating the pot or getting raised. Flash (preflop raiser) and the blinds checked, so betting here would often fold out weaker hands but also get called/raised by better holdings and could induce a shove from the short stack (Elon, ~1.3k) that I don’t want to be committed to without a made hand. Plan: check and reevaluate on the turn — call small probing bets if the price plus implied odds justify continuing to chase the set, but fold to medium/large bets or raises (or an all‑in) where my equity and implied odds don’t cover the price.

On the turn, the board is 8s Td 9h 9s. I have 5h 6h. The turn card pairs the board and brings a flush draw. I have a gutshot straight draw needing a 7 to make a 6-high straight (6-7-8-9-T), but this is the low end of the straight and vulnerable on a paired board. Given the 4-way action and the fact that the flop was checked around, ranges are wide but likely contain some showdown value like Ax or pairs that checked back. Betting here with 5-high serves no purpose as I fold out nothing better and get called by everything that beats me. My plan is to check and fold to any bet, hoping to see a free river card potentially.

Turn pairs the 9s, making the board even more coordinated with possible trips, straights (J7, T8, QJ), and completed spade flush draws. My Kd6d remains king-high with no pair, no straight draw (flop gutshot was weak anyway), and only backdoor diamonds—pure air multiway. Pot 1200, my stack 1300 (shallow ~1:1 SPR). Pro (SB calling station) checked, now acting first postflop checks. Behind are Flash (UTG raiser, checked flop—could be strong or trapping) and Minni (loose button deep stack). Betting commits most of my stack with low fold equity against 3 opponents; value bets crush us, bluffs get called by wide ranges. Checking preserves stack, takes free river showdown if checked around or pot control. Fold to significant action behind, as king-high loses to most calling/showdown hands. Consistent with flop plan: check non-nutted boards multiway.

The turn pairs the nine, making the board even more dangerous (8s-Td-9h-9s). While I have top pair with an Ace kicker, any player holding a nine now has trips, and the straights (QJ, 76) are still possible. In a four-way pot, the likelihood of someone having a piece of this board is high. After Pro and Elon checked, I will check as well to maintain pot control and see how Minni acts behind me. Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap river or evaluate a bet from Minni without over-committing my stack on a highly coordinated board.

I came into this hand set‑mining with 4♦4♥ and missed the flop. The turn (8♠–10♦–9♥–9♠) pairs the board and makes my small pair have very little showdown value — only ~8.5% to hit a set by the river. This is a multiway pot against an UTG opener and callers; many hands in their ranges (9x, Tx, 8x, two‑card straights) now dominate or have strong draws. Betting here would either fold out worse hands but get called/raised by better holdings and could induce a shove from the short stack (Elon) that I don't want to face without a set. With position and deep stacks the high EV line is pot control: check and see a cheap river. I’ll evaluate any small probe bets on price, but will fold to medium/large bets or shoves.

On the River, the board reads 8s Td 9h 9s As. The Ace of Spades is a terrible card for me to bluff into three opponents. It completes the backdoor spade flush draw and hits the Ace-high range that opponents (especially the pre-flop raiser or caller) might have been checking down. With 5-high, I have zero showdown value, but the probability of one of the three opponents having an Ace, a 9, a flush, or a straight is extremely high given the connected nature of the board. Bluffing into a field of three on a 'scare card' that likely improved someone is negative EV. The pot is small (1200), and preserving my stack is prioritized over a low-percentage bluff.

River Ace of Spades completes the spade flush draw on a highly coordinated board (paired 9s already had straights and trips possible). My Kd6d is king-high with no pair, no flush, no straight—pure air that loses to any pair or better. Multiway pot (1200) with shallow stack (1300, SPR ~1:1), Pro (SB calling station type) checked first, signaling possible weakness or trap. Betting commits nearly all-in with minimal fold equity against Flash's UTG range (overpairs, Tx, Jx that beat me), Minni's loose calling range, and Pro's station tendencies—value gets called, bluffs get called. Checking allows cheap showdown where king-high has decent equity vs. missed draws/bluffs after full check-around flop/turn. Preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 76 hands left; no need to bluff into field.

On the river, the Ace of Spades (As) improves my hand to two pair (Aces and Tens). The board remains extremely coordinated with many potential straights (QJ, 76) and trips (any hand with a 9). Since I am in a four-way pot and two players have already checked to me, the safest path to realizing my showdown value is to check. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, but I could easily be raised by a Nine or a straight, putting me in a difficult position. By checking, I hope to see a cheap showdown or evaluate a potential bet from Minni, the last player to act.

Check. On the river my 4♦4♥ makes two pair (nines and fours) on 8♠‑T♦‑9♥‑9♠‑A♠. In a multiway pot facing an UTG opener and callers, many hands beat me (any 9s = trips, Tx, Ax, 8x for better two‑pair, and higher pocket pairs). Everyone checked through, so there’s no free value to extract and a river bet largely gets called by better hands or folds out hands I already beat. Betting risks building a pot I’m likely losing and could induce a shove from the short stack; checking preserves my stack and forces opponents to show. I’ll check to showdown and fold to any medium/large river bet (only consider calling an extremely small, obvious blocking probe, but none has occurred).

T8s from UTG with 36.5 BB. This is a marginal hand from early position. While suited connectors have playability, UTG requires a tighter range. With 4 players behind including some very deep stacks, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG+1 with 6h6s — a playable pocket pair in a 5-handed game. Stacks are deep overall (I have 27,150) so I want both fold equity and to preserve implied odds for set-mining. Opening is preferable to just calling: it takes the initiative, reduces multi-way callers, and extracts value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll make a standard 3x BB open to 300 (bigger than the min 200 to discourage loose callers but small enough to keep postflop implied odds). If the short SB (Elon, 1,250) shoves I’ll call — 66 has good equity vs a wide shove. Versus a large 3-bet from a deep stack (Flash/Pro) I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold to heavy aggression unless it’s an all-in where I’m getting proper odds.

I am holding 9s4c on the button, which is a very weak unsuited hand (trash hand). Facing a raise to 300 from Minni in UTG+1, who is the chip leader with a massive stack, indicates a range that crushes my 94o. Even though I have position, the hand has extremely poor playability and equity. There is a short stack in the small blind who might shove, and the big blind is yet to act. Calling or raising here would be a significant mistake. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Short stack of 12.5BB in SB OOP with Q8o facing an UTG+1 raise from deep-stacked Minni, who has been aggressive historically. Q8o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop and mediocre equity (~40%) vs a typical EP calling range. Calling commits 20% of stack into a pot OOP with no initiative. Shoving risks being called by better hands while fold equity is uncertain against Minni's stack and style. Folding preserves stack for premium shoving spots.

I am in the Big Blind with a mid-pocket pair (77). Minni, the chip leader, has opened for 300 from early position (UTG+1 in 5-handed). While 77 is a strong hand, I am out of position against a deep-stacked and active opponent. Calling allows me to close the pre-flop action, keep the pot manageable, and see a flop. This strategy also allows for 'set-mining' where I can potentially stack the leader if I hit a Three-of-a-Kind, while minimizing losses if overcards come on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sevens. The flop has come Ace-high with a Ten (Ah Tc 4h). This board significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as Minni is likely to have many Ace-x and Ten-x combinations. My pocket sevens are currently a marginal hand that is likely behind any Ace or Ten. Given that I am out of position and Minni has a range advantage, the standard and most prudent play is to check. This allows me to see how the aggressor proceeds and potentially see a turn card cheaply if they check back.

I opened UTG+1 to 300 and the BB (Flash) just checked — this is a classic c‑bet spot. The flop A♥ T♣ 4♥ hits my perceived opening range (I can credibly represent Ax or A4) and Flash’s check looks like weakness or a missed hand. With an underpair (6♥6♠) I want to take the initiative, get folds from broadway hands and unpaired overcards, and deny free cards that could improve speculative callers. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (~325 into 650) — big enough to extract value/fold equity but small relative to our deep stacks so I’m not overcommitted if he resists. If he folds I take it; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and will fold to strong check‑raises or large aggression (66 is vulnerable to an Ace/Top pair).

I am in the Big Blind with pocket sevens. The flop is Ace-high and also contains a Ten (Ah Tc 4h), which fits perfectly into the pre-flop raising range of Minni (UTG+1). My pocket pair is now an underpair to two cards on the board. Minni's continuation bet of 1/3 pot is standard for someone holding an Ace or even air, but given I am out of position against the chip leader and have very few ways to improve my hand (only two non-heart sevens would really help), calling is likely to lead to further difficult decisions on the turn and river. I will fold now to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

Ace-King of clubs is a premium hand — must open from UTG. Pot is 150 and the price to just call is 100, but I want to take the initiative, narrow the field and build value with good equity. Standard sizing here is about 3x the big blind; raising to 300 (total) pressures the blinds (Flash in SB and Claude in BB) and forces the short-stack on the button (Elon) to decide for all his chips. I keep postflop flexibility with a deep 27,500 stack and can respond to a 3-bet or shove accordingly. Raise to 300.

I am in UTG+1 with J9o, facing a raise to 3BB from Minni in UTG. Despite Minni's loose-aggressive image and frequent opening, J9o is a very weak hand to play from early position against an UTG open. It is easily dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, ATs, K9s, etc. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me who could squeeze or wake up with a premium hand. Calling or 3-betting here would be a long-term losing play. The standard and prudent action is to fold.

T6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has shown aggression but UTG suggests a strong range. On the button with a short stack of 12.5BB, calling 300 into a growing pot commits too much equity without domination potential or implied odds. Raising or shoving is -EV with this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket deuces (22). Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 3x the big blind from UTG in a 5-handed game. While pocket pairs have set-mining value, 22 is the weakest pair and playing it out of position against a loose-aggressive chip leader is difficult. Furthermore, Claude in the Big Blind has a relatively short stack (35.5 BB) and may choose to shove or squeeze, forcing me off my hand. Given the stack dynamics and my position, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

KhTh is a solid suited broadway hand in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). Minni has been very active throughout, and I'll have position postflop. This is a clear defend - folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is marginal against UTG. Call and see a flop.

Flopped top two pair (K-T) on Ks-Ts-3s, but the monotone spade board is dangerous since I have no spade. My hand is very strong but vulnerable to any single spade in villain's range. Checking to the preflop aggressor (Minni) is standard here - if they c-bet, I can either check-raise to charge draws or call and evaluate. Don't want to donk bet into this board texture.

I have top pair with the nut kicker (A♣K♣) on K♠T♠3♠ — a very strong hand but the board is draw-heavy with three spades. Claude defended the big blind to my UTG raise, so his range is wide (Kx, Tx, spade draws, some pockets). I want to charge spade draws and get value from worse kings/tens/one-pair hands while keeping the pot manageable against a short-ish stack, so I make a standard continuation bet of roughly 1/2 the pot (325 into 650). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (protect/continue vs draws, pot-control vs suspicious action); if he check-raises big or shoves I’ll re-assess but will give strong consideration to calling a shove given his range and the pot odds.

Top two pair on monotone spade board. Very strong but vulnerable. Minni's 325 bet (50% pot) is a standard c-bet - could be a flush, flush draw, or just continuation betting. I should raise to protect against any single spade draw and get value from worse hands. If they have the flush already, they'll likely 3-bet and I can fold. Raising to ~900 charges draws appropriately.

I have top pair with the nut kicker (A♣K♣) on a monotone K♠T♠3♠ board. Claude’s large check-raise to 900 is polarizing — it contains two-spade flushes and sets, but also some bluffs and weaker Kx from a BB defending range. Calling the extra 575 (total 900 committed this street) is cheap relative to my 26,875 stack and the pot (1,875) and keeps worse hands and bluffs in while controlling the pot size against made flushes. Raising to 1,475 or shoving would bloat the pot and risk committing with a hand that is behind many of Claude’s credible holdings. I’ll call, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn (fold to decisive spade turn action or large shove; continue extracting value vs passive lines).

Turn brought the 9c - a brick that doesn't complete any draws. Minni just called my flop raise rather than 3-betting, which makes a made flush less likely. Their range is probably weighted toward flush draws with a single spade, pair+draw combos, or possibly a set. With top two pair, pot is 2450 and I have 2450 behind (SPR ~1). I should bet for value and protection - around half pot to charge draws while keeping them in with worse hands. If they raise, I'm pot-committed anyway with this hand strength.

I have top pair with the nut kicker (A♣K♣) on K♠ T♠ 3♠ 9♣. Claude's line (flop check-raise then a big turn bet) is polarizing — it does include spade flushes and sets, but also worse Kx, Tx combos and bluffs. The pot is 3,650 and I'm being asked 1,200 (~3:1 pot odds); Claude is effectively pot-committing with a short stack (~1,250), so calling is low-risk and the +EV play. Raising would bloat the pot into many hands that already beat me (flushes) and folding is too tight given the odds and his wide defending range. I will call 1,200 to keep worse hands and bluffs in and re-evaluate on the river (be cautious if a spade appears or he shows further extreme aggression).

River Kd gives me Kings full of Tens - a monster! I went from being vulnerable to flushes to having the third nuts. Minni called flop raise and turn bet, likely holding a flush, flush draw, trips, or pair+draw. Now any flush loses to my boat. With only 1250 left into a 4850 pot, I should jam for value - any flush/trips/two pair will likely call this small river bet.

I’ve rivered trips (K♣ with Ks and Kd on board) — a very strong hand. The only realistic hands that beat me are a spade flush (would require two spades in Claude’s hole cards) or a full house/quads from pocket tens/treys (or the rare quads). Claude’s line (flop check-raise, turn bet, small river shove) is polar, but his river amount is small (1,250 into a 6,100 pot) and his stack shows he’s effectively all-in. Folding is too tight given my hand and the pot odds; raising is pointless here because he can’t call more (no other players are left to build a side pot). Calling 1,250 is low-cost, gets value from worse Kx/Tx and bluffs, and forces a showdown against his polarized range. I will call.

I am in UTG (effectively Hijack in 5-handed play) with 98s. While this is a candidate for a loose open in a deep-stacked game, the player to my immediate left (Elon) has a short stack of 12.5 BB. This is a prime stack depth for reshoving over an open. If I raise and Elon jams, I would be put in a difficult spot, likely having to fold or flip for a chunk of chips with marginal equity. Given the stack dynamics and position, folding is the disciplined, low-variance play to preserve my healthy stack.

73o is one of the worst possible starting hands, completely uncoordinated and offsuit. In UTG+1 position with 5 active players remaining, this is a clear fold regardless of short stack (12.5BB). No steal equity, dominated by any calling range. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 6d 5c. This is a speculative hand, but I am in position. The action has folded to me. My stack is healthy at 14750. However, the blinds, Claude and Minni, have been active. In this tournament format where I want to maximize my stack over 150 hands, standard button play would suggest a raise or a fold. With 65 offsuit, it's generally a fold even from the button against observant opponents, but given the circumstances and the desire to steal the blinds, a raise is possible. However, looking at Hand #76, both Claude and Minni were willing to commit significant chips. I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to maintain my stack.

Folded to me in SB with K2o against BB. This is a heads-up spot where K2o is playable. Minni has been aggressive throughout. I'll raise to try to take down the blinds or play in position-neutral HU pot with a king-high hand. Raising to 200 is standard.

Claude opened to 200 from the small blind; the pot is 300 and I only need 100 more to call (200 total committed). 6♣8♥ is marginal but has enough post‑flop playability and the price is good — calling requires ~25% equity to break even. I don’t want to 3‑bet bluff out of position with a weak, unsuited hand and risk bloating the pot; instead I’ll defend the big blind and see a flop. Postflop plan: fold to heavy, well‑sized pressure on dry high‑card boards; continue/raise when I connect (pair + redraws or strong draws) and exploit Claude’s likely wide SB opening range.

Monotone heart board (9h Th 4h) and I have K2 with no hearts - completely missed. No pair, no draw. This is a terrible spot to c-bet into Minni who defends wide from BB. Any heart gives them a flush draw, and T9 are common BB defend hands. Checking and giving up is the best play here.

I check. Claude (SB) checked to me on a 9♥-10♥-4♥ flop; the pot is 400 and there is nothing to call. I hold 6♣8♥ — a very playable drawing hand: I have a four‑card heart draw (I hold 8♥ with three hearts on board) plus a gutshot to a 7. Roughly 12 outs (~45% to improve by the river). Because I’m in position and there’s no bet, betting now would bloat the pot with only drawing equity and will get called by hands that contain higher hearts (which would dominate my flush) or by made pairs. Checking preserves equity for free, keeps the pot controllable against a shorter-stacked SB (7150) and lets me apply pressure or extract value on a favorable turn. Plan: see the turn for free; if I hit a flush/straight or a safe pair I’ll bet/raise for value, if I miss I’ll fold to heavy, well‑sized aggression but can consider a delayed semi‑bluff on a harmless turn card.

Turn is 6s which changes nothing for me. Still have K-high with no hearts on a 3-heart board. Minni checked behind on flop - could be weak or slow-playing a heart. No reason to bluff here with zero equity. Check and fold to any bet.

I check. On the turn I have 6♣8♥ on 9♥-10♥-4♥-6♠: that gives me a pair of sixes plus a four‑card heart flush draw (and a 7 gives me a straight). Unique river outs are ~13 (9 hearts + three non‑heart 7s + the one non‑heart 6) ≈ 28% to improve. Claude checked both flop and turn, showing weakness (could be a missed overcard, a small pair, or a capped SB wide-opening range) so I don’t need to inflate the pot out of position with only a marginal pair + draw. Checking preserves fold equity, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me realize equity with one card to come. Plan: if I hit the flush/two‑pair/trips on the river I will bet/raise for value; if the river is a blank and Claude bets big I’ll fold to strong pressure, but I can consider a small delayed semi‑bluff on a harmless river or a small bluff if he checks and the river improves my representable range.

River pairs the tens. I have K-high with no hearts. Minni checked flop and turn, likely weak or showdown value. Board is now 9h Th 4h 6s Tc. If I bet, I only get called by better hands. K-high has some showdown value against Minni's weakest holdings like missed draws. Checking is best - might win against complete air at showdown.

Claude opened from the SB preflop then checked flop and turn — a very passive line that usually represents a missed overcard, a small pair, or a capped wide opening range. I hold 6c8h: I paired my sixes on the turn but missed my heart draw on the river (Tc). The river also paired the board (two Tens), which increases the number of hands that now beat me (any Ten, any pair >6). With the hand as a lone small pair I have thin showdown value — I only beat pure air/overcards and some bluffs. A river bluff here would need to fold out a large portion of Claude’s checking range to be profitable, but many of his check-back combos (weak pairs, any Ten, some sticky overcards) will simply call a small bet, so my fold equity is low and the risk of being called by better hands is high. Pot is small (400) relative to stacks, so the lowest-variance +EV play is to take the showdown rather than bluff into a passive opponent. Action: check to reveal; if Claude then leads a polarized, large sizing I’ll fold, but I won’t c-bet the river as a bluff.

65o is a trash hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. Early position requires a tight range. My stack is 12.5BB, short but not desperate; shoving trash has poor equity against calling ranges of aggressive opponents like seen in history (e.g., Minni, Claude, Flash often 3-bet/raise light). Folding preserves stack for better spots like premiums or suited connectors in position. Pot odds poor for limp/call (100 into 250 effective ~40% odds needed post-flop with dominated hand). Standard fold to maximize EV long-term.

In a 5-max game, being UTG+1 (effectively the Cutoff position), 5s 4s is a marginal opening hand. While it is often an open in standard GTO ranges, the table dynamic suggests that both Minni (Small Blind) and Pro (Big Blind) are quite active and aggressive, which reduces the fold equity of a pre-flop raise. Additionally, Minni's large stack allows them to put significant pressure on speculative hands post-flop. Given that I have a healthy stack of 14,750 (147.5 BB) and we are about halfway through the hand limit, I prefer to fold this marginal suited connector and wait for a better spot rather than potentially playing a bloated pot out of position or facing a 3-bet.
KQo on the button is a strong hand. With Elon and Flash already folded, I'm in excellent position to raise and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot in position. Standard raise to 2.5x BB.

Facing a 250 button open from the BTN while I’m in the small blind with Th3s (offsuit, weak and frequently dominated). I’d be out of position postflop and the big blind still has to act — calling 200 more to play OOP multiway is a marginal defense with poor playability. A light 3‑bet is too thin here (and risks a 4‑bet or a difficult spot OOP). With a 24k stack and plenty of hands left, I’ll conserve chips and fold this weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad-2s facing a 2.5x raise from Claude on the Button. The pot is currently 400 chips (250 from Claude + 50 from SB + 100 from me), and I need to call 150 more. This gives me pot odds of 150/550, or approximately 27%. Against a typical wide Button opening range, A2o has roughly 45-50% equity. While being out of position makes it harder to realize this equity, the price is too good to fold an Ace. A 3-bet is possible as a bluff, but flat calling to close the action and see a flop is the standard, lower-variance play with this specific hand strength.

I hold Ad-2s on a flop of 2c-7s-Qh. I have bottom pair with the top kicker. The board is rainbow and relatively dry. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, Claude (Button). Leading out (donk betting) is generally not optimal here as it folds out bluffs and isolates me against better hands. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If Claude bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but checking is the clear first move to induce bluffs or see a free turn.

I flopped top pair top kicker (Queens with King kicker) on a dry 2-7-Q board. Pro checked to me. This is a clear value betting spot - I should bet to extract value from weaker queens, pocket pairs below Q, and any draws. Betting around 60% pot.

I hold Ad-2s on a Q-7-2 rainbow board. I have bottom pair with the top kicker. I checked to the pre-flop raiser (Claude), who bet 325 into a pot of 550. This is a standard continuation bet on a dry board, which Claude would make with a wide range including Qx, pocket pairs, 7x, and many air hands or draws (like straight draws or just overcards). Given the Button vs Big Blind dynamic, Claude's range is wide enough that my bottom pair is ahead of his bluffs (King-high, Jack-high, etc.). Folding here would be too tight as I need to defend some portion of my range on this board, and A2 is near the top of my 'weak pair' range. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling allows me to keep his bluffs in the pot and re-evaluate on the turn. I am getting decent pot odds to call.

I hold Ad-2s on a board of 2c-7s-Qh-6s. I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. The turn card (6s) brings a second spade and connects some straight draws (89 now makes a straight, 45 makes a straight). As the pre-flop caller out of position, I checked and called a c-bet on the flop. Leading out on the turn (donk betting) is generally suboptimal with a weak made hand, as it likely only gets called by better hands (better pairs, strong draws) and folds out the bluffs I am currently beating. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff. Therefore, checking is the standard play to see if the opponent continues their aggression or takes a free card.

I have top pair top kicker on a 2-7-Q-6 board with a spade flush draw now possible. Pro called my flop bet and checked again. I should continue betting for value - my hand is strong and I want to charge flush draws and extract value from weaker queens or pocket pairs. Betting around 65% pot.

I hold Ad-2s on a board of 2c-7s-Qh-6s. I have bottom pair with top kicker. After check-calling the flop, I am facing a second barrel from Claude (Button) of 775 into a pot of 1975. The turn card (6s) is significant as it completes potential straights (4-5, 8-9) and brings a flush draw. A double barrel on this card usually signifies a hand that has improved or is semi-bluffing with strong equity (like a flush draw + pair or straight draw). My bottom pair is currently losing to any Queen, Seven, Six, or pocket pair higher than 22. While I beat pure bluffs, I am out of position and facing aggression that represents strength. The price (calling 775 to win ~2750, ~22% pot odds) is tempting, but the reverse implied odds are poor, and I likely face a difficult decision on the river. My hand is near the bottom of my continuing range, and I have many better hands to defend with (Qx, 7x, spade draws). Therefore, folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the UTG position in a 5-handed game holding Ace-Seven offsuit. While this is on the lower end of an opening range, in a short-handed game, it is typically strong enough to raise and attempt to take control of the pot. My stack is healthy at over 140 big blinds, allowing me to play aggressively. Elon is very short-stacked (11.5 BB), so I must be prepared for a potential shove from him, which I would likely have the odds to call if I've already committed chips. I will open for 2.5x the big blind.
A3o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. Out of position with poor playability and a weak kicker. Not worth calling or 3-betting here - clear fold.

Fold. Facing an UTG open to 250 from Flash, Q♦4♣ on the button is a weak, often-dominated holding with poor post‑flop playability. Calling 250 into a 400 pot gives ~38% pot odds but my equity versus an UTG opening range is generally lower than that; a minimal 3‑bet to 400 is a thin bluff and invites a squeeze from the SB (Pro) or a shove/call from the short BB (Elon). With a deep stack I should wait for a better, playable spot on the button rather than get involved out of position against a strong opener.

I hold Ten-Seven offsuit in the Small Blind facing a raise to 250 from Flash in UTG. This is a very weak hand, particularly against an early position open which typically indicates a strong range. Being out of position and with a hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, calling is not profitable. The short-stacked Big Blind behind me also adds a risk of a squeeze jam. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Preflop in BB with 57o vs UTG raise to 2.5BB from Flash, a competent player based on history. Hand is offsuit, poorly connected, low equity (~25-30%) against a strong UTG range (pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways/connectors). Stack is 11.5BB, short but not desperate; shoving 57o is -EV as it's dominated often and poor postflop. Calling 1.5BB into 4BB pot offers ~2.7:1 but hand plays poorly OOP with bad equity realization. Best to fold and wait for better spot to ladder up.
K5o is too weak to open from UTG. Poor hand strength and bad position with 4 players behind me. Easy fold.

Ks3c is a weak, unsuited king with a very poor kicker and very little postflop playability from early position (UTG+1). With four players left to act (including a short‑stack SB who can shove and deeper players who 3‑bet), opening here risks getting dominated or forced into a bad all‑in. Calling 100 leads to a multiway, out‑of‑position pot with a marginal hand; raising to 200 invites pressure. Preserve a healthy 240BB stack and fold preflop to wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Jd 5d. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong stealing position, J5s is a marginal hand. The Small Blind (Elon) is sitting on a short stack of 11 BBs and might be looking for a spot to shove all-in. If he does, I would have to fold, wasting my opening bet. The Big Blind is deep-stacked and might defend widely. Without antes to increase the pot odds for stealing, and given the risk of a re-raise from the short stack, folding is the prudent play.

Short stack with ~11BB (1100 chips). Hand is 6s2s: suited but extremely weak, low cards, poor postflop playability, dominated often. Folded to SB after UTG folds, Pro folds button (possibly passive or strong). Calling 50 into 150 pot commits ~5% stack to a speculative hand unlikely to improve enough vs BB defense. Raising/shoving too thin for this trash hand. Discipline: fold weak hands, wait for better spots (premiums, suited aces/connectors) to ladder up. Maximizes EV in tournament.

4♣3♦ (4c3d) is a very weak hand to open from UTG in a five-handed game. It's offsuit with minimal straight/flush potential and will be dominated by most players' raising ranges. There's 100 to call (min-raise to 200 available) and I'll be out of position versus the button and blinds — the table has been aggressive with many 250–300+ opens, so this becomes a difficult postflop spot with poor equity. With a deep but finite stack, preserving chips and waiting for a playable hand in position is the best long-term strategy. Fold.

5-handed play, acting as Cutoff effectively after UTG fold. Hand QJo is marginal. With a short stack (Elon, 11BB) on the button who is liable to shove, opening creates a high-variance spot where I might be priced in to call off a significant chunk of chips with a likely dominated or flipping hand. My stack is healthy (120BB+), so preserving it by avoiding marginal spots against desperate short stacks is prudent. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Short stack with 11BB on the button in an unopened pot (folded to me preflop). Qh4h is a suited queen-high hand with straight and flush potential, playable for a shove from this position. Excellent fold equity against deep-stacked blinds who won't call light vs a button jam. Shoving maximizes pressure, steals blinds frequently, or sets up a double-up spot if called. Limping or small raise risks domination or multiway pot as short stack; fold too tight given M ~11.

I have Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. The Button (Elon) has moved all-in for 1100 chips (11 big blinds). To maximize my expected value, I should raise to isolate Elon's short-stack range and discourage the Big Blind (Claude) from entering the pot with a speculative hand. A raise to 3100 total is appropriate to either take the pot heads-up against Elon or put Claude to a difficult decision.

AQs is a premium hand facing a 3-bet. With my stack of 7675 and 3000 to call, I'm in an awkward SPR spot if I flat - only ~4675 behind into a ~7300 pot. Given effective stack depth (~77BB starting), AQs plays best as a 4-bet shove here. I'm ahead of Flash's 3-bet bluffs and have good equity against their value range. Shoving puts maximum pressure and I'm committed regardless if I put in the min-raise.

I have Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand. After my initial 3-bet to 3100, Claude has 4-bet to 7675, leaving himself with only 100 chips. Elon is already all-in. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.6 to 1) and the strength of AKo, I cannot fold here. I will raise to 7775 to put Claude all-in as well and take the hand to a showdown, as I have significant equity against most of their ranges and am already heavily committed to the pot.

I'm already all-in for 7575 chips with AQs. I only have 100 chips remaining and need to call 100 to complete. This is automatic - I've already committed my stack with a premium hand. Folding 100 into a 16550 pot would be absurd.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit. In a three-handed game, any King on the button is a strong candidate for an opening raise to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy, and while Pro (Big Blind) has a shorter stack of about 120 big blinds, I still have plenty of room to play post-flop. I'll stick to my standard opening size of 2.5x to try and take the pot down now or play a controlled pot in position.

Three-handed, button opened to 250 — a wide range in this spot. T7o is marginal but has reasonable equity vs a button open and calling costs only 200 more (I will have 250 total in the pot including my small blind). I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with this hand (a min-raise to 400 is too small to fold many hands and a larger 3-bet is unnecessary here). I’ll call to see a flop, fold to a significant 3-bet preflop, and play cautiously OOP postflop — continue with top pair or strong draws, otherwise check-fold vs heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button (Flash) to 250 and a call from the Small Blind (Minni). The pot is currently 600, and I need to call 150 to see the flop, offering me 4:1 pot odds (20% equity required). However, my hand (Qd 4s) is very weak (unsuited, poor kicker, low connectivity). In a 3-way pot, sitting in the middle position post-flop (SB acts, then BB, then BTN), realizing equity with Q4o is extremely difficult. While the pot odds are tempting, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make this a losing call in the long run. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

Flop is 7♦ J♣ 4♥ and my hand T♥7♣: I have one pair (sevens) with a mediocre 10 kicker — a marginal but real made hand. I’m out of position against the button who opened a wide range and will c-bet frequently. The board is fairly dry with an overcard (J) that beats me often, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a thin value-bet. Checking controls the pot size, lets me induce the opponent to bluff/c-bet, and keeps my options open. Plan: check and call reasonable (small-to-medium) continuation bets to realize equity; fold to big check-raises or polarizing large bets. If checked to, I can take a free turn and re-evaluate on the turn (bet small for value/bluff-catch or check back depending on the card).

The flop is dry and disconnected (7d-Jc-4h). As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. Minni checked to me, and while King-high has some showdown value, a bet here can fold out overcards, small connectors that missed, or potentially some weak pocket pairs. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain pressure and take control of the hand.

I have T7o on 7♦ J♣ 4♥ — a made middle pair with a 10 kicker. Button opened wide preflop and is betting 300 into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot), which represents a very wide range that includes lots of air and many hands I currently beat. Calling is the right, pot-control play OOP: it gets good odds (I need ~20% equity to call 300 to win ~1500), keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. I avoid raising because my kicker is weak and raising OOP would bloat the pot against hands (Jx, overcards) that dominate me. Plan: call the 300, fold to a large check-raise or polarized shove, and if we're checked to on the turn consider a small thin-value bet or check back depending on the card.

I have T7o (middle pair with a weak ten-kicker) on 7♦ J♣ 4♥ 5♠ and I'm out of position versus a button who opened wide and barreled the flop. The 5♠ is essentially a blank relative to my hand — it doesn't complete many obvious straights or flushes — so my pair is still a marginal but real hand. Betting OOP for thin value will get called by many better hands (Jx, Kx, Ax) and can inflate the pot unnecessarily; checking controls the pot, keeps bluffs and worse pairs in, and lets me see how the button reacts. Plan: check and call a small-to-medium bet on the turn if they stab; fold to a large polarized raise/shove. If checked to on later streets I’ll re-evaluate (small thin value bet on a safe river or check back to reach showdown).

The turn card is the 5s, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (4-5-6-7, needing a 3 or an 8). Minni has checked to me for a second time after calling my flop continuation bet. My King-high currently has some showdown value against her missed draws or lower equity hands, but it is unlikely to fold out a Jack or a strong 7 with another bet. Given that I have some equity to improve and want to see a cheap river, checking back is a safe, high-percentage play to control the pot size while retaining the possibility of winning at showdown or hitting my straight.

I have T7o (pair of 7s, 10 kicker) on 7♦ J♣ 4♥ 5♠ 8♣. Button opened wide preflop, c-bet a small 1/3-pot on the flop and then checked the turn — consistent with a lot of air/overcards and some pot-control Jx/medium pairs. The river 8♣ is a relatively safe card for my hand (it does give some 6x straights and pairs up hands with an 8), but it didn’t put a scary coordinated texture on the board. My pair is real but marginal: it beats many missed overcards and bluffs in his capped checking range, yet loses to Jx, 8x, 66 (straight) and stronger pairs/overpairs. Pot is only 1,200 and stacks are deep; betting here risks bloating the pot with a weak kicker and can get raised by hands that already beat me. Checking to showdown is the lowest-variance, +EV line — I’ll check and take a showdown. If villain bets a small amount I’ll call for value vs worse and fold to a large polarized raise/shove.

The river 8c completes a straight for me (4-5-6-7-8). After checking back on the turn, the 8 is a perfect card to improve my hand. Minni has checked to me for the third time in this hand, which often indicates a marginal made hand like a Jack or a Seven that is looking to go to showdown cheaply. Since I now hold a very strong hand, I will place a value bet of 800 into the 1200 pot. This sizing is designed to get called by any Jack or potentially a Seven, while also looking somewhat like a bluff from a player who checked the turn and is now trying to take the pot away on a straight-completing card.

Flash (button) opened wide preflop, c‑bet small on the flop, checked the turn and now puts in a smallish river bet (800 into 2000). That line is very consistent with a capped, wide range containing lots of missed overcards and bluffs plus some pot‑controlled Jx — my T7o (pair of 7s) beats many of those hands. Pot odds to call are 800/(2000+800) ≈ 28.6%. Given villain’s line I expect my hand to have at least that equity vs his betting range. Raising is unattractive (weak kicker, will get called or crushed by Jx/stronger pairs) and folding surrenders too many worse hands. I will call to reach showdown and fold only to a large raise/shove.

I have pocket kings on the button in a three-handed pot — the best non‑Ace starting hand. With pot 150 and only 100 to call I want to build the pot and isolate the blinds while charging speculative hands, so I raise to 4x the big blind (400 total). This sizing is large enough to fold out the weakest holdings and deny free cards, but small relative to the deep stacks (my 22,650; SB Pro 12,025; BB Flash 25,175) so worse hands will call and I can extract value postflop. If I face a sizable 3‑bet I will re-evaluate (likely call to see a flop heads‑up or 4‑bet versus a polarized shove); postflop I plan to c‑bet for value/protection and proceed cautiously if an Ace hits.

I am in the Small Blind with K2s facing a 4x raise (400) from the Button (Minni). Although Minni is an aggressive player who opens frequently, the 4BB sizing is larger than the typical 3BB open seen in previous hands, which makes the price to call worse (need 39% equity) and potentially signals a stronger hand. Playing K2s out of position against a strong range is difficult; if I hit a King, I am often dominated, and flush draws are hard to play aggressively OOP. Given the stack depth and the poor price/position combination, folding is the most positive EV decision.

I am in the big blind with 9c 6s. Minni has raised to 400 from the button, and the small blind has folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (300 to win 550), 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Defending this hand against a 4x open from an aggressive opponent like Minni is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am often dominated or playing with minimal equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 7d Tc. This is a weak off-suit hand (T7o). With 3 active players, ranges are wider, but T7o is still below the threshold for a profitable open-raise, especially against two large stacks in the blinds who may defend aggressively. My stack is healthy at 120 BBs, so there is no need to force action with marginal holdings. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Js 4s, a suited hand with a high card. Since the Button has folded, it is a blind-vs-blind situation against Minni. Standard strategy in this spot is to play a wide range of hands to put pressure on the Big Blind or to see a flop. Raising to 3x the big blind is a common steal attempt that allows me to take down the pot immediately or maintain the lead heading into the flop. If Minni calls, I have a hand that can flop flush draws or a pair of Jacks, which is often strong enough in a head-up pot.

I’m in the big blind with Ah3c facing a 300 open from the small blind. It’s cheap to defend (200 more to call into a 400 pot) and heads‑up I want to see flops with a nut‑Ace even though the kicker is weak. Flash has been opening wide and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3‑bet — flat‑calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me evaluate the flop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy continued aggression on dry boards, and continue for value or apply pressure if I pair the Ace or pick up a strong draw.

I have flopped top pair with a flush draw, which is an exceptionally strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. My goal is to build the pot for value while also protecting my hand against potential straight draws or overcards. A standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) is appropriate here to get called by weaker jacks, middle pairs, or draws while maintaining my range aggression from pre-flop.

Flash opened wide from the SB and fired a small 300 c-bet into a 600 pot. I have A♥3♣ — an Ace-high with a very weak kicker, no pair and no meaningful draw on J♥6♠5♠ (there are two spades on board, so I’m susceptible to spade draws and to any Jx). Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand that’s often behind; calling is cheap (300 into a 900 pot) and lets me see a turn card where I can continue if I pair the Ace, hit a 3, or pick up a safer line. I will call 300 now, keep the pot manageable, and fold to large continued aggression or re-evaluate on the turn for value/bluff opportunities.

On the turn, the 2nd of diamonds is largely a blank that shouldn't have improved Minni's range significantly. I still hold top pair with a jack and have a nut flush draw, making this an extremely strong hand in a head-up pot. I will continue to bet for value to get called by weaker jacks, middle pairs (6x, 5x), or straight draws. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) allows me to build the pot and charge any draws Minni might be holding while maintaining my aggressive image.

I called the flop to see a turn and keep the pot manageable against Flash’s wide SB opening. The turn 2♦ is a blank for my A♥3♣ — I have only Ace-high, no spade and no real redraw. Flash upsizes from 300 to 800 into roughly 1,200, so calling 800 to win ~2,000 requires ≈40% equity. I only have ~6 outs to a pair (3 Aces + 3 treys → ~13% to improve to a pair by the river), and many of those pair-outs still lose to a likely Jx/6x/5x or spade combo he can reasonably have. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: I’m out of position, have poor blockers to spade combos, and would often be bloating the pot with no fold equity vs his value-heavy line. Preserve the stack and fold.

On the button in a 3-handed game, T9o is a standard opening hand. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and leverage position throughout the hand. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x opening size to put pressure on the blinds.

3♣6♥ is a very weak, poorly connected offsuit hand and is heavily dominated by a typical BTN open range to 300. Pot is 450 and I must call 250, which gives me ~35.7% pot odds — well above the equity this hand has vs an open. I’ll be out of position postflop and the BB still acts behind, so calling or a light 3-bet is just throwing chips away or creating marginal spots. Preserve my deep stack and fold here.

I hold [Ah Qc] in the Big Blind, a premium hand in 3-handed play. The Button (Flash), who is the chip leader and active, opens to 300. With 120 BB effective stacks, 3-betting is a viable option for value, but doing so out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent risks facing a 4-bet or playing a bloated pot with a hand that can be tricky post-flop. Flat calling disguises my hand strength, keeps the opponent's range wide (including many dominated hands like AJ, AT, KQ), and offers excellent pot odds. I decide to call to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a strong kicker (Queen) on an A-9-2 board with two clubs. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash on the Button) to see their action. I have a very strong hand that can comfortably call a continuation bet or bet for value on later streets if checked back. Leading out (donk betting) here is unnecessary and unbalanced.

On this Ace-high flop, I have flopped middle pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker. While my hand has some value, betting into an Ace-high board often results in getting called by better (any Ace) and folding out hands I currently beat. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, protect my checking range, and allow myself to realize my equity. This also serves to induce bluffs from the Big Blind on later streets if they perceive my check as weakness.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a strong Queen kicker on an A-9-2-9 board. The turn card (9h) pairs the board. My opponent (Flash) checked back the flop, which often indicates a capped range (weak Ace, mid-pair, or air), though a slow-played monster or trip nines are possible but less likely given pre-flop and flop action. 

Strategic options:
1. Bet: Betting denies equity to draws and gets value from weak Aces or pocket pairs that might call a small bet. However, given the Ace on the board, worse hands often fold, and better hands (Trip 9s, Boats) will raise.
2. Check: Checking is consistent with my flop action and controls the pot size. It allows Flash to bluff if they interpret the check as weakness or to value bet a worse hand like a weak Ace. It also avoids getting raised off my equity.

Decision: I will check. My hand is strong enough to call a bet, and checking keeps Flash's range wider, potentially inducing bluffs or allowing them to catch up slightly to pay off on the river. I want to get to showdown efficiently or catch a bluff.

On the turn, the nine of hearts completes my three-of-a-kind. Having checked the flop, I have successfully disguised the strength of my hand. My opponent has checked twice, indicating they likely do not have a strong Ace or have a hand they are looking to take to showdown cheaply. Now is the time to start building the pot for value. I expect to be called by any Ace, small pairs, or club flush draws. A bet of 400 into the 650 pot (approx. 60% pot) is a standard size to generate value without scaring off marginal hands.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on a paired board (A-9-2-9). My opponent checked back the flop, which usually signals weakness or a medium-strength hand, rather than a monster, especially on an Ace-high board with a flush draw where they would likely bet for protection or value. Their turn bet of 400 into 650 after the board pairs could represent a 9, but it is also consistent with a delayed c-bet with a worse Ace, a pocket pair like KK/QQ, or a bluff. Raising here would likely fold out their bluffs and isolate me against trips or full houses. Calling keeps their range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands on the river. My hand is too strong to fold but vulnerable enough to a 3-bet/jam that calling is the optimal play to control the pot and realize equity.

On the River, the 7c completes the flush draw, making the board [9d 2c Ac 9h 7c]. I hold [Ah Qc] (Top Pair, Top Kicker essentially, as AK is unlikely given pre-flop/flop action). The opponent (Flash) checked back the flop, which typically caps their range and makes a flush less likely (as draws often c-bet). Their turn bet was small. Leading out here (donk betting) is unattractive because worse hands (like weak Aces or pocket pairs) likely fold on this scary runout, and better hands (flushes, trips) will just call or raise. Checking is the best option to induce bluffs from missed draws or to get to showdown cheaply against a marginal value hand like a weak Ace that might bet small or check back.

On the river, I have three-of-a-kind (Nines) with a Ten kicker. The board is now 9-2-A-9-7 with three clubs. After checking the flop to disguise my hand and betting the turn for value when the second nine appeared, I am in a prime position to get one last street of value. Pro's check-call on the turn suggests they likely have a marginal made hand, most probably an Ace or a pocket pair, or perhaps a draw that just missed. While the river 7c completes a backdoor flush, the likelihood of Pro having exactly two clubs and calling the turn bet on a paired board is relatively low compared to the frequency of them holding an Ace. I will place a value bet of 1,000 chips into the 1,450 pot (approximately 70% pot). This size is large enough to maximize profit from an Ace while still being a price that most players at this level will pay with top pair.

The opponent (Flash) checked back the flop on an A-9-2 board, which caps their range and makes strong hands like strong Aces or sets less likely, as those typically bet for value or protection. The turn paired the board (9h) and they made a small bet, which I called. The river brought a third club (7c), completing a potential flush. However, I hold the Qc, which is a significant blocker to the flush combos my opponent could have (e.g., they cannot have QcJc, QcTc). Additionally, the Ac is on the board, so they cannot have the nut flush. The opponent's line of checking the flop and betting turn/river is consistent with a trip 9, a rivered flush, or thin value with a worse Ace (like AJ/AT), but also contains bluffs.
