Given the pot odds (calling 1000 to win 3450, ~29% equity needed) and my strong hand strength (Top Pair, strong kicker) combined with the flush blocker, I beat enough of their value range (worse Aces) and catch enough bluffs to make a profitable call.

On the button with A♣T♥, deep stacks (~221 BB) and two blinds to steal — this is a standard open-raise spot. ATo is strong enough to raise for value and fold equity; I don’t want to just limp and give the blinds cheap flops. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total) — big enough to discourage easy calls and define the pot size, but small enough to keep weaker hands and suited connectors in for value. If I face a small 3-bet I’ll continue selectively; against a large 3-bet I’ll fold to preserve my deep stack.

I am in the Small Blind with [Ad 5d], facing a Button raise to 300 from Minni. A5s is a strong hand that plays well post-flop due to flush and straight connectivity, as well as the Ace blocker. In a 3-handed game, this is well within the defending range against a Button open. While a 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, previous hands suggest Minni can be sticky post-flop, making a 3-bet less desirable as I would likely get called and have to play a bloated pot out of position. Flat calling keeps the pot manageable, keeps Minni's range wide (including dominated hands), and offers good implied odds. I will call the additional 250 chips to match the 300 bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 73 suited. Minni opened for 3x from the button and Pro called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting favorable pot odds to see a flop (200 to call into a pot of 700), 73s is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Given my significant chip lead, I want to avoid high-variance, marginal situations where I'm likely dominated. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I have Ace-high with backdoor straight and flush potential on a low, connected board (8h 7c 3h). Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni) on a board that likely connects with their range (sets, overpairs, straight draws, flush draws), leading out is not profitable. My hand has insufficient equity to build a pot, but enough showdown value to check and see what the opponent does. The standard line is to check to the aggressor. If Minni bets, I will likely fold as I lack immediate draws or a pair.

I was the preflop aggressor and hold A♣T♥ on 8♥-7♣-3♥. I have two overcards and a backdoor heart-runner-runner flush possibility, and the SB checked to me — a standard continuation bet here is the right play to leverage fold equity and deny free cards. A half-pot c-bet (≈350 into the 700 pot) will fold out many Kx, worse Ax hands and speculative callers while keeping the pot controlled if called. If Pro responds with a significant raise I’ll respect that and fold since I have no pair; if he simply calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn with additional information.

On the flop of 8h-7c-3h, I hold Ace-high (Ad5d) with no immediate straight or flush draws (backdoor straight possibilities only). Minni bets half-pot (350 into 700). Being out of position with a weak hand on a wet board that favors the aggressor's range or connects well with it, and lacking strong equity or implied odds to continue, checking and folding to a bet is the standard play. I cannot profitably call 350 chips with just Ace-high and poor playability on future streets.

I am holding Ten-Six offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. Despite the positional advantage, T6o is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. With 100BB deep stacks, there is no need to try to steal the blinds with bottom-tier holdings, especially against deep-stacked opponents in the blinds who may defend or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I hold Jack-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a strong starting hand in a blind-versus-blind battle. With the Button folding, I want to apply pressure to the Big Blind and either take the pot down now or play a controlled pot with a range advantage. I will raise to 300.

I’m the big blind with K♦2♠ facing a raise to 300 from the small blind (to call 200 into a 400 pot = ~33% pot odds). K2o is a very weak hand heads‑up: it’s badly dominated by the opener’s likely range (Ax, Kx, broadways) and has poor post‑flop playability, especially out of position. I don’t have useful blockers for a light 3‑bet bluff and calling commits me to a large SPR with a subpar holding. With deep stacks and only three players left I’ll preserve chips and fold this marginal spot.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (2s, 2h). In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is relatively strong. I'll make a standard 3x open to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. If called, I'll have positional advantage throughout the hand. My goal is to either win the blinds immediately or flop a set for a big payoff.

Call — commit 300 total (add 250). 6c7c is a suited connector with good playability in a 3-handed, deep-stacked spot: small investment for strong implied odds to hit straights and flushes. Flash’s open to 300 is likely fairly wide, so calling to see a flop is preferable to 3-betting out of position with a speculative hand. If the BB (Pro) cold-calls we keep good multiway equity; if I face a sizeable 3-bet I’ll fold and preserve chips. Postflop plan: continue with strong draws or pairs, control the pot with marginal holdings, and try to extract when I hit.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh5h. Flash raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 more, offering me pot odds of 200/900 or roughly 22%. Although I will be out of position relative to the raiser and sandwiched between the two players, J5s has enough equity (likely around 25-28% against wide ranges) to justify a call at this price. The high stack-to-pot ratio allows me to peel and try to hit a flush or strong pair, while easily folding if I miss.

I check. I flopped a marginal made hand — middle pair (6♣7♣ pairing the 6♠) on a fairly dry T♦-6♠-4♥ board. This is a three‑way pot with the original raiser (Flash, wide opening range) and the BB (Pro, calling range). Betting out of position with a medium pair here risks bloating the pot against hands that dominate me (T, bigger pairs) and doesn’t gain much value vs pure overcards. Checking exercises pot control and lets opponents reveal strength. Plan: if Flash (or Pro) fires a small-to-medium c-bet (~1/3–1/2 pot ≈ 300–450) I will call and reevaluate on the turn; I will fold to large-sized aggression or a check-raise because I’m likely behind. If checked to, I’ll see the turn and only start building the pot if I improve (two pair/straight) or the turn is a safe card that I can reasonably represent. The objective is to realize equity, avoid committing lots of chips OOP with a marginal holding, and extract value only when the hand improves.

I hold Jh5h on a flop of 4h Td 6s. I have no pair and no immediate straight or flush draw, only a backdoor flush draw (one heart on board). Being out of position against two opponents, including the pre-flop raiser still to act, leading out is not profitable. My hand has very little equity. The standard play is to check and evaluate based on the action behind me. If faced with a bet, I will likely fold.

The flop of 10-6-4 rainbow is relatively dry and likely missed both the small and big blinds' calling ranges. Both opponents have checked to me, indicating weakness. Although my pocket deuces are weak, a continuation bet of 450 (50% of the pot) should be enough to fold out overcards like KQ, AJ, or QJ which have significant equity against me. If I am called, I can check back on many turn cards to try to realize my slim showdown value or fold to any further aggression.

Call. Flash’s 450 c‑bet in a three‑way pot is a small/medium sizing and I have a marginal but real hand: middle pair (6♣) with some backdoor potential and decent showdown value. Calling 450 more (total committed = 300 preflop + 450 = 750) gets roughly 3:1 pot odds versus the current pot (~1350) so I only need ~25% equity — which my hand has against a wide c‑betting range. Raising OOP with a single pair here would bloat the pot and be reckless; folding surrenders equity too cheaply. Plan: see the turn, fold to large turn aggression if I don’t improve, and start building only with two pair/trips or a clear improvement.

I hold Jh5h on a board of 4h-Td-6s. I checked the flop, Flash bet 450 (half pot), and Minni called. I have no pair, no immediate draw, and only weak backdoor straight and flush potential. With two opponents showing interest in the pot—one betting and one calling—my Jack-high is almost certainly behind and has very little equity to improve. The pot odds of 5:1 are not sufficient to chase runner-runner outs out of position. The correct play is to fold.

Check. I have middle pair (6♣) on 4♥-T♦-6♠-J♣. Flash c-bet the flop and then checked the turn — the Jack is a scary card that improves a lot of his range (Jx, Tx, broadways) and his check looks like pot control or weakness. I only have 5 clean outs (two remaining 6s + three 7s ≈ 11% to improve), so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal made hand. The pot (1,800) is small relative to our stacks, so checking preserves chips, realizes equity, and lets me fold to large aggression or extract value if I improve. Plan: check to the river; if Flash bets the river I’ll call small/medium bets but fold large polarizing shoves — if I hit trips/two‑pair I’ll value-bet/raise.

On this turn (Jack of Clubs), my pocket deuces are very weak and likely behind any hand Minni used to call the flop. Minni's range likely includes 10x hands, middle pairs, or straight draws like 78 or 89. While the Jack might have missed some of those, it also improves others (like JT or QJ). Since I have almost no showdown value but some very slim equity if she is on a draw, checking back is the most prudent play. Betting here would be a low-frequency bluff that is unlikely to fold out a Ten or better. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and see a free river.

Check. I end the river with only a single pair (6s) — no flush or straight — and the preflop raiser’s line (open, flop c‑bet 450, then check turn) followed by a river check is ambiguous: it contains both value hands that beat me (Tx/Jx/Qx, two pair/sets) and lots of missed overcards that will call a bluff or fold. The pot is small (1,800) relative to our deep stacks, and I have little fold equity and poor showdown odds against the likely calling portion of his range. Betting here mostly bluffs into a caller-heavy range and needlessly bloats the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking to showdown preserves chips and realizes my limited equity; if Flash now bets I’ll call small/medium value bets but fold to large, polarized shoves as planned.

On the river, the board reads 4-T-6-J-Q. My pocket deuces have very little showdown value, but they do technically beat any 'high card' hand like missed draws (though the T, J, and Q on the board make it easy for my opponent to have a better hand or have improved). Minni checked the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. This indicates she likely has a marginal made hand (like a Ten) or a missed draw. Since I'm unlikely to fold out a Ten or any better pair with a single bet, and I have minimal showdown value that might occasionally win against a pure bluff/missed draw, the best play is to check behind and see the showdown for free. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me.

I’m on the button with A♢9♣ in a three-handed pot. This is a strong opening spot — good hand and maximum positional advantage. Pot is 150 and the min raise is 200, so I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to take the initiative, gain fold equity against the small blind, and isolate the big blind. Size is big enough to pressure blinds but small relative to our deep stack (23500) so we retain flexibility postflop. If called I’ll play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable flops, control pot size on bad boards); if met with a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate and often fold to heavy aggression out of position.

I am in the Small Blind with [Th 5s], facing a 3bb raise from Minni on the Button. This is a very weak off-suit hand with poor playability and low equity against an opening range. Being out of position makes it even harder to realize any equity. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding, and 3-betting is not viable. Folding is the only reasonable option.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 3BB. While the pot odds for a call are somewhat tempting in a short-handed game, J3o is a extremely weak hand with poor playability and equity. Being out of position against an aggressive opponent like Minni, who has shown willingness to put pressure on post-flop, makes this an easy fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with Jc9c. This is a strong suited connector that falls well within a standard opening range from the Button, especially given the positional advantage. My stack is deep enough (approx. 97 BB) to play post-flop comfortably. Recent table action shows opens around 2.5x-3x. I will raise to 250 (2.5 BB) to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to take the pot down pre-flop or play a pot in position with a hand that has good playability.

I'm in the Small Blind with Q2 suited. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive to call the button raise, Q2s is a very weak hand to play out of position against both the button and potentially the big blind. With a healthy chip lead and 3 players remaining, there's no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I'll wait for better spots.

Facing a button open to 250 with 150 to call and the small blind folded, I have 9♠4♦ — a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability and few blockers. I’ll be out of position postflop versus the button and this hand rarely has the equity or fold-equity to continue profitably. Calling 150 into a ~400 pot requires decent equity (~27%) and 9-4o doesn’t meet that against a standard button opening range; 3-betting light is also undesirable with this junk. Preserve my deep stack for better spots — fold.

In a 3-handed game, the Button should be opening a wide range of hands to apply pressure on the blinds. King-Four offsuit is a marginal but standard opening hand from the Button in this format. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play the hand in position if called by Minni or Pro.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have Td4d — a weak but suited hand with backdoor straight/flush potential. Calling 250 more (300 total committed including my 50 SB) is cheap relative to my 23.5k stack and preserves flexibility postflop. I don’t want to 3-bet light out of position with this holding; if the BB 3-bets to a meaningful size I’ll fold preflop. Plan: flat to see a flop, continue only with strong made hands or robust draws, otherwise check and pot-control or fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with As3s facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win 700, or 3.5:1 immediate odds, 22% equity required). A3s is a strong candidate for defending the blind as it retains equity well against opening ranges and can make strong hands (flushes, straights) in a multi-way pot. A squeeze raise is possible but high variance given the deep stacks and multi-way action. Calling closes the action and allows me to realize equity for a cheap price.

Flop: K♠ 2♦ T♠. I hold T♦4♦ — second pair (pair of tens) with a very weak kicker and only a runner-runner (backdoor) diamond possibility. This is a showdown-capable but vulnerable hand in a three-way pot (button opened, BB called). I'm out of position vs the button, so I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. I will check and evaluate opponents' actions: call small/medium continuation bets for value (they'll include bluffs and worse pairs), but fold to large raises or over-aggressive sizing that indicate a King or better. If action checks through I’ll re-assess the turn and only lead or raise if I improve or get clear fold equity.

I hold the nut flush draw (As 3s) on a Ks 2d Ts board in a multi-way pot. Acting out of position in the Big Blind after the Small Blind checked, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash) on the Button. Leading (donk betting) is an option but checking allows me to see the action, potentially check-raise for value/semi-bluff if the Button bets, or take a free card if it checks through. I have significant equity but no made hand yet, so I will check and react to future aggression.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board with potential flush and straight draws. Since both opponents checked, I will bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws like QJ or two spades. A bet of 500 into a 900 pot is a standard continuation bet size that should get calls from worse kings, tens, or draws.

Flash (button) fired a small c‑bet 500 into a 1400 pot on K♠ 2♦ T♠. I have T♦4♦ — second pair with a very poor kicker and only backdoor diamond equity. In a 3‑way pot out of position my priority is pot control and realizing equity rather than bloating the pot with a vulnerable hand. 500 is a small sizing (~35% pot) so calling is cheap (calling 500 would give me ≈19% break‑even) and keeps worse tens and bluffs in. Raising OOP with this kicker is unnecessary and often a mistake. Plan: call the 500, see the turn, and only commit further if I improve (trips/two‑pair/flush) or get a clear read; fold to large aggression that strongly represents a King/overpair/set.

I hold the nut flush draw (As 3s) on a Ks 2d Ts board. Facing a bet of 500 from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, the pot is 1900 and I need to call 500. This offers me pot odds of roughly 20.8%. My equity with the nut flush draw is significantly higher than this (approx. 36% to hit by the river, ~19% on the next card), making a call clearly profitable. While a check-raise is an option to exert pressure, the Small Blind's call indicates they likely have a made hand (like Kx or Tx) or a strong draw, reducing my fold equity. Being out of position and closing the action, calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity cheaply.

I check. I only have second pair (T♦4♦) with a very weak kicker and no realistic flush draw now — the Q♥ on the turn killed the backdoor diamond possibility. This is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position vs an opener (Flash) who has shown frequent aggression and a passive BB (Pro) who often just calls. With two opponents and a marginal made hand my priority is pot control and showdown value rather than building the pot. Betting here risks getting raised off my hand by Kx/Qx or two‑pair hands and bloating the pot unnecessarily. Plan: check and see how opponents act. If someone fires a small turn bet (roughly ≤ ~1/3–1/2 pot) I will call to keep worse tens and bluffs in. If faced with a large bet or a raise (heavy sizing or shove), I will fold — that sizing strongly represents K/Q/sets/two‑pair that beat me. If action checks through to the river I’ll re‑evaluate and only invest further with improvement or a clear, exploitable read.

On the turn, the board reads Ks 2d Ts Qh. The pot is 2400. I hold As 3s, giving me the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). The Small Blind has checked to me. The Queen of Hearts interacts heavily with the board, completing straights (AJ, J9) and improving two-pair combinations (KQ, QT). As the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, leading out here (donk betting) is unlikely to generate folds from better hands like top pair or two pair, and I do not want to face a raise that would force me off my strong equity. Checking is the standard play to potentially see a free river or evaluate the Button's sizing if they choose to bet. I retain strong drawing potential to the nuts.

The Turn card (Queen of Hearts) is a coordination-heavy card that improves many hands that would have called the flop, such as KQ, QJ, or even J9. My top pair with a weak kicker (4) has significant showdown value, but it is no longer strong enough to bet for value against two opponents on this texture. Betting risks a check-raise from better hands or straights, and checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. If the river is relatively safe, I can look to call a modest bet or check it down to showdown.

I have T♦4♦ — second pair (tens) but a very weak kicker on a K♠ 2♦ T♠ Q♥ 5♥ board. This is a three‑way pot and both opponents checked the turn and river, which suggests neither is eager to build the pot; Flash can be aggressive but he chose pot control here and Pro is passive. Betting now risks getting raised off by Kx/Qx/sets that beat me and would likely fold out worse hands I want value from; a small probe bet may not get many profitable calls. Given the multiway action and my vulnerable holding, pot control and taking it to showdown is the least risky, highest‑EV line. I will check and go to showdown.

I missed my flush draw and hold Ace-high (As 3s) on a Ks 2d Ts Qh 5h board. The pot is 2400. The Small Blind has checked to me. I have virtually zero showdown value against any pair, which is very likely to be held by at least one of my two opponents given the flop action (bet and call). Bluffing is unattractive because I have to get through two players on a board that connects well with calling ranges (Kx, Qx, Tx, Straights). The Turn check-around suggests opponents might have marginal made hands (like weak Kx or Qx) rather than air, which they won't fold easily to a river stab. My best option is to check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if the Button bets, preserving my remaining stack of ~9000 chips.

I have top pair with the lowest possible kicker on a board that is quite coordinated. After betting the flop and being called by both opponents, the turn checked around. On the river, both opponents have checked again. This sequence suggests that neither opponent has a powerhouse hand like a straight or two-pair, as they likely would have bet the river to extract value after the turn was checked. However, my kicker (4) is very weak, meaning I could easily be out-kicked by another King. By checking back, I can safely realize my equity and win the pot against missed draws, Tens, or Queens, while avoiding the risk of a check-raise or being called only by hands that beat me.

Three-handed on the button with pocket deuces and deep stacks (my 22,750 vs SB 8,975 and BB 28,125). Deuces are primarily set-mining hands, but on the button in a 3-handed game I want to use position and some fold equity rather than limp. A raise isolates the field, can take down the blinds right away or create a heads-up pot where my postflop play is easier. I’ll open to 300 (3x the big blind) — bigger than the minimum 200 to avoid giving correct odds cheaply and to discourage light calls/steals, but still small enough to keep postflop maneuverability. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call a small-ish 3-bet to set-mine if the price is right, and proceed cautiously postflop (check/control pot unless I hit a set).

I am holding King-Deuce offsuit (Ks 2h) in the Small Blind. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 (3BB). While I get reasonable pot odds to call, K2o is a very weak hand that suffers significantly from being out of position. It often ends up dominated or unable to realize its equity. Defending this hand against a 3x open is generally a losing play in the long run. Folding preserves my stack of roughly 90BB without taking unnecessary risks.

I am in the Big Blind holding J5o. The Button (Minni) has raised to 3x the big blind. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), J5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. Folding is the standard play here.

I am on the Button with [9c 2s], which is an extremely weak hand (offsuit trash). With 3 players left and being the short stack (approx 90bb, but significantly behind the other two), I should be looking for better spots to open or steal. Opening here risks getting called or 3-bet by the deeper stacks in the blinds, forcing me to fold or play a bloated pot with garbage. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 4s. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). 64 offsuit is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against an active player like Minni. While I could attempt a steal or complete the blind, the most prudent and standard play with this hand is to fold and wait for a better spot, preserving my stack for higher equity situations.

I am on the button with 43 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in the game. While the button is a prime position to steal blinds, 43o has almost no post-flop playability and very little equity if called. Given that Pro is relatively short-stacked in the Big Blind and might be looking to defend or shove, opening with such a weak hand is unnecessarily risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Heads-up from the small blind with K♣6♣ — a suited King that plays well heads-up. I want to take the initiative rather than limp; a minimum raise to 200 isolates the BB, folds out some weaker holdings, and keeps the pot manageable with a deep stack. If Pro folds I scoop the 150; if he calls I’ll c-bet dry flops and play position/postflop pot control vs resistance. Calling is passive and folding is too weak here, so raise to 200 (total).

I am in the Big Blind with KTo against a min-raise from the Small Blind (Minni). This is a standard Blind vs Blind scenario. KTo is a strong hand in this configuration, well above the median strength. I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) to call, needing only 25% equity, while KTo has significantly more against a typical wide SB stealing range. Playing in position post-flop is advantageous. A 3-bet is an option but calling keeps the opponent's range wide and manages pot size. I will call to see a flop in position.

Flop: Kd 9s 5c — I have Kc6c: top pair with a weak kicker and a small backdoor club. I was the preflop aggressor, so a continuation bet is standard to get value from worse Kx, 9x, small pairs and to deny free cards to draws. I’ll bet ~60% of the pot (250 into 400): large enough to charge draws and extract value, small enough versus this opponent’s 8.8k stack to keep worse hands calling. Plan: if called, proceed cautiously on the turn; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll heavily discount my kicker and fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing/read.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a dry K-9-5 rainbow board. My opponent, the pre-flop raiser from the Small Blind, has c-bet 250 into a pot of 400. My hand is very strong in a Blind vs Blind configuration. Raising here would likely fold out worse hands (bluffs, lower pairs) and isolate me against better Kings or sets. Calling is the superior option as it keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or value betting with worse hands on later streets. I am in position, which gives me an advantage for the rest of the hand.

Preflop I raised to take the initiative with Kc6c and c‑bet the flop after seeing K♦9♠5♣. I have top pair but a very weak kicker. The Q♦ on the turn is a damaging card — it gives a lot of hands in Villain’s flop‑call range (Qx, KQ, Q9, etc.) the lead and doesn’t improve my kicker. With ~900 in the pot and Villain still deep (~8.5k effective), betting here risks getting check‑raised or committing to a large pot with a marginal holding. I prefer pot control and more information: check the turn, see how Villain reacts, call reasonably sized bets from worse Kx or bluffs, but fold to large overbets that represent two‑pair/sets or strong Qx lines. Checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me re‑evaluate on the river.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a board of Kd-9s-5c-Qd. The Queen on the turn introduces straight possibilities (J-T is now the nuts), and I have picked up a gutshot straight draw myself. My opponent, Minni (SB), checked the turn after c-betting the flop. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, my hand is quite strong, but the board texture is becoming coordinated. Betting here might fold out hands I beat (bluffs) or isolate me against better hands (KQ, AK, sets, or straights). Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid being check-raised, and potentially induce bluffs or get value on the river from weaker pairs (Qx, 9x) that might call a bet later. It also gives me a free look at the river to potentially hit my straight or improve to two pair. Checking creates a deception that keeps my opponent's range wide.

I have Kc6c — top pair with a weak kicker. I c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn for pot control when Q♦ hit (it brings Qx/two‑pair combos into villain’s range). Villain checked the turn as well, showing weakness or a medium hand (worse Kx, pocket 9s/5s, or a missed draw). The river 2♣ doesn’t change that story. With a small pot (900) I want thin value from worse Kx and small pairs while keeping the pot from ballooning against a stronger hand. A half‑pot value bet (≈450) is the right balance: it’s large enough to get called by worse and small pairs, but small enough to avoid committing to a big pot; if I face a large raise or shove I’ll heavily discount my kicker and fold. Action: bet 450 into 900.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker on a board of Kd-9s-5c-Qd-2c. The action went: Pre-flop call, Flop call, Turn check-check. On the river, the Small Blind leads for 450 into a pot of 900. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, ranges are wide. The opponent's check on the turn suggests they likely don't have a monster or a strong King that wants to build the pot, although a slow-played straight (JT) or two pair is possible. The river bet of half-pot could represents thin value from a Queen, a weaker King, or a bluff. Given I have top pair and the pot odds require only 25% equity, this is a clear call. Raising achieves nothing as worse hands fold and better hands call.

8♣3♣ is a very weak, often-dominated hand — not worth initiating a raise against two deep, capable players. Calling 100 is cheap relative to my 22,050 stack and preserves position advantage (I’ll be last to act postflop). I’ll see a flop for a small price and only continue with clear equity (pair, strong club draw, or a favorable board texture). If I face significant aggression (large raise/3-bet) I’ll fold; if checked to I’ll generally c-bet only with good flops or strong draws to avoid bloating the pot with junk.

I am in the Small Blind holding 6d 3s, which is a very weak unsuited hand. The Button (Minni) has limped in for 100. Although the price to complete is very cheap (50 chips to win a pot of 250, offering 5:1 odds), playing 63o out of position against two players (Button and Big Blind) is generally negative EV due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. I am the shortest stack of the three (though still deep at nearly 100BB), and preserving chips by avoiding unplayable spots is prudent. Folding is the disciplined decision here.

I have T8o in the Big Blind. Minni limped from the button and Pro folded from the Small Blind. Given that I am already in the hand and T8o is a mediocre hand to play out of position, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent and standard play here. This keeps the pot small and allows me to see if I can improve on the board without further investment.

I have Ten-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) on a board of 7-J-A. Since I am first to act on the flop in a limped pot and the board contains two high cards that likely connect with a button-limping range, checking is the most prudent play to see a free turn card without inflating the pot with a weak hand.

I called preflop cheaply to see a flop, but this board (A♦ J♥ 7♠) completely misses 8♣3♣ — no pair and no realistic draw (only a remote backdoor club possibility). Against one deep, capable opponent who has shown a lot of check/call lines, a c-bet here has poor fold equity and will often be called by Ax, Jx or pocket pairs, putting me in a losing spot. Pot is small (250) vs our deep stack, so I want pot control and flexibility. I’ll check and see my opponent’s action: if they bet I will fold the hand (unless the bet is absurdly tiny and clearly exploitable); if they check back I can reassess the turn and bluff only on very favourable cards (e.g. a club or a low card that improves my story).

On the turn, the five of diamonds doesn't significantly change the board state. I still hold ten-high with a gutshot straight draw to a nine. Given that the flop was checked around in a limped pot, I see no reason to start betting now with a hand that has very little showdown value. Checking again is the best play to try and see a free river card, allowing me to realize my equity if I hit my straight without inflating the pot while out of position.

I have 8♣3♣ on A♦ J♥ 7♠ 5♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw. The pot is tiny (250) vs very deep stacks (me 21,950, Flash 27,975). Flash has shown passive check/call lines on the flop and turn, so my turn c-bet would have very little fold equity and will often be called by Ax, Jx or pocket pairs. I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. Plan: keep the pot small and retain flexibility — check. If Flash leads here I will fold to any meaningful bet (only consider calling/exploiting if the bet is absurdly tiny). If we check through to the river I’ll reassess and only attempt a river bluff on a card that credibly improves my story and offers real fold equity; otherwise I’ll check down.

On the river, the board is 7-J-A-5-6 and I hold ten-high. I missed my gutshot straight draw (needing a nine). This has been a limped pot that was checked on the flop and turn, suggesting neither of us has a strong hand. While I could attempt a bluff to take down the small pot, my hand has virtually no showdown value, and it's quite possible Minni is holding a small pair or a better high card like King-high or Queen-high. Given the small size of the pot and my position, checking to see if I can win a cheap showdown or simply folding to a bet is the most disciplined approach.

Check. I have no pair or realistic draw on A♦ J♥ 7♠ 5♦ 6♠ and the pot is tiny (250) versus very deep stacks. Flash has shown a passive check/call line on flop and turn and is unlikely to fold many hands (A‑x, J‑x, pocket pairs) to a river bluff, so my fold equity is minimal. Betting here would bloat the pot with air and lose EV when called; checking controls the pot and preserves stack. I will fold to any meaningful river bet from Flash.

I am on the Button with J2s (Jack-Two suited). While being on the Button in a 3-handed game usually encourages a wide opening range to steal the blinds, J2s is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, often dominated by better Jacks or weak kickers. My opponents, Flash (SB) and Minni (BB), have shown tendencies to defend or 3-bet, making a steal attempt with this holding negative EV. I am the shortest stack and should preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with QsTs, which is a very strong hand for 3-handed play. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Minni) to beat. I have a significant chip lead. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or to build a pot where I have a range advantage and a strong starting hand.

I have A♥5♥ in the big blind facing a small-blind open to 300. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total). With both stacks very deep (~200+ BBs) A5s is too strong to fold heads-up — it has good equity vs a wide SB opening range and plays well postflop (nut-suit, can make top-pair Aces). I prefer to call rather than 3-bet: a small 3-bet would leave me out of position on the flop while building a bigger pot against an aggressive opponent, increasing variance unnecessarily. Calling gets correct pot odds (200 to win ~600 → ~25% equity needed), keeps the pot manageable, and lets me realize equity or use postflop aggression when I connect. Plan: see the flop and evaluate — bet/protect when I hit an Ace or have strong draws, play cautiously (fold or check-call small c-bets) when completely missed.

The flop has come monotone (7h-8h-Jh), and I do not hold any hearts in my hand. Although I have a gutshot straight draw and two overcards, this board is extremely dangerous as any heart in the opponent's range now has a flush or a high flush draw. I am out of position against Minni. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and see how she reacts. Betting here into a monotone board without a heart is risky and prone to a raise that would force me to fold my equity.

I hold A♥5♥ on 7♥ J♥ 8♥ — the nut flush. Opponent checked to me after a SB open and my preflop call, so this is the time to extract value and charge draws while denying free cards that could give a pocket pair a full house later. A ~2/3-pot value bet (400 into 600) will get called by worse hearts, J/8/top-pair hands and medium pairs while pricing out some equity for straight/heart draws. If Flash check-raises big or jams, I’ll call the flop with the nut flush; if he flat-calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue betting thinly on non-pairing cards and pot-control if the board pairs. Bet 400.

The flop is extremely dangerous as it is monotone with three hearts (7h-8h-Jh). I am holding QsTs, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and two overcards, but crucially, I do not have a heart in my hand. This means I have no flush draw and no blockers to the flush. Minni's bet of 400 into a 600 pot (offering 2.5:1 odds) is a strong lead on such a coordinated board. In 3-handed play, I should be aggressive, but calling here without a heart is poor strategy as I would be 'peeling' with very few clean outs. Even if a 9 comes, if it is the 9 of hearts, the board contains four to a flush. Folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Seven suited (J7s) is a strong enough hand to open from the Button. Being in position allows me to control the pot size and apply pressure to the blinds. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to either win the blinds immediately or set up a post-flop advantage.

Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 more to call (total commitment 300). K♠5♠ is marginal but playable in a three-handed pot — with deep stacks (≈222bb) I can realize equity post‑flop, whereas a light 3‑bet here (min to 500) would have little fold equity and a large 3‑bet with K5s OOP is poor strategy. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable and see a flop; plan is to fold to a sizable 3‑bet, otherwise proceed on flops that give me a king, spade draw, or good equity lines and play cautiously on dry boards out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 9s, an off-suit connector. The Button (Flash) raised to 300 (3bb), and the Small Blind (Minni) called. The pot is 700, and I need to call 200 to see a flop, giving me 4.5:1 odds. While the price is attractive, 89o performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially when out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. My hand is easily dominated and has poor playability post-flop compared to suited variants. The Small Blind's call suggests a range that likely connects well with boards I might hit. To avoid bleeding chips in a low-equity spot out of position, I will fold.

I called preflop planning to continue with a king or a spade draw — this flop gives me both. With K♠5♠ on T♦2♠6♠ I have the nut-flush draw plus an overcard. That’s ~12 clean outs (9 spades + 3 non-spade kings) and roughly ~45% to improve by river, plus good implied odds with deep stacks. Out of position, betting is preferable to passively giving the raiser a free card; a half-to-2/3 pot semi-bluff gets folds from weaker hands, charges other draws, and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll make a constructive bet here (~0.5–0.6 pot). If I face a large check-raise I’ll reassess (likely fold to an extreme check-raise given I’m OOP with only a draw); I’ll call reasonable raises to realize equity and proceed based on turn action.

I have Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw and one overcard on a board that I likely missed. Minni's lead (donk bet) for 400 into a 700 pot is a strong indication of either a Ten, a draw, or a proactive attempt to take control of the hand. While I am in position, my equity against a range that leads this board is insufficient to justify a call, especially since I would likely face a second barrel on the turn. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities in this three-handed dynamic.

I open-raise to 3x (300). On the button with A♠6♥ I have position on both blinds and a decent ace to steal; limp/call keeps too many bad hands in and invites multiway situations where A6o is weak. A 300 sizing is large enough to pick off the blinds often but small enough to preserve fold equity and allow me to fold/defend against strong 3-bets. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet favorable A-high or dry flops and play cautiously on coordinated/draw-heavy boards; fold or proceed conservatively if faced with heavy 4-bet action. If everyone folds I win the pot; if I’m 3-bet to an oversized amount I’ll lean to folding given hand quality.

I hold Queen-Ten of Clubs in the Small Blind. The player on the Button (Minni), who is the chip leader and has been active, opens for a standard 3x raise to 300. My hand is strong enough to play against a Button opening range, possessing good post-flop playability (straight and flush potential). Folding would be too tight given the hand strength. While a 3-bet is an option to isolate, playing out of position against a deep stack suggests a flat call is a lower-variance and solid play to keep their range wide and see a flop. I will call the additional 250 chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit. The Button (Minni) opened for 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) flat-called. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700, giving me pot odds of 3.5:1. QJo is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard open and a flat-call in 3-handed play. I will call to see the flop and close the pre-flop action.

On a board of Kh-6s-Qd, I have middle pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker. In a 3-way pot from the Small Blind, leading out (donk betting) is generally not optimal as it folds out worse hands and gets action from better hands (Kings, better Queens, sets). The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser (Minni) to see if they continue. My hand has decent showdown value and some backdoor potential, so I intend to check-call a standard continuation bet or check-fold to heavy action depending on sizing, but for now, checking is the correct first move.

On this flop of Kh-6s-Qd, I have hit middle pair with a decent kicker (Jack). Pro (the Small Blind) has checked to me. In a multi-way pot (3 players) where the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is still to act, checking is the standard and most prudent play with a medium-strength hand like middle pair. This allows me to see how Minni acts and keeps the pot controlled. If Minni bets, I will likely call depending on the sizing, but leading out here (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or draws, making the hand harder to play out of position.

Flop: Kh 6s Qd — I hold A♠6♥ (pair of sixes with the Ace kicker). Both opponents checked, showing weakness or passive hands. With two overcards on board my pair is vulnerable but still has decent showdown value against a lot of calling ranges (small pockets, A‑x, weaker pairs, floats). I want to take the initiative, deny free cards to K/Q overcards and extract thin value. A half‑pot c‑bet (~50% pot) is the right balance: it folds out many overcards/floaters, gets called by worse hands, and keeps the pot manageable with two opponents. I will bet 450. If I get called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check/controlled second‑barrel only on safe cards, fold to strong turn aggression if the board pairs K/Q or a scary draw completes). If faced with a big bet/raise on the flop I’ll strongly consider folding — my hand is not strong enough to hero call large pressure.

I hold Middle Pair (Queens) on a K-Q-6 board. The pre-flop raiser (Minni) has made a standard continuation bet of half the pot (450 into 900). Given Minni's wide Button opening range, they could be betting with a King, a better Queen, a draw (like J-T), or air. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet, as I have reasonable showdown value and block some straights. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands (Kings, AQ, KQ). Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn. The pot odds of 3:1 are favorable for calling with second pair.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of Kh-6s-Qd. The pre-flop aggressor, Minni, has bet 450 into a pot that was 900, and Pro has called. I am being offered 4:1 pot odds to call (450 to win a total pot that will be 2250). While a King or a better Queen (like AQ) is certainly in their ranges, my hand is too strong to fold to a small bet in a three-way pot, especially given the odds. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands. Therefore, calling is the best play to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot manageable.

On the Turn, the board is Kh-6s-Qd-7d. I hold Second Pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker. The pot is multi-way (3 players) and I am out of position in the Small Blind. The pre-flop aggressor (Minni) c-betted the flop and Flash (BB) called, indicating strength or draws from both. Leading out here (donk betting) would likely only get action from Kings or better Queens, while folding out hands I beat. My goal is to pot control and try to reach showdown or evaluate if Minni fires a second barrel. Checking is the standard play to the aggressor.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of Kh-6s-Qd-7d. In this three-way pot, Pro (the Small Blind) has checked to me. Since the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is still to act behind me, checking is the most appropriate move. My hand has showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value against two opponents, especially after both called a flop bet. By checking, I can keep the pot controlled and see what Minni does, maintaining flexibility for the river.

I check. I have A♠6♥ — second pair with the best kicker, but this is a multiway pot and my hand is vulnerable to Kx/Qx and to the diamond draw the turn (7♦) brings. Both opponents checked the turn which shows weakness/pot control; betting into two callers often gets value only from hands that already beat me (K/Q) or from small pairs that will call and still be ahead. I prefer pot control: keep the pot at 2250, avoid facing a check-raise or bloating the pot with a medium-strength hand, and see a cheap river. Plan: call a modest river bet but fold to large aggression unless I improve (A/6 or a safe brick where I can credibly represent strength). If river pairs the board or improves me, I’ll re-evaluate then.

On the River, the board is Kh-6s-Qd-7d-8d. The back-door diamond flush draw has completed. The action checked through on the Turn, suggesting opponents may not have strong Kings or two-pair hands, though the River card improves flush draws. I hold Second Pair (Queens), which has reasonable showdown value in a pot where the Turn was checked around. Betting out is unlikely to get value from worse hands (like mid-pairs) and isolates me against better hands (Kings, flushes). Checking is the best option to try to get to showdown cheaply. I will check and evaluate any subsequent bets.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of Kh-6s-Qd-7d-8d. Both opponents have checked to me on the river. The turn was checked around, and the Small Blind (Pro) has now checked the river as well. This sequence suggests that no one is holding a particularly strong hand like a King, a flush, or a straight. While my Queen has significant showdown value, betting here is risky; I'm unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I could be check-raised by a trapped King or a backdoor flush. Checking back ensures I get to see the showdown and keep the pot at its current size.

I check to go to showdown. I have A♠6♥ — second pair with the ace kicker — but this is a three‑way pot and the river 8♦ brings three diamonds (Q♦7♦8♦), so two‑card diamond hands can now beat me. Both opponents checked the turn and the river, showing passivity/pot control. Betting into two callers risks getting value only from hands that already beat me (Kx/Qx or a made flush/straight), and a bluff is unlikely to fold the hands that beat me. My pair has decent showdown value against missed draws and small pairs, so I keep the pot at 2250 and let them show down. If someone bets a small amount I will call; I will fold to large river aggression.

I am on the Button with King-Jack offsuit (Kh Js) in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand, well within the standard opening range for the button. With a stack of roughly 90 big blinds, I want to apply pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage. Limping is passive and folding is too tight. A standard open-raise is the best play here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately. Based on recent tournament trends, a raise to 2.5x (250 chips) is a standard size that balances pot building with risk management.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in the game. Pro, on the Button, has raised to 250. Given my position and the poor quality of my cards, there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Call 150 (commit to 250 total). Facing a button open to 2.5bb in 3-handed, Ts8o is a marginal but defendable hand — folding is too tight and a tiny 3-bet to 400 won’t fold many hands but will bloat the pot out of position. Pot odds for the call are ~27% (call 150 into ~400), and against the button’s wide open range I have enough equity to continue. Plan: see the flop and play cautiously OOP — fold to strong, committed aggression unless I hit a meaningful piece (pair, two pair, strong draw) or can credibly float and realize equity on later streets.

Check. I called preflop to see a cheap flop; on 6h 9c Qc I have T8o — no pair, a double-gutshot (J or 7 gives a straight — ~8 outs) and only a very remote runner‑runner club flush. I'm out of position vs the button's wide open range, and there hasn't been a c-bet yet. Betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and will often be called by better Qx/Kx/overpairs; instead I keep the pot small and realize equity. Plan: check to see the turn; call a small c-bet to continue and fold to heavy committed aggression. If checked to me on later streets I can barreled as a bluff selectively or take a free card to hit my outs.

I raised pre-flop with Kh Js on the button and the Big Blind called. The flop comes 6h 9c Qc. This texture connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range (pairs of 9s or 6s, Queen-X, straight draws like T8, J8, 87, and flush draws). Although I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (a Ten makes the straight), betting here is risky because I might get check-raised or floated by better hands or draws, forcing me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot small, and lets me see a turn card which could improve my hand (a K, J, or T). It is a standard pot control line with a marginal hand on a wet board.

Check. I’m out of position with T♠8♣ on 6♥‑9♣‑Q♣‑4♥ — no made hand and only straight outs (7 or J = 8 outs ≈17.4% to hit on the river). Both players checked the flop and the button checked the turn, so betting now would bloat a small pot with a marginal draw and will often be called by hands (Qx/9x/Kx/Ax) that dominate me or deny little fold equity. Checking preserves pot control, gives a free river to realize my ~17% equity, and keeps options open: call a small river bet (good pot odds), fold to large aggression, or value-bet/bluff on specific river runouts if appropriate. This is the least risky + EV‑positive plan OOP vs an unknown but wide preflop raiser.

On the turn, the board is 6h 9c Qc 4h. The pot is 550. My opponent (Minni) has checked to me again. I hold Kh Js, giving me King-high, a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), and one overcard to the current second pair. The 4h introduces a second flush draw (hearts) in addition to the clubs. Minni's check-check line signals weakness, but her range still includes many pairs (9x, 6x, 4x) or draws that might call a bet. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, but since I have showdown value with K-high (beating missed draws) and decent equity to improve on the river (hitting a King or Ten), checking back is the superior low-variance play. It guarantees I see the river card for free to potentially hit my straight or top pair, avoiding the risk of being check-raised off my equity.

Board runs out 6♥ 9♣ Q♣ 4♥ A♣ and I hold T♠8♣ — I missed. I have no pair and only one club; a flush would require the opponent to hold two clubs (rare). Both players checked the flop and turn, so there was no message of strong aggression and the pot is only 550. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity (button can comfortably call with Qx/Ax/9x and many showdown hands), so bloat the pot unnecessarily. Best line is to check back and take the showdown — fold if I face large river aggression rather than spew chips with a bluff that’s unlikely to fold better hands.

I arrived at the river with King-high after checking back the flop and turn. The board is now [6h 9c Qc 4h Ac]. The river card brings an Ace and completes a possible club flush. My King-high has very little showdown value, beating only busted straight draws like 87 or T8. However, the board texture is very wet and hits the Big Blind's defending range hard (containing many Ax hands and random clubs). Since I showed weakness by checking two streets, a sudden bet on this scare card might not be credible enough to fold out a pair (like a 6 or 9), and certainly won't fold an Ace or a flush. Betting likely only folds hands I am already beating, or gets called by better. Therefore, checking back and minimizing loss is the correct play.

J2o is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. As the chip leader on the button, while I want to maintain pressure, this hand is too weak to justify an open or a call against the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

Heads-up preflop from the small blind with Qh3d — a marginal offsuit hand and out of position. I’ve already posted 50; calling the extra 50 (100 total committed) is a cheap way to see a flop and realize equity against the BB. Min-raising to 200 would bloat the pot with a weak hand and invite a 3-bet from Pro (stack ~9175). My plan is to defend by calling, evaluate the flop, and only continue aggressively if I hit a reasonable piece or have decent draw equity; otherwise give up to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 7c. The Button folded, and the Small Blind limped (completed to 100). My hand is 6-7 offsuit, which is a playable connector but generally weak. Since there is no raise to call, I can see a flop for free. Raising here would turn my hand into a bluff, but given the hand strength and being out of position post-flop, checking is the standard and lowest variance play to realize equity. I will check and proceed to the flop.

Flop is 9c 2h Tc (rainbow). I hold Qh3d — I missed the flop and have virtually no draw. However this is a dry board and we are heads-up vs the BB who checked; their range from the big blind (a lot of overcards, weak pairs and some ten/nine combos) will include many hands that fold to pressure. Rather than passively giving up position, I want to apply low-risk pressure with fold equity. I’ll make a standard c-bet of about half the pot (100 into 200). That size is big enough to force folds from single overcards and weak holdings but small enough to avoid committing myself OOP with air. If I get a raise I’ll release; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only with improvement or a clear plan to barrel.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot against the Small Blind. The flop comes 9c 2h Tc. I hold 6s 7c, giving me 6-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The Small Blind leads out for 100 (half pot). While I am in position, my hand is very weak. A gutshot has only 4 outs (~8.5% chance to hit on the turn), and the pot odds of 3:1 (25% required equity) do not justify a call based on direct odds alone. My implied odds are limited in a small pot, and the board texture (flush draw present) makes my draw vulnerable even if I hit. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips.

I’m on the button with A♠T♦ in a 3-handed pot. This is a strong, playable hand with position and deep stacks (I have ~217bb, effective to SB ~91bb). I want initiative and fold equity — a standard 3x open is appropriate to isolate the small blind and build a pot when I’m likely ahead. Raising to 300 (3x) accomplishes that; calling 100 is too passive and a min-raise to 200 is too small. Plan postflop: c-bet dry/A-high flops to take it down, pot-control or check on coordinated/wet boards, call small 3-bets and fold to large 3-bets depending on sizing and opponent, and use positional advantage to extract value or fold safely.

I am holding 4c 8d in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand (trash). Minni raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. Facing a raise with 8-4 offsuit out of position is a clear fold situation. The pot odds do not justify calling with a hand that has poor equity and playability, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. I will fold and preserve my stack of 91BB.

I am holding Jack-Ten of Clubs, a very strong suited connector that performs well in 3-handed play. Minni has raised from the button, which is a standard play. I am getting roughly 2.25 to 1 odds to call. While I could 3-bet for value or to take control of the hand, calling allows me to defend my big blind efficiently, see a flop, and use my stack advantage to outplay my opponent post-flop without bloating the pot out of position.

On this flop of 6d-8h-5c, I have two overcards (J and T) and a few backdoor straight and flush possibilities. However, the board is very coordinated and likely interacts well with a Button opening range. As the caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to see how Minni values her hand on this texture. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called or raised by hands that have me beat, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a free card or evaluate a bet based on its size.

I opened to 300 from the button and was called by the BB; pot is 650. Flop 6♦ 8♥ 5♣ is very dry and misses the BB’s cold-call range a lot. I hold A♠ T♦ — two clean overcards and a backdoor diamond — plus position. This is a textbook continuation-bet spot: it applies pressure, folds out many inferior hands, and keeps control of the hand. I size to ~1/2 pot (325) to get good fold equity while leaving maneuvering room on the turn. If Flash folds we take the pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet A/T turns, pot-control or check back on dangerous/connected turns, consider a second barrel only on favorable cards). Fold or strongly tighten line vs a large check-raise which will represent sets/two-pair.

Minni is continuation betting for approximately half the pot on a low, connected board (6d-8h-5c). My Jack-Ten of Clubs provides two overcards and backdoor straight and flush potential. Given that I am getting 4-to-1 pot odds (calling 325 to win a total pot of 1300), folding would be overly tight. I have sufficient equity and playability to see a turn card, especially since Minni's button opening range is wide. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and Minni's subsequent action.

The turn card is the Jack of Spades, giving me top pair with a Ten kicker. While this is a significant improvement, the board remains highly coordinated with straight possibilities (any 7). As I have been checking and calling up to this point, checking again is the most strategic move. It allows Minni to continue her aggression with bluffs or semi-bluffs, and it protects me from overcommitting if she has already completed a straight. I plan to check-call any reasonable bet to see the river.

Turn decision: board 6♦ 8♥ 5♣ J♠, pot 1,300. I hold A♠ T♦ — no pair, just two overcards and no realistic draw. I c-bet the dry flop and was called, so the caller’s range contains a lot of medium/small pairs (5s/6s/8s), some Jx, and sticky broadway/overcards. The turn J is a relatively “bad” card for my hand: it improves many hands that called the flop and reduces my fold equity. Betting here would be a bluff into a range that will often call (or check-raise with the very strong hands), and a value bet is thin because I’m behind any pair.

Plan: pot-control — check back the turn. I want to avoid bloating a pot where I’m likely behind, preserve stack depth, and wait for a river that gives clear value (an A or T) or a blank that I can consider a delayed bluff only if villain shows clear weakness. On a blank river I’ll be prepared to check-call reasonable bets (fold to large polarized barrels) or take a pot if villain mistakenly fires thin bluffs.

On the river, the board has become extremely coordinated with the Seven of Hearts, completing various straight possibilities (any 9 or 4). I hold top pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a monster like a set or a made straight at that point, though she could have been checking behind with a draw that has now improved. By checking, I allow her to bluff with missed draws or check behind with mediocre showdown value that she wouldn't call a bet with. Betting myself risks being raised by a straight and doesn't get much value from worse hands given how scary the board is. I will check and plan to call a reasonable bet, as her turn check makes a high-value hand less likely.

I check back. Board is 6♦-8♥-5♣-J♠-7♥, pot 1,300; I hold A♠T♦ with no pair. I c-bet the dry flop and correctly checked the turn to control the pot, and villain’s line (call flop, check turn, check river) looks call‑heavy — small/medium pairs (5s/6s/8s), some Jx or stubborn overcards that want showdown. I have almost no value and limited bluff equity: a half‑pot river bluff would need ~33% fold frequency, and given how often this player calls down those small/medium pairs that fold equity is unlikely. With deep stacks and poor showdown value, pot control is the prudent play — check and see a showdown. If villain bets into me I’ll fold to a sizable polarized bet and only call very small thin value bets.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with a healthy stack (91 BB). K5s is a marginal but playable hand, especially in position with fewer players. It falls within a standard button opening range to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My opponents have larger stacks and have been active, but I need to maintain some aggression to avoid being blinded out or exploited for playing too tight. A standard raise to 250 (2.5 BB) is consistent with my previous opening sizes for both value and speculative hands, balancing my range.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Pro opened with a standard raise from the button, which likely represents a wide range in a 3-handed game. I want to build a pot immediately and maximize value. Calling would allow the Big Blind to enter the pot cheaply or see a flop for free, so I am 3-betting to 850 to take control of the hand and narrow the field.

I’m on the big blind with 6♠7♣ facing a button open to 250 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 850. Calling 750 more into the current ~1200 pot would make the pot ~1950, so I’d need ≈38.5% equity to break even. 6‑7 offsuit has poor equity and poor multiway playability versus an open+3‑bet range, and I’d be out of position and likely dominated postflop. A 4‑bet is way too strong with this hand. Conserve chips and fold — wait for a better spot.

I opened to 250 with K5s on the button, a standard steal attempt. Flash (SB), the chip leader, has 3-bet to 850. The pot odds are roughly 33% (call 600 to win 1800), but K5s performs poorly against a standard 3-bet range due to domination issues (dominated by AK, KQ, KJ) and limited playability post-flop if I don't flop a flush draw or two pair. Facing a 3.4x 3-bet from the big stack, K5s is a disciplined fold. I have position, but the reverse implied odds and the strength of the opponent's range make calling -EV.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with a King-high hand. In this configuration, K6o is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and leverage my positional advantage. Pro is relatively short-stacked and may be playing a 'push or fold' strategy, while Minni has a healthy stack but will be out of position for the rest of the hand. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to take control of the pot early.

I have A♥7♣ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I need to commit 250 more). A7o is worth defending in 3-handed — it has decent playability and can hit top pair — but it's vulnerable to domination and is marginal as a 3-bet bluff. With deep stacks and the big blind (Pro) still to act (and capable of squeezing), I prefer to defend and see a flop rather than 3-bet or fold. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity; I'll proceed post-flop and re-evaluate. Total commit to call: 300.

I am in the Big Blind with [6h 8h], a suited one-gapper. Flash raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called in the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 (300 Flash + 300 Minni + 100 My BB). I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 200/900, or approximately 22%. My hand has good playability post-flop (straights, flushes) and defends well enough against a Button open and a Small Blind flat to justify the price. Folding would be too tight given the excellent odds and deep stacks (I have ~87BB). I will call to close the action and see a flop.

I defended A♥7♣ preflop in a 3-handed spot to realize equity. Flop (T♠ 8♣ 5♠) leaves me with only ace-high and no meaningful backdoors (I can’t make a flush with one club). The raiser’s range and the caller’s range contain lots of tens, pairs and broadway cards that beat me, and betting into two opponents with a marginal hand is giving away chips unnecessarily. I want to keep the pot small and see a turn card — check controls the pot and preserves my stack while keeping weaker hands in that I can beat or call small bets from. Plan: check-call a small continuation bet (≈ ≤1/3 pot) to realize equity and fold to large bets/raises; if checked through re-evaluate on the turn and look to take a free card or barrel a favorable turn.

I hold Middle Pair (Eights) on a Ten-high connected board [5s 8c Ts]. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flash on the Button) and sandwiched between him and the Small Blind. Leading out (donk betting) with a marginal made hand like middle pair is generally poor strategy as it folds out worse hands (bluffs) and isolates me against better hands (Top Pair, Overpairs). The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Flash bets, I can decide whether to call based on the sizing, given I beat straight draws and overcards. If it checks through, I am happy to see a free turn.

I am on the button in a multi-way pot with King-high on a board of 5s-8c-Ts. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture hits many components of my opponents' calling ranges, including various straight draws and middle pairs. In a three-way pot, the success rate of a continuation bet decreases significantly. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid getting check-raised by the shorter stack (Pro), and get to see a free turn card to potentially improve or re-evaluate the strength of my hand based on my opponents' subsequent actions.

Turn: T♠ 8♣ 5♠ 3♣. I hold A♥7♣ — still just Ace-high, no pair and no realistic flush draw (I have one club; even with the turn club I can’t make a five-card flush by the river). Flop plan was pot control and that remains correct: I don’t have enough equity or fold equity to profitably bet into two opponents here. Both opponents checked the flop, so their ranges contain many missed hands and medium pairs; betting into two callers will frequently be called by better hands and put me in awkward spots on later streets. Pot is 900 — I’m happy to preserve chips and realize equity for a cheap river. Action plan: check and see what they do; call small turn bets to realize equity but fold to large raises. If checked through I’ll re-evaluate on the river and only invest further if I pair the Ace/7 or pick up a clear fold-target card.

On the turn, the board is [5s 8c Ts 3c]. The flop checked through, which caps the ranges of my opponents somewhat, suggesting neither holds a strong Ten or better, though they could be pot-controlling. The 3c introduces a second flush draw (clubs) alongside the spades. I hold middle pair (Eights) with a weak kicker. Being out of position in a multi-way pot, my goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch bluffs. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff-catcher against a raise or isolates me against better hands (like a delayed Ten or a slow-played set, though unlikely). Checking allows me to evaluate the Button's action; if they bet now after the flop checked through, it could be a stab or a value bet with a draw/pair, which I can consider calling. If it checks through again, I'm happy to see a river. My hand is too marginal to value bet confidently.

On the turn, the board is 5s-8c-Ts-3c. Both Minni and Pro have checked for the second time, indicating weakness or a willingness to see a cheap showdown. My King-high has some marginal showdown value, but it's unlikely to be the best hand in a three-way pot. Betting here doesn't accomplish much, as better hands like a Ten or an Eight are unlikely to fold, and I'd prefer to see a free river card to potentially improve to a King or simply reach showdown as cheaply as possible. Checking back maintains pot control and keeps my range balanced.

The river paired me (A♥7♣) giving top pair with a medium kicker on 5♠ 8♣ T♠ 3♣ A♣. I checked earlier to control the pot and realized equity; now I should try to extract thin value while keeping the pot manageable against two-pair/flushes that beat me. With both opponents checked through, their ranges include many worse A‑x and single-pair hands that will call a small value bet. I’ll bet about one-third of the pot (300) to get called by worse aces and medium pairs but avoid bloating the pot. If I face a sizable raise (showing strong two‑pair/flush/trips), I’ll fold — those hands dominate my kicker.

The river card is the Ace of Clubs, making the board [5s 8c Ts 3c Ac]. This card is poor for my hand (Pair of Eights) as it brings an overcard and completes a runner-runner flush draw. Minni leads for 300 (1/3 pot). This small sizing is often indicative of a blocker bet with a weak Ace, a Ten, or a flush that fears a check-raise, or a thin value bet. Given Minni flatted pre-flop from the Small Blind, their range contains many Ax hands and suited connectors that could have made a flush. Furthermore, Flash (the pre-flop raiser) is still to act behind me and could easily have an Ace or better. My hand beats only pure bluffs, and with the Ace and possible flush arriving, the number of bluffs I beat is significantly reduced. I am getting good odds (20%), but being sandwiched and holding only 4th pair makes this a fold to preserve chips.

The river card is an Ace, which is a significant scare card. I checked the flop and turn, which indicates a marginal hand or a surrender. Minni's lead of 300 into a 900 pot (1/3 pot) on the river after checking twice suggests she either hit the Ace, is value-betting a pair of Tens or Eights, or is attempting a cheap bluff. While the price to call is attractive (4-to-1), King-high has virtually no showdown value here. Almost any hand Minni would call with pre-flop and see a river with has me beaten, including missed straight draws that might have accidentally paired or simply out-kick my King-high. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Three-handed, I'm on the button with 8♣6♣ — a playable, suited connector that plays very well in position. Limping (calling 100) surrenders initiative and gives the big blind great pot odds to see flops; a button open is standard here. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB) to seize initiative, pick up fold equity against the blinds, and keep the pot a reasonable size if I get called — leaving plenty of postflop maneuvering room versus both stacks. If I face a large 3-bet I can fold this speculative hand, but in position I want to be the aggressor and build pots when I hit.

I am in the Small Blind holding 5s 4c, a very weak offsuit connector. Minni on the Button has raised to 300 (3BB). Facing a raise from the Button, my hand has very poor equity and playability, especially since I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding, and a 3-bet bluff is too risky given the hand's lack of blockers and post-flop potential. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300 in this 3-handed game. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1 to call, J5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent like Minni. Given that I have a comfortable stack and there are still plenty of hands left in the tournament, I will opt to fold this hand and look for better spots to commit chips.

I am on the Button with Q5o, which is a very weak hand even in a 3-handed game. I have 85 BBs, so I am not desperate to steal. The Small Blind (Flash) is the chip leader and recently 3-bet me (Hand #102), making a steal attempt with trash risky. It is better to preserve chips and wait for a playable hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). Jack-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand. While I am the chip leader and could consider limping to see a cheap flop or raising as a steal, playing this hand out of position against an active player like Minni is not ideal. I will fold to avoid getting involved in a potentially tricky pot with a weak hand.

I am holding 2-3 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am on the button and have a significant chip lead, there is no strategic reason to attempt a steal or enter the pot with such a poor hand, especially since Pro is relatively short-stacked and might be looking for an opportunity to shove or defend aggressively. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have A♠T♦ in the small blind heads-up vs the BB — a strong hand worth taking the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) to build the pot, isolate the single opponent, and price out speculative holdings while keeping post‑flop playability. Size is large enough to show strength but small relative to deep stacks so I can comfortably continue to a flop or call a modest 3‑bet; if Pro 3‑bets huge I’ll reevaluate (likely fold to an over‑aggressive shove, call smaller 3‑bets). Plan: take lead on favorable flops with continuation bets and control pot size when marginal.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 2d against a Small Blind raise to 300 (3BB). The pot odds require about 33% equity to call (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). While 62s has some playability being suited, it is a very weak hand (bottom 20% of hands). Against a typical SB raising range which is often wide but not 100%, 62s usually has around 30-34% equity, making it a marginal to losing call even with position. Given the 3x sizing which applies more pressure than a min-raise, and having a speculative hand that relies heavily on hitting flush draws or two pair, folding is the prudent long-term play to preserve chips.

I’m on the button with A♦4♠ in a 3-handed pot — this is a strong positional hand and a good spot to seize the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB) to pick up the blinds or isolate heads-up, while keeping the sizing reasonable versus possible 3-bets from deeper stacks. If I get a small 3-bet I may call and play postflop in position; I’ll fold to a large 3-bet that commits a big portion of my stack. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable textures, use position to probe, and abandon marginal boards against heavy resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o, a very weak hand. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. Although Minni is active, J3o is too weak to defend out of position, even with decent stack depth (83.75 BB). Calling would lead to difficult post-flop situations dominated by better Jacks or pairs. 3-betting is not optimal with this specific hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision.

I am holding Ah Qh in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button, which is a position where players typically open with a wide range of hands. AQ suited is a premium hand, especially in a 3-handed game. I want to build the pot and take the initiative by 3-betting. A standard 3-bet size here would be around 3x to 4x the initial raise. I will raise to 1000 to put pressure on Minni and extract value from worse hands like smaller Aces, Kings, or broadway cards that might call.

Call to 1000. I opened to 300 on the button with A♦4♠ and Flash made a fairly small 3‑bet to 1000. With position and deep stacks A4o is worth defending — it’s too weak to 4‑bet bluff but too playable to fold. The call (700 more into a ~1350 pot, ~34% immediate odds) lets me realize equity and exploit positional advantage postflop. Plan: see a flop, c‑bet favorable textures or check back and probe; fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I’ve missed, but continue/shove if I hit top pair or a strong draw.

I am the pre-flop 3-bettor and the board has come out Td-Tc-Ts. This gives me trips with an Ace kicker. Since three of the four Tens are on the board, it is mathematically very unlikely that Minni holds the final Ten. I have a range advantage here, as my 3-bet range includes high pocket pairs (JJ-AA) that Minni might not have. I will lead with a small continuation bet of roughly 1/3 of the pot (700) to extract value from her pocket pairs or high-card hands like AK or AJ, while keeping the pot manageable in case she is slow-playing the case Ten or a larger pocket pair.

Preflop I called the 3‑bet to use position, but this flop is one of the worst possible textures for A4.
