Board T♦ T♣ T♠ gives trips to the board — my best current hand is TTT with A4 kickers (TTT A 4). Flash 3‑bet preflop and now fires a small lead (700 into a 2750 pot). Calling 700 costs 700/(2750+700) ≈ 20% of the final pot, but my realistic equity is well below that. I have 6 outs (three Aces + three 4s) to make a full house by the river — about a 24% raw chance — and even if I hit an Ace I can still be behind (e.g. vs pocket Aces) or crushed by quads/full houses from pocket pairs. Raising as a bluff is thin against a 3‑bettor who is likely value‑heavy here. Preserving a 20k+ stack and waiting for a cleaner spot is the higher EV play. Fold.

I hold Pocket Aces (AcAs) on the Button, the best starting hand in poker. With 3 active players, my goal is to build the pot immediately and extract value. I should enter the pot with a standard raise to initiate action, hoping for a call or a 3-bet from the blinds who have deep stacks. A raise to 250 is consistent with standard opening sizes and disguises the strength of my hand compared to a limp or an unusually large raise.

I am in the Small Blind holding Trey of Clubs and Eight of Spades (3c 8s), which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While I have the largest stack at the table, calling out of position with such a disconnected hand is mathematically unprofitable. Additionally, the Big Blind (Minni) still has the option to 3-bet or call, further reducing the value of a call. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Pro (button) opened to 250. I’m in the big blind with 2♠2♣. With deep effective stacks (I 20825, Pro 8125) and heads-up versus a wide button open, pocket deuces are best played as a set-mining call rather than risking a small 3‑bet out of position. The pot is 400 and I must add 150 more — calling 150 (250 total committed) preserves implied odds to hit a set on the flop and keeps the pot manageable OOP. A min‑raise to 400 gains little fold equity and can turn the hand into a marginal spot vs stronger ranges or a 4‑bet. Call 250 total and reevaluate on the flop.

I called preflop as a set‑mining call with 2♠2♣. The flop 7♣‑4♥‑8♦ is a dry, rainbow board that missed me — I have an underpair and I’m out of position versus a wide button open. With deep effective stacks I want to keep the pot small and realize my equity rather than bloating the pot OOP. Checking preserves implied odds to hit a set, avoids getting raised off the hand by stronger pairs, and lets me call reasonable flop/turn bets. Plan: check now, call small–medium barreled bets to see turns, fold to large aggression unless I improve to a set.

I hold Pocket Aces (AcAs) on a flop of 7c 4h 8d. The board is somewhat connected, offering straight draws (56, 69, etc.) and hitting the Big Blind's calling range (8x, 7x, pocket pairs), but I likely have the best hand. Checking back would under-represent my hand and give a free card to draws, which is risky on this texture. I want to extract value from top pairs (8x), middle pairs, and draws while denying equity to hands with random backdoor potential. A c-bet of around 45% of the pot (250 into 550) is a standard size that balances value extraction with keeping the opponent's calling range wide enough to include worse hands.

Call 250. I have pocket 2s on 7♣-4♥-8♦ — a small but made hand and good set-mining candidate against a wide button open. Pro’s c-bet is small (~30% pot), so calling is cheap and preserves the implied-odds line: if I hit a set I can extract a large stack; if I don’t improve I can fold to heavy turn aggression. Raising OOP with a low pocket pair bloats the pot and creates difficult decisions; folding here surrenders equity for too little. Plan: call the 250 to see the turn, fold to big turn bets if I don’t improve, and if I hit a set pursue value (raise/shove) given effective stacks.

I just hit a set on the turn (board 7c-4h-8d-2d with my 2♠2♣). Pot = 1050, button (Pro) checked — he opened preflop and c-bet small on the flop, then checked the turn, a line consistent with medium-strength hands or pot control. This is a strong but somewhat disguised hand versus his wide opening range, and with ~7.9k effective behind there’s plenty of money to extract. I want to get value from hands like 8x/7x/mid pocket pairs and deny free rivers to any straight-type combos, so I’ll bet around half the pot to keep worse hands calling while not folding them out. Size chosen: ~50% pot = 525. If called I’ll re-evaluate the river for a larger extraction; if he check-raises big I’ll assume he’s very strong but given I hold a set I will commit (or put him to a very tough decision).

The turn is the 2d, bringing the board to 7c 4h 8d 2d. My opponent (Minni) donk bets 525 into a pot of 1050 after check-calling the flop. This line often represents a hand that has picked up equity (like a flush draw) or a made hand trying to set a price (like top pair or two pair). The 2d is a relatively blank card unless they hold specifically 22, 72, 82, or 42, or A2 suited. While 56 made a straight on the flop, a check-call line there suggests they might not have it, or are trapping. My overpair (AA) is still very likely ahead of their range, which includes top pairs (8x), draws, and smaller pairs. Raising here would likely fold out bluffs and isolate me against two-pair or better. Calling keeps their range wide, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to re-evaluate on the river. The bet size is small (1/2 pot), offering me good odds to see a showdown.

I have a disguised set (2♠2♣) on 7♣‑4♥‑8♦‑2♦‑3♥. Villain opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop and called my turn bet — a line consistent with single‑pair hands (8x, 7x, A8/A7, 7‑8 combos) and medium pocket pairs. Very few hands actually beat me (higher sets or rare 7+8 two‑pair combos), so checking the river would surrender value. I want a size that gets called by worse one‑pair / two‑pair hands but doesn’t bloat the pot unnecessarily against the rare monsters, so I’ll bet roughly half the pot. Bet 1/2 pot = 1050 to extract value; a large raise from him will be polarizing and I’ll re‑evaluate, but this size should be called by the hands I beat.

I hold Pocket Aces (AcAs) on a final board of 7c 4h 8d 2d 3h. The only made straight is 5-6, which was possible on the flop. The opponent check-called the flop, then led the turn (donk bet) on a 2d, and continues to bet half-pot on the river. The turn lead on a brick often signifies a semi-bluff (like a flush draw that missed) or a made hand like top pair trying to set the price. The river 3h completes no flush draws. While Minni could have a set (33/22) or the straight (56), the half-pot sizing and the turn lead are also consistent with hands I beat, such as top pair (8x) or a missed draw bluffing. Given I am getting 3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity), folding the top of my range (overpair) is exploitable. Raising is not viable as I likely only get called by better hands. A call is the most +EV play.

In a three-handed game, opening from the button with T8o is a standard aggressive play to attempt to win the blinds or play the hand in position. While the hand is marginal, the button advantage allows for a wide opening range. Pro in the big blind has a medium-short stack of 62 BBs, so I will use a standard 2.5x raise size to keep the pot manageable and allow for an easy fold if faced with a shove.

Fold. I have 9♠2♠ — a very weak hand even though suited. Flash opened to 250 from the button and there's still the big blind to act, so calling 200 to play a 3‑way pot out of position is a poor long‑term play: my hand will be dominated by a large portion of the button’s raising range and has very little equity multiway (well below the ~25% equity required by the 200→600 pot odds). A 3‑bet bluff to 400 is also inappropriate — this hand won’t fold out better holdings and will be crushed if called. With a healthy 23,000 stack and lots of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a playable spot is the correct decision.

The opponent on the button (Flash) raised to 250 (2.5 BB). I am in the Big Blind with Q5o. While the pot odds are roughly 27% (calling 150 to win 400), Q5 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Even in a 3-handed game where ranges are wider, Q5o is dominated by most Qx hands and struggles to realize equity post-flop. As the short stack (62 BBs) facing the chip leader, preserving chips is preferable to defending marginally negative EV spots. I will fold.

Three-handed on the button I should be opening a wide range — 3♦4♦ is very playable in position with good implied odds and backdoor flush/straight potential. Limping hands here surrenders my positional advantage and gives the big blind cheap odds to see flops; a small raise (min to 200) is too weak and invites 3-bets. I will open-raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to maximize fold equity against the short-stacked SB (Pro) and play heads-up in position vs Flash. Plan: if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously with marginal holdings; if Pro shoves (all-in) I’ll fold this marginal hand to preserve my stack; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate but lean to folding unless the sizing is small and profitable to continue.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit (Qh 8s). Minni, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 300 (3BB). Although Minni's opening range from the Button is likely wide, Q8o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against a larger stack who can apply pressure post-flop. Calling 3BBs here would likely be a negative EV play due to poor playability and reverse implied odds (domination by better Queens or Eights). 3-betting is too risky given my stack size relative to the opponents. The prudent decision is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 53s (suited connectors/gappers) and Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call. With a stack of over 30,000, calling 200 is very affordable. 53s plays reasonably well in a multi-way pot if the Small Blind calls, or heads-up if the Small Blind folds. I am getting 2.25:1 on a call (or even better if Pro calls). Given our deep stacks, I'll take a flop to see if I can hit something disguised against Minni's likely wide button range.

I have 5-high on a Jack-Nine-Six board with two diamonds. This board doesn't connect with my hand at all, as I have no pairs, no flush draws, and only very remote backdoor straight possibilities. Being out of position against Minni, the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most prudent play is to check and see how she reacts. I have no reason to lead out and build a pot with such low equity.

I opened preflop and got called; on Js 9♦ 6♦ I hold 3♦4♦ — a strong diamond draw (9 outs: ~19% to hit on the turn, ~35% by the river) with little current showdown value. As the preflop aggressor heads-up this is a textbook semi‑bluff spot: c-bet to fold out overcards/weak pairs, deny equity to non-diamond hands, and build a pot I can win if I hit. I’ll size to ~60% pot (400 chips) — large enough to generate fold equity and get value from worse draws, but small enough to control the pot versus deep stacks. Plan: if called I’ll press when a diamond arrives (value) or proceed cautiously on blank turns (check or double-barrel only on safe cards); fold to a large check-raise/all-in since I’m primarily drawing.

I have completely missed this flop with five-high and have no viable draws to a straight or flush. The board (Jack-Nine-Six with two diamonds) is fairly coordinated and likely connects with a standard Button opening range. Given that I am out of position and Minni has followed up her pre-flop raise with a significant continuation bet, there is no reason to continue with this hand. I will preserve my stack and look for better spots.

In a 3-handed game with deep stacks, I hold J9o on the Button. While this is a borderline steal candidate, my opponents in the blinds (Flash and Minni) possess massive stacks relative to mine (roughly 4x and 5x my stack respectively) and have shown active tendencies, including defending and 3-betting. Opening with an off-suit connector like J9o invites high-variance spots or forced folds against aggression where I am at a disadvantage. Preserving my 60BB stack for hands with better playability or equity is the prudent strategy here.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 3d, a very weak hand. Pro on the Button has folded, leaving only myself and Minni in the Big Blind. Minni has been playing aggressively and currently holds the second-largest stack. While it only costs an additional 50 chips to call, Q3o performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a capable opponent. Given my significant chip lead and the proximity to the tournament's end, there is no need to enter a marginal pot with a sub-par hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. In a three-handed game, while aggression is important, there is no strategic justification for playing this hand from the button, especially with a short-stacked player in the Big Blind (Pro) who might be looking for a spot to shove. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my chip lead.

Heads-up vs the big blind (Pro). A♣8♥ is a strong heads-up hand — worth taking the initiative despite being out of position. With a deep stack (~233BB) I want to open-raise to apply pressure, charge worse aces and broadways, and narrow the BB's defending range. I’ll raise to ~3x the big blind (300 total). If I face a large 3-bet or an all-in I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to extreme aggression); if called I plan to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry boards.

Current situation: 3 players left. Blinds 50/100. I am in the Big Blind with [2c Kh]. Action: Flash folds, Minni (SB) raises to 300. I have position on Minni post-flop. The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 400 (after my call, 200/600 = 33%). While K2o has reasonable raw equity against a wide SB opening range, the 3x raise size makes the price worse than a standard min-raise. K2o suffers from poor playability (reverse implied odds with the weak kicker). Against an aggressive opponent like Minni who is opening for 3x, defending K2o is marginal to losing. I choose to fold to preserve chips for better spots.

3-handed table — button should open a wide range. J♥7♠ is marginal but profitable to raise from the button to seize initiative and isolate one opponent. With blinds 50/100 (pot 150) I'll raise to 3x the BB (300) — big enough to get fold equity vs the SB and discourage limp-calling while keeping the pot controllable against deep stacks (my 23500, Pro ~6000, Flash ~30350). Plan: continuation-bet favorable flops and use position to apply pressure; fold to a large 3-bet from SB, and proceed cautiously vs strong resistance postflop.

I am holding 2h 3c in the Small Blind, which is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from the Button (Minni) to 300, I am out of position with a garbage hand. My stack is 60 BBs, so I have plenty of time to wait for better opportunities. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky against the chip leader. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7h 3s, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Although the price to call is only 200 chips, 73 offsuit plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. As the chip leader, I want to avoid unnecessary losses with trash hands. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am on the Button with QJo in a 3-handed game. My stack is 60BB, which allows for standard play. QJo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. My standard opening size has been 2.5x (250), which has worked well in previous hands (e.g., Hand #90, #99, #108). Raising puts pressure on the blinds; if they fold, I take the pot; if they call, I play in position. I will raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x. While I have a significant chip lead and the pot odds are tempting, Jack-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position, especially with a deep-stacked Big Blind yet to act. Given the current tournament stage (hand 114 of 150), preserving my stack and avoiding tricky post-flop situations with weak hands is the most prudent strategy. Folding is the standard play here to minimize risk.

Pro opened to 250 from the button. I’m in the big blind with A♠9♥ — a strong heads-up hand but one with a medium kicker that is vulnerable to better Ax hands. Calling 150 more (total commitment 250) is the standard play: it keeps my range wide, gets a good price to see a flop (150 into ~400), and avoids bloating a pot out of position by 3-betting and polarizing. Effective stacks (~57bb vs Pro) are deep enough to play postflop, so I’ll call and evaluate the flop — play aggressively if I hit top pair or strong draws, otherwise control pot size and fold to heavy aggression.

Flop: 8c 9d Qd. I hold A♠9♥ — middle pair with the best possible kicker against most worse nines and a lot of the preflop raiser’s checking range. There are two diamonds on board so I need to deny equity to flush draws and charge any straight/diamond draws and weaker pairs. Pro checked the flop, showing weakness or a wide checking range I can exploit. I’ll bet for value and protection: ~1/2 pot is big enough to charge draws and get called by worse (K9, T9, A9-type hands, weak Qx floats) while folding out pure air. Plan: bet 300; if Pro raises substantially I’ll assume he has a strong Q or a big draw and will fold to conserve my stack; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed depending on the card (continue vs safe cards/if I improve, pot control vs dangerous cards).

I hold QJo on a board of 8c 9d Qd. I have Top Pair with a good kicker. My opponent, Minni, has donk-bet (led into the pre-flop raiser) 300 into a pot of 550. Reviewing Minni's history (e.g., Hand #86, #96, #97), they have a tendency to donk-bet with a polarized range that includes Top Pair weak kicker and semi-bluffs/air. Raising here would likely fold out their bluffs and isolate me against better hands or strong draws. Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range wide, control the pot size in position, and re-evaluate on the turn. Given the wet texture, I want to continue, but a call is superior to a raise to extract value from their aggressive tendencies.

Turn 7♦ completes a three-diamond board (Q♦ 9♦ 8♣ 7♦). I have A♠9♥ — just one pair with no diamond and a vulnerable hand against any two-diamond holdings. Pro called my flop bet, then checked the turn; that line can be either weakness (Qx, medium pair) or a trap from a completed flush, so betting into this texture risks getting raised/flattened by better hands. Pot control is the priority: check and let him show aggression. Plan: if he checks back we go to showdown; if he bets a small-to-medium amount I’ll call to extract from worse Qx/9x hands; if he fires a large bet or jams I’ll fold because I’m often behind to a flush or two pair. Therefore: check.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of 8c 9d Qd 7d. The turn card (7d) is very coordinated, bringing a third diamond for a flush and completing a straight for J-T. Minni donk-bet the flop but checked the turn. This check likely indicates pot control with a marginal made hand (like a worse Q, 9, or 8) or fear of the wet board texture, though a trap with a flush or straight is possible. Betting here yields little value as worse hands likely fold on this scary board, and better hands (flushes, straights, two pair) will call or raise, putting me in a difficult spot. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid being check-raised, and realize my equity to the river where I can re-evaluate or showdown cheaply.

River (8♦) pairs the board and there are now four diamonds out (9♦, Q♦, 7♦, 8♦). I hold A♠9♥ — only one pair (nines) with no diamond. Pro’s line (raise preflop, call my flop bet 300, check turn and check river) is consistent with a lot of hands that beat me: any single diamond in his hand is now a flush, Qx or 9x give him two pair, and hands like 8x or combos including an 8 could make trips/full house. Betting here would have very poor value — most hands that will call me (diamonds, Qx, 9x) beat me, and the hands I actually beat are unlikely to call a river bet. I also have essentially no fold equity against his calling/checking range. With the pot only 1,150 and a short-ish opponent (Pro ~5,450), I don’t want to bloat my risk when I’m usually behind. Best play is to check and take my chances at showdown.

The board is extremely wet: [8c, 9d, Qd, 7d, 8d]. There are four diamonds on the board, making a flush very likely for any opponent holding a single diamond. Additionally, the board is paired (8s), bringing full house possibilities into play. I hold [Jh, Qc], giving me Two Pair (Queens and Eights). Minni donk-bet the flop but checked the Turn and River as the board got progressively scarier (completing the flush and pairing the board). Betting here has no value: worse hands (like a 9 or a lower pair without a diamond) will fold, and better hands (any flush, trips, full house) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to get to showdown cheaply and potentially win against a hand like a 9x or a busted straight draw that lacks a diamond.

I have pocket sixes on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially play in position against the blinds. My stack is healthy, and while Pro is the short stack, 66 is strong enough to open and evaluate based on their reaction.

I have 8♣4♠ (8h4s) in the small blind facing a button open to 300 in a three-handed pot. This is a very weak, poorly-connected hand that will be out of position versus both opponents postflop and is frequently dominated by the button's opening range. The pot is 450 and I must call 250 (300 total) — needing ~35.7% equity to break even — which 8‑4o doesn't have against these ranges. Deep stacks don't change that: speculative play out of position here is costly and unnecessary. Preserve the stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 8c facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). Although I have 65 BBs, 85o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win 650 (~30.7%), but my realized equity will likely be significantly lower than my raw equity due to position and hand strength. Against a 3x open, folding is the standard and prudent play to preserve chips.

Pocket kings on the button in a three-handed spot is a clear value hand — I want to take the lead preflop, isolate one opponent if possible, and deny cheap odds to speculative hands. The pot is 150 and to-call is 100; the minimum raise is 200. Opponents: Pro (SB) has ~6.4k and opens/plays aggressively from the small blind at times; Flash (BB) is very deep and capable of 3-bets. I choose a sizing that builds a decent pot and reduces callers but doesn't overcommit against a big 3-bet or shove. Raising to 600 (total) is a strong, standard open in this spot: it pressures single-pair and drawing hands, keeps us in control postflop, and still leaves room to maneuver against a 3-bet. Postflop plan — continue as the aggressor with solid C-bets for value and protection; if Flash 3-bets moderately I'll call and play postflop (or 4-bet shove only vs very large 3-bets depending on sizes); if Pro (shorter stack) shoves, I'll heavily favor a call since KK has excellent equity vs his shove range. Overall this line maximizes fold equity and value while managing tournament risk.

The opponent (Minni) has consistently raised to 300 (3BB) in previous hands. A sudden increase to a 600 (6BB) open raise represents a significant deviation and likely indicates a very strong hand range or a specific polarized strategy. Holding Q8s in the Small Blind puts me in the worst position with a hand that is easily dominated (by AQ, KQ, pairs) and suffers from reverse implied odds, especially against such a large opening size. Calling requires committing nearly 10% of my stack pre-flop with a marginal hand out of position. Preserving my 64BB stack for better opportunities is the prudent decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened with a large raise to 600, which is significantly larger than the standard 2x-3x opening. While I have a large stack and the odds to call are okay, Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the size of the raise and the fact that I'll be playing the rest of the hand first-to-act, I will choose the conservative route and fold, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with Jc8c. This is a suited two-gapper with decent playability post-flop. In a 3-handed configuration, the Button opening range is quite wide (typically 40-50%+), and J8s falls well within this range. My stack is 64.5 BBs, allowing me to open-raise comfortably without being pot-committed. The blinds (Flash and Minni) have large stacks and may defend, but I will have the advantage of position post-flop. A standard raise size of 2.5 BB (250 chips) is appropriate to balance between stealing the blinds and building a pot with a playable hand.

I have pocket tens (TT), which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. Pro is opening from the button with a relatively short stack (62 BBs). I will 3-bet to 850 to take the initiative, charge Minni (the Big Blind) to enter the pot, and potentially isolate Pro or get them to commit their stack with a weaker range. TT is well ahead of most button-opening ranges.

Fold. I’m in the big blind with 9♠8♥ facing a 3-bet to 850 from the small blind after the button opened to 250. Pot is 1200 and I must call 750 (to 850 total) — with an offsuit, marginal connector out of position this is a poor spot. The button still acts and has a 6,200 stack (can shove/isolate), while the SB is clearly aggressive; calling likely puts me into a multi-way OOP pot with difficult post‑flop decisions and limited implied odds for this hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better, positionally favorable spot.

I opened to 250 with Jc8c on the Button, and the Small Blind (Flash) 3-bet to 850. The Big Blind folded. I need to call 600 chips into a pot of 1200, giving me 33% pot odds. While I have position, J8s is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by the SB's 3-betting range (which includes strong aces, pairs, and better suited broadways). Against a standard strong 3-bet range, my equity is right around 32-33%, making this a break-even spot at best purely on math, but with significant reverse implied odds if I hit a pair of Jacks. As the shortest stack at the table (though still deep with ~60BB), I should avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots against the chip leaders. Folding is the disciplined decision.

I am on the button with Ac 8s in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand for this position. With a significant chip lead and only 32 hands remaining in the tournament, I want to maintain pressure on the shorter stacks. Pro (Big Blind) is relatively short with 6,100 chips, so I expect them to defend or shove frequently. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I have 6♦7♦ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300. With deep effective stacks and a playable suited connector, I want to realize equity and keep the pot manageable — calling preserves implied odds and lets me see a flop with multi-way potential. 3-betting here is suboptimal (I’ll be out of position versus the opener and could get isolated by the BB’s response), and folding is too tight vs a 3x open given these stacks. Plan: call the extra 250 (300 total), see the flop, play draws aggressively if I hit strong equity (flush/straight/pair+draw) and control the pot or fold to heavy pressure if I miss and face big action.

Current blinds are 50/100. I am in the Big Blind with [8d, 3d]. The Button (Flash) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Minni) called. The pot is currently 700 chips, and it costs me 200 chips to call, offering pot odds of roughly 22%. Although the price is cheap, [8d, 3d] is a very weak hand (bottom 20% range) and plays poorly in multi-way pots, especially when sandwiched between the Small Blind and the aggressor on the Button. The reverse implied odds are high (dominating flushes, kicker problems), and equity realization will be difficult out of position. As the short stack with 61BBs, preserving chips is preferable to defending a marginal-at-best hand in a 3-way pot.

Flop Js 9d 6s with 6♦7♦: I’ve flopped middle pair (sixes) with a weak kicker and only a runner‑runner diamond possibility. I’m out of position to the preflop opener (Flash), whose c‑bet range contains many hands that currently beat me (Jx, 9x, overpairs) but also lots I beat (Ax/Kx/Qx, some draws). With stacks deep relative to the pot (stacks ~22.8k / 30.4k and pot 700) I want to keep the pot under control OOP and realize equity rather than build a big pot with a marginal hand. Checking lets him c‑bet so I can call a standard small/half‑pot bet (break‑even ~25% equity) and fold to large aggression, or see a free turn if he checks back. Plan: check now, call reasonable c‑bets to continue, fold to big raises unless I improve.

I have Ace-high on a board that is somewhat connected and likely to have hit the Small Blind's calling range (which often includes middle pairs, suited connectors, and broadway cards). While I was the pre-flop aggressor, betting into a board like J-9-6 is risky against a large stack like Minni's, as she is unlikely to fold a Jack, a Nine, or a straight draw. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity by seeing a free turn card, keeping the pot manageable against the only other player who can significantly damage my stack.

I’m first to act OOP with 6♦7♦ — I have middle pair (sixes) with a weak kicker. The 2♠ turn doesn’t improve me and actually gives the opener more spade/flush equity; Flash checked the flop, so his range is wide (lots of Jx/9x/overcards and some draws). With a tiny pot (700) and deep stacks, my priority is pot control OOP: betting here risks a raise and a difficult decision with a marginal hand, while checking keeps the pot small and lets me realise equity or fold to clear strength. Plan: check the turn, see the river, and only continue vs a small bet or if I improve — fold to heavy aggression.

The turn brings the deuce of spades, completing a potential flush. I hold the eight of spades, giving me a flush draw to the fourth-best flush. Minni has checked for the second street in a row, which suggests she is likely not holding a strong hand or a completed flush. Given that I have Ace-high and a flush draw, I have significant equity. Checking back here allows me to see a free river card and potentially realize my equity without risking a check-raise from the only player who can seriously damage my chip lead. This maintains pot control and follows the strategy of minimizing risk against the other big stack.

I have middle pair (6s) with a weak kicker and I’m out of position. Flash checked every street, showing a lot of weakness or a desire to pot‑control/slowplay, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. The pot is only 700 and our stacks are deep — a thin river bet would get called by some worse high‑cards but can also be called or raised by many hands that beat me (Qx, Jx, 9x). Betting risks facing a raise and a difficult decision with little equity. Best line is pot control: check to showdown and only call a small bet; fold to meaningful aggression. Action: check.

I am holding Ace-high (Ac 8s) on a board of Js-9d-6s-2s-Qd. The action has been checked through the flop and turn, and Minni has checked to me again on the river. My Ace-high has a reasonable amount of showdown value against missed straight draws like T8 or 75, or smaller Ace-high hands. While a bet might force a fold from a weak pair, there is little incentive to turn a hand with showdown value into a bluff in a small pot, especially as the tournament leader. Checking back allows me to reach showdown safely and preserve my stack.

Button with 5c7h — a weak, offsuit hand but cheap to see a flop in position. The pot is only 150 and calling 100 is low-risk: I keep the pot small, retain positional advantage, and can fold to significant preflop aggression. Flash (BB) is deep and defends wide, and Pro (SB) is relatively short and could shove if I open-raise, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I will call to realize equity and play the flop in position; if faced with a sizable 3-bet/shove I'll fold this holding.

I am in the Small Blind with K8o. The Button (Minni) has limped for 100. The pot is currently 250. It costs me 50 to call. With pot odds of 5:1, folding is too tight given I have a King. Raising is risky as I will be out of position against a deep-stacked opponent who might be trapping or willing to call, and my hand isn't strong enough to value raise comfortably. The best play is to complete the blind cheaply and see a flop.

I am in the big blind with 9c-8c, a strong suited connector that performs well in multi-way pots. The button limped and the small blind completed, so I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to build the pot. Given that I have the chip lead and am out of position against Minni, checking is the most prudent play to realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily. This allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay my opponents on later streets if I connect well.

In a limped pot, I flopped middle pair (8s) with a King kicker on a Td 5s 8s board. The board is wet with a flush draw. Being out of position in the Small Blind, leading out (donk betting) is risky as it bloats the pot with a marginal hand and opens me up to raises from draws or better hands (Tens). Checking is the standard and safest line to control the pot size. I intend to call a reasonable bet given my pair and kicker, but for now, I will check to see the action from the Button and Big Blind.

I have middle pair (8s) with a 9 kicker in a limped, three-way pot. The board is [Td 5s 8s], which contains a flush draw and potential straight draws. After Pro checks in the small blind, I will also check. Middle pair is a marginal hand that benefits from keeping the pot small, especially when out of position against the button (Minni). By checking, I can see how the button reacts and decide my next move on the turn based on the action and the card that falls.

Flop Td 5s 8s — I hold 5c7h: middle pair with a weak kicker, but I’m on the button and both players checked to me. In position I can extract thin value from worse one-pair hands and charge any draws while keeping the pot manageable. A half-pot c-bet is the right balance: it gets called by weaker pairs/overcards and many floats, and it makes players pay for turn cards that could outdraw me. I will bet 150 into the 300 pot. Plan: fold to a large check-raise/shove (I’m beat by sets/top pair strong tens/eights); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously — raise/continue only with improvement or a clear read.

I hold middle pair (Eights) with a King kicker on a T-8-5 board. The board has a flush draw (two spades), and I hold the King of spades, which is a relevant blocker/potential back door draw, though not to the nuts. The opponent on the button bet half pot (150 into 300) in a limped pot. This range includes top pair (Tens), draws (flush/straight), and some weaker pairs or air. My hand is currently ahead of draws and air, and has decent equity against some made hands. Getting 3:1 pot odds, folding is too tight for middle pair with a good kicker. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws. The prudent play is to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have middle pair (eights) with a nine kicker on a board of T-5-8. In this three-way limped pot, Minni (the button) has made a half-pot bet, and Pro (the small blind) has called. My hand is too strong to fold given the excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win 600), as I likely have significant equity against their ranges which include flush draws, straight draws (like 6-7 or 7-9), and lower pairs. However, raising is unnecessary as it would bloat the pot and potentially force out weaker hands I currently beat, while getting me into trouble against a Ten or a better Eight. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn card.

I turned two pair (Kings and Eights) on a board of Td 5s 8s Kd. This is a very strong hand, currently only beaten by sets (TT, 55, though unlikely given action) as there are no possible straights yet (gaps are 6,7 and 9 and J,Q). The board is wet with straight draws (QJ, 97, 76) and flush draws (spades and diamonds). Minni bet the flop and is the aggressor. Flash called behind. Checking is the standard line to allow the aggressor to barrel again with a King, a Ten, or a semi-bluff with a draw. Leading out might fold out bluffs or weaker hands that would otherwise bet. I want to extract maximum value, and checking to induce a bet is the best play.

I currently hold middle pair (eights) with a nine kicker on a board of T-5-8-K. The King of Diamonds is an overcard to my pair and potentially improves my opponents' ranges in this three-way pot. Since I am out of position and the pot was limped pre-flop, my hand is a classic bluff-catcher or a marginal showdown-value hand. Checking is the most appropriate action here to control the pot size and see how Minni (the button) acts. If Minni checks behind, I get a free look at the river; if Minni bets, I can re-evaluate based on the bet sizing and Pro's reaction.

I c-bet the flop (150) to extract thin value and charge draws; both players called. The turn (Kd) doesn’t improve my 5♣7♥ and actually helps many Kx combos that beat me. Both opponents checked to me, which can be weakness or a trap, but with two callers and Pro being relatively short (5,850) who could shove, my middle pair is too marginal to build the pot. Per my pre-stated plan to proceed cautiously when I don’t improve, I’ll check back the turn and see the river. If I face significant river pressure I will fold unless I improve or get a very strong read; if checked to on the river I’ll re-evaluate based on the runout and opponent tendencies.

I hold K8o and have turned two pair on a board of Td 5s 8s Kd, with the river falling Jh. The final board is Td 5s 8s Kd Jh. The pot is 750. Minni (BTN) bet the flop but checked back the turn, which caps her range and makes strong hands like better two-pair (KJ, KT) or sets unlikely, as those would typically bet the turn for protection and value. The river Jack completes potential straights for Q9 and AQ, and gives opponents with KJ a better two pair. However, given the turn check, I am likely ahead of Minni's range (weak Kings, Tens, Jacks, or missed draws). Being out of position with a stack of roughly 60BBs relative to the blinds but effectively 25-30BBs, I want to get to showdown cheaply. Betting forces me to fold to a raise and likely only gets called by hands that beat me or chop, whereas checking may induce a bluff from missed draws or a thin value bet from a worse hand like a Jack or weak King. Checking is the prudent play to realize my equity.

The river is the Jack of Hearts, completing a very coordinated board (Td-5s-8s-Kd-Jh). I have a pair of eights, which was middle pair on the flop but is now the fourth pair on the board. In a three-way pot that was checked around on the turn, my hand has some marginal showdown value, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if significant action occurs. Betting would likely only get called by better hands (Jacks, Tens, Kings, or straights) and fold out hands I already beat. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply and potentially win against missed draws or a lower pair (like a 5). Given Pro has already checked, I will check as well.

Board: Td 5s 8s Kd Jh. I hold 5c7h — a single pair of fives with a weak kicker. I c-bet the flop to extract and was called; the K on the turn brought many Kx and broadway combos that now beat me, so I checked turn and saw both opponents show more passive lines by checking back. On the river both players checked again, which is either missed draws/weak pairs or a cautious line with marginal made hands. Betting here would largely fold out worse hands (so little value) and could induce a shove or a call from hands that already beat me (Tx/Jx/Kx), especially risky given Pro is short and Flash calls wide. With two opponents and only thin value, the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and take showdown rather than build the pot with a marginal hand.

I hold 7s 2s on the button. Despite the positional advantage, 7-2 suited is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em due to its lack of high card value and poor connectivity. With a healthy stack of 66 BBs, there is no pressure to make a move with such a speculative hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Folded to me in the small blind with Ace-Six offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the big blind, as Ace-high is ahead of a wide range of hands that the big blind might defend with.

I’m in the big blind with J♥8♣ facing a 300 open from the small blind. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me to call — I’m getting ~33% pot odds (200 to win ~600), and with deep stacks (≈230bb) J8o has reasonable equity and post‑flop playability against what looks like a fairly wide/regular open range from Flash. A 3‑bet would be a marginal bluff and can get me in trouble if he jams or 4‑bets; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call to 300 total, see the flop and proceed (cautious vs big action on dry high boards, aggressive with top pair or strong draws).

As the pre-flop aggressor, I'm making a standard continuation bet on this King-high flop which generally favors my perceived range. Betting 300 (half the pot) allows me to take down the pot immediately if Minni missed, while still allowing me to get away cheaply if she shows strength. My Ace-high has some showdown value, but I want to maintain the initiative.

Board K♠ 9♠ 4♥; I have J♥8♣ — no pair, only 6 clean outs (3 Js + 3 8s) to pair by river. Flash bet 300 into a 600 pot (900 after his bet) so calling 300 would risk 300 to win 1200 — I need ~25% equity to break even. My two‑card equity to make at least a pair is ~24–25% (marginal), but several factors push me to call: Flash opened from the SB and likely has a fairly wide c‑bet range, I'm acting after him (can float and use position), stacks are deep so implied odds are meaningful, and a raise/bluff here with almost no showdown equity is risky. Plan: call 300 to see the turn, fold to large aggression if he ramps up, and if I pick up a pair or get a favorable runout I can apply pressure or extract value. Therefore: call 300.

The turn pairs the King on the board. Since I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, checking now is a prudent move to control the pot. My Ace-high still has showdown value against her draws (like hearts) or smaller pairs that might check back. If she bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking allows me to navigate the hand without over-committing with just Ace-high on a paired board.

Board is K♥ 9♠ 4♥ / K♦ — I hold J♥8♣ with no pair and no real draws. Flash was the pre‑flop raiser and c‑bettor on the flop but checked the turn, which is consistent with either a missed continuation range or a trap (now trips) after the turn pairs the board. Betting here with virtually no showdown equity is risky — I can easily get called by A‑x or run into Kx and get jammed on. With deep stacks I should preserve chips and see the river for a free card or to use position on the river. Action: check and re‑evaluate based on the river card and Flash’s action.

The board has become quite coordinated with the King pairing on the turn and a Queen appearing on the river. My Ace-high (A6) has lost relative strength as the board developed. However, after Minni checked back the turn, it's less likely she has a King. The Queen might have helped some of her range (like AQ or QJ), but many of her hands that would call a flop bet but check a turn are either small pairs or missed draws. By checking, I'm looking to get to a cheap showdown. I don't see much value in betting as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I can potentially catch a bluff or win against missed draws by checking.

Check. On 4h 9s Kh Kd Qh with Jh8c I have no pair and essentially J‑high; my hand has almost no showdown value. Flash opened pre, c‑bet the flop (I called) then checked the turn and checked the river — that line is consistent with both missed c‑bet hands and Kx/trip/Qx type hands that will call a river bluff. The river Q improves many calling hands (Qx, Kx, pairs) and my single heart doesn’t produce a flush — fold equity is small. The pot (~1200) is small relative to stacks, so risking chips for a thin bluff is negative EV. I check and will fold to any significant river raise/shove; if checked through I take my free showdown.

I am on the button with 8s 6c. While it is a 3-handed game and I am the chip leader, 86 offsuit is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop or against a 3-bet. The Big Blind (Pro) has a 65 BB stack and might be looking to shove or 3-bet aggressively to climb back into the game. Given my current lead and the marginal nature of the hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

Heads-up from the small blind with A♥T♠ — a strong, playable hand that plays well postflop and has good showdown value. Button folded so I’m opening against the BB (Pro) who has 6.5k (≈65bb). I want initiative and fold equity without committing too much OOP, so I’ll raise to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total). If called I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and range (likely fold to an isolating shove/very large 3-bet).

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4c facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni in the Small Blind. Although I would have position post-flop and Minni is opening frequently, 94s is a very weak hand (two-gapper, low ranks). Against a 3bb open, the pot odds (call 200 to win 400, 33%) are not compelling enough to defend with this specific holding, which will often be dominated or fail to realize its equity. Preserving my stack (65bb) for better spots is prudent.

Hole cards 8♠3♥ — a very weak, offsuit, unplayable hand. Although I’m on the button in a three-handed spot (which sometimes justifies wider opens), 8-3 has almost no equity and terrible post‑flop playability. The pot is only 150 and calling 100 or min‑raising to 200 to try a steal is unnecessary risk against two capable opponents (Pro will often isolate/3‑bet; Flash is deep and calls). Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o, a very weak hand. The Button (Minni) has folded. The Big Blind (Flash) has a massive stack (300BB+) compared to my 64BB. J5o performs poorly post-flop and is dominated by most calling ranges. While blind-vs-blind scenarios encourage wider ranges, J5o is typically at the bottom of or outside a profitable opening range, especially out of position against a chip leader who can apply significant pressure. Preserving my 64BB stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy here.

I hold Pocket Aces (As Ad) on the Button, the best starting hand in Hold'em. My stack is 64.5 BBs. The table is 3-handed. It is standard and optimal to raise for value here to build the pot and charge the blinds to see a flop. A raise to 2.5x (250) is a standard opening size that balances value generation with keeping opponents in the hand.

I am holding 8c 4s in the small blind. Pro, who is the short stack at the table, has raised from the button. This is a standard steal position, but my hand is essentially trash and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. There is no reason to defend with such a weak hand against a raise, even if the raise is small.

Heads-up as the big blind against a button open to 250 (SB folded). I have T♥9♦ — a playable, connected hand that does well in multi-street play heads-up and has plenty of equity against a typical open range. The pot is 400 and I only need 150 to call, so I’m getting ~27% pot‑odds (150/(400+150)). That’s well within the hand’s equity vs a BTN opening range, and with deep stacks I have good implied odds to realize value when I connect. I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a marginal 3‑bet; I’ll call, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (bet/raise when I hit or check‑fold / pot‑control when I miss).

Heads‑up, out of position on a Q♦ 7♣ 6♦ flop with T♥9♦. I missed — no pair — but I have a gutshot to an 8 (4 clean outs) plus three remaining tens and three remaining nines as pair‑outs (roughly ~30–35% equity to improve to a competitive hand by river). With deep stacks and out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand; checking lets me realize equity and see how Pro reacts. Plan: check and call small/medium continuation bets (≤ ~1/2 pot) to see the turn, fold to very large sizing that prices me poorly, and if I hit an 8/9/10 or pick up a diamond on the turn I’ll start betting or raising for value/semi‑bluff accordingly. If Pro checks back I’ll either check down or take a controlled stab on a safe turn card.

I hold Pocket Aces on a Qd 7c 6d board. This texture is somewhat wet with straight and flush draws present. While I likely have the best hand, checking back gives free cards to draws that could outdraw me or kill my action on the turn. I want to extract value from top pairs (Qx), middle pairs, and draws. A continuation bet of roughly 1/3 to 1/2 pot is standard to get value while keeping the opponent's range relatively wide. The pot is 550, so a bet of 200 achieves this purpose.

Heads‑up out of position on Q♦7♣6♦ with T♥9♦. I missed the flop but have 10 clean outs to improve (4 eights, 3 tens, 3 nines) — roughly ~38% to hit by the river — plus a tiny double‑diamond backdoor possibility. Pro's flop sizing is small (~200 into ~750), so calling 200 gives me ~21% pot odds (200/(750+200)), well below my hand's equity. Calling keeps the pot controlled OOP, realizes equity, and lets me continue on favorable turns (8/9/10 or a harmless card). Raising OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and is unnecessary; folding is too passive given the price. Plan: call the 200; if I pair or make the straight on the turn I'll press for value, if I miss and face large turn aggression I'll fold.

Out of position with Th9d on Qd 7c 6d 4d. The turn puts me on a very healthy drawing hand: I now have a four‑card diamond flush draw (9 diamond outs) plus additional straight and pair outs (unique outs ≈ 17) — roughly ~36–38% to improve by the river. Pro checked to me, which shows weakness/pot‑control and makes a lead OOP riskier (I can easily run into a check‑raise or a call from better Qx hands). I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with only a draw, so I’ll check and realize my equity for one card. Plan: take the free river — if Pro bets a small/medium sizing on the river (roughly ≤ ~50–60% of the pot) I will call for equity; if I hit a flush/straight/pair I’ll extract value; if I miss and face a large river bet I will fold. Checking keeps options open and minimizes risk while preserving strong equity to win the pot on the river.

I hold the Ad on a board of Qd 7c 6d 4d, giving me the nut flush. The board is now very scary with three diamonds, and the 4d connects with potential straights. Betting here likely folds out all hands worse than a flush or a set, and since I block the nut flush draw, it is less likely my opponent has a strong draw to call with. Checking back allows me to disguise the strength of my hand (trapping), potentially inducing a bluff from Minni on the river or allowing her to improve to a second-best hand (like a lower flush or full house) that can pay off a value bet on the river. I cannot be outdrawn, so giving a free card is not risky.

Check. I made a marginal one‑pair (9s) on the river, but the line (BTN open preflop, small flop c‑bet, then check turn) is highly consistent with a lot of Qx / medium value hands and missed diamond draws. Many of those Qx combos still beat my pair and will happily call a small river value bet; a small bet would mainly fold out air and get called by hands that beat me, making a value bet thin or negative EV. Pot is only 950 and villain still has 6k behind — no reason to bloat the pot OOP with a second‑best hand. I’ll check to realize showdown equity; if Pro then fires a small river bet I’ll call for value (and fold to a large jam).

I hold Pocket Aces (As Ad) on a board of Qd-7c-6d-4d-9c. There are three diamonds on the board, meaning I hold a pair of Aces with the Ace of Diamonds blocker, not a flush. The previous thought process appears to have misread the board state; strictly speaking, I have one pair. My opponent checked the flop, checked the turn, and checked the river. This line suggests a marginal made hand like Top Pair (Qx), a middle pair, or a missed draw. While I likely have the best hand, the board is very wet with flush and straight possibilities (5-8 completes a straight). Betting for thin value against a Queen is risky because the runout is scary for one-pair hands, meaning she might fold worse hands, and I would only get action from hands that beat me (Two Pair, Sets, Straights, Flushes). Furthermore, betting opens me up to a check-raise which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value and win the pot against Qx without exposing my stack to variance on a dangerous board.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with King-Four suited. This is a standard opening range hand. I want to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand with position. Pro is the short stack in the Big Blind with about 68 BBs, so I need to be aware of potential shoves, but Kc4c is strong enough to open here. I'll stick with my standard opening size of 300 chips.

Pocket nines in the SB vs a 3BB button open are worth playing but I'm out of position postflop. The min‑raise to 500 is too small to accomplish a clean isolation and would often commit me OOP into a bigger pot or induce 3‑bets; folding is too weak with this holding. I’ll call the extra 250 (300 total including my 50 SB) to keep the pot controlled, preserve deep stack SPR for postflop play and set‑mining, and re‑evaluate based on the BB’s action — folding to a large 3‑bet or continuing heads‑up vs the button if the BB folds.

I am in the Big Blind with [8c Td] facing a Button raise from Flash and a Small Blind call from Minni. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call, offering pot odds of approximately 22% (200/900). While the price is cheap, T8 offsuit is a weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially when acting out of position relative to the pre-flop aggressor. My raw equity might be close to the pot odds, but my realizable equity will be significantly lower due to positional disadvantage and the likelihood of domination. As the shortest stack (approx 68 BBs), I should preserve chips and avoid marginal spots with low playability. Folding is the prudent decision.

I have a very strong hand — pocket 9s turned into a set on Q♠ 9♦ 4♥. I'm heads‑up vs the preflop aggressor (button) whose range is wide (many Qx, KQ, broadway hands, some A‑x and bluffs). This is a relatively dry flop with little equity for most of his air, so I should bet for value and to deny free cards. A ~50% pot bet keeps worse one‑pair hands and some worse queens calling while not pricing out too much action from stronger hands. Plan: bet for value now (if he raises big I'll reevaluate and lean toward calling/shoving given stack depth; if called I will continue extracting on later streets).

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. Minni has led out for half the pot after calling pre-flop. In a three-handed game, bottom pair is often good enough to continue for at least one street, especially in position. Raising seems unnecessary as it would likely only be called by better hands, and folding is too weak. I will call to see how the turn develops.

I flopped a set of nines (9c9h on Qs 9d 4h) and led the flop for 350; Flash called. The turn is 3c — a very dry card with essentially no legitimate draws to worry about. Pot is 1400 and both stacks are deep (21650 vs 30100), so I should keep extracting value rather than slow‑playing. Flash’s calling range from the flop includes a lot of Qx, KQ/broadway combos, weaker pocket pairs and occasional bluffs — many of which will pay to see another bet. I’ll bet ~60–70% pot to charge one‑pair hands and medium pairs while not pricing them all out: bet 900. If he flats I can continue extracting on the river; if he raises big I’ll strongly consider calling or shoving given the strength of my hand.

In this hand, I am on the Button with bottom pair (fours) and a King kicker. Pre-flop, I raised and Minni called from the Small Blind. On the flop of Qs-9d-4h, Minni led out with a small donk bet of 350, which I called in position to see if the action would slow down on the turn. The turn is the 3c, a relatively blank card. Minni has now fired a second, much larger barrel of 900 into a 1400 pot. This 'donk-and-barrel' line in a three-handed game typically represents a polarized range, but given the dry nature of the board, it is more likely that Minni has at least a 9 or a Queen, or is aggressively playing a straight draw like J-T. With only bottom pair and the action escalating, my hand is too weak to continue against significant pressure. Calling again would likely lead to a difficult decision on the river, and raising would turn my marginal value hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

Button with Q♠J♥ — a playable, positionally strong hand. Pot is only 150 and it’s just the blinds to act, so I want to take the initiative and apply pressure rather than limp. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (to 300 total) — big enough to fold out weak blinds and isolate heads-up, small enough that I’m not overcommitted if the short SB jams. Postflop I’ll use position to continue with top-pair/strong draws and proceed cautiously versus heavy 3-bets.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Minni). Although I have a playable stack of 68 BBs, playing K7o out of position is difficult and likely -EV. The hand is easily dominated by better Kings and Aces that the opener likely has. Additionally, the Big Blind is yet to act and could squeeze. Given the stack depths and the marginal nature of the hand, folding is the standard and safest play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am holding Ace-Six offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised from the button in a 3-handed game, which is a very wide range. With a stack of 30,000 and the pot offering me 2.25:1 odds (calling 200 to win 450), this is a mandatory defend. I have the chip lead and can afford to see a flop. Raising is an option, but calling allows me to keep the pot controlled out of position against the second-largest stack.

On a very dry, paired board like 2-5-5, I have significant showdown value with Ace-high. Standard play in the Big Blind against a button raiser is to check the flop to the aggressor. My hand doesn't need much protection, and I can comfortably check-call most reasonable bet sizes to evaluate the turn.

Flop: 2♦-5♥-5♦. I hold Q♠J♥ — two overcards, no diamonds, no pair. Pot is 650 and both stacks are deep (I: 22,750; Flash: 29,800). This is a dry, paired low flop where my hand has only ~6 outs (~24% to improve by river) and very little backdoor equity. Flash checked the flop after calling preflop; given his tendency to call down and the likelihood he continues with middle pairs and pocket pairs, a continuation bet here is a thin bluff that will get called too often. I prefer pot control and to realize equity in position: check and see a turn. Plan: if he checks back we take a free card; if he bets a small amount on the turn I can call to try to hit, and I’ll fold to a large bet/raise unless I improve. Checking preserves chips and keeps our options open.

I am holding Ace-high on a board that remains very dry after the 8c turn. Minni's decision to check back on the flop (2-5-5) often signifies a weak-to-medium range or a total whiff. My Ace-high likely still has the best of it, but betting here doesn't achieve much; better hands like pocket pairs or an 8 won't fold, and I'd rather not face a raise. Checking is the most efficient way to realize my equity and reach a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from a weaker hand.

Board 2♦-5♥-5♦-8♣, I hold Q♠J♥ — just two overcards with only ~6 outs to pair on the river (~6/46 ≈ 13%). Pot is small (650) and it’s checked to me. Flash has shown a passive, sticky line (called preflop, checked flop and turn) so my bluffing/folding equity is low — a turn bet will be called too often by worse and seldom fold better hands. With deep stacks and little to gain, I prefer pot control and to realize equity in position: check and see the river. Plan: if he checks back I’ll show down; if he fires a small bet on the river I can call to try to hit my pair, but I’ll fold to a large bet/raise unless I improve.

I have Ace-high on a board that has been checked through on both the flop and the turn. This sequence heavily suggests that Minni has a weak range, likely consisting of missed high cards or small pocket pairs. Ace-high has decent showdown value here, and there is little to be gained by betting, as I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand or get called by a worse one. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win the pot.

I have Q♠J♥ on 2♦-5♥-5♦-8♣-7♣ — no pair, just Q-high. The board is a paired, low, fairly dry texture and Flash has taken a very passive/calling line (called preflop, checked/called flop and turn). That line is consistent with medium pairs, single-pair hands, or sticky showdown hands that will call a river bluff, so my fold equity is minimal and I don’t block the few hands that can make a straight (4+6). The pot is only 650 while both stacks are deep, so there’s no justification to risk chips with a thin river bluff. I’ll check and take it down at showdown; if Flash suddenly fires a meaningful value-sized bet I’ll fold (only an unusually tiny blocker bet would merit a call).

I am on the Button with Ac3c in a 3-handed game. This is a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. Having the Ace gives me blocker value, and the suited nature provides flush potential. My stack is 6800 (68 BBs), which is deep enough to play aggressively in position. Folding would be too passive given the short-handed dynamic. I will raise to 2.5x (250) to charge the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if I get called.

I am the chip leader with 30,400 chips and holding 6s 4c in the Small Blind. Pro has raised to 2.5x the big blind from the Button. 6-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand and playing it out of position against the button raiser, especially with the Big Blind still to act, is unprofitable. Given that I have a comfortable lead and want to maximize my stack over the 150 hands, there is no reason to take a marginal risk here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the big blind with 2♦8♥ facing a BTN open to 250 (pot 400, I must call 150). 2-8 offsuit is one of the weakest hands — unsuited, poorly connected and frequently dominated by a BTN opening range. Calling 150 into 400 gives ~27% pot odds; this hand’s equity versus a reasonable BTN open is well below that and postflop play will be extremely autopilot/marginal. A tiny 3-bet bluff to 400 is possible in theory, but with no useful blockers and ~65BB effective stacks it’s not a good spot to manufacture aggression. I’ll preserve chips and fold to the raise.

I am on the button with 3s 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Even in a three-handed game, this hand has very little equity and poor playability. Given that I am the chip leader and the tournament is approaching its final hands, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks or play sub-optimal hands from the button, especially since the short-stacked Big Blind might look to shove over an open.

I'm on the small blind with 2♦8♥ (very weak). Button has folded, so it's heads-up vs Pro (BB 6,850). Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call. Raising to the min (200 total) is a small, low-variance steal attempt that gives me initiative and decent fold equity — the raise risks an extra 150 to win the 150 in the pot, so I only need ~50% fold frequency to break even on the raise. If BB folds I pick up the pot; if BB calls I'll continuation-bet selectively and be prepared to fold to strong resistance since 2-8o plays poorly out of position. If BB 3-bets I will largely fold and conserve chips. Given the folded button and the small raise size, I prefer to apply pressure here rather than passively limp or auto-fold the blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Js Ts facing a min-raise from the Small Blind (Minni). The pot odds are excellent (getting 3:1, calling 100 to win a pot of 300), and JTs is a very strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop. Additionally, I will have a positional advantage over the Small Blind throughout the hand. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here to realize my equity and leverage position.

I raised preflop as a steal and Pro called. The flop A♠ 5♣ 6♥ is very unfavorable for my holding 2♦8♥ — no pair and no meaningful draw. The Ace on the board heavily favors the caller’s range (Ax, 5x/6x, small pairs) so my bluff equity OOP is thin and a c-bet will often be called. I don’t want to bloat the pot with nothing; I’ll check to control pot size, fold to any significant bet, and only consider calling a very small probe (≈≤1/3 pot) if I pick up a clear read that Pro c-bets extremely wide and will give up later. If Pro checks back I get a free turn to reassess. Action: check.

I am in the Big Blind with TsJs on a flop of As5c6h. Minni (SB) min-raised pre-flop and I called. Minni checked the flop. The board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Ax), though ranges are wide in blind-vs-blind. I have Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw but no immediate straight draws or pairs. Betting here is risky as it is unlikely to fold out an Ace or a pair, and I have little equity when called. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a Ten, Jack, or Spade on the turn, or evaluating Minni's action on later streets. As the short stack, preserving chips and avoiding low-equity bluffs is preferred.

I raised preflop as a steal and was called. The flop A♠5♣6♥ already crushed my 2♦8♥ (no pair, no meaningful draw) so I checked to control the pot. The Q♠ on the turn changes little — I still have no showdown value or clean bluffs OOP. Betting here would be a thin bluff into a calling range that contains many A‑x, Q‑x and small pairs; with Pro's ~6.75k effective stack against a 400 pot he can call light and I shouldn’t bloat a pot OOP with nothing. I’ll check to keep the pot small, see what Pro does on the river, and fold to any meaningful aggression.

On the Turn, the board is As 5c 6h Qs. I hold Ts Js, giving me a flush draw (with the As and Qs on board) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). Minni (SB) has checked to me again. While I have significant equity (approx. 12 outs), I currently only have Jack-high. Betting has merit as a semi-bluff to fold out small pairs or air, but if I get check-raised, I may be forced to fold my equity. Checking back guarantees I see the River card for free, fully realizing my equity. If I hit my flush or straight on the River, I can value bet then. Given the Blind vs Blind dynamic and Minni's passive line, taking the free card is the lower-variance play that maximizes my realization of equity.

I hold 2♦8♥ on A♠ 5♣ 6♥ Q♠ J♣ — no pair, no meaningful backdoor. I raised preflop as a steal and Pro called; he then checked the flop and turn, which is a passive line that can represent either a weak/marginal hand or a slow‑played strong hand (many Ax and small pairs are in his calling range). With the Ace on board and a deep opponent stack (6,750) who can comfortably call small river bluffs, my out‑of‑position bluff equity is thin. Betting here risks getting called by any Ace, any pair or many showdown‑worthy hands; the expected fold frequency is unlikely to be high enough to make a bluff profitable. I therefore avoid bloat and protect my stack: check and concede the initiative — plan to fold to any meaningful river bet.

I hold Ten of Spades and Jack of Spades on a board of As 5c 6h Qs Jc. The river Jack gives me a pair of Jacks (third pair). The action has gone check-check on the flop and turn, and the Small Blind (Minni) has checked to me on the river. Minni's range likely consists of weak made hands (low pairs, weak Aces checking for pot control) or missed draws. With third pair, I have clear showdown value. Betting is unlikely to extract value from worse hands (like 5x or 6x) as the board contains three overcards to those pairs, and better hands (Ax, Qx) will certainly call. Therefore, checking back to realize my showdown value is the optimal play.

I’m on the button with Q♠8♦ facing a raise to 200 (100 to call) with deep stacks (I have ~224bb). Q8o is marginal but has good positional value — calling preserves position and lets me see a flop cheaply against a likely wide opener. A 3‑bet here would be too speculative with this holding and folding surrenders too much equity and opportunity. Plan: call 100 to play the flop in position; c-bet or apply pressure on favorable Q/draw flops, and fold to strong aggression on dry boards unless I improve.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h4h, a suited two-gapper. The Button (Minni) has limped for 100. The pot is currently 200 (100 limp + 50 SB + 50 pending from BB, though BB is 100). Wait, pot calculation: 100 (Button) + 50 (SB) + 100 (BB anticipated) = 250 total pot after my call. To call, I need to put in 50 chips. This gives me 5:1 pot odds. My hand has reasonable playability multi-way due to flush and straight potential. Being out of position is a negative, but the price is extremely cheap (less than 1% of my stack). Folding is too tight given the odds, and raising is an unnecessary bluff with a weak hand OOP. Completing is the standard and correct play.

I have a pocket pair of sixes in the Big Blind. Both the Button and the Small Blind have limped into the pot. While checking to see a free flop is a low-variance play, raising here allows me to take control of the hand, define my opponents' ranges, and potentially win the pot immediately. A raise to 500 (5x the BB) is a standard sizing to punish limpers in a 3-handed game.

I will call to 500 (put 400 more in). Q♠8♦ is marginal but on the button with very deep stacks it’s worth seeing a flop in position against a likely wide/aggressive BB. The pot is 700 and calling 400 would make the pot 1,100, so I need ~36% equity to break even on the call; plus deep stacks give good implied odds to realize extra value when I hit. A 3‑bet/raise with Q8o is too thin — I don’t have the blockers or post‑flop playability to make a bluff 3‑bet profitable here, and folding is too nitty given position and stack depths. Plan: see the flop and play based on texture — continue with top pair or strong draws, be cautious on dry Qx boards unless I improve, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression without improvement.

Initially, I completed the small blind getting excellent odds, but the situation has changed drastically with the Big Blind raising to 500 (5BB) and the Button calling. The pot is now 1100, and I need to call 400 more. This gives me pot odds of 2.75:1, requiring about 27% equity. My hand (8h4h) is a weak suited two-gapper that performs poorly multi-way, especially out of position against a likely strong range from the Big Blind and a calling range from the Button. My equity against these ranges is likely below 20%. Preserving my stack (currently ~70BB) is preferable to entering a bloated pot with a marginal hand OOP. Folding is the clear prudent choice.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a board of Th-4d-6s. This is an extremely strong hand, particularly in a heads-up pot against the button who limped pre-flop. My objective is to build a large pot and get maximum value from hands like pocket pairs, top-pair (Tx), or straight draws (78, 57). I am out of position, so I will lead out with a bet of 650, which is approximately 60% of the pot, to price in draws and extract value from marginal made hands.

The flop T♥‑4♦‑6♠ is very dry and my Q♠8♦ has no pair and no realistic draw (only 6 outs: three queens + three eights). The pot is 1,750 and the bet is 650, so I need ~27.1% equity to break even on a call (650 / (1750+650) ≈ 27.1%). My probability to hit one of those 6 outs by the river is only ≈24.1% (1 - (41/47)*(40/46)), which is below the needed equity. Even if I pair up, many of those pairs will still lose to tens, overpairs or stronger holdings from the preflop raiser. Flash was the preflop aggressor and is continuing with strength here, so calling to “float” is a negative‑EV play on this dry texture. My preflop plan was to fold to strong aggression on dry boards unless I improved — that applies now. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 6c 5s, which is a weak off-suit connector. Although I have position, my hand strength is insufficient to profitably open-raise, especially against two deep-stacked opponents (Flash and Minni) who can easily defend or 3-bet. With 70 BBs, I can afford to wait for better opportunities. Folding is the standard play here.

I am the chip leader at a three-handed table and currently in the Small Blind. With Ace-Eight offsuit and the Button folding, I have a strong starting hand for a blind-versus-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on Minni in the Big Blind and capitalize on my hand strength and stack advantage.

Heads-up vs the SB who raised to 300. Pot is 400 and I have to call 200 more (total commitment 300). With deep stacks (21850 vs 30700) and a wide SB opening range, defending from the BB is reasonable. 9♦3♦ is a weak hand but suited with backdoor flush/straight potential; I only need ~33% equity to justify the call (200 into a 600 final pot after my call). I won’t 3-bet — 3♣9♦ has poor standalone value and a small 3-bet to 500 won’t gain reliable fold equity while bloating a pot out of position. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to see the flop, then play cautiously OOP (give up to heavy aggression on dry boards, pursue draws when the texture is favorable).

I have top pair, top kicker (Ace-Eight) on a board of 8-2-7 with two hearts. This is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to get value from weaker hands like a 7, smaller 8s, or heart draws, and to protect my equity against potential overcards.

Call. I hold 9♦3♦ on 8♥-2♥-7♣ and Flash (SB) c-bet 300 into a 900 pot. Calling 300 more (total commit 600) makes the pot ~1200, so I need ~25% equity to justify the call. I have ~6 clean pair-outs (three 9s + three 3s) giving ~24% to hit a pair by river plus a small amount of runner-runner backdoor equity — borderline but acceptable against a wide SB opening/c-bet range and given the small bet size. Raising OOP with nothing is a mistake (no fold equity, poor showdown value) and folding is too tight for the cheap price. Plan: call to realize equity, play cautiously on the turn — fold to significant aggression if I miss, continue only if I pair, pick up diamonds, or get a favorable turn action.

On the turn, the board is 8h-2h-7c-6d. I still have top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace-Eight). While the 6d connects the board slightly, making some straights possible (like 9-5 or 5-4), those are less likely for Minni to have called a pre-flop raise and a flop bet with, though not impossible. I hold the Ace of Hearts, which is a crucial blocker to the nut flush draw. I want to continue betting for value against her likely range of middle pairs (7x, 6x), smaller eights, and heart draws. A bet of 750 (approximately 62% of the pot) applies pressure and protects my equity while extracting value before the river.

Pot is 1950 and I must call 750. I hold 9♦3♦ on 8♥–2♥–7♣–6♦. On the turn I have 10 realistic outs (three 3s + three 9s + four 5s) ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river. Break‑even equity to call is ~750/(1950+750)=27.8%, so a pure pot‑odds call is slightly negative. I still call: Flash is a wide, aggressive SB raiser so his range contains many bluffs/weak pairs and I have strong implied value if I hit the 5 for the straight (or pair a 3/9). Raising OOP with nothing is a mistake (no reliable fold equity); folding now is too nitty after investing on the flop. Plan: call 750 to see the river, fold to very large river aggression if I miss, but extract value or raise if I make the straight or a clear two‑pair/better on the river.

The river 4s is a potentially dangerous card as any 5 now makes a straight (8-7-6-5-4). My top pair with the best kicker (Ace-Eight) was strong on the flop and turn, but the board has become highly connected. By checking, I can control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from missed heart draws or weaker pairs that she might have been calling with. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by many worse hands, and I could be opening myself up to a raise or calling into a straight or two-pair (like 8-7, 7-6, or 6-4). I will check and evaluate based on her action.

I missed my draw — I hold 9♦3♦ and the river (8♥–2♥–7♣–6♦–4♠) gives me nothing (no pair, no straight, no flush). Flash’s line (SB open, flop c‑bet, turn bet, then check river) is consistent with medium‑strength made hands (pocket pairs, one‑pair) or missed heart draws. A bluff here has limited fold equity because I called both the flop and turn and my range is weighted toward draws/weaker hands; any reasonable bet will be called by most one‑pair hands and risks a raise from a strong holding. Pot control is the better play: avoid throwing more chips away on a pure bluff with poor blockers and go to showdown for free. I check.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a three-handed game. This is a premium starting hand for this situation. With the chip lead and only 20 hands remaining in the tournament, I want to maintain aggression. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a superior range.

I’m in the small blind with J♠5♠ facing a button open to 300.
