J5s is a marginal suited hand that has some equity (flush/straight possibilities) but plays poorly out of position and is often dominated by the button’s raising range. With healthy stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot by 3-betting a weak hand (BB behind can complicate things and I can get 3-bet), and folding here is too passive given the cheap price to see a flop. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and potentially play heads-up versus an often-wide button range if the BB folds. Plan: call to see the flop; proceed aggressively only with clear equity (made pair, strong draw) and otherwise be prepared to check/fold to heavy action. If I face a squeeze/3-bet I’ll fold pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q3o. The Button (Flash) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Minni) called. The pot is 700 and I need to call 200, giving me pot odds of roughly 22%. Although the price is attractive, Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly in multi-way pots, especially out of position against the initial raiser. It suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated Queen) and low playability. My equity against two reasonable ranges is likely below the required 22% to break even, and realizing that equity will be difficult. Therefore, preserving my stack of ~70BB is preferable to bleeding chips with a marginal holdings in a bad spot.

Check. Out of position with J♠5♠ on 7♠ T♥ 9♥ — I have no pair, only a gutshot to an 8 (4 outs) and a runner-runner spade possibility (very unlikely). The button opened preflop and will c-bet this connected board frequently, so I want to keep the pot under control and realize equity rather than bloating the pot OOP with a weak hand. With the pot at 700 and deep stacks, I’ll check and evaluate Flash’s action: call small continuation bets to see the turn (my approx chance to make a straight by river is ~16%), but fold to large bets or raises. If I improve (an 8 or a pair) I’ll play aggressively for value; if Flash checks back I take a free turn to re-evaluate.

On a board of 7s-Th-9h, the texture is highly coordinated and favors the caller's range (Minni), which likely contains many middling connectors, pairs, and heart draws. While I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8 to complete a Jack-high straight), betting here is risky as it's unlikely to generate many folds from hands that have a piece of this board. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep the pot manageable while maintaining my significant chip lead. I can re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and Minni's action.

Board 7♠ T♥ 9♥ 6♦, my hand J♠5♠ — I’ve missed. The turn kills any backdoor flush (only one card left), so my only realistic out is an 8 (4 outs, ≈8.7%) which would give a board straight. I have no pair and very little showdown value OOP. Flash checked the flop, showing either weakness or a check‑call line, so betting the turn as a pure bluff has poor fold equity and simply bloats the pot with deep stacks. Priority is pot control and seeing the river for free; I’ll check and re-evaluate the river — only continue aggressively if I hit or the bet sizing/reads justify a call/bluff.

The board is extremely coordinated (7s-Th-9h-6d), meaning many hands in Minni's SB calling range have likely connected or have significant draws. Any 8 has now made a straight. While Minni has checked twice, betting Ace-high into this texture is risky because it is unlikely to fold out better hands (like pairs or straights) and doesn't have enough value to be a standard value bet. By checking back, I maintain pot control, realize my equity, and keep the pot small as the chip leader. If an 8 falls on the river, I make a straight; otherwise, I may still win at showdown if Minni is on a missed draw.

I missed — J♠5♠ has no pair on 7♠ T♥ 9♥ 6♦ 4♣. Preflop Flash opened and then checked flop/turn, which looks like a passive/check‑back line (weak made hands or showdown‑willing broadways). On the turn my only realistic out was an 8 (4 outs ≈ 8.7%); the river 4♣ gives me nothing. Betting as a bluff here has very low fold equity — Flash will frequently call river bets with any one‑pair hand that beats J‑high or with showdownable air — so risking chips for a small pot is a negative EV. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the tourney, the prudent line is pot control: check and fold to a river bet. If Flash checks back we go to showdown (likely losing to any pair); if he bets I will fold. Action: check.

The board is extremely connected with a one-card straight (any 8). I have AJ, which is just Ace-high on this texture. Minni has checked all three streets. While I could represent an 8 by betting, it's more prudent to take the free showdown as the chip leader. Ace-high has some marginal showdown value against missed heart draws, and there's no need to risk chips in a small pot when the board is this dangerous.

Three-handed on the button with 6♦T♥. Not a premium hand, but position + deep stack (203bb) and a shortish SB (69bb) make an open profitable frequently. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to just call; calling is too passive and invites cheap multiway pots. A min-raise to 200 is too small (gives good odds to callers and invites squeezes). I want a standard open size to (1) fold out a lot of blinds’ weak holdings, (2) keep worse hands in sometimes, and (3) leave room to fold to a large 3-bet. Plan: raise to 3x BB (300). If faced with a large 3-bet I’ll fold this marginal hand; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and play straightforwardly. Maximize fold equity while preserving postflop maneuverability.

I hold 6s 4s in the Small Blind. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 (3x BB). Being out of position with a weak suited one-gapper against a 3x open makes calling unprofitable, as it will be difficult to realize equity. The pot odds (calling 250 to win roughly 550 effectively) do not justify the disadvantage of position and hand strength. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack of 69 BBs.

With 75o in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x open from the button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, this hand performs poorly post-flop and I am currently the chip leader in the late stages of the tournament. I prefer to wait for better opportunities rather than defending with the bottom of my range.

I am on the Button with 7d 3d. This is a weak suited hand (bottom 20% range). While I have position and a healthy stack of 69 BBs, the opponents in the blinds (Flash and Minni) both cover me significantly with large stacks (326 BB and 203 BB respectively). Opening here risks getting 3-bet or called by stronger ranges, leaving me out of position against deep stacks with a weak holding. In a 3-handed game, I should open wide from the button, but 73s is below the threshold for a profitable open, especially given the stack dynamics. The risk of bleeding chips or facing aggression outweighs the potential reward of stealing the 1.5 BB pot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey of Hearts. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). While my hand is suited, it is very weak (T3s). Minni has the second-largest stack and has shown a willingness to defend. Since I have a significant chip lead and we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, there is no need to enter a pot out of position with a marginal hand against my strongest remaining opponent. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Six of spades. In a three-handed game, T6s is a marginal but acceptable hand to open with for a steal, especially given my chip lead. Opening from the button allows me to exert pressure on the blinds (Minni and Pro) and potentially take the pot pre-flop or outplay them post-flop with positional advantage. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I have JJ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 in a 3-handed game. In 3-handed play ranges are wide and pocket jacks are a strong value hand worth applying pressure with — I want to isolate the opener, gain fold equity vs weaker broadways/suited connectors, and build a pot when I’m likely ahead. I’ll 3-bet to ~3x the open (900 total) — big enough to discourage multiway action and get value from worse pairs and many Ax/Kx holdings, but small enough relative to effective stacks to allow comfortable postflop play. If I face a sizable 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate (often folding or proceeding cautiously depending on sizing); but here a 3-bet to 900 is the best balance of value and fold equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6h, a very weak unsuited hand. The action involves a Button open from Flash and a Small Blind 3-bet from Minni to 900. Facing a raise and a re-raise indicates significant strength from my opponents. My hand has very poor equity against their likely ranges, and I am the short stack with 6800 chips. Calling 800 chips here would be a losing play given the poor pot odds relative to my equity and playability. The correct decision is to fold immediately.

I opened Ten-Six of spades from the button, which is a standard steal attempt in a three-handed game. However, Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the small blind. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, T6s is too weak to comfortably call a 3-bet, even at this price. Calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop against a range that typically includes strong high cards or pairs. With only 17 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy lead, I should avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands in 3-bet pots. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Button vs only the two blinds (SB Pro 6,750; BB Flash 32,200). I have 3♥4♦ offsuit — a weak hand but on the button in 3‑handed play this is a standard open-steal candidate. A minimum open to 200 (2x BB) applies pressure, preserves stack, and buys the pot often; if I face a large 3‑bet from the SB I’ll fold given my very poor equity, but if I get one caller I can use position to C‑bet favorable flops and fold to heavy resistance. Raise small to maximize fold equity while keeping postflop decisions simple.

I hold 7c 6h in the Small Blind facing a min-raise (200) from the Button (Minni). Although the price to call is cheap (150 chips), 76o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. The potential for the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall further complicates the situation. Playing weak off-suit connectors from the worst position is generally a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 6d. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. With a call of only 100 more required into a pot that will be 450, I'm getting excellent pot odds (3.5 to 1). Although I will be out of position, Qd 6d is suited and has enough high-card value to defend against a standard button opening range in a three-handed game. I will call to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 6d. The flop is 5s 9d Ad, giving me a backdoor diamond flush draw and Queen-high. Since Minni was the pre-flop raiser on the button, this Ace-high board fits their range well. I will check to the aggressor and see how they act before deciding whether to continue in the hand.

I was the preflop raiser (button) and Flashed called from the big blind. Flop A♦ 9♦ 5♠: I have 3♥4♦ — no pair, but I do have a wheel (A‑2‑3‑4‑5) gutshot (four 2s = 4 outs) and a very small backdoor diamond possibility. Flash called preflop with a fairly wide defending range (Ax, broadways, pocket pairs, suited connectors, some diamonds). As the preflop aggressor in position I should c‑bet a meaningful but small size to (1) fold out hands like KQ/KJ/medium broadways and some small pairs that missed, (2) maintain pot control vs actual Ax or sets/pairs and (3) keep lines open to realize fold equity while preserving stack depth.

Sizing: the pot is 450. I’ll make a ~1/3‑pot continuation bet (~150). That amount buys a lot of folds from weak holdings yet is small enough to call with draws/Ax hands; if I’m called I can proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue aggressively if I improve (a 2 completes my gutshot or a diamond improves my backdoor draw) or face very weak resistance. If I face a raise or strong turn action without improvement I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack.

Action: small c‑bet 150 to leverage position, fold equity, and pot control.

I have a flush draw with the Queen and Six of diamonds on a board of 5s-9d-Ad. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a 450 pot. This small sizing (1/3 pot) gives me excellent pot odds of 4:1 to call. Given my flush draw and the fact that I am out of position, I will call the bet to try and realize my equity. A raise is unnecessary here as I want to see the turn cheaply and avoid being re-raised off my draw by a strong Ace.

On the turn, the board is 5s 9d Ad 7h. I have a flush draw with Qd 6d. I checked and called a small bet on the flop. Checking here on the turn is the most consistent play while out of position. It allows me to see how Minni reacts to the 7h and potentially see a free river card if they decide to check back. If Minni bets, I will re-evaluate based on the pot odds offered, but for now, checking is the standard move with a draw in this spot.

Turn (7♥) doesn't improve my 3♥4♦. After c‑betting 150 on the flop I was called; I now have only four 2s (≈8–9% to hit a wheel on the river) and my backdoor diamond route is gone. Flash's call/call line is consistent with many Ax, pairs (9x, 5x, mid pairs) or sticky broadways — hands that will often call a turn bet, so my fold equity is limited and I’d be investing chips with very poor raw equity. With the pot already 750 and deep stacks behind, I prefer pot control and to keep options open: check and see the river. If I miss the river I can fold to significant aggression; if I hit a 2 I can extract value. Action: check to preserve stack and realize equity while avoiding a futile semi‑bluff on a wet Ace‑high board.

I am first to act on the river with Queen-high after my diamond flush draw failed to materialize. The board is 5s-9d-Ad-7h-Jc. The turn went check-check after I called a small continuation bet from Minni on the flop. My hand has no showdown value, but the pot is relatively small (750 chips) and the board is Ace-high, which hits a lot of Minni's button-opening range. While Minni's turn check might indicate weakness, they could also be trapping or looking for a cheap showdown with a marginal hand like a 9 or a weak Ace. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament, I prefer to check and concede the pot rather than attempting a low-probability bluff into a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor.

I have 3♥4♦ and missed — no pair, no straight or flush on the river (board A♦ 9♦ 5♠ 7♥ J♣). I c-bet a small 150 on the 450-pot flop and was called; both checked the turn. Opponent’s call on the flop and subsequent pot‑control line is consistent with many Ax, pairs (9x/5x), or sticky broadways — hands that will often call a small river bluff or at least not fold enough to justify a large river bluff. My raw showdown equity is essentially zero and my fold equity is low against this range. With ~20550 behind and only 750 in the pot, there’s no need to risk chips on a low‑EV bluff late in the tournament; pot control and preserving stack for better spots maximizes long‑term expectation. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful raise.

Current hand is 135/150. I am on the Button with [Ah 5s] and a stack of 67.5 BB. In a 3-handed game, A5o is well within the standard opening range from the Button (typically top 40-50% of hands). My opponents are deep-stacked, but I have position. Folding is too tight given the few hands remaining and the value of the Ace blocker. I will make a standard open raise to steal the blinds or play in position.

I have a pocket pair of sevens in the small blind. The button (Pro) opened for 2.5x. Sevens are strong enough to 3-bet for value and to isolate the short-stacked button. Raising to 800 puts pressure on both the button and the big blind while allowing me to take control of the hand.

Fold. 2♣6♣ (2-6 offsuit) is one of the weakest hands preflop: very poor equity and terrible post‑flop playability. Pro opened and Flash 3‑bet to 800 — ranges here are strong enough that I’m routinely dominated or drawing very thin. Calling 700 to play a multiway pot out of (middle) position vs two opponents is a losing, high‑variance line; 4‑betting to 1350 as a bluff is too risky with no blocker value and will either commit chips or get called by better. Preserve the 20k stack for a better spot and fold.

I opened A5o from the Button, which is a standard steal attempt. Flash, the chip leader in the Small Blind, 3-bet to 800. While I have position, A5o is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet. It is frequently dominated by Flash's value range (stronger Aces) and plays poorly post-flop compared to suited connectors or pocket pairs. Calling requires investing ~5.5 BBs more with a hand that likely has equity issues (reverse implied odds). A 4-bet bluff is possible given the Ace blocker, but riskier given my stack is the shortest and Flash covers me significantly. The most standard and +EV line maximizing stack preservation for better spots is to fold.

With 15 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip lead, my primary goal is to maintain my stack and avoid unnecessary risks. 8-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While opening wide from the button is a standard 3-handed strategy, this specific hand is too poor to play, especially with two active opponents behind me who can either shove (Pro) or defend (Minni). I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads‑up from the small blind with Ah6c. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to complete — calling keeps me out of position with a hand that is often dominated by better A‑x. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure on the big blind, who has a reasonably wide defending range. I will raise to 200 total (a ~2x BB open from the SB) to pick up folds and play the pot with initiative if called. Postflop plan: continuation‑bet favorable flops (any ace, low cards or drawy boards) and control the pot on cold, disconnected boards; fold to significant preflop 3‑bets because my kicker is weak unless the 3‑bet sizing is very small and I have good odds to continue. Raise to 200 balances fold equity and postflop playability while risking a small portion of my 20400 stack.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, facing a min-raise from the Small Blind, I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) requiring only ~25% equity to call. With Ten-Eight offsuit, I have sufficient equity against a wide SB opening range. Additionally, I will have the advantage of position post-flop, which helps in realizing my equity. Folding would be too tight given the price and position.

Board: 4♦ K♠ 4♥. I hold A♥6♣ — only Ace‑high with a weak kicker on a paired, King‑high flop. Pro called my 2x SB open so his range includes many Kx hands and pocket pairs (including 44) that dominate me. I have position, so the lowest‑variance, highest‑information play is to check and control the pot. If he checks back I see a free turn and can re‑evaluate; if he bets a small probe (to target missed SB c‑bets) I can call to realize showdown value versus bluffs and weaker Ax, but I will fold to a large bet or shove since calling off a big portion of my stack with just A‑high on a Kxx board is incorrect. Checking preserves stack, keeps better hands in his range honest, and lets me exploit bluffs or improve on later streets.

In a Blind vs Blind limped/min-raised pot, the flop comes K-4-4 rainbow. The opponent checks to me. I hold Ten-Eight offsuit which has missed the board completely, leaving me with just Ten-high. The board texture is very dry. While a bet might fold out some Queen-high or Jack-high hands, the opponent's checking range likely includes many Kings, pocket pairs, or Ace-highs that will call a bet. Since I have very little equity and no draw, and the pot is small, there is no need to bloat the pot. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card where I might improve to a pair, and keeps my stack preserved.

Board: 4♦ K♠ 4♥ 2♣. I hold A♥6♣ — only Ace‑high with a weak kicker. Pro cold‑called my SB open and both checked the paired, king‑high flop; his range includes many hands that currently beat me (Kx, any 4, medium pocket pairs) and some missed overcards. The turn 2♣ is a blank for my range and there are no meaningful draws to charge. Betting here would have very limited fold equity — a small bet will be called by Kx and pairs that beat me, and a larger bet just risks a big portion of my stack facing a call or shove from a shorter opponent. With position and poor equity, the lowest‑variance, highest‑information play is to check and see the river. If Pro bets a small probe I’ll call to realize showdown value; I’ll fold to a large shove. On the river I’ll reassess and only apply pressure or value‑bet if I improve or the opponent shows clear weakness.

The turn is a 2 of clubs, which is a brick and changes nothing. The board remains very dry (K-4-4-2). My opponent (Minni) checks again. While this signals weakness, my hand (Ten-high) has very little showdown value and no draw. In a blind-vs-blind scenario, my opponent's checking range often includes Ace-highs or small pairs that might call a stab. Betting likely only folds out hands I am already beating or flipping with (like Q-high), while getting called by anything better. Checking back allows me to see a free river card to potentially pair my Ten or Eight, or get to showdown cheaply, preserving my stack of 63 BBs.

I rivered top pair (A♥6♣) on 4♦ K♠ 4♥ 2♣ A♠. Pro cold‑called my SB open preflop and has been totally passive (checked flop and turn), which points toward misses, medium pairs or cautious Kx slowplays rather than aggressive value lines. The river Ace significantly improves my showdown value — I now beat Kx, most medium pocket pairs and missed broadways, and I only lose to Ax with a better kicker (some AQ/AJ combos), which are less frequent given his line and the blockers I hold. The pot is small (400) relative to both stacks, so a modest value bet is the right play: it extracts calls from worse A/x, Kx and small pairs while keeping the risk controlled. I’ll bet about half the pot (200). If he raises/shoves big I’ll give it up — my kicker is weak and his large raise would represent a strong value hand. Betting 200 maximizes thin value without turning this into a high‑variance confrontation.

On the River, the board is [4d, Ks, 4h, 2c, As]. The Ace on the river significantly improves the opponent's range, especially hands that were checking down like Ace-high. My hand, Ten-high, has virtually no showdown value as it loses to any pair, Ace-high, King-high, Queen-high, and Jack-high. The opponent's bet of 200 into 400 is small, often indicative of thin value (like a weak Ace or King) or a blocker bet. Getting 3:1 pot odds requires me to be good 25% of the time, which is highly unlikely given the board texture and action. Bluff-raising is too risky without relevant blockers on this runout. Folding is the only prudent option.

I’m on the button with T♠9♥ — a playable, positionally strong hand. Small blind has folded so I’m opening heads-up against the big blind. Rather than limp (which gives the big blind correct odds and initiative), I’ll raise to take the lead, deny proper odds for a cheap multiway flop, and generate fold equity. With deep stacks (~206 BB) I can comfortably play postflop if called; a 3x BB open (300 total) is a standard, balanced sizing that puts pressure on the big blind while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash 3-bets I’ll reassess based on sizing and read; if he calls I’ll use position to play the flop aggressively.

The current hand is 137 of 150, and we are 3-handed. I am in the Small Blind with [Ac 8s], facing a raise to 300 from Minni on the Button. While A8o has decent equity against a wide button opening range (likely 50%+), playing this hand out of position (OOP) is difficult. Calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall, and post-flop playability with an off-suit weak Ace is poor (reverse implied odds). 3-betting is an option to take initiative, but at 62BB deep, it risks a significant portion of my stack with a marginal hand against the chip leaders. Given my position as the short stack (though relatively deep) and the desire to minimize variance in marginal spots OOP, folding is the disciplined decision to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am holding a solid middle pocket pair (7h 7s) in the Big Blind. In a three-handed game, pocket sevens are quite strong against a button opening range. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a significant stack lead, I want to see a flop while keeping the pot relatively controlled out of position. Calling here is a standard and safe play that allows me to either hit a set or evaluate on various board textures without committing too many chips pre-flop. I'm getting excellent odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 450).

On a flop of Qd-6s-6d, my pocket sevens are currently a decent hand, forming two pair (Sevens and Sixes). However, the board is dangerous as any Queen or any Six currently has me beat, and there are many overcards that could fall on the turn and river. As the first to act, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, reacts to the paired board. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 137 of 150), I want to avoid building a large pot with a marginal made hand. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking to the raiser is standard.

I raised preflop to 300 and the BB called; flop is Q♦ 6♠ 6♦ and I have T♠9♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw. The pot is 650 and Flash (BB) checked to me. As the preflop aggressor with position and deep stacks I want to leverage fold equity and keep the initiative, but I don’t want to commit too many chips with a pure bluff. A small continuation bet (~1/3–40% pot) is the right balance: it will fold a lot of missed overcards and weak holdings from his wide defending range while keeping the pot controllable if called. I’ll bet 250. If he folds I take down the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and generally steer toward pot control unless I pick up equity or a favorable runout; if he raises I’ll fold because my hand has poor showdown value.

On a board of Qd-6s-6d, my pocket sevens are a marginal made hand. Minni's continuation bet is relatively small (250 into a pot of 650), offering me favorable odds of 3.6-to-1 to call. While I am losing to any Queen, any Six, or pocket pairs 88+, my hand remains ahead of a significant portion of a button's wide opening and continuation-betting range, such as smaller pairs or high-card bluffs like AK or AJ. Raising here would likely only force out hands I already beat and bloat the pot when I am out of position. Given my substantial chip lead and the late stage of the tournament, calling is the most efficient way to see a turn card cheaply and control the size of the pot.

The turn card is the Deuce of Spades, which is a relative brick. The board is now Qd-6s-6d-2s. My pocket sevens are still a decent bluff-catcher, beating all of Minni's air and smaller pocket pairs, while losing to any Queen, any Six, or higher pocket pairs. After I called the flop bet, Minni has checked back on the turn. This check often indicates a hand that has some showdown value but doesn't want to build a bigger pot (like a weak Queen or a medium pocket pair) or a complete air-ball that is giving up. Since I am first to act on the turn (after the previous street's action) and the current state indicates the turn action has checked around to me or Minni checked behind, I will check again to keep the pot small and reach showdown cheaply, or to induce a bluff on the river.

I opened the action preflop and c-bet the Q♦6♠6♦ flop (bet 250) and was called. Turn is 2♠ — the board (Q♦ 6♠ 6♦ 2♠) is very dry for my T♠9♥: I have no pair and there are no realistic 5-card draws I can make (a flush is impossible from here and straight outs are remote). Villain called my flop bet, so his range contains many hands that will continue (Qx, underpairs, some 6x/set combos and occasional float/call hands). With essentially zero equity and no reason to inflate the pot, I should control the pot size and avoid getting raised off a marginal bluff catch or committing chips with air. I’ll check back the turn, see a free river, and re-evaluate — folding to meaningful aggression on later streets unless I improve or pick up a very favorable read.

On the river, the board is double-paired (Qd-6s-6d-2s-2d). My pocket sevens form the two-pair hand 7-7-6-6-Q, which beats any Ace-high hand (6-6-2-2-A) and smaller pocket pairs like 33-55. Minni's check on the turn strongly suggests she does not have a monster hand like a Queen, a Six, or a high pocket pair, as those hands would typically bet for value on such a dry board. Since I am out of position and have moderate showdown value, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to reach showdown for free or potentially catch a desperate bluff from a hand I already beat. Betting wouldn't accomplish much, as better hands like 88+ or any remaining Queens won't fold, and weaker hands are unlikely to call.

Board is Q♦6♠6♦2♠2♦ and I hold T♠9♥ — I have no pair and no realistic draw. Villain called my flop c-bet and then checked both turn and river; that line is consistent with Qx, underpairs or a slowplayed made hand — most of which beat my hand at showdown. A river bluff has poor fold equity versus this passive caller and little to gain: betting risks chips with a very weak hand and is unlikely to fold the hands that beat me. With a deep stack and nothing to gain, the correct play is to check and take the showdown (and fold to any substantial bet if they become aggressive).

I am on the Button with [9s 3d], which is an extremely weak hand (bottom of the range). Although I have position, opening with trash like 93o is generally a losing play, especially against two deep-stacked opponents in the blinds who can easily defend or 3-bet. With a stack of 62.5 BBs, I have plenty of room to wait for better spots. Folding is the standard and correct decision here.

I have a significant chip lead with only 12 hands remaining in the tournament. In this 3-handed situation, after the button folds, I am in the Small Blind with T8o. This is a standard hand to open-raise to steal the Big Blind. I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni. If Minni re-raises, I can comfortably fold and maintain my lead. If Minni calls, I'll use my position and stack size to navigate the flop.

I’m in the big blind with 6♥8♦ facing a 3x SB open to 300. This is a weak offsuit hand but the call is cheap — I only need to put 200 more (300 total), giving roughly 3:1 pot odds to see a flop. Flash’s SB opening range is likely wide here and with deep stacks I can realize equity and use postflop position to play the hand. 3-betting this hand is too thin (and the min-raise size is unattractive); folding would be overly nitty given the price. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: continue if I hit or have strong draws, otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

I have middle pair (eights) with a marginal kicker on a board containing a Queen and a spade flush draw. Being out of position against Minni, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size with a marginal made hand and see how Minni reacts. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or catch bluffs from high-card hands and missed draws. If she bets a standard amount, I plan to call to see the turn.

I have 8♦6♥ and flopped a middle pair (pair of eights) on Q♦ 8♠ 4♠. Flash checked the flop after a preflop raise — that line usually indicates a missed c-bet, a spade draw, or a wide/unwilling-to-build pot range. I’m out of position and the board carries two spades and a top-pair queen that beat me. With deep stacks there’s no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that’s vulnerable to Qx, sets, and spade draws. Checking controls the size of the pot, keeps worse hands and draws in to pay off later, and lets me evaluate the turn action (call reasonable bets, raise only with clear equity or strong reads). I’ll check and proceed based on Flash’s turn action.

The turn brings a second four, pairing the board. My pair of eights remains a solid marginal hand. Since I checked the flop and Minni checked behind, it is unlikely she has a Queen or a four, though a four is possible in her big blind defending range. I will check again to continue my pot control strategy. This allows me to see a river for free or potentially catch a bluff from her missed spade draws or high-card air. Given my massive chip lead and the late stage of the tournament, there is no need to build a large pot with a mid-strength hand.

I check. I currently have a marginal made hand — middle pair with 8♦6♥ on Q♦ 8♠ 4♠ 4♥ — and I’m out of position to Flash, who opened preflop and has checked both flop and turn. The turn 4 increases the number of hands that beat me (44, Qx, some slow-played sets) and the board still contains spade draw combinations that can continue. Betting here would bloat the pot with a hand that is vulnerable to lots of stronger holdings and could get raised; checking keeps the pot under control, keeps worse hands and bluffs in the pot, and lets me make a cleaner decision on the river. Plan: if Flash leads small on the river I will call for showdown (range contains many missed draws and weak pairs); if Flash puts in a large, polarized bet or raises I will fold — we’re usually behind. Checking preserves fold equity and avoids committing with a marginal hand.

I have a medium-strength hand with a pair of eights. The flop and turn were checked through by both players, which suggests that Minni likely does not have a Queen or a four. On the river, the five of hearts is relatively inconsequential. Since the pot is small and I have a significant chip lead, checking is the safest and most effective way to reach a showdown. If Minni checks back, my pair of eights is very likely the best hand. If she bets, her range is polarized towards missed draws or air, making a call reasonable to catch a bluff.

I have a marginal made hand — middle pair (8♦) on a paired, somewhat wet board (Q♦ 8♠ 4♠ 4♥ 5♥). Flash opened preflop but has shown complete passivity (check-check-check), which is consistent with missed spade draws, weak showdown hands or a slow-played strong hand (Qx or a 4). Betting here would bloat the pot and only get value from worse hands that will often fold to pressure (A/K/J high) while getting called by all hands that beat me (Qx, 44, some 8x) — a thin, high-variance line for a small pot. With stacks deep and 12 hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and taking showdown with a real but vulnerable pair is the +EV line. I’ll check to showdown and fold to aggression (if any) — we keep risk controlled and let opponent have the initiative to bluff or value-bet first.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. With the tournament nearing its end and me holding a substantial chip lead, it is important to maintain pressure while avoiding high-variance situations. Raising to 300 is a standard opening move from the button to pick up the blinds or take control of the hand in position. If either Minni or the short-stacked Pro raises significantly or jams, I can easily fold to preserve my stack for better spots, as Ten-Eight offsuit does not play well against a 3-bet or shove range.

I’m in the small blind with KcTs facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must add 250). KTo is a playable broadway but I’m out of position, so 3-betting here is suboptimal unless I have blockers or a better hand. Pot odds are ~35.7% (call 250 to win 450) and the button is likely opening a fairly wide range, so a flat call to see a flop is the best risk/reward play. Plan: call to 300 total, play the flop cautiously — continue with top pair/good draws, fold to heavy turn/river pressure when missed. I’ll avoid a small min-raise or a bloated 3-bet OOP with this hand.

The current pot is 700 chips. I am in the Big Blind with Qs 5h facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. I need to call 200 chips to see the flop, offering me pot odds of roughly 4.5 to 1 (requiring ~22% equity). While the price is attractive, Q5 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against two opponents. It suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated Queen) and will struggle to realize its equity. Given I am the shortest stack (though still reasonably deep at ~61 BBs), preserving chips for better spots is preferable to defending a marginal hand like this multi-way. Folding is the disciplined play.

Board is 4♦6♣2♣ and I hold K♣T♠ — no pair, two overcards and only a backdoor (runner-runner) club. Flash was the preflop raiser and will c-bet this dry flop a lot; I’m out of position. Best EV line is to control the pot and realize equity rather than firing a bluff OOP. I’ll check to induce a continuation bet I can call with my overcards/backdoor, fold to large polarization or raise, and only invest more if I improve (pair a K/T) or pick up a real draw. If Villain checks back I take a free turn; if they bet small-to-medium (~≤½ pot) I call, fold to big overbets.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a low, disconnected board of 4d-6c-2c. While my Ten-Eight offsuit did not hit this flop, I still have two overcards. Minni's check indicates weakness or a desire to see a cheap turn. By firing a standard continuation bet of 350 (half the pot), I can often take the pot down immediately if she has missed the board with her calling range (like high cards that failed to pair). Given my significant stack lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament, this small investment maintains the initiative without putting my lead at risk.

I have K♣T♠ on 4♦6♣2♣ — no pair, two overcards and only a backdoor club (would need runner‑runner). Button (Flash) opened preflop and is c‑betting a small 350 into a 1050 pot (~33% pot). Calling 350 to see the turn is cheap: it’s roughly 350 to win the 1400 pot (≈25% break‑even equity). Against a wide button c‑bet range my two overcards + K blocker give me reasonable equity and the chance to pair up on the turn/river (~24% to hit K or T by river), so the correct low‑risk line OOP is to call and realize equity. I won’t try to bluff‑raise OOP with no fold equity and poor playability; I’ll call, see the turn, and proceed only if I improve (top pair or a strong draw) — otherwise fold to significant turn/river pressure.

Check. The turn A♣ doesn't pair my KTs — I currently have no made hand and only a one-card flush draw to the river (K♣ + 6♣ + 2♣ + A♣ = 4 clubs; 9 outs ≈ 9/46 ≈ 19.6%). Flash's line (button open to 300, small 350 c-bet on a dry 4-6-2, then check turn) narrows him toward Ax/pot-control pairs or missed broadway cards — hands that will often call a bet and rarely fold to a lone OOP bluff. Betting here would bloat the pot OOP with limited equity and insufficient fold equity (e.g. a 700 bluff needs ~33% fold frequency; a 300 probe needs ~18%), so the higher EV line is to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: check to see the river for free; if the river completes my flush I’ll value-bet, if I miss I’ll call small bets but fold to large river pressure unless I pair up.

On the turn, the Ace of Clubs is a significant scare card. It also gives me a flush draw since I hold the Ten of Clubs and there are three clubs on the board. While the Ace is a good card to continue represent a strong range, I am currently the chip leader with only 11 hands remaining in the tournament. Checking back is the most prudent play here; it allows me to see a free river card to potentially hit my flush, keeps the pot manageable, and avoids the risk of being check-raised off my equity by Minni.

I check. River 5♠ leaves me with K♣T♠ on 4♦-6♣-2♣-A♣-5♠ — no pair, no flush; just king-high and a K♣ blocker. Flash’s line (open, small c-bet on the dry 4‑6‑2, then check the A♣ on the turn) narrows him toward Ax, medium pairs or pot‑control holdings that will frequently call a river bluff or check back. The pot is 1,400 and I have very little showdown value and limited fold equity OOP — a profitable bluff would require an unrealistically high fold frequency from this opponent. Given my stack (19,450) and tournament context, preserving chips and avoiding a marginal bluff is correct. I will check to see a showdown and plan to fold to any meaningful river bet; only consider calling a very tiny probe if I have a strong read that Flash is bluffing.

On the river, the board is 4d-6c-2c-Ac-5s. I have Ten-high with the Ten of Clubs. After Minni checked the turn and now the river, it's possible she has a weak pair or a missed draw, but she could also be trapping with a weak Ace or a small straight (any 3). Given that I am the significant chip leader and there are only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips on a bluff in a relatively small pot. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free, preserves my stack, and avoids any potential check-raise situation from Minni. My primary goal now is to cruise to the finish line with my lead intact.

On the button in a 3-handed pot I should be opening a fairly wide range. J♦6♣ (6h Jd) is marginal but playable in position — I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds and keep the pot heads-up where my position matters. Flash is very deep and aggressive while Pro (SB) is shorter (~61bb) and may call or shove; I’ll use a standard 3xBB open to 300 to get fold equity and keep the pot a manageable size. If I face a big 3-bet or shove from either, I’ll concede with this hand to protect my deep stack.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7s 6d, an unsuited connector. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Although I am getting reasonable pot odds, playing 76o out of position against a button raiser and potentially the Big Blind is a losing strategy in the long run. My stack is the shortest at 6100 (61 BBs), so I should preserve chips for better opportunities rather than defending with a weak speculative hand out of position. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, even in a three-handed game. Given that I am the chip leader with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to call a raise out of position with a hand that has such poor playability. Folding is the most prudent move to protect my lead.

I am on the Button with 5s 6d (offsuit connectors). My stack is 61 BBs, which is healthy but I am the short stack compared to the other two. 65o is a weak hand, even for a button steal, as it lacks high card strength and suited equity. With 10 hands remaining, there is no urgency to force action with sub-par holdings. Folding preserves my stack and avoids difficult post-flop situations against deep-stacked opponents who may defend wide.

I am holding 3h 7s in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While it only costs 50 chips to call and see a flop after the Button folded, I would be playing out of position against the Big Blind with a hand that has very little post-flop potential. Given that there are only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and I have a substantial stack lead, I will choose the disciplined path and fold to avoid unnecessary losses.

I am holding 9d 3s on the button. While I have a significant chip lead with only 9 hands remaining in the tournament, 9-3 offsuit is far too weak a hand to open, even in a three-handed game. Both opponents have shown willingness to defend their blinds or play aggressively. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to apply pressure.

With the button folded this is effectively heads-up vs Pro (big blind, 6,000). K♠3♦ is a marginal hand, but heads‑up it's reasonable to open for initiative. The pot is 150 and the min-raise is 200 total — I'll make the small standard min-raise to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the BB's wide defending range. The raise is small relative to our 21,000 stack so it preserves flexibility; if Pro responds with a committed shove I won't risk a large portion of my stack with K3o and will fold to a clear all‑in unless it reads like a pure bluff. If he calls or folds I can continue post‑flop in position with the initiative. Raise to 200 total.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, facing a min-raise from the Small Blind, I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) requiring only 25% equity to call. My hand, Queen-Five suited, has significantly more equity than that against a typical wide blind-stealing range. Additionally, I will have the advantage of position post-flop. Calling allows me to realize my equity and play a pot in position with a suited Queen, which is a standard defense.

I was the preflop aggressor (min-raise) and we’re now OOP on a J♣Q♣7♠ rainbow flop with K3o — only a single overcard and little showdown value. Versus a BB calling a tiny raise his range is wide (many one-pairs, Qx/Jx, A-high, broadways and some small pairs/draws), so a continuation bet is correct to use initiative and generate fold equity. I’ll size modestly to control the pot and maximize folds: ~50% pot (200). If he folds we take the pot; if he calls we retain a manageable pot and can continue only if we pair the King or get a clear read. I will fold to significant check-raises/all-ins without improving, preserving my stack against a short-stacked opponent’s strong action.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a weak kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a Q-J-7 board. Facing a half-pot c-bet from the Small Blind in a Blind vs Blind scenario, my hand is well ahead of their wide range which includes second pairs, straight draws, and pure bluffs. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands (better Queens, two pair, sets) while folding out the hands I dominate. Calling allows me to keep their bluffing range wide, control the pot size given my weak kicker, and realize my equity. The price is excellent to see a turn card.

Turn (3♥) pairs my 3♦ giving me a weak, bottom pair with a King kicker. Pot is only 800 and Pro is short (≈5.7k) — he called my flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is heavy with Jx/Qx, some pairs and broadways; many of those hands now beat me. There are few meaningful draws left that I need to charge, and a bet here mostly folds worse hands and keeps better hands that dominate me. I don’t want to inflate the pot and face a shove from a short stack where I’m often behind, so I’ll control the pot and see how Pro reacts. Plan: check to keep the pot manageable, call small bets to reach the river for a cheap showdown, and fold to large overbets/shoves unless I have a good read that it’s a bluff.

On the Turn, the board is [7s Jd Qc 3h]. I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a weak kicker (5). The opponent (Minni), who raised pre-flop and c-bet the flop, has now checked. The 3h is a relative brick. My hand has showdown value, but betting is thin for value because better Queens dominate me, and I mostly fold out hands I am already beating (bluffs). Checking back allows me to control the pot size, avoid facing a check-raise, and realize my equity while inducing potential bluffs on the river. I am happy to take a free card to see a showdown.

I hold K♠3♦ — bottom pair (3s) with a King kicker on 7♠ J♦ Q♣ 3♥ 2♥. Pro called my tiny preflop raise and my 200 flop c‑bet, then both of us checked the turn. His calling range is heavy with Jx/Qx, some mid pairs and broadways that mostly beat my pair of threes. The river 2♥ is a largely irrelevant card (only creates a flush if he has two hearts), so betting here would be attempting to fold out hands that are often still beating me and risks a shove from a strong value hand given his ~5.7k stack. Pot is only 800 — I don’t want to bloat the pot with a very marginal made hand. I’ll check to get to showdown; if he bets a small amount I can call for pot odds, but I’ll fold to a large shove. Checking preserves chips and is the +EV line here.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a weak kicker (5). The action went: Pre-flop call, Flop call, Turn check-check, and now River check from the opponent. The board is [7s Jd Qc 3h 2h], which is relatively dry with no completed flush or obvious straight draws hitting. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, the opponent's range is wide. Their check-check line on Turn and River suggests either a marginal made hand (like Jx or a weak Qx) or a give-up. While I have good showdown value, betting is thin because better Queens (which are plentiful in their range given the pre-flop raise) will call, and worse hands like Jx or underpairs might fold or only call a very small amount. Additionally, checking back eliminates the risk of facing a check-raise or value-owning myself against a better kicker. I have sufficient showdown value to win against their missed draws and second pairs, so taking the free showdown is the highest EV and lowest variance play.

Button with T♥J♦ in a 3-handed pot — deep stacks (I ~206BB, SB ~64BB, BB very deep). Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me: calling 100 is passive and gives opponents good odds; min-raising to 200 is tiny and invites aggressive responses. I want the initiative and to use position, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300). This size applies pressure to the blinds, denies cheap flops, and keeps postflop play comfortable (good SPR to maneuver). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy aggression from the deep 3-bettor), but versus one or two callers I’m happy to play postflop positionally. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and maximize expected value.

I am in the Small Blind with [Jh 4d], a very weak off-suit hand. The Button (Minni) has opened to 300 (3bb). Despite having a playable stack of ~64bb, playing J4o out of position is a losing proposition in the long run. The hand has poor equity against an opening range and very poor playability post-flop. Calling would be bleeding chips, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary with this specific holding given the tournament stage (Hand 143/150) and my relative stack size. The correct play is to fold.

I am currently the chip leader with 32,750 chips and only 8 hands remaining in the tournament. In 3-handed play, Ace-Ten offsuit (Ad Tc) is a very strong hand, especially against a standard Button opening range from Minni. I have successfully 3-bet Minni in the past (Hand #107) and won. By raising now, I can either take down the pot immediately or take control of the hand going into the flop. Given the tournament stage, I want to maintain my lead and put pressure on the second-largest stack. A raise to 900 is a standard size to define the situation.

I opened to 300 on the button with T♥J♦ to take the initiative. Flash (BB) 3-bets to 900 — folding TJs heads-up here is too nitty: I have position and a hand with good playability and reasonable equity versus a 3-betting range that includes many broadways and bluffs. The pot is 1250 and I must call 600 more, so my break-even equity is ~32% (600 to win ~1850) — which TJs can achieve against a wide-ish 3-bet range. A light 4-bet is too thin here: min-4-betting invites larger action from a very deep chip leader and I don't want to build a bloated pot OOP against a likely stronger or capable 5-bet range. Plan: call 600 more (900 total committed), see the flop in position, and use positional advantage to fold to strong aggression or to apply pressure when the flop favors my range.

On this very dry flop of 2d-3c-7s, I maintain a significant range advantage as the pre-flop 3-bettor. My range contains all the premium overpairs (88-AA) which are less likely to be in Minni's range after she just called the 3-bet. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 750 (approx. 40% pot) to maintain the initiative, extract value from her weaker hands, and potentially fold out her overcards like KQ or KJ. Given my large chip lead and the few hands remaining in the tournament, I want to control the action without over-risking my stack.

I’m on the button with T♥J♦. Preflop I called Flash’s 3‑bet to play the hand in position (900 committed). The flop is a very dry 2♦-3♣-7♠ and Flash leads for 750 into a 1850 pot (pot now 2600). Calling 750 would make the pot 3350 and my break‑even equity is roughly 750/3350 ≈ 22.4%. Against a typical BB 3‑bet/c‑bet range (some pairs and many broadways/bluffs), TJ has enough equity with two overcards to clear that threshold and I also realize more equity in position. Raising here as a bluff bloats the pot against very deep, capable opponents and risks getting shipped back into a huge pot while I’m currently behind to many value hands. Plan: call 750 to keep the pot manageable, use position to evaluate the turn — fold to clear strength, continue aggressively if I hit a T or J or if turn texture and opponent tendencies allow a credible semi‑bluff/value line.

The turn 6d is relatively neutral but does complete some gutshots and gives equity to hands like 45s or 76s. Minni calling the 3-bet pre-flop and the flop continuation bet suggests she likely has a made hand like a mid-pocket pair (44-99) or a pair of sevens. My Ace-high still has some showdown value, but betting again is unlikely to fold out better hands and only builds a larger pot against the second-largest stack. Since I am the chip leader with only a few hands left in the tournament, pot control is my priority here. Checking allows me to see a river card and evaluate whether to call a small bet or take the hand to showdown.

Position and recap: I opened to 300 on the button with T♥J♦, Flash 3‑bet to 900 and I called. Flop (2♦-3♣-7♠) Flash bet and I called. Turn is 6♦ and Flash checks to me. I have two overcards (T,J) but no pair and no realistic backdoor flush — J♦ plus two diamonds on board cannot make a flush by the river. My chance to improve to a pair on the river is only ~13% (6 outs).

Range/read: Flash’s line (3‑bet preflop, c‑bet flop, then check turn) looks like a mix of medium/strong holdings and some air: medium pairs and controlled value hands will check the turn for pot control, and many bluffs or float hands will check and give up if I apply real aggression. If I turn into the aggressor I risk building a large pot OOP against a very deep, capable opponent and will often run into hands that call or raise me profitably (pocket pairs, overpairs, made broadway combos).

Decision: check. Checking controls the pot, preserves stack depth, and lets me see the river for a real chance to pair up or pick a better bluff spot. If the river gives me a T or J I can extract value; if it bricks I’ll evaluate a small river bluff only if the card and my read justify it. If Flash bets the river large, I’ll fold; if he fires small I may call for showdown depending on sizing and line.

The river King is an overcard to the board, but given the previous action—Minni calling my pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet, followed by both of us checking the turn—her range likely consists of medium-strength hands such as a pair of sevens or a pocket pair like 88 or 99. My Ace-high has some showdown value against her missed draws or weaker Ace-high hands, but a bet here is unlikely to fold out her made hands or get called by anything worse. With the tournament nearing its end (Hand 143 of 150) and holding a significant chip lead, I will choose the low-variance path and check to reach a showdown, prioritizing pot control and stack protection.

I missed — T♠J♦ has no pair on 2♦-3♣-7♠-6♦-K♠. Flash’s line (3‑bet pre, c‑bet flop, check turn, check river) looks like pot‑control/value hands plus some bluffs. The river King actually improves a lot of his value combos (AK, Kx) and leaves many medium pairs happy to check for showdown. I have no value to extract and limited fold equity if I try to bluff (any Kx or pocket pair will usually call, and Flash is deep/capable). Betting risks building a big pot with essentially zero showdown equity; folding to a bet would be the correct response anyway. Best play is to check and take the showdown — avoid unnecessary risk and preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with Ac 2h, a marginal hand but playable from this position in a 3-handed game. I have previously opened similar weak Aces (A3o in Hand 126) successfully. My stack is roughly 64 BBs, allowing me to open-raise and fold to a 3-bet without crippling myself. The Ace acts as a blocker, reducing the probability that the blinds hold an Ace. Stealing the blinds here is a low-risk way to add to my stack in the final stages of the tournament. I will raise to 2.5 BBs (250) to apply pressure.

I am the chip leader with a comfortable margin as we approach the end of the tournament (Hand 144/150). My current hand, 6c 5h, is very weak and difficult to play out of position. Pro, the button, has raised to 2.5BB. While I could attempt a 3-bet bluff or call to see a flop, 6-5 offsuit doesn't play well enough to justify the risk of inflating a pot against the button or inviting the big blind (Minni) into the hand. My priority is protecting my lead and waiting for higher equity situations.

I’m in the big blind with Tc3s (offsuit) facing a button open to 250 into a 400 pot. To call I must commit 150; that gives me ~150/(400+150)=27% pot‑odds. T3o is a very weak, unconnected offsuit hand with poor equity and postflop playability against a BTN opening range in 3‑handed play — it’s unlikely to have the ~27% equity needed. A min 3‑bet to 400 buys almost no fold equity and an all‑in bluff is unnecessary and easily called by better hands. With a healthy stack and a shortish opponent, the correct, low-variance line is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with A3o. The stacks are quite shallow for Pro (65bb) and Minni (188bb), while I have a dominant lead (345bb). Given it's hand 145 out of 150, I want to maintain my lead and apply pressure on the shorter stacks without overcomitting. Ace-high is a strong opening hand three-handed. I will raise to 300 to steal the blinds or take control of the pot early.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB with Q♦2♦. Pot is 450 and I must call 250 (total 300) — break‑even equity ≈ 250/(450+250) ≈ 35.7%. Q2o is a weak, frequently dominated hand versus a BTN opening range and has poor postflop playability out of position, especially with the big blind (Pro) still to act. A min‑3bet to 500 is a weak bluff here: I have little fold equity and poor cards to continue with if called or 4‑bet. Preserve my stack and avoid marginal OOP spots — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Tc Jd, facing a standard 3x raise from the Button (Flash). My stack is 65 BB, so I have plenty of room to play post-flop. JTo is a defendable hand against a wide Button range, especially getting good pot odds (calling 200 to win 450). Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary risk against the chip leader out of position. I will call to realize my equity.

I hold Ten-Jack on a King-Eight-Nine flop, giving me an open-ended straight draw (outs: Queens and Sevens). Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Flash), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see if they continue with a c-bet. If they check back, I get a free look at the turn. If they bet, I have strong equity to call. Leading out (donk betting) is less optimal as it denies me the chance to capture bluffs or see a free card.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a King-high board that favors my perceived range. Having the Ace of Spades gives me some backdoor equity for a nut flush. Pro checked from the Big Blind, which is a standard move. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (roughly half the pot) to represent the King and put pressure on Pro's marginal holdings. Given the tournament is nearing its end and I have a substantial lead, I want to maintain initiative without overextending.

I hold Ten-Jack on a K-8-9 board, giving me an open-ended straight draw. I need a Queen or Seven to complete the straight. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 650) from the Button, I am getting excellent pot odds (roughly 4:1 direct odds) to continue. My equity with an open-ended straight draw is approximately 32% by the river, which is greater than the 24% equity required to call. Raising is an option but increases variance out of position against the chip leader. Calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity cheaply.

I hold Ten-Jack on a K-8-9-2 board, retaining my open-ended straight draw (needing a Queen or Seven). The Turn card (2h) is a blank and does not improve my hand or change the board texture significantly. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who bet the flop, the standard play is to check. This allows me to potentially see a free river card if the opponent checks back. Leading out (donk betting) is risky and unlikely to be profitable here. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's subsequent action.

The turn card (2h) is a complete brick that doesn't change the board state. My opponent called the flop continuation bet, suggesting they have a piece of the board (Kx, 9x, 8x) or a draw (spades, JT, etc.). Given that I have a massive stack lead and we are at hand 145 of 150, I want to minimize unnecessary risk. Ace-high still has some showdown value against missed draws, and betting again is unlikely to fold out a King or a mid-pair from Pro's relatively short stack. I will check behind to control the pot size and see the river for free.

The river is the Ten of Hearts (Th), making the final board Ks-8s-9c-2h-Th. This improves my hand from a missed straight draw to a pair of Tens. The river card also completes several straight possibilities (Q-J makes the nut straight, J-7 makes the lower straight). Flash checked back the turn, which often indicates a capped range—likely a weak King, middle pair, or a draw. My pair of Tens has some showdown value, but betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like 8x or Ace-high) given the scary board texture, and betting unlikely folds out a King. Checking allows me to potentially win at showdown if Flash checks back with a weaker hand or air. If Flash bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but leading out turns my hand into a bluff that might not work or a thin value bet that only gets called by better. Checking is the standard line out of position with a marginal made hand.

On the river, the board is Ks-8s-9c-2h-Th. I have Ace-high. My opponent in the Big Blind checked to me for the third time this hand (if we count the flop). They called my flop continuation bet but have showed no aggression since. While it is possible I could bluff them off a weak pair of 8s or 9s, my Ace-high has significant showdown value against missed spade draws or other air. With only 5 hands remaining and a massive lead, the most prudent strategy is to check behind, minimize risk, and see the showdown.

Three-handed, on the button with 3♥7♥ and deep effective stacks (I ~188bb, SB Pro ~72bb, BB Flash ~338bb). In 3-handed play I should open very wide; 3♥7♥ has some playability (backdoor flush/straight) and gains a lot from position. Limping or calling 100 invites multiway pots where this hand’s equity is poor; a small open gives fold equity and lets me take the initiative postflop. I’ll make a standard button open to 300 (3x the BB) — large enough to pressure the blinds and narrow callers but small enough not to commit too many chips with a marginal hand. If I face significant 3-bet aggression I can fold and preserve my stack; if called I can use position to realize equity or pick spots postflop.

I hold 6s 2c in the Small Blind, which is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from the Button (Minni) to 300, I am out of position with a hand that has very poor playability and equity. Even though I am getting decent pot odds, my equity realization out of position with this trash hand will be very low. Preserving my stack of 7200 for better spots in the final few hands is the correct play.

I am currently the chip leader with a substantial stack of 33,800 and only 4 hands remaining in the tournament. My primary goal is to preserve my lead and avoid unnecessary risks. I have been dealt 2h 5d in the big blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Although the pot odds for calling are roughly 2.25 to 1, defending such a poor hand out of position against a competent opponent who has a significant stack (18,550) is not strategic at this stage. Folding allows me to maintain my chip lead without risking chips on a low-equity hand.

I am on the Button with T3s, which is a very weak hand (bottom 20-30%). Although it is 3-handed and I am in position, the opponents in the blinds (Flash and Minni) have significantly larger stacks (337 BB and 189 BB vs my 72 BB) and have shown tendencies to be active and aggressive. Opening this hand risks getting 3-bet or called by better ranges, putting me in a difficult post-flop spot with a marginal hand. With only 4 hands left and a healthy stack of 72 BBs, there is no need to force action with such a weak holding. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with AKo, a premier starting hand. After the Button folds, it's a battle between the blinds. I have a significant stack advantage over Minni (the Big Blind). Raising here is mandatory for value and to exert pressure. A standard 3x raise (to 300) is effective in this spot to build the pot and capitalize on my hand's strength.

Heads-up now (Pro folded) and Flash (SB) opened to 300. I hold Jc9d — a playable hand heads‑up but not strong enough to iso‑3bet often. Pot shows 400 and I need to add 200 (total commit will be 300). Calling gives me correct pot odds: 200/(400+200)=33% equity required. Versus a fairly wide SB opening range J9o has sufficient equity and I have deep stacks (18900) to see a flop and play post‑flop. A minimum raise to 500 is too small to accomplish much and a larger 3‑bet would commit more out of position with a marginal holding. Plan: call to see a flop; continue with top pair/good draws, exercise pot control or fold to strong continuation bets when I miss.

I have flopped Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a very dry board (A-9-2 rainbow). This is an ideal situation to bet for value and protection. Minni's calling range in the Big Blind likely includes many weaker Aces (A3-AJ), pocket pairs (33-88), and some high cards that might have hit a piece of the board like a 9. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to build the pot and extract value from these hands.

I have Jc9d — second pair (pair of 9s) with a decent Jack kicker on Ad 2s 9c. Villain (SB) opened wide preflop and is firing a small c‑bet (300 into a 900 pot). Calling is the best line: the call requires 300 into a 1200 total (300/(900+300)=25% pot odds) and my hand has enough equity versus a wide c‑betting range. Raising out of position with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and risk getting committed against A‑x, two‑pair, or sets; a min‑raise also won’t gain much fold equity but will commit more. Plan: call to control the pot, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to significant strength, raise or value‑bet if I improve or see clear weakness).

I have turned top two pair (Aces and Kings) on a relatively dry board. I am significantly ahead of most of Minni's range, which likely consists of weaker Aces, pocket pairs, or middle pairs like a Nine. The King on the turn is an excellent card as it improves my hand to top two pair while potentially giving Minni a second-best hand if she has a King or is drawing to a straight. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to continue building the pot and extracting value, positioning myself for a large river bet.

Call. I have second pair (9s) with a Jack kicker on Ad 2s 9c Ks. Villain opened wide from the SB and c‑bet the flop small, then fired 800 on the turn. Calling 800 into a 2000 pot requires ~28.6% equity (800/(2000+800)), and my hand has enough showdown value vs a wide line to justify a call. Raising OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and will be poorly priced against A/K, two‑pair or sets; folding is too weak because many worse hands and bluffs continue. Plan: call to control the pot and keep bluffs in; re‑evaluate on the river and fold to a large polarized shove or big value bet, otherwise seek value or pot‑control on the river.

I have Top Two Pair (Aces and Kings) on a board of Ad-2s-9c-Ks-Tc. My hand is extremely strong, second only to potential sets or the straight (QJ). Minni has checked and called on the flop and turn, which strongly suggests she has a made hand she's unwilling to fold, likely a weaker Ace like AQ, AJ, or AT. While the Ten on the river completes the QJ straight, I cannot play fearfully given the strength of my hand and the need to maximize my stack as we approach the end of the tournament (Hand 147). I will place a value bet of 1800 into the 2800 pot, which is large enough to get paid by any Ace in her range while representing the strength of my hand.

I have Jc9d — second pair (nines) with a decent Jack kicker on Ad 2s 9c Ks Tc. Flash opened wide preflop and has triple‑barreled moderate sizes (300 flop / 800 turn / 1800 river), which contains a lot of value (Ax, Kx, Tx, two‑pairs, sets) but also a non‑trivial bluff fraction given the wide opening range and small flop sizing. Calling 1800 into a 4600 pot requires ~28% equity (1800 / (4600+1800) ≈ 28.1%), and my hand has reasonable showdown value vs a mixed continuation range. Raising OOP with a marginal second pair is thin — it bloats the pot and will be called by better hands while folding out only some bluffs. Folding surrenders too much without the equity justification. Plan: call 1800 to go to showdown and avoid bloating the pot; re‑evaluate only if Flash jams or shows a highly polarized raise.

With only three hands remaining in the tournament and holding a massive chip lead, my primary objective is to maintain my stack. Hand 8s 4h is a trash hand and does not warrant a raise or a call, especially since the shorter stacks (Minni and Pro) may feel pressured to defend or shove. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here to avoid unnecessary risk.

Heads-up vs Pro (BB 7,100). I’m in the small blind (50 posted) with 3h9h — suited but a very weak hand. In heads-up the small blind will have position after the flop, so defending a wide range is reasonable, but this hand isn’t strong enough to open-raise and build a bigger pot. I’ll just call the 50 more (100 total committed) to see a flop. Plan: continue only with decent connection (pair, two pair, trips) or strong draws; otherwise check and fold to heavy aggression. Preserves stack and keeps pot small with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h Th, a weak hand. The Small Blind (Minni) has limped in. Given that there are only 3 hands left in the tournament and I am the short stack, there is no incentive to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding. Raising has negative expected value compared to seeing a free flop. Checking allows me to see the flop for free and potentially hit a strong hand or draw (flush/straight) disguised. My goal is to preserve chips unless I hit the board hard.

I limped 3♥9♥ preflop and on Q♥‑2♠‑7♣ I’ve missed — I have no pair and only a backdoor (runner‑runner) heart flush possibility. Pot is tiny (200) relative to our stack (15,700) and the BB (Pro) is short (7,100). In heads‑up with position, pot control is the right play: check to see how Pro reacts. I will only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw, or if he makes a very small probe bet I can call to realize equity (~24% to pair by the river, ~4% for runner‑runner flush); I’ll fold to meaningful aggression. This preserves chips and keeps options on later streets.

I hold bottom pair (Deuces) on a Qh 2s 7c board. The pot is small (200) in a limped blind-vs-blind scenario. Minni checked to me. My hand has showdown value but is not strong enough to confidently value bet, as I likely only get called by better hands (7x, Qx) and fold out worse hands. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially improve on the turn (Two, Ten, or Heart). There is no need to bloat the pot with a marginal made hand.

Board on the turn is Qh‑2s‑7c‑8h and I hold 3h9h. I don’t have a made hand but I do have a strong one‑card flush draw (3h,9h + Qh,8h = 4 hearts) — 9 outs, ~19.6% to hit on the river. Pot is tiny (200) compared with our stacks (I = 15,700; Pro = 7,100) and I have position. Both players checked the flop and Pro has been passive here, so pot control and realizing equity from position is attractive.

Betting as a semi‑bluff is possible, but with a minimum bet ~100 the math is marginal vs a call: if called my expectation on the turn is negative (EV if called ≈ 39.1 − 60.9 = −21.8 chips for b=100), and betting is only +EV if the opponent folds >~9.8% of the time. There’s also the realistic risk of facing a shove from the short‑stacked Pro, which would force a difficult decision with only a draw.

Plan: keep the pot small and realize equity from position — check. If Pro makes a small probe I will call to see the river (good pot odds and implied odds to hit the flush). If he jams big I’ll fold to preserve stack (unless pot odds/implied odds justify a call). If the river gives me my flush I’ll value bet; if I miss I’ll re‑evaluate a river bluff based on Pro’s river action.

I hold bottom pair (Deuces) and have picked up a Ten-high flush draw on the turn (Qh 2s 7c 8h). The pot is small (200) in a limped blind-vs-blind battle. The Small Blind has checked to me on both the flop and turn, signalling a likely weak range. While I could bet to deny equity to overcards, checking back is superior as it guarantees I realize my equity by seeing the river card for free. If I hit my flush or improve on the river, I can value bet then. If I miss, my pair of deuces still has some showdown value. Avoiding a check-raise situation preserves my stack and equity.

I have 3♥9♥ and the river (Q♥‑2♠‑7♣‑8♥‑A♥) completes a five‑card heart flush (A‑high via the board). This is a strong value hand but not invulnerable — any K♥, J♥ or T♥ in Pro’s hand would beat my 9♥ as the third highest heart. Pro has shown a very passive line (check flop, check turn) and the pot is tiny (200) relative to both stacks (I = 15,700; Pro = 7,100). A small value bet is optimal: it extracts calls from worse hands (Ax, medium pairs, smaller hearts, Kx) while keeping the risk limited. Betting 100 (≈1/2 pot) gives the villain ~33% break‑even calling frequency, which should induce thin calls from many hands in his passive range. If he raises/shoves big into this small pot it’s usually polarized to better hearts or a rare bluff — I’ll fold to a large jam to protect my stack. Plan: bet 100 for value; call a single‑raise if small, fold to a shove unless the sizing/polarization indicates a bluff.

I hold a flush with the Ten of Hearts (Board: Qh 2s 7c 8h Ah). My flush is the Ace-Queen-Ten-Eight-Two combination. This is the 3rd nut flush, losing only to a player holding the King of Hearts or Jack of Hearts. The opponent's line (limp preflop, check flop/turn, small 1/2 pot bet on river) is extremely passive and indicative of a marginal hand like top pair (Ax), a weak two pair, or a smaller flush. The small bet of 100 into 200 suggests a blocker bet or thin value. Given the strength of my hand relative to the opponent's perceived range, I can raise for value to extract chips from worse made hands that might feel committed to call a small raise. A raise to 500 targets these marginal hands while not risking too much of my stack against the small possibility of a higher flush.

Board: Qh 2s 7c 8h Ah. I bet 100 on the river and Pro raised to 500. Pot is 800 and I must call 400 more (total 500). Required equity = 400/(800+400) = 33.3%. I hold 3♥9♥ — an Ace‑high heart flush. That’s a strong but vulnerable hand: only a two‑heart combo containing K, J or T will beat me. Counting remaining hearts (K,J,T,7,6,5,4,2) gives C(8,2)=28 two‑heart combos; 18 of those contain at least one K/J/T and beat my 9‑high flush, 10 are lower and I beat them. Versus a pure two‑heart value range my equity ≈ 10/28 ≈ 36% (slightly above the 33% break‑even), and the raiser’s range will likely include some non‑heart bluffs/weak pairs that I also beat, so my real equity should be at least marginally higher. Raising myself is unnecessary and risky — a raise won’t extract much more value and exposes me to a shove by a better heart. Calling 400 (total 500 committed) gives correct pot odds, realizes equity, and preserves stack size. Therefore: call 500 (total).

I'm on the button with 6♠5♥ (offsuit) — a marginal but connected hand that plays well in position. There have been no raises; pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and build pots when I'm last to act. I raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): large enough to pick up the blinds often and to get some fold equity against both opponents, but small relative to my deep ~152BB stack so I can fold to heavy 3-bets without committing too much.

Plan if action continues: fold to a sizable 3‑bet from either opponent with this hand; call small 3‑bets only with pot‑odds and appropriate reads. If called, use positional advantage — C‑bet favorable low/connected flops, check and control the pot on dry high-card boards, and look for straight/2‑pair/paired board opportunities to continue. This open is an exploitative positional play to maximize chips with relatively little risk.

Current hand is 149/150. I am in the Small Blind with [Kd 9h]. Minni (BTN) opens to 300 (3BB). My stack is 7750 (77.5 BB). K9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a 3x raise which indicates some strength or a standard steal attempt. Calling leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind (who covers everyone) and puts me OOP post-flop. 3-betting is too risky with this holding. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve stack for the final hand.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Nine suited, a strong hand for defending against a button raise. Minni opened for 300 from the button, and the small blind folded. The pot odds are very favorable, needing to call 200 to win a total pot of 450. Given the late stage of the tournament (hand 149) and my significant chip lead, I will call to see the flop and play a controlled pot. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary given my stack protection goals.

I am the massive chip leader with only two hands remaining in the tournament. My primary goal is to protect my stack and avoid unnecessary confrontations. On this dry K-high flop, my J-high hand has little showdown value, though it possesses a backdoor diamond flush draw. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, to see her action.
