Given the tournament situation, I have no reason to bluff or play aggressively out of position against a player who could potentially damage my stack.

I raised preflop from the button and flopped a small made hand: pair of sixes (6♠ vs board 6♦ K♥ 2♣). Flash (BB) checked, pot is 650 and I have position. This is a good spot to take the initiative with a medium c-bet — my hand has value vs many of BB’s calling hands (small pairs, floats, suited connectors) and a half‑pot size keeps fold equity while not committing a large portion of my stack. I’ll bet 325 (≈50% pot) to get value from worse hands, deny free cards to overcards that might float, and retain the flexibility to fold to a sizable check‑raise. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; if I face substantial aggression I’ll give up with bottom pair.

I currently hold a massive chip lead with only two hands remaining in the tournament. My primary objective is to minimize risk and protect my stack to ensure a first-place finish. On this King-high board, my Jack-high hand has very little equity against Minni's range, which likely includes Kings, pocket pairs, or better. While I have a backdoor diamond draw, calling out of position against the only opponent who has a significant stack is unnecessary. I will fold here to avoid any potential for a large loss, as my current stack is more than sufficient to win the tournament regardless of this hand's outcome.

It is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am on the Button with a suited one-gapper (7h5h). My stack is 7750 (77.5 BBs), which is the shortest, but still deep. In a 3-handed game, opening suited connectors from the Button is a standard positive expected value play to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that retains equity well post-flop. Given the goal is to maximize stack size, taking +EV spots is the correct strategy. I will make a standard open raise to 2.5x the big blind.

This is the final hand of the tournament (Hand 150), and I am the significant chip leader with 36,650 chips. Pro (the button) has raised to 250. I am in the Small Blind with K6o, which is a marginal hand to defend out of position against a button raise, especially with the Big Blind (Minni) still to act. Since my primary goal is to maximize my stack and I am already the clear leader, there is no strategic reason to take a marginal or negative expected value (EV) risk in the very last hand. Folding preserves my stack and avoids any potential for a large loss to either Pro or Minni, securing my position as the top stack.

Facing a button raise to 250 from the only active opponent (SB folded). Pot is 400 and I need to call 150 more (total commitment 250) — break-even equity ≈ 27.3%. I have 8♥4♥ (suited, low) out of position. 8-4s is marginal, but vs a button opening range heads-up it has enough equity and playability (backdoor flush/straight potential) to defend for this price. A 3-bet here would be thin: the hand has poor fold equity and is hard to play OOP, and Pro can 4-bet or call and deny me postflop leverage. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed cautiously — continue with pairs, strong draws or two-way boards, and fold to heavy pressure on dry boards.

Board J♣ 2♦ 6♣ — I hold 8♥4♥: missed the flop, only backdoor heart/backdoor straight potential and no pair. I am out of position to an opener who raises a lot; betting OOP here as a bluff is thin because I have very little fold equity and will often be called by better hands. Best play is pot control: check and see a turn for free, folding to any sizable bet. If I get a very small bet I can call for the correct pot odds to chase backdoors, but otherwise preserve my stack and re-evaluate on the turn (bluff or turn-barrel only if the board and equities become favorable).

It is the final hand of the tournament. I opened pre-flop with 7h5h and the Big Blind called. The flop is 2d Jc 6c. This board texture connects reasonably well with a Big Blind defending range, which contains many Jx, 6x, 2x, and club flush draws. My hand has only 7-high with no immediate straight or flush draw (only backdoor straight potential). While a continuation bet is standard on many boards, doing so here with no equity when called and no blockers to the flush draw is risky, especially given the opponent's tendency to check-call or check-raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (hitting a 5, 7, 3, or 4 on the turn) for free and avoids inflating the pot with a very weak hand. I will control the pot size and check.

Check. The pot is small (550) and leading as a bluff here has very little fold equity; I’d be routinely called by Qx/Jx/pair/overcards and can get raised into tough decisions. Best play is pot control: check and see the river for free. If Pro fires the river I’ll fold to large bets and only call very small bets that give correct pot odds for ≈13% equity (roughly ≤15% of the pot).

It is the final hand (#150) of the tournament. I am on the Button with 7h5h. The pot is 550. I checked back the flop on 2d Jc 6c. The turn is the Qs. My opponent (Minni) checks to me again. I have 7-high and very little equity. Minni has shown a tendency to check-call and play passively with made hands. The Queen hits the Big Blind defending range (Qx, QJ, QT) as well as the Jack connected with the flop. Attempting to bluff here against a sticky opponent who might have paired the Queen or Jack, or holding a pair of 6s/2s, is risky. Checking back allows me to see a free river where I might improve to a pair or find a better bluffing opportunity, while preserving my stack in the final hand. There is no need to bloat the pot with zero showdown value.

I missed completely with 8♥4♥ on 2♦ J♣ 6♣ Q♠ A♣. I have no pair and no club; the river completed a three-club board so many hands in Pro’s preflop raising range now beat me (flushes, A‑x clubs, Jx/Qx, pairs). I have almost no fold equity and I don’t block the strong club/A/Q combos, so a river bluff is thin and likely to be called or raised into a bad spot. Pot control is the best line — check to showdown and preserve my 15.3k stack. (If Pro bets large I’d fold; only a tiny bet would be worth a call on correct pot odds.)

It is the final hand of the tournament (#150). I have 7h5h on a board of 2d Jc 6c Qs Ac. I missed all my draws and hold 7-high, which has zero showdown value. The river card (Ace of Clubs) completes a possible flush and puts a third broadway card on the board. While this is a scare card that I could theoretically represent, my line of checking back the flop and turn makes it difficult to credibly represent a strong hand like a flush or a strong Ace, as I would likely have bet those earlier to build the pot or protect equity. My opponent (Minni) defended the Big Blind and has checked three streets. Her range likely includes weak pairs (2x, 6x, Jx), Queens, or random Ax hands that she is potting controlling, as well as completed flushes she decided to check. Given that the Ace hits the Big Blind defending range quite hard (Ax is a common defend), and my check-check-bet line looks suspicious, the likelihood of generating a fold from a pair or better is questionable. In a stack-maximization scenario, risking chips on a low-probability bluff for a small pot (550) is less optimal than preserving the 7500 chips I currently have. I will check back and concede the pot.
